name: AAC (Advanced Audio Coding)

beginner.oneLiner: AAC is a widely used audio compression format that provides better sound quality than MP3 at the same file size, commonly found on Apple platforms and streaming services.
beginner.analogy: If MP3 is a reliable older car that everyone knows how to drive, AAC is the next model year — same idea, more efficient engine, better mileage (quality per file size). It is the default on iPhones and iTunes, so you have almost certainly heard AAC audio.
beginner.whatToDo: If you are targeting iOS or macOS, AAC is a natural choice for compressed audio since Apple platforms have hardware-accelerated AAC decoding. For cross-platform games, Vorbis (Ogg) is often simpler since it avoids licensing considerations.

intermediate.oneLiner: AAC is a lossy audio codec standardized in MPEG-2 and MPEG-4 that offers improved coding efficiency over MP3, supporting bitrates from 8 kbps to 529 kbps with profiles like AAC-LC, HE-AAC, and HE-AACv2.
intermediate.details: AAC-LC (Low Complexity) is the most common profile, used at 128-256 kbps for music. HE-AAC (High Efficiency) adds spectral band replication for better low-bitrate performance (32-80 kbps), ideal for streaming. HE-AACv2 adds parametric stereo for very low bitrates. AAC supports up to 48 channels, sample rates up to 96 kHz, and is the mandatory audio codec for MPEG-4 video. Container formats include .m4a, .mp4, and .aac. Quality-wise, AAC-LC at 128 kbps is roughly equivalent to MP3 at 160-192 kbps.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use AAC when targeting Apple platforms where hardware decoding is available, or when your audio middleware supports it. For mobile games on iOS, AAC can reduce CPU overhead through hardware decoding. For cross-platform projects, weigh AAC against Vorbis: Vorbis is open and royalty-free, while AAC may require licensing depending on distribution.

advanced.oneLiner: AAC is an MPEG-standardized perceptual audio codec using MDCT, temporal noise shaping, and psychoacoustic modeling to achieve high coding efficiency, with multiple profiles trading complexity for compression performance.
advanced.technicalNotes: AAC uses a 1024 or 960-sample MDCT with Kaiser-Bessel derived windows, temporal noise shaping (TNS), perceptual noise substitution, and mid/side stereo coding. The encoder is not standardized — only the bitstream and decoder are — so encoder quality varies (Apple's CoreAudio encoder and Fraunhofer FDK AAC are considered best). For games, the licensing situation is nuanced: decoding is free on platforms with built-in decoders (iOS, Android), but distributing your own decoder may require a patent license from Via Licensing. Latency is moderate (~40-50 ms for AAC-LC at standard frame sizes). For real-time applications, Opus is preferred.

vs.mp3: AAC provides roughly 25-30% better compression efficiency than MP3 at equivalent quality. MP3 is patent-free (since 2017) and universally supported. AAC has broader channel support and better low-bitrate performance.
vs.opus: Opus outperforms AAC at nearly all bitrates and has lower latency, and is fully royalty-free. AAC has wider hardware decoder support on mobile platforms, which can save CPU.
vs.vorbis: Vorbis and AAC-LC are comparable in quality at typical music bitrates. Vorbis is fully open and royalty-free. AAC has hardware decoding advantages on Apple and some Android devices.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a dedicated AAC import option. On iOS, Unity can leverage the platform's hardware AAC decoder. Import audio as WAV and select Vorbis compression in import settings for cross-platform use.
unityAdapter.apiRef: No direct AAC API in Unity. On iOS, AudioClip loading may use the system's AAC decoder transparently if the source file is AAC-encoded.
unityAdapter.importTip: For iOS-specific builds, you can use the 'Hardware Decoding' option in the platform-specific audio import settings to leverage the device's AAC hardware decoder, reducing CPU load for music playback.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Do not assume AAC hardware decoding is available on all platforms. Android hardware AAC decoding varies by device. For reliable cross-platform behavior, use Vorbis compression in Unity's import settings.

misconceptions: AAC is an Apple-only format. AAC is an international standard (ISO/IEC 13818-7 and 14496-3) supported on Android, Windows, and most platforms. Apple simply adopted it as their default.
AAC is always better than MP3. At very high bitrates (256+ kbps), the difference between AAC and MP3 is negligible for most listeners. The advantage is most noticeable at lower bitrates.
AAC is lossless. AAC is lossy compression. For lossless, Apple uses ALAC. AAC discards audio data deemed inaudible by psychoacoustic models.

name: Additive Synthesis

beginner.oneLiner: Additive synthesis builds complex sounds by layering many simple sine waves together at different pitches and volumes.
beginner.analogy: Think of an orchestra: each instrument plays one note (a sine wave). When they all play together at different pitches and volumes, they create a rich, complex sound. Additive synthesis works the same way, stacking simple tones to build any sound you want.
beginner.whatToDo: Use additive synthesis when you want to build sounds from scratch with precise control over every harmonic. It is great for creating evolving pads, bell-like tones, organ sounds, and unique sci-fi textures. Start with a few harmonics and add more to increase complexity.

intermediate.oneLiner: Additive synthesis constructs complex timbres by summing multiple sine wave oscillators (partials) with independent control over each partial's frequency, amplitude, and phase over time.
intermediate.details: Based on Fourier theory, any sound can be represented as a sum of sine waves. In additive synthesis, you control each partial individually: its frequency (harmonic or inharmonic), amplitude envelope, and phase. Harmonic partials (integer multiples of the fundamental) create pitched, musical tones. Inharmonic partials (non-integer ratios) create metallic, bell-like, or noisy textures. The power of additive synthesis lies in animating each partial's amplitude over time, enabling spectral morphing and evolving textures that are difficult to achieve with other methods.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use additive synthesis for bell and chime sounds, evolving ambient textures, organ tones, and resynthesizing analyzed recordings. It excels at sounds where you need precise spectral control. In game audio, it is particularly useful for procedural UI sounds, magical effects, and morphing environmental tones that respond to game parameters.

advanced.oneLiner: Additive synthesis implements the inverse Fourier transform in real time, summing N sinusoidal partials with time-varying amplitude, frequency, and phase envelopes to construct arbitrary spectral content.
advanced.technicalNotes: The output is y(t) = sum(A_k(t) * sin(2*pi*f_k(t)*t + phi_k)) for k=1..N partials. CPU cost scales linearly with partial count. For real-time game audio, limit to 32-128 partials and use wavetable lookup for the sine function. Inverse FFT (IFFT) synthesis is an optimization: set spectral bins directly and perform IFFT per frame, amortizing the cost. Spectral analysis (via FFT or STFT) of recorded sounds can extract partial data for resynthesis or morphing. In Unity, implement in OnAudioFilterRead by maintaining an array of oscillator states (phase accumulators) and summing their outputs. Group parameter updates per audio buffer to reduce overhead. Band-limited synthesis is not a concern since sine waves have no harmonics to alias.

vs.subtractive-synthesis: Subtractive synthesis starts with harmonically rich waveforms and removes content with filters. Additive synthesis starts with nothing and builds up content by adding individual partials, offering more precision but at higher CPU cost.
vs.fm-synthesis: FM synthesis generates complex spectra by modulating one oscillator's frequency with another, producing many sidebands efficiently. Additive synthesis controls each spectral component individually but requires more oscillators to achieve similar complexity.
vs.wavetable-synthesis: Wavetable synthesis reads from pre-computed waveform tables, which can approximate additive results at lower CPU cost but with less real-time control over individual partials.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in additive synthesis. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead or use a third-party synth plugin.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create an array of phase accumulators (one per partial). Each frame, increment each phase by (2 * pi * frequency / sampleRate) and sum sin(phase) * amplitude for all partials. Normalize the output by dividing by the number of active partials to prevent clipping.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Additive synthesis with many partials is CPU-intensive. In a game context, pre-render complex additive sounds to audio files and reserve real-time synthesis for simple procedural elements with fewer than 32 partials.

misconceptions: Additive synthesis is not limited to harmonic sounds. By using non-integer frequency ratios between partials, you can create inharmonic, noisy, and metallic timbres.
More partials does not always mean a better sound. A well-designed sound with 8-16 carefully tuned partials can sound richer than hundreds of poorly configured ones.

Additive synthesis is not obsolete. While it is computationally heavier than subtractive or FM synthesis, modern hardware makes it practical, and its precision is unmatched for certain sound design tasks.

name: ADPCM

beginner.oneLiner: ADPCM is a lightweight compression format that makes audio files about 3.5 times smaller with very little CPU cost to play back.
beginner.analogy: Imagine writing down only the changes between each step instead of the full position each time — like saying 'move 2 steps right' instead of 'I'm at position 47.' That's how ADPCM saves space.
beginner.whatToDo: Use ADPCM for short-to-medium sound effects that you play frequently, like gunshots, footsteps, or item pickups. It's a great middle ground between uncompressed PCM and heavier Vorbis compression.

intermediate.oneLiner: ADPCM (Adaptive Differential Pulse Code Modulation) encodes the difference between consecutive samples with an adaptive step size, achieving roughly 3.5:1 compression with minimal CPU overhead.
intermediate.details: Instead of storing absolute sample values, ADPCM stores the delta (difference) between consecutive samples using 4 bits per sample instead of 16. The 'adaptive' part means the quantization step size adjusts dynamically based on signal characteristics. This yields a fixed 3.5:1 compression ratio over 16-bit PCM. Decode cost is a simple table lookup and addition per sample, making it far cheaper than Vorbis or MP3 decoding. Quality is lower than Vorbis at equivalent file sizes, with some added noise floor, but this is rarely noticeable for sound effects.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use ADPCM for medium-length sound effects (1-10 seconds) that are played frequently — weapon sounds, character vocalizations, environmental SFX. It reduces memory by ~3.5x without meaningful CPU cost. Avoid it for music or detailed ambience where the added noise floor may be audible.

advanced.oneLiner: ADPCM encodes 16-bit PCM as 4-bit adaptive deltas with per-block predictors, offering fixed 3.5:1 compression and O(n) decode with negligible CPU cost per sample.
advanced.technicalNotes: IMA ADPCM (the variant used by most game engines) processes audio in blocks of 1017 samples (for stereo) or 2036 (mono). Each block has a header containing the initial predictor value and step index. Decoding is a tight loop: read 4-bit nibble, look up step size in a 89-entry table, compute sample = predictor + (nibble * step), update step index. This is branch-free and cache-friendly. The fixed 4-bit encoding means compression ratio is constant regardless of content. Quantization noise is most audible on quiet, tonal content (sustained pads, sine tones). For transient-heavy SFX, artifacts are masked by the signal. Unity's ADPCM implementation stores data in a block-interleaved format compatible with streaming.

vs.pcm: ADPCM uses ~3.5x less memory with barely measurable CPU increase. The tradeoff is a slightly raised noise floor, roughly -40 dB SNR loss.
vs.vorbis: Vorbis achieves 5-15x compression with better quality, but decode cost is 10-50x higher per sample. ADPCM is better for high-polyphony scenarios (many simultaneous sounds).
vs.mp3: MP3 offers higher compression than ADPCM with better quality but much higher CPU cost. ADPCM is preferred for latency-sensitive, high-frequency playback.

unityAdapter.location: AudioClip Inspector > Compression Format > ADPCM
unityAdapter.importTip: ADPCM with 'Compressed In Memory' is the sweet spot for most sound effects. You get 3.5x memory savings with almost no CPU overhead. Pair this with 'Load In Background' for large SFX banks.
unityAdapter.gotcha: ADPCM is a lossy format despite sometimes being grouped with PCM. Repeatedly encoding to ADPCM from ADPCM (e.g., re-importing processed files) will degrade quality. Always keep WAV originals.

misconceptions: ADPCM is not lossless. It introduces quantization noise, especially on quiet or tonal signals. It's lossy, just with a different and simpler compression approach than MP3 or Vorbis.
The compression ratio is always ~3.5:1 regardless of content. Unlike Vorbis or MP3, you cannot trade quality for smaller files — the ratio is fixed by the 4-bit encoding.
ADPCM is not outdated or low quality. It remains one of the most practical formats for game SFX due to its decode efficiency, and is actively used in AAA titles.

name: ADSR Envelope

beginner.oneLiner: ADSR describes how a sound's volume changes over time, from the moment it starts to when it fades away.
beginner.analogy: Think of pressing a piano key: the sound quickly rises when you press it (Attack), drops slightly to a steady level (Decay to Sustain), then fades when you release the key (Release). ADSR is like a blueprint for that volume shape.
beginner.whatToDo: When importing sounds for your game, listen for how they start and end. If a sound cuts off too abruptly, you likely need a longer Release. If it feels sluggish at the start, shorten the Attack.

intermediate.oneLiner: ADSR is a four-stage envelope that shapes amplitude over time: Attack (ramp up), Decay (initial drop), Sustain (held level), and Release (fade after note-off).
intermediate.details: Attack is the time from silence to peak amplitude. Decay is the time from peak to the Sustain level. Sustain is not a time value but an amplitude level held as long as the note is active. Release is the time from note-off to silence. These four parameters let you sculpt percussive hits (short A, no S), pads (long A, high S, long R), or plucks (short A, medium D, low S).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use ADSR shaping on synthesized UI sounds, weapon charge-ups, engine loops, or any procedurally generated audio. In FMOD/Wwise, ADSR-like automation curves on volume give dynamic control without needing multiple audio files.

advanced.oneLiner: ADSR is a piecewise envelope generator, typically implemented as a state machine driving an amplitude multiplier through linear or exponential segments.
advanced.technicalNotes: Exponential curves sound more natural than linear ones because human loudness perception is logarithmic. Many implementations use a leaky integrator per stage: output += (target - output) * coefficient. Watch for zipper noise when envelope parameters change mid-note; smoothing or per-sample interpolation is essential. In Unity, AnimationCurve can model arbitrary envelopes beyond simple ADSR, evaluated via AnimationCurve.Evaluate(t) in OnAudioFilterRead.

vs.ar-envelope: AR (Attack-Release) is a simplified two-stage envelope with no Decay or Sustain, useful for simple one-shot transients.
vs.multi-stage-envelope: Multi-stage envelopes (DAHDSR, MSEG) add extra segments like Hold or multiple Decay stages for more complex shaping.

unityAdapter.location: Custom scripting via MonoBehaviour using OnAudioFilterRead or AnimationCurve for envelope shaping.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create an AnimationCurve with four keys representing A, D, S, and R stages. Evaluate it over time to multiply against your audio signal's amplitude in OnAudioFilterRead.
unityAdapter.gotcha: OnAudioFilterRead runs on the audio thread, not the main thread. Avoid allocations, Debug.Log, or accessing most Unity APIs inside it. Use lock-free communication (volatile fields or NativeArray) to pass envelope parameters from the main thread.

misconceptions: Sustain is a time value. It is actually an amplitude level; the sound stays at that level until the note-off event, regardless of duration.
ADSR only applies to synthesizers. Game engines use the same concept for volume fades, crossfades, and dynamic mix automation.

name: AIFF

beginner.oneLiner: AIFF is an uncompressed audio format from Apple — functionally the same as WAV but more common on Mac-based audio workflows.
beginner.analogy: AIFF and WAV are like two different brands of identical shipping containers — the contents (your audio) are the same, just the label on the outside is different.
beginner.whatToDo: If your sound designer delivers AIFF files, import them directly into your engine. They work just like WAV. No need to convert beforehand.

intermediate.oneLiner: AIFF (Audio Interchange File Format) is Apple's uncompressed PCM audio container, functionally equivalent to WAV with identical quality and file sizes.
intermediate.details: AIFF uses the IFF (Interchange File Format) container from Electronic Arts/Amiga lineage, adopted by Apple. It stores the same linear PCM data as WAV. AIFF-C is a variant that supports compression (including IMA ADPCM), but it is rarely used in game development. File sizes are identical to WAV for the same sample rate, bit depth, and channel count.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use AIFF when your audio pipeline is Mac-based or when collaborating with sound designers who work in Logic Pro or other Apple DAWs. For cross-platform teams, WAV is the safer default since Windows tools have broader WAV support.

advanced.oneLiner: AIFF is a big-endian IFF container for linear PCM data, with identical audio fidelity to WAV's little-endian RIFF container.
advanced.technicalNotes: The primary technical difference is byte order: AIFF uses big-endian storage while WAV uses little-endian. Modern audio engines and tools handle both transparently. AIFF supports embedded metadata chunks (markers, comments, instrument data) which some DAWs use for loop points. Like WAV, AIFF has a practical 4 GB file size limit in its standard form. AIFF-C (compressed) exists but is not widely used in games.

vs.wav: Functionally identical for game audio purposes. WAV is more common in Windows-centric and cross-platform game development. AIFF is more common in Apple/pro-audio ecosystems. Both store uncompressed PCM with the same quality and size.
vs.ogg: Same tradeoff as WAV vs OGG — AIFF is uncompressed and large; OGG is lossy-compressed and small. Use AIFF as source, compress to OGG for builds.

unityAdapter.location: Import .aif or .aiff files into Assets. Unity treats them identically to WAV in the Audio Clip Inspector.
unityAdapter.importTip: All the same import settings and compression options available for WAV apply to AIFF. Choose your compression format based on the clip's use case, not the source format.
unityAdapter.gotcha: There is no quality or performance difference between importing AIFF versus WAV into Unity. The engine decodes both to raw PCM and then applies your chosen compression setting at build time.

misconceptions: AIFF is not higher quality than WAV — both are uncompressed PCM containers with identical audio fidelity.
You do not need to convert AIFF to WAV before importing into Unity or most game engines. They handle both formats natively.

name: Ambisonics

beginner.oneLiner: Ambisonics is a way of recording or encoding sound that captures a full 3D soundfield, so it can be played back from any direction around the listener.
beginner.analogy: Imagine placing a microphone in the center of a room that records sound from every direction at once — up, down, left, right, front, back — like a 360-degree camera but for audio. When you play it back, you can rotate your head and the sound stays fixed in the real world, just like in VR.
beginner.whatToDo: Use ambisonic audio files for immersive environment beds or 360-degree ambient recordings. Import them as ambisonic clips and let your spatialization plugin decode them based on the listener's orientation.

intermediate.oneLiner: Ambisonics is a full-sphere surround sound format that encodes a soundfield into spherical harmonic components, allowing speaker-layout-independent playback and real-time rotation.
intermediate.details: First-order ambisonics (FOA) uses four channels (W, X, Y, Z) representing the omnidirectional pressure and three figure-eight directional components. Higher-order ambisonics (HOA) adds more channels for sharper spatial resolution. The key advantage is that the format is independent of any particular speaker layout — the same B-format file can be decoded to stereo, 5.1, 7.1, or binaural headphones.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use ambisonics for VR environment beds, 360-degree video audio, and ambient soundscapes where the listener can rotate freely. For point sources in games, standard mono sources with a spatializer are usually more efficient. Ambisonics shines for pre-recorded environmental audio that needs to respond to head tracking.

advanced.oneLiner: Ambisonics represents a soundfield as a truncated spherical harmonic decomposition, with order N requiring (N+1)² channels, enabling resolution-scalable encoding and format-agnostic decoding to arbitrary playback configurations.
advanced.technicalNotes: First-order ambisonics (4 channels) gives roughly ±30° localization blur; third-order (16 channels) narrows this to roughly ±10°. AmbiX (ACN/SN3D) is the modern standard, replacing the older FuMa convention. Decoding involves multiplying the B-format signal by a speaker-specific or HRTF-specific decoding matrix. For binaural decoding, each spherical harmonic channel is convolved with corresponding HRTF filters. In games, ambisonics is often used as an intermediate bus: point sources are encoded into an ambisonic bus, then the bus is decoded once for the output format, reducing per-source HRTF convolutions.

vs.spatialization: Spatialization positions individual mono sources in 3D space. Ambisonics encodes an entire soundfield into a speaker-independent format. You can spatialize mono sources into an ambisonic bus, then decode the bus for output.
vs.binaural-audio: Binaural audio is a two-channel format designed specifically for headphone playback. Ambisonics is a multi-channel intermediate format that can be decoded to binaural, surround, or any other layout.

unityAdapter.location: Import an ambisonic audio file and Unity will detect it automatically. Set the AudioSource to use the Ambisonic flag. Requires a spatializer plugin that supports ambisonic decoding (e.g., Resonance Audio, Steam Audio).
unityAdapter.importTip: In the AudioClip import settings, enable 'Ambisonic' to tell Unity the file is B-format rather than standard multichannel audio. Pair it with a spatializer plugin that handles ambisonic decoding for your target output format.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in spatializer does not decode ambisonics — you need a third-party plugin like Resonance Audio or Steam Audio. Without one, ambisonic clips will play as flat multichannel audio with no spatial rotation.

misconceptions: Ambisonics is the same as surround sound — surround sound is tied to a specific speaker layout (5.1, 7.1), while ambisonics is layout-independent and can be decoded to any configuration including headphones.
You need special hardware to use ambisonics — any standard headphones or speaker setup can play decoded ambisonic audio. The encoding is the special part, not the playback.
Higher-order ambisonics is always better — more channels mean sharper localization but also more CPU, memory, and bandwidth. First-order is often sufficient for ambient beds in games.

name: Amplitude

beginner.oneLiner: Amplitude is how strong or loud a sound wave is. Higher amplitude means louder sound.
beginner.analogy: Think of a wave at the beach. A tiny ripple has low amplitude — you barely notice it. A huge wave has high amplitude — it knocks you over. Sound works the same way: bigger waves in the air mean louder sounds.
beginner.whatToDo: When you adjust volume sliders in your game, you're changing amplitude. Keep your sound effects at a consistent amplitude level so players don't get blasted by one effect and can barely hear another.

intermediate.oneLiner: Amplitude is the magnitude of a sound wave's pressure variation, determining its perceived loudness. In digital audio, it's the sample value at any given point in time.
intermediate.details: In PCM audio, each sample is an amplitude measurement. For 16-bit audio, values range from -32768 to +32767. Normalized float audio uses -1.0 to +1.0. Peak amplitude is the maximum absolute value in a signal. RMS (Root Mean Square) amplitude better correlates with perceived loudness because it accounts for the signal over time, not just peaks. Doubling the amplitude of a signal increases its level by ~6 dB. Amplitude is independent of frequency — a quiet high-pitched sound and a quiet low-pitched sound can have the same amplitude.
intermediate.whenToUse: Normalize your sound effects to a consistent peak amplitude (e.g., -1 dBFS peak) during production so you have a baseline for mixing. Use RMS-based loudness matching (like LUFS) for more perceptually even results. In-engine, control volume via the AudioSource volume property (0.0 to 1.0 maps linearly to amplitude).

advanced.oneLiner: Amplitude is the instantaneous or statistical magnitude of a digital audio signal, measured as peak, RMS, or perceptual loudness (LUFS), and is the primary domain affected by bit depth quantization.
advanced.technicalNotes: In the time domain, amplitude is the sample value. In the frequency domain, amplitude of each bin represents the magnitude of that frequency component. Peak amplitude is trivial to compute but poorly correlates with loudness; RMS over a window (typically 300-400ms) is better; LUFS (Loudness Units Full Scale, ITU-R BS.1770) applies K-weighting and gating for the best perceptual match. In Unity, AudioSource.volume is a linear amplitude multiplier applied before the mixer. For proper dB-based volume control, convert: amplitude = Mathf.Pow(10, dB / 20f). Clipping occurs when amplitude exceeds the representable range (> 1.0 in float, > 32767 in 16-bit int), producing harsh distortion.

vs.frequency: Amplitude determines loudness; frequency determines pitch. They are independent properties of a sound wave. A sound can be loud at any pitch, and quiet at any pitch.
vs.decibel: Amplitude is the raw measurement; decibels are a logarithmic scale for expressing amplitude ratios. A 6 dB increase corresponds to doubling the amplitude.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > Volume slider (0 to 1)
unityAdapter.importTip: AudioSource.volume = 1.0 means 'play at the amplitude stored in the clip.' Values above 1.0 are not supported on AudioSource directly. Use an AudioMixer group with a positive gain if you need to boost beyond original level.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.volume is linear, not logarithmic. Setting volume to 0.5 halves the amplitude (~-6 dB), but humans perceive this as only a moderate reduction. For a perceptually linear volume slider, use a logarithmic curve: volume = Mathf.Pow(sliderValue, 2.5) or similar.

misconceptions: Amplitude and loudness are related but not the same. Loudness is a perceptual measure that depends on frequency content, duration, and human hearing sensitivity. A sound with high amplitude at 3 kHz will seem louder than the same amplitude at 100 Hz.
Doubling the amplitude does not double the perceived loudness. Perceived loudness roughly doubles with every 10 dB increase (~3.16x amplitude increase).
Clipping is not just 'too loud' — it replaces the peaks of the waveform with flat lines, introducing harsh harmonics. Always leave headroom in your mix.

name: Attenuation

beginner.oneLiner: Attenuation is how sound gets quieter the farther you are from it, just like in real life.
beginner.analogy: Stand next to someone talking and they sound loud. Walk fifty meters away and they are much quieter. Walk another fifty meters and you can barely hear them. Attenuation is this natural fading of volume with distance, and games simulate it so closer sounds are louder and distant sounds are softer.
beginner.whatToDo: On your 3D AudioSources, set the min distance (where sound is at full volume) and max distance (where it becomes silent or nearly silent). Choose a rolloff mode: Logarithmic for realistic falloff, Linear for predictable behavior, or Custom for hand-tuned curves.

intermediate.oneLiner: Attenuation is the reduction of a sound's amplitude as a function of distance between the source and listener, governed by rolloff curves that model how quickly volume decreases.
intermediate.details: Rolloff modes: Logarithmic rolloff follows the inverse-square law (volume halves every doubling of distance), which is physically accurate. Linear rolloff decreases volume at a constant rate from min to max distance, which is simpler to predict and design for. Custom rolloff uses a hand-drawn AnimationCurve for full control. Min distance defines the radius of full volume. Max distance defines where the sound reaches its minimum volume. In logarithmic mode, the sound technically never reaches zero, so max distance defines a practical cutoff.
intermediate.whenToUse: Configure attenuation for every 3D sound in your game. Use logarithmic rolloff for general realism. Use linear rolloff when you need predictable, easy-to-design distance behavior (common in many shipped games). Use custom curves when you need specific behavior: for example, a fire that stays audible over a large area then quickly drops off, or a whisper that fades very fast. Set min distance based on the source's size — a bonfire might have a 2m min distance, a whisper might have 0.5m.

advanced.oneLiner: Attenuation models the inverse-square law or custom transfer functions mapping source-listener distance to gain, with rolloff curves controlling the perceptual falloff and max distance defining the culling boundary for voice management.
advanced.technicalNotes: Real-world sound follows inverse-square law: intensity decreases proportional to 1/r^2 (6 dB per doubling of distance). Unity's logarithmic rolloff approximates this. For large worlds, logarithmic rolloff can keep distant sounds barely audible consuming voices without being heard — use max distance as a hard culling boundary. Custom curves (AnimationCurve) offer the best design control; many AAA games use custom S-curves that maintain audibility at mid-range then drop sharply. For voice management, sounds beyond max distance should be virtualized (stop playing but retain state) to free audio channels. Consider frequency-dependent attenuation for extra realism: air absorbs high frequencies faster than lows, so very distant sounds should also be low-pass filtered. This is called air absorption and is available in middleware and spatializer plugins.

vs.occlusion: Attenuation is distance-based volume reduction in open space. Occlusion is barrier-based filtering and volume reduction. They stack: a sound can be both far away (attenuated) and behind a wall (occluded).
vs.doppler-effect: Attenuation changes volume based on distance. Doppler changes pitch based on relative velocity. They are independent effects that combine for realistic 3D audio.
vs.spatialization: Spatialization handles directional placement (where in the stereo/surround field). Attenuation handles volume over distance (how loud from here). Together they define the full spatial character of a sound.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > 3D Sound Settings. Configure Volume Rolloff (Logarithmic, Linear, or Custom Rolloff), Min Distance, and Max Distance. The curve editor is available when Custom Rolloff is selected.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set rolloffMode to AudioRolloffMode.Custom and assign a custom AnimationCurve to AudioSource.SetCustomCurve(AudioSourceCurveType.CustomRolloff, curve) for precise falloff control. Min distance is the radius at which the sound is full volume. Max distance is where the rolloff curve reaches its end. Use the Scene view audio visualization (click the audio toggle) to see rolloff gizmos.
unityAdapter.gotcha: With Logarithmic Rolloff, the Max Distance does NOT mean the sound is silent at that range — it means the rolloff calculation stops there and the sound stays at whatever volume it has reached. Sounds can remain faintly audible well beyond max distance. If you need silence at max distance, use Linear or Custom rolloff. Also, changing Max Distance with Logarithmic Rolloff has minimal effect on the curve shape — it mainly affects Min Distance.

misconceptions: Max Distance means the sound is silent at that range — with Logarithmic Rolloff in Unity, max distance is where the rolloff curve flattens, not where volume hits zero. The sound may still be faintly audible.

Linear rolloff is unrealistic and should be avoided — many shipped games use linear rolloff because it is predictable and easy to design for. Realism is less important than the sound behaving how you need it to.

Attenuation only affects volume — advanced attenuation systems also apply frequency-dependent filtering (air absorption), where distant sounds lose high frequencies, adding realism beyond simple volume reduction.

You should use the same attenuation settings for all sounds — different sounds need different curves. A thunderclap is audible for kilometers; a whisper fades in meters. Tune min/max distance per sound type.

name: Audio Bank

beginner.oneLiner: An audio bank is a package file that bundles together a group of related sounds so your game can load them as one unit, like a folder of sounds compressed into a single file.
beginner.analogy: Think of a bank as a suitcase packed for a specific trip. Instead of grabbing individual items from your entire closet every time you need something, you pack one suitcase with everything you need for a beach vacation and another for a ski trip. Audio banks work the same way — you pack all the combat sounds into one bank, all the menu sounds into another, and load only the banks you need for the current game situation.
beginner.whatToDo: In FMOD or Wwise, organize your sounds into logical banks: one for the main menu, one for common gameplay sounds, one per level for level-specific audio. Load the common bank at startup and load/unload level banks as the player moves between areas. This keeps memory usage manageable.

intermediate.oneLiner: An audio bank (SoundBank) is a binary file containing encoded audio assets and their associated event metadata, built by middleware authoring tools and loaded/unloaded at runtime to manage memory and loading times.
intermediate.details: Banks serve two purposes: they package audio data efficiently (often with platform-specific encoding) and they control memory granularity. Rather than loading every sound in your game into memory, you load specific banks based on context. Bank organization strategies: Init/Master bank (always loaded, contains bus structure and global events), Common bank (frequently used sounds loaded at startup), Level-specific banks (loaded during level transitions), On-demand banks (loaded just before needed, e.g., cutscene audio). Banks can contain audio data in-memory (fast playback, uses RAM), streamed references (low memory, reads from disk at playback time), or a mix of both. FMOD uses .bank files; Wwise uses .bnk files. Both include encoded audio and event definitions.
intermediate.whenToUse: Always organize sounds into banks for middleware projects. Start with a Master bank (buses and global sounds), a Common bank (frequently used SFX), and per-level banks. Load Common at startup and swap level banks during loading screens. Monitor bank memory in the middleware profiler to ensure you are within platform budgets (mobile: 50-150 MB audio memory, desktop: 200-500 MB).

advanced.oneLiner: Audio banks are platform-specific binary containers produced by the middleware build pipeline, encapsulating Vorbis/ADPCM/Opus-encoded audio streams alongside serialized event metadata, loaded into managed heaps with explicit lifecycle control for runtime memory budgeting.
advanced.technicalNotes: FMOD banks: the Init bank contains the mixer bus topology and global DSP settings; it must be loaded first and unloaded last. Subsequent banks contain audio data and event definitions. The build process encodes audio to platform-optimal formats (Vorbis for desktop, ADPCM for mobile SFX, Opus for Switch). Bank loading is asynchronous and can target specific memory pools. Wwise SoundBanks: the Init.bnk contains bus hierarchy and plugin registrations. Media can be stored inline in the bank or in separate .wem files for more granular streaming. Wwise supports auto-banking (automatically grouping sounds based on event usage analysis). Memory management: both middleware track per-bank memory usage in their profilers. For large open-world games, use a streaming bank approach where banks are loaded/unloaded based on player position using trigger volumes. Bank serialization includes codec headers, sample maps, event graphs, and RTPC curve data.

vs.streaming: Banks store audio in memory; streaming reads from disk during playback. Banks offer instant playback; streaming reduces memory at the cost of disk I/O and slight latency. Banks often contain a mix: short SFX in-memory, long ambiences as stream references.
vs.compressed-in-memory: Compressed in memory is the loading strategy for audio within a bank. The bank is the container; compressed-in-memory is how the audio data inside it is stored and accessed at runtime.
vs.sound-event: Events are the playback triggers defined within banks. The bank contains both the event definitions (what to play, how to play it) and the audio data (the actual encoded samples).

unityAdapter.location: FMOD: Banks are built in FMOD Studio (File > Build) and placed in StreamingAssets/FMOD. Wwise: Banks are generated in the Wwise authoring tool (Generate SoundBanks) and placed in StreamingAssets/Wwise.
unityAdapter.apiRef: FMOD: FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.LoadBank(string bankName), FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.UnloadBank(string bankName). Wwise: AkBankManager.LoadBank(string bankName), AkBankManager.UnloadBank(string bankName)
unityAdapter.importTip: Load your Init/Master bank in a bootstrap scene that persists throughout the game. Load level banks during loading screens using async loading. Listen for the bank load completion callback before triggering events that depend on that bank's content, otherwise you will get silent events.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Triggering an FMOD event or Wwise event before its bank is loaded results in silence with no error in many configurations. Always verify bank load state before playing events, especially during scene transitions. Also, forgetting to rebuild banks after editing sounds in the authoring tool is the most common cause of 'my changes are not working.'

misconceptions: You should put all sounds in one bank — a single monolithic bank wastes memory by loading everything, including sounds not needed for the current game context. Split banks by usage pattern (level, category, frequency of use).

Banks load instantly — bank loading involves disk I/O and decompression. Large banks can take noticeable time to load, especially on mobile or consoles. Always load banks asynchronously during loading screens, not during gameplay.

Bank organization does not matter for small games — even small games benefit from separating the init bank from content banks. It establishes good practices and prevents memory issues as the project grows.

name: Audio Buffer Size

beginner.oneLiner: Buffer size is the chunk of audio data your game prepares at a time before sending it to your speakers.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a waiter carrying plates from the kitchen to your table. A small tray (small buffer) means food arrives faster but the waiter makes more trips and could drop something. A large tray (large buffer) means fewer trips but longer waits. Audio buffers work the same way: smaller is faster but riskier.
beginner.whatToDo: In your game engine's audio settings, choose a smaller buffer size for action games needing responsive audio, and a larger buffer for ambient or narrative games where stability matters more. Start with the engine default and adjust if you hear pops/clicks (buffer too small) or feel input lag (buffer too large).

intermediate.oneLiner: The audio buffer size determines how many samples are processed per callback cycle, directly trading off between latency and CPU stability.
intermediate.details: Common buffer sizes are 128, 256, 512, and 1024 samples. At 48 kHz: 128 samples = ~2.7 ms, 256 = ~5.3 ms, 512 = ~10.7 ms, 1024 = ~21.3 ms per buffer. Most systems double-buffer (ping-pong), so multiply by 2 for actual minimum latency. Smaller buffers mean the audio callback fires more frequently, giving the CPU less time to fill each buffer. If the CPU cannot fill the buffer in time, you get an underrun (audio glitch/pop). More complex audio graphs (many voices, effects, reverbs) require more CPU per callback, making smaller buffers harder to sustain.
intermediate.whenToUse: For rhythm games and competitive shooters, use 256 or fewer samples. For open-world games with dense audio, 512-1024 is safer. Profile on your lowest-spec target hardware. On mobile, 256 samples is often the practical minimum without glitches.

advanced.oneLiner: Buffer size defines the period of the real-time audio callback, governing the trade-off between scheduling granularity/latency and the probability of deadline misses (underruns).
advanced.technicalNotes: The audio callback must complete within (buffer_size / sample_rate) seconds. At 256 samples / 48 kHz, you have 5.3 ms to fill the buffer. Any processing that exceeds this (voice mixing, convolution reverb, FFT analysis) causes an underrun. Lock-free ring buffers are used between the game thread and audio thread to pass parameters without blocking. In Unity, the DSP Buffer Size setting maps to FMOD's internal buffer: 'Best Latency' = 256, 'Good Latency' = 512, 'Best Performance' = 1024 (values may vary by platform). On Android, buffer size must align with the device's native burst size (often 192 or 240 frames) for optimal AAudio performance.

vs.latency: Buffer size is the primary tunable for latency, but total latency also includes OS mixer, driver, and hardware delays. Reducing buffer size helps but cannot eliminate all latency.
vs.voice-count: More active voices mean more work per buffer callback. Reducing voice count (via priority culling) can allow smaller buffer sizes without underruns.

unityAdapter.location: Project Settings > Audio > DSP Buffer Size dropdown: Best Latency, Good Latency, Best Performance.
unityAdapter.importTip: Call AudioSettings.GetDSPBufferSize(out int bufferLength, out int numBuffers) at runtime to confirm actual values. Log this on each target platform since the OS may override your setting.
unityAdapter.gotcha: 'Best Latency' on Android can cause audio glitches if the device's native buffer size doesn't align with Unity's requested size. Test on multiple devices. Consider using Unity's Android Audio Latency settings or the Oboe plugin for finer control.

misconceptions: A 512-sample buffer means 512 ms of latency. At 48 kHz, 512 samples is about 10.7 ms, not 512 ms. Latency in milliseconds depends on both buffer size AND sample rate.
You should always use the smallest buffer possible. Too small a buffer on insufficient hardware causes audio glitches that are worse than slightly higher latency. Balance is key.

name: Audio Bus

beginner.oneLiner: An audio bus is a channel that groups multiple sounds together so you can control their volume and effects as a unit.
beginner.analogy: Think of a bus like a folder on your desktop. Instead of adjusting the volume of every single sound effect individually, you put all footstep sounds in one bus, all music in another, and all UI sounds in a third. Now you can turn down all footsteps at once by adjusting one slider.
beginner.whatToDo: Create at least three buses in your game: one for Music, one for Sound Effects, and one for UI. Route all relevant AudioSources to the correct bus. Expose the bus volumes in your settings menu so players can adjust them independently.

intermediate.oneLiner: An audio bus (or mixer group) is a signal routing path that sums multiple audio sources, allowing shared volume control, effects processing, and hierarchical mixing.
intermediate.details: Buses are organized in a hierarchy: individual sources feed into sub-buses (e.g., Footsteps, Weapons, Ambient), which feed into category buses (e.g., SFX, Music, Dialog), which all feed into the Master bus. Effects placed on a bus (EQ, compression, reverb sends) apply to all audio routed through it. This hierarchical structure mirrors film/TV mixing conventions (dialog, music, effects stems) and enables efficient mixing: adjust one fader to affect dozens of sounds. Buses also enable ducking (lowering SFX when dialog plays) and snapshots (reconfiguring the entire mix for different game states).
intermediate.whenToUse: Set up your bus hierarchy early in development. A typical game needs: Master > Music, Master > Dialog, Master > SFX > (Weapons, Footsteps, Ambient, Impacts), Master > UI. Add more sub-buses as your audio complexity grows. Expose top-level bus volumes as player-facing settings.

advanced.oneLiner: An audio bus is a summing node in a directed acyclic graph (DAG) audio routing topology, supporting insert effects, auxiliary sends, and hierarchical gain staging.
advanced.technicalNotes: In Unity's Audio Mixer, groups are buses. Each group can have insert effects (processed in order on the group's signal), send effects (tapped signal routed to an aux bus), and receive nodes. The mixer evaluates bottom-up: leaf groups mix first, then feed parent groups. Gain staging is critical: each bus adds headroom requirements. If four sources each peak at -6 dBFS, their sum on a bus can peak at +6 dBFS (coherent worst case). Apply attenuation at each bus level to prevent summing clipping. Use AudioMixer.SetFloat() to automate bus parameters at runtime (volume, effect wet/dry) and AudioMixer.TransitionToSnapshots() for blend-based mix state changes.

vs.send-effect: A bus processes its direct input signals with insert effects. A send taps a copy of a signal from one bus and routes it to an auxiliary bus (like a shared reverb), keeping the original dry signal intact.
vs.audio-source: An AudioSource is a single sound emitter. A bus aggregates multiple AudioSources for collective processing and volume control.

unityAdapter.location: Window > Audio > Audio Mixer. Create groups (buses) by right-clicking in the mixer hierarchy. Assign AudioSources to groups via the AudioSource component's Output field.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create your mixer hierarchy before placing AudioSources in scenes. Assign groups by dragging the AudioMixerGroup asset into the AudioSource's Output slot, or set it via script: audioSource.outputAudioMixerGroup = myGroup.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If an AudioSource has no Output group assigned, it routes directly to the AudioListener bypassing the mixer entirely. This means mixer effects, snapshots, and volume faders will not affect it. Always assign every AudioSource to a mixer group.

misconceptions: You only need a master volume slider. Without separate buses, players cannot independently adjust music, SFX, and dialog, which is an accessibility issue and a common player complaint.
Buses are only for volume control. Buses also host effects processing (EQ, compression, reverb), enable ducking, support snapshots, and provide metering for debugging mix issues.

name: Audio Callback

beginner.oneLiner: An audio callback is a function that the system calls repeatedly to ask your code for the next chunk of audio data, like a conveyor belt requesting the next batch of sound.
beginner.analogy: Think of a DJ at a radio station who has a light that blinks every few seconds saying 'give me the next 10 seconds of audio NOW.' The DJ has to have something ready every single time the light blinks. If they fumble and miss even once, there is dead air. An audio callback works the same way — your code must fill a buffer with samples before the deadline, every time, without fail.
beginner.whatToDo: In Unity, you can use the OnAudioFilterRead method to write custom audio processing. Keep it simple — only do math on the audio samples. Do not load files, create objects, or call most Unity APIs inside this function, because it runs on the audio thread and has strict timing requirements.

intermediate.oneLiner: An audio callback is a function invoked by the audio system on the audio thread at regular intervals, passing a buffer that your code must fill with processed samples before the next callback fires.
intermediate.details: The audio system calls your callback function with a float array (the buffer) and expects you to write sample data into it. The callback fires every bufferSize/sampleRate seconds (e.g., every 5.3 ms for 256 samples at 48 kHz). You can read the incoming samples (for effects processing), write new samples (for synthesis), or modify them in place (for filters). The callback runs on the audio thread, so all the real-time rules apply: no memory allocation, no locks, no I/O, no blocking calls. If your callback takes too long, the audio system does not wait — it outputs silence or stale data, causing an audible glitch.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use audio callbacks when you need custom DSP processing, procedural audio generation, or real-time audio analysis. In Unity, implement OnAudioFilterRead on a MonoBehaviour attached to a GameObject with an AudioSource. For Web Audio, use AudioWorkletProcessor.process(). Always pre-allocate any data structures you need before the callback starts running.

advanced.oneLiner: An audio callback is a hard-real-time function pointer registered with the audio HAL (Hardware Abstraction Layer), invoked by interrupt-driven scheduling at intervals of bufferSize/sampleRate seconds, executing on a high-priority thread with a strict deadline of one buffer period.
advanced.technicalNotes: The callback contract: you receive a pointer to an output buffer (and optionally an input buffer for effects), a frame count, and timing information. You must write exactly frameCount * channels samples and return within the buffer period. Exceeding the deadline causes an underrun. The callback should be branchless where possible, avoid cache misses (process samples sequentially), and never call functions with unbounded execution time. On iOS, exceeding the deadline repeatedly triggers the OS watchdog, which can kill your app. Common callback signatures: CoreAudio uses AURenderCallback, WASAPI uses IAudioRenderClient, ASIO uses bufferSwitch, PortAudio uses PaStreamCallback, Web Audio uses AudioWorkletProcessor.process(). In Unity, OnAudioFilterRead(float[] data, int channels) is called per-AudioSource in the DSP chain, allowing per-source processing.

vs.audio-thread: The audio thread is the OS-level thread. The audio callback is the function that runs on that thread. The thread is the execution context; the callback is your code within it.
vs.dsp: DSP is the processing you do inside the callback (filtering, synthesis, mixing). The callback is the scheduling mechanism that gives you the opportunity to run DSP code.
vs.audio-worklet: AudioWorklet is the Web Audio API's callback mechanism. It runs in a dedicated thread (like an audio callback) but uses a message-port pattern for parameter communication instead of shared memory.

unityAdapter.location: Add a script with OnAudioFilterRead(float[] data, int channels) to any GameObject that has an AudioSource. The method is automatically called by Unity's audio pipeline on the audio thread.
unityAdapter.apiRef: MonoBehaviour.OnAudioFilterRead(float[] data, int channels) — called on the audio thread, data is interleaved float samples in [-1, 1] range
unityAdapter.importTip: The data array in OnAudioFilterRead contains interleaved samples: for stereo, data[0] is left channel sample 0, data[1] is right channel sample 0, data[2] is left channel sample 1, and so on. The array length is bufferSize * channels. Process all samples in the array each callback invocation.
unityAdapter.gotcha: OnAudioFilterRead is called even when the AudioSource is not playing if the component is enabled. If you only want to process when audio is active, check if the incoming buffer is all zeros. Also, any unhandled exception in OnAudioFilterRead will silently kill the audio callback — you will hear silence with no error in the console.

misconceptions: The audio callback runs on the main thread — it does not. It runs on a separate high-priority audio thread. Treating it like normal game code (allocating memory, accessing GameObjects) will cause crashes or glitches.
If the callback is too slow, the audio system will just wait — it cannot wait. The hardware DAC consumes samples at a fixed rate. A late callback means an underrun, not a delay.

You only need audio callbacks for advanced use cases — callbacks are how all real-time audio works internally. You are always using them indirectly. Understanding them helps debug latency and dropout issues.

name: Audio Channel

beginner.oneLiner: Audio channels define how many independent audio signals make up a sound — mono is one, stereo is two (left and right), surround is more.
beginner.analogy: Channels are like lanes on a highway. Mono is a single-lane road — all sound goes the same way. Stereo is two lanes letting sound travel to your left and right ears separately. Surround adds more lanes all around you.
beginner.whatToDo: Use mono for most in-game sound effects (footsteps, impacts, UI sounds) — they will be spatialized by the engine. Use stereo for music and ambient beds. Avoid stereo SFX unless you specifically need a wide, non-positional sound.

intermediate.oneLiner: Channels represent discrete audio signals within a clip. Mono (1 channel) is standard for positional 3D sounds; stereo (2 channels) for music and non-spatialized audio.
intermediate.details: A mono clip contains one waveform. A stereo clip contains two (left and right). Surround formats like 5.1 use six channels (front left, center, front right, rear left, rear right, LFE/subwoofer). In games, 3D-spatialized sounds should be mono — the engine pans them in real-time based on listener position. Stereo clips played as 3D sounds waste memory (double the data) and produce unpredictable spatialization since the engine collapses or ignores the stereo field. File size scales linearly with channel count: stereo is 2x mono, 5.1 is 6x mono.
intermediate.whenToUse: Mono: all positional/3D sound effects. Stereo: background music, ambient soundscapes, UI sounds that should feel 'in your head.' 5.1/7.1: typically only for cinematic cutscenes or pre-rendered content, and only if your target platform supports surround output.

advanced.oneLiner: Channel configuration determines the discrete signal count per audio frame, affecting memory footprint, spatialization behavior, and output routing.
advanced.technicalNotes: Unity's AudioSource spatialBlend parameter (0=2D, 1=3D) determines how channels are handled. At spatialBlend=1, stereo clips are typically summed to mono before spatialization, wasting the extra channel data. For Ambisonics (used in VR), Unity supports first-order ambisonic clips (4 channels in AmbiX format). Channel routing to physical speakers is handled by the platform audio layer (WASAPI, CoreAudio, AAudio) or middleware (FMOD, Wwise). Interleaved channel storage (LRLRLR) is standard in PCM; some engines de-interleave on load for SIMD processing.

vs.spatial-blend: Channel count is a property of the audio asset; spatial blend is a runtime parameter controlling 2D vs 3D positioning. A mono clip with spatial blend 1.0 gives the cleanest 3D positioning.
vs.surround-panning: Hardware surround output routes engine-spatialized mono sources to multiple speakers. This is different from authoring 5.1 surround files — runtime spatialization of mono sources is more flexible and memory-efficient.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Clip Inspector shows channel count. AudioSource component's 'Spatial Blend' slider controls 2D/3D behavior. Output routing is configured in Audio Mixer.
unityAdapter.importTip: Check the Audio Clip Inspector to verify channel count after import. If a stereo file should be 3D, consider converting to mono in your DAW first to halve memory usage and ensure clean spatialization.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Setting Spatial Blend to 3D on a stereo clip does not give you true stereo 3D sound — Unity will collapse it. Always use mono clips for 3D positional audio to avoid wasting memory and getting unexpected panning.

misconceptions: Stereo does not mean 'better quality' than mono — it means two channels. For 3D game sounds, mono is the correct choice because the engine handles positioning dynamically.
Surround sound in games is usually generated at runtime by spatializing mono sources — you do not need to ship 5.1 audio files for players to experience surround sound.

name: Audio Clipping

beginner.oneLiner: Clipping happens when an audio signal is too loud for the system to handle, causing harsh distortion.
beginner.analogy: Imagine pouring water into a glass that's already full. The excess spills over and makes a mess. In audio, when the signal exceeds the maximum level (0 dBFS), the tops of the waveform get chopped off flat, creating a harsh, crunchy distortion.
beginner.whatToDo: Watch for red meters in your mixer or audio tool. If they hit the top, turn the volume down on the source or the bus. Always leave some headroom (keep peaks below -3 dBFS) so that layering multiple sounds does not push the mix into clipping.

intermediate.oneLiner: Clipping occurs when a signal exceeds the maximum representable amplitude, causing the waveform to be truncated and producing harmonic and inharmonic distortion.
intermediate.details: Digital clipping happens at 0 dBFS (the maximum value of the sample format). Unlike analog soft clipping (which adds mild harmonic saturation), digital clipping is hard: sample values are clamped, introducing odd harmonics and intermodulation distortion that sounds harsh. In games, clipping commonly occurs when many sounds play simultaneously and their summed amplitudes exceed the master bus ceiling. Even if individual clips are fine, additive stacking causes bus clipping.
intermediate.whenToUse: Monitor your master bus during gameplay, especially during intense moments (combat, explosions). Use a limiter on the master bus as a safety net. Implement a dynamic mixing strategy with ducking or sound prioritization to prevent too many loud sounds from summing.

advanced.oneLiner: Digital clipping is hard limiting at the quantization ceiling, producing aliased harmonic distortion proportional to the degree and duration of the clipping event.
advanced.technicalNotes: Clipped samples create a square-wave-like plateau, injecting energy at odd harmonics of the fundamental. Floating-point audio pipelines (like Unity's internal mixer) can exceed 0 dBFS without clipping internally, but the final output to the DAC clips at the hardware ceiling. Inter-sample peaks (true peaks between samples that exceed 0 dBFS when reconstructed) can cause clipping in the DAC even when no individual sample exceeds 0. True peak limiting accounts for this. In Unity, AudioSource.volume and AudioMixerGroup attenuation are multiplicative; monitoring AudioListener output level is key.

vs.soft-clipping: Soft clipping (saturation) gradually compresses peaks, adding even harmonics that sound warmer. Often used intentionally as a creative effect.
vs.limiting: A limiter prevents clipping by dynamically reducing gain before the signal hits the ceiling, preserving waveform shape at the cost of reduced dynamic range.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer window (Window > Audio > Audio Mixer). Monitor the master group's VU meter for clipping indicators.
unityAdapter.importTip: Normalize your source audio files to peak around -3 dBFS to -6 dBFS before importing. This gives you headroom for runtime volume adjustments and additive mixing.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's Audio Mixer operates in floating point internally, so inter-group routing won't clip mid-pipeline. However, the final output to the audio device will clip if the Master group output exceeds 0 dBFS. Always put a limiter or attenuator effect on the Master group.

misconceptions: If individual sounds are not clipping, the mix won't clip. Multiple sounds summed together can easily exceed 0 dBFS even if each one peaks well below it.
Clipping is always bad. Soft clipping and saturation are used intentionally for warmth and character, but uncontrolled digital hard clipping is almost always undesirable.

name: Audio Graph

beginner.oneLiner: An audio graph is a visual and logical map of how sounds flow through your audio system — from sources through effects and mixers to the final output, like a plumbing diagram for sound.
beginner.analogy: Think of a water system in a building. Water flows from many faucets (audio sources) through pipes that merge together (mixer buses), pass through filters (effects like reverb or EQ), and eventually reach the main drain (your speakers). An audio graph is the blueprint of this plumbing — it shows every path sound can take, every junction where sounds combine, and every processing stage along the way.
beginner.whatToDo: In Unity, open the Audio Mixer window to see and edit your audio graph visually. Create groups (buses) for different sound categories — SFX, Music, UI, Ambient — and add effects to each group. The hierarchy you build in the Audio Mixer is your audio graph.

intermediate.oneLiner: An audio graph is a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of audio processing nodes — sources, effects, mixers, and output — that defines the signal flow and processing order of your entire audio pipeline.
intermediate.details: Each node in the graph either generates audio (sources, oscillators), transforms it (filters, reverb, compressor), routes it (mixer, splitter, send), or outputs it (listener, hardware output). Nodes connect via edges representing audio data flow. The graph is processed bottom-up each audio callback: leaf nodes (sources) produce samples, intermediate nodes (effects, mixers) process and combine them, and the root node writes to the hardware buffer. The graph structure determines processing order — a reverb node on a bus processes the sum of all sources routed to that bus. Modern audio systems (Web Audio API, FMOD, Wwise) expose graph manipulation as their primary API, letting you connect and disconnect nodes at runtime.
intermediate.whenToUse: Design your audio graph early in development. Group sounds logically (SFX, music, dialogue, ambience) into separate buses. Apply shared effects at the bus level rather than per-source for efficiency. Use sends to route sounds to shared reverb buses. Keep the graph as shallow as possible — deep chains add latency and make debugging harder.

advanced.oneLiner: An audio graph is a DAG of processing nodes evaluated in topological order on the audio thread, where each node's process() method consumes input buffers and produces output buffers, with the graph topology determining the mixing and effect routing for the entire pipeline.
advanced.technicalNotes: Graph evaluation: the audio thread traverses the DAG in topological order (sources first, master output last). Each node calls its process(inputBuffers, outputBuffer, frameCount) method. Nodes with multiple inputs sum them before processing (implicit mix). Nodes with multiple outputs (sends) write to multiple downstream buffers. Graph modifications (adding/removing nodes, changing connections) must be thread-safe — typically done via a command queue that the audio thread processes at the start of each callback. Hot-swapping subgraphs allows runtime audio pipeline reconfiguration without glitches. Unity's Audio Mixer implements a fixed-topology graph defined in the editor. FMOD and Wwise expose more dynamic graph construction. Web Audio API provides full runtime graph manipulation via AudioNode.connect()/disconnect(). For performance, processing order should maximize cache locality — process all instances of one node type before moving to the next (batch processing).

vs.audio-bus: A bus is a single node (or chain of nodes) in the audio graph that acts as a mixing point. The audio graph is the full topology connecting all buses, sources, and effects.
vs.audio-mixer: The Audio Mixer is Unity's implementation of an audio graph. Other engines and APIs (Web Audio, FMOD) use different graph implementations with varying degrees of runtime flexibility.
vs.dsp: DSP is the processing that happens inside individual graph nodes. The audio graph defines how those nodes are connected and the order in which they process.

unityAdapter.location: Window > Audio > Audio Mixer. The mixer window displays the graph as a hierarchy of groups (buses) with effect slots. Each group is a node; the hierarchy defines the signal flow.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer, AudioMixerGroup, AudioMixerSnapshot. AudioSource.outputAudioMixerGroup routes a source into the graph.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set AudioSource.outputAudioMixerGroup on every AudioSource to route it into your mixer graph. Sources without a mixer group go directly to the master output, bypassing all your buses and effects. Create at minimum: Master, SFX, Music, UI, and Ambience groups.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's Audio Mixer graph is static — you define it in the editor and cannot add or remove groups at runtime. You can change volumes, effect parameters, and snapshots at runtime, but the graph topology is fixed. If you need dynamic routing, consider FMOD or Wwise integration.

misconceptions: The audio graph is just the mixer UI — the graph is the logical processing pipeline that runs on the audio thread. The mixer window is a visual editor for it, but the graph exists and runs regardless of whether you have the editor open.

Every sound needs its own effects chain — sharing effects across a bus is far more efficient. Route 50 footstep sounds to a single SFX bus with one reverb, rather than giving each source its own reverb instance.

Audio graph changes are instant — modifying the graph topology at runtime (adding nodes, changing connections) requires thread-safe mechanisms. Careless modification can cause clicks, pops, or crashes.

name: Audio Interpolation

beginner.oneLiner: Audio interpolation estimates sample values between existing samples, which is needed whenever you change a sound's pitch or convert between different sample rates.
beginner.analogy: Imagine you have a connect-the-dots drawing with dots every centimeter. Now you need dots every half centimeter — but you only have the original dots. Interpolation is guessing where the new dots should go based on the existing ones. Simple guessing (straight lines between dots) works okay; smarter guessing (smooth curves) gives better results. Audio interpolation does this for sound samples whenever the playback speed does not perfectly match the original.
beginner.whatToDo: When pitching sounds up or down in your game, the engine interpolates automatically. Higher-quality interpolation sounds smoother but costs more CPU. For most game audio, the default engine interpolation is fine. If pitched sounds have audible artifacts (metallic or grainy quality), check if the engine offers a higher-quality interpolation setting.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio interpolation reconstructs sample values at fractional positions between stored samples, required for pitch shifting, sample-rate conversion, and any playback that reads between discrete sample points.
intermediate.details: When you play a sound at a different pitch, the playback pointer advances by a non-integer step through the sample data. For example, a one-octave pitch-up reads every other sample (step = 2.0), but a 1.5x pitch reads at fractional positions. Interpolation estimates the value at these fractional positions. Methods ranked by quality and cost: Nearest-neighbor (zero-order) — picks the closest sample. Cheap but introduces noise and aliasing. Linear — draws a straight line between two adjacent samples. Low cost, decent for SFX. Cubic (Hermite, Catmull-Rom) — fits a smooth curve through four samples. Good quality-to-cost ratio, common in games. Sinc (band-limited) — theoretically perfect reconstruction using a windowed sinc filter. Expensive but minimal artifacts.
intermediate.whenToUse: Linear interpolation is adequate for transient-heavy sound effects that are pitched within a small range (0.8x to 1.2x). Use cubic interpolation for musical content, sustained tones, or sounds pitched over a wide range. Sinc interpolation is reserved for offline processing or high-fidelity contexts where CPU is not constrained. Sample-rate conversion (e.g., 44.1 kHz to 48 kHz) also uses interpolation and benefits from higher quality.

advanced.oneLiner: Audio interpolation performs fractional-delay sample reconstruction via polynomial (linear, cubic) or band-limited (windowed sinc) methods, with quality determined by the interpolation kernel's frequency response and its suppression of imaging/aliasing artifacts.
advanced.technicalNotes: Linear interpolation is a 2-point FIR with a triangular frequency response — it rolls off high frequencies and produces imaging above Nyquist/2 at non-unity rates. Cubic Hermite uses 4 points and preserves more high-frequency content. Sinc interpolation uses a windowed (Kaiser, Blackman) sinc kernel of L taps — effectively an L-point FIR lowpass filter at the lower of the input and output Nyquist frequencies. For downsampling, the sinc filter must also serve as an anti-aliasing filter, so the kernel should be widened. Polyphase implementations pre-compute sinc kernel values for quantized fractional delays, avoiding per-sample sinc evaluation. Quality is measured by the stopband rejection of the interpolation filter — linear achieves roughly 13 dB, cubic 25-30 dB, 16-point sinc achieves 80+ dB. For game audio pitch-shifting, cubic interpolation is the sweet spot. For sample-rate conversion, use a polyphase sinc resampler with at least 16 taps.

vs.sample-rate: Sample rate determines the grid spacing. Interpolation reconstructs values between grid points. Higher sample rates reduce the need for interpolation when pitching sounds, because the original grid is finer.
vs.pitch: Pitch shifting is achieved by reading samples at a different rate, which requires interpolation. The interpolation quality directly affects how clean the pitched sound is — poor interpolation at extreme pitch shifts produces metallic or noisy artifacts.
vs.nyquist: The Nyquist theorem guarantees perfect reconstruction with ideal sinc interpolation. In practice, finite-length sinc approximations introduce small errors that increase with pitch shift amount.

unityAdapter.location: AudioClip Inspector > Sample Rate Setting controls how clips are resampled. Pitch shifting via AudioSource.pitch uses Unity's internal interpolation (linear or cubic depending on platform).
unityAdapter.importTip: Set AudioImporter.sampleRateSetting to PreserveSampleRate to avoid quality loss from unnecessary resampling at import time. Let the engine handle runtime resampling when pitch-shifting. If you hear aliasing on pitched-up sounds, reimport the source at a higher sample rate (96 kHz) to give the interpolator more headroom.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioSource.pitch at extreme values (above 2x or below 0.5x) can produce audible artifacts. For extreme pitch shifts, consider pre-rendering pitched variants offline or using a granular synthesis approach instead of real-time resampling.

misconceptions: Linear interpolation is always good enough — for transient-heavy SFX at small pitch shifts it is fine, but on sustained tones or music even small pitch shifts reveal its frequency rolloff and aliasing artifacts.

Interpolation creates new audio information — it estimates (reconstructs) values based on existing samples. It cannot recover frequencies that were lost to sampling or add detail that was not in the original recording.

Higher sample rate files do not need interpolation — they still do when pitched, but the finer sample grid means the interpolator has more data points to work with, producing better results at extreme pitch shifts.

name: Audio Jitter

beginner.oneLiner: Audio jitter is the inconsistency in timing when audio data arrives or gets processed, causing small but audible glitches, clicks, or uneven playback.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a drummer who usually hits exactly on the beat but occasionally hits slightly early or late. Those tiny timing errors make the rhythm feel unsteady. Audio jitter is the same thing happening to your audio data — samples arriving at slightly inconsistent intervals, causing pops, clicks, or stuttering.
beginner.whatToDo: Use appropriately sized audio buffers to absorb timing variations. If you hear occasional clicks or stuttering, increase your buffer size. Keep your audio processing lightweight to ensure the audio thread meets its deadlines consistently.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio jitter is the variation in the time interval between successive audio buffer deliveries or clock cycles, causing timing inconsistencies that manifest as clicks, dropouts, or pitch instability.
intermediate.details: Jitter occurs at multiple levels: clock jitter (imprecision in the sample clock), buffer jitter (variation in when the OS delivers audio callbacks), and network jitter (variation in packet arrival for streamed audio). In game audio, the most common source is buffer jitter — the audio thread must fill a buffer before the hardware needs it. If the game's main thread or GC pauses the audio thread, the buffer empties (underrun) and you hear a click or dropout. Larger buffers provide more tolerance but increase latency.
intermediate.whenToUse: Address jitter when you hear intermittent clicks, pops, or micro-stutters in audio playback. Profile your audio thread to ensure it completes within its deadline (buffer_size / sample_rate seconds). Avoid allocating memory, locking mutexes, or doing heavy computation on the audio thread. On mobile, where thread scheduling is less predictable, use larger buffers.

advanced.oneLiner: Audio jitter quantifies the statistical variance in inter-buffer or inter-sample timing, with sources including clock skew, OS scheduling latency, interrupt servicing delays, and garbage collection pauses, requiring deterministic real-time thread scheduling and lock-free architectures to mitigate.
advanced.technicalNotes: Clock jitter in DACs causes non-uniform sample timing, introducing noise and distortion proportional to signal frequency (a 1 ns jitter at 20 kHz introduces ~-98 dBFS distortion). Buffer jitter is the primary concern in game engines — the audio callback must execute in under buffer_size/sample_rate seconds (e.g., 5.3 ms for 256 samples at 48 kHz). Use lock-free ring buffers for cross-thread communication, avoid system calls and memory allocation in the audio callback, and pin the audio thread to a dedicated core if the platform supports it. Unity's AudioSource scheduling (PlayScheduled) provides sample-accurate timing that is immune to frame-rate jitter, unlike Play() which is frame-dependent.

vs.latency: Latency is the fixed delay between triggering a sound and hearing it. Jitter is the variation in that delay over time. Low latency with high jitter produces inconsistent timing; low jitter with moderate latency produces consistent, predictable playback.
vs.buffer-size: Buffer size determines the amount of audio data queued ahead of playback. Larger buffers absorb more jitter but increase latency. Smaller buffers reduce latency but are less tolerant of timing inconsistencies.

unityAdapter.location: Edit > Project Settings > Audio > DSP Buffer Size. Choose 'Default', 'Best Latency', or 'Best Performance' to trade jitter tolerance against latency.
unityAdapter.importTip: For most games, use 'Best Performance' (larger buffer) to minimize jitter-related glitches. Switch to 'Best Latency' only for rhythm games or applications where input-to-audio delay is critical. Use PlayScheduled() instead of Play() for timing-critical sound events.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's GC can cause multi-millisecond pauses on the main thread, and while the audio thread is separate, cross-thread communication can still be affected. Minimize per-frame allocations and use incremental GC to reduce audio glitches caused by garbage collection spikes.

misconceptions: Jitter and latency are the same thing — latency is a fixed delay, jitter is the variation in timing. You can have low latency with high jitter (inconsistent but fast) or high latency with low jitter (consistent but delayed).

Bigger buffers fix all jitter problems — buffers help with timing variance, but if your audio processing exceeds the buffer deadline, no buffer size will prevent dropouts. Optimize your audio thread workload.
Jitter is only a problem for professional audio — game players notice audio clicks, pops, and stutters even if they cannot name the cause. Jitter-free playback is important for any polished game.

name: Audio Latency

beginner.oneLiner: Audio latency is the delay between when a sound is triggered and when you actually hear it.
beginner.analogy: Think of ordering food at a restaurant. The time between placing your order and the food arriving is latency. In games, it is the gap between pressing a button and hearing the sound. If it is too long (over ~100 ms), gameplay feels sluggish and disconnected.
beginner.whatToDo: Keep your game's audio latency low by using compressed-in-memory formats for frequent short sounds (gunshots, footsteps) and streaming only for long audio like music. Test on your target platform, as mobile devices often have higher latency than PC.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio latency is the total time from a play request to audible output, determined by buffer size, sample rate, driver overhead, and hardware DAC conversion.
intermediate.details: Total latency has multiple contributors: the game engine's frame time (when the play call happens relative to the audio thread), the audio buffer size (the chunk of samples the OS processes at once), driver overhead (WASAPI, CoreAudio, ALSA), and DAC hardware conversion. A 512-sample buffer at 48 kHz adds ~10.7 ms per buffer. Most pipelines double-buffer, so actual latency is 2x the buffer duration plus driver overhead. For rhythm games or tight interactive feedback, target under 20 ms total. For ambient sounds, 50-100 ms is acceptable.
intermediate.whenToUse: Profile audio latency on each target platform. On mobile (Android especially), latency can exceed 100 ms with default settings.
