Use platform-specific low-latency APIs: AAudio/Oboe on Android, AVAudioSession with low-latency category on iOS. In Unity, set the DSP Buffer Size to 'Best Latency' in Project Settings for responsive games.

advanced.oneLiner: End-to-end audio latency is the sum of application-level scheduling delay, ring buffer fill time, kernel-mode mixing latency, and DAC conversion time.
advanced.technicalNotes: On Windows, exclusive-mode WASAPI can achieve ~3 ms latency by bypassing the system mixer. Shared-mode WASAPI adds the Windows Audio Session mixer's buffer (~10 ms). On Android, the Oboe library auto-selects AAudio (low latency) or OpenSL ES as fallback. Unity's FMOD-based audio engine adds its own buffer on top of the OS buffer. For critical-path sounds (hit confirms, rhythm game beats), consider scheduling sounds ahead of time with AudioSource.PlayScheduled(dspTime) to align precisely with the audio clock rather than relying on frame-synced Play() calls. Jitter (variance in latency) is often more perceptible than consistent latency.

vs.buffer-size: Buffer size is one component of latency. Smaller buffers reduce latency but increase CPU load and risk of audio glitches (underruns).
vs.frame-rate: Game frame rate affects when Play() is called relative to the audio thread. At 30 FPS, a sound triggered at the start of a frame has up to 33 ms of frame-induced delay before the audio thread picks it up.

unityAdapter.location: Project Settings > Audio > DSP Buffer Size (Best Latency / Good Latency / Best Performance).
unityAdapter.importTip: Set DSP Buffer Size to 'Best Latency' for rhythm games or action games where audio responsiveness is critical. Use 'Best Performance' for ambient/narrative games where low CPU usage matters more than latency.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.Play() is dispatched on the next audio DSP tick, not instantly. For frame-accurate scheduling, use AudioSource.PlayScheduled(AudioSettings.dspTime + offset) which operates on the audio clock. Also, 'Best Latency' increases CPU usage and may cause glitches on low-end mobile hardware.

misconceptions: Audio latency is only about buffer size. The full pipeline includes frame timing, engine overhead, OS mixer, driver, and hardware DAC. Buffer size is just one piece.
Latency doesn't matter for non-rhythm games. Even in shooters and platformers, latency above 50-80 ms makes hit feedback feel disconnected and reduces player satisfaction.

name: Audio Masking

beginner.oneLiner: Audio masking is the phenomenon where one sound makes another sound harder or impossible to hear, usually because they occupy similar frequency ranges.
beginner.analogy: Try having a conversation at a loud concert. The music masks your friend's voice because both occupy similar frequency ranges. But you can hear a whistle cutting through because it is at a different frequency. In your game mix, loud sounds can mask quieter but important ones in the same way.
beginner.whatToDo: Make sure important sounds (dialogue, critical gameplay cues) do not share frequency ranges with loud background sounds. Use EQ to carve space: cut a notch in the music around 2-4 kHz to let dialogue through, or duck music volume when dialogue plays.

intermediate.oneLiner: Masking occurs when the auditory threshold for one sound is raised by the presence of another sound, governed by critical band overlap, relative levels, and temporal proximity.
intermediate.details: Simultaneous masking happens when sounds overlap in time and frequency — a louder sound raises the hearing threshold for quieter sounds in the same critical band. Temporal masking includes forward masking (a loud sound makes subsequent quiet sounds inaudible for 50-200 ms) and backward masking (a loud sound arriving shortly after a quiet sound can retroactively make it inaudible for 5-20 ms). Masking is strongest within the same critical band and falls off as frequency separation increases.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use masking knowledge to prioritize your mix: ensure critical gameplay sounds live in uncontested frequency ranges. Apply sidechain compression to automatically duck background elements when important sounds play. When layering sounds, ensure each layer contributes unique frequency content — redundant layers in the same band just create mud without added clarity. Audio codecs exploit masking to remove inaudible data.

advanced.oneLiner: Auditory masking raises the detection threshold of a target signal through energetic masking (spectral overlap within critical bands), temporal masking (pre- and post-masking windows), and informational masking (cognitive load from competing similar signals).
advanced.technicalNotes: The masking threshold spreads asymmetrically on the Bark scale: a masker at frequency f raises thresholds more for frequencies above f than below (the upward spread of masking). This means low-frequency sounds are more effective maskers than high-frequency sounds. Masking is exploited by perceptual codecs: MPEG psychoacoustic models (PAM I/II) compute masking thresholds per critical band and allocate bits only to audible components. In game mixing, calculate potential masking conflicts by analyzing the spectral centroid and bandwidth of concurrent sounds. Priority systems can incorporate masking awareness — if a new sound would be fully masked, skip it to save CPU without perceptual loss.

vs.eq: EQ modifies the frequency content of a signal. Masking is a perceptual phenomenon. EQ is a tool used to reduce masking by separating sounds in the frequency domain.
vs.bark-scale: The Bark scale divides frequency into critical bands. Masking occurs most strongly within a single critical band. The Bark scale is the framework; masking is the phenomenon that occurs within that framework.

unityAdapter.location: Apply masking management through the Audio Mixer: use EQ effects to separate frequency ranges and Duck Volume effects to automatically reduce bus levels when another bus is active.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer Duck Volume effect, AudioMixerGroup EQ
unityAdapter.importTip: Set up a Duck Volume effect on your music bus that triggers from the dialogue bus. This automatically reduces music when dialogue plays, preventing masking of speech. Typical settings: threshold -20 dB, ratio 3:1, attack 50 ms, release 500 ms.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's Duck Volume uses a simple sidechain mechanism. It ducks the entire bus uniformly — it cannot selectively duck only the frequencies that conflict. For more precise masking management, use middleware with multiband ducking or dynamic EQ.

misconceptions: Making everything louder prevents masking — louder is not the solution. If two sounds occupy the same frequency range, making them both louder just raises the overall level without improving clarity. Separate them in frequency or time instead.

Masking only happens with similar sounds — even very different sounds can mask each other if they have overlapping frequency content. A rumbling engine can mask a low-pitched dialogue line despite sounding nothing alike.

You can always hear every sound in a mix — the human ear has limited frequency resolution. In dense mixes with many concurrent sources, most of the detail is perceptually masked and inaudible. Strategic prioritization is better than trying to make everything audible.

name: Audio Metering

beginner.oneLiner: Audio metering is the use of visual level displays to monitor how loud your audio signal is at various points in the mixing chain, helping you avoid clipping and maintain balanced levels.
beginner.analogy: Metering is like the speedometer in your car. You need to see how fast you are going to avoid speeding (clipping) and to maintain a steady pace (consistent loudness). Different meters show different things — like having a speedometer for current speed and another for average speed over the trip.
beginner.whatToDo: Watch the level meters in your audio mixer while playing your game. Keep peaks below 0 dB to avoid clipping. Use the meters to ensure all sound categories (music, SFX, dialogue) are balanced relative to each other.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio metering encompasses peak meters, RMS meters, VU meters, and loudness meters (LUFS), each providing different perspectives on signal level for monitoring, gain staging, and compliance.
intermediate.details: Peak meters show the instantaneous maximum sample value — essential for preventing digital clipping. RMS meters show the average power, which correlates better with perceived loudness. VU meters are a legacy analog standard with 300 ms integration time. LUFS meters (ITU-R BS.1770) provide the most accurate perceived loudness reading using psychoacoustic weighting. In practice, you want both peak and loudness metering: peak for technical safety, loudness for perceptual balance.
intermediate.whenToUse: Place peak meters on every bus to catch clipping. Use LUFS or RMS metering on the master bus and dialogue bus to maintain consistent perceived loudness. Check metering during gameplay, not just in the audio editor — simultaneous sounds can sum to unexpected levels. Set up metering before you start mixing and refer to it throughout development.

advanced.oneLiner: Audio metering systems implement various ballistic models and integration windows — from sample-accurate true peak detection (ISP per ITU-R BS.1770-4) to K-weighted gated loudness integration (LUFS) — to provide complementary technical and perceptual level monitoring.
advanced.technicalNotes: True peak metering oversamples the signal (typically 4x) to detect inter-sample peaks that exceed 0 dBFS after D/A conversion, which sample-peak meters miss. For game audio, the key metering chain is: true peak on master (prevent ISP clipping), short-term LUFS on master (monitor moment-to-moment loudness), integrated LUFS for overall program loudness. K-weighting in LUFS accounts for head diffraction and low-frequency insensitivity. For real-time game profiling, implement lightweight RMS metering per bus to identify loudness imbalances. Correlate metering data with gameplay events to catch loudness spikes in specific scenarios (e.g., combat, cutscenes).

vs.peak-level: Peak metering shows the maximum instantaneous sample value. It is one specific type of metering. Full metering practice combines peak, RMS, and loudness (LUFS) for a complete picture.
vs.loudness-unit: LUFS is a specific metering standard focused on perceived loudness. Metering is the broader practice of monitoring levels using various measurement types including but not limited to LUFS.

unityAdapter.location: Unity's Audio Mixer window shows basic peak-level meters for each group. For LUFS and RMS metering, use third-party tools or middleware profilers.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer level meters (visual only), AudioSource.GetOutputData() for custom metering
unityAdapter.importTip: Build a simple runtime metering display by reading AudioSource.GetOutputData() and computing peak and RMS values per frame. Display as an on-screen overlay during development to monitor levels in real gameplay scenarios.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in mixer meters only show peak levels, not RMS or LUFS. They also update at the UI frame rate, which can miss fast transients. For accurate metering, process the raw audio data in OnAudioFilterRead or use middleware tools.

misconceptions: Peak meters tell you how loud something sounds — peaks measure instantaneous maxima, not perceived loudness. A signal with high peaks can sound quieter than one with lower peaks but higher average energy.

If the meters are not red, the audio is fine — even without clipping, poor gain staging can cause muddy mixes, masking, or inconsistent loudness. Metering is a diagnostic tool, not a pass/fail indicator.
You only need to meter the master bus — metering individual buses catches problems at their source. A clipped sub-bus signal remains distorted even after the master bus attenuates it.

name: Audio Mixer

beginner.oneLiner: The Audio Mixer lets you group sounds together and control their volume, add effects, and manage the overall mix of your game.
beginner.analogy: Think of it like a mixing board at a concert. Each slider controls a different group of sounds -- one for music, one for sound effects, one for dialogue -- and you can adjust them independently.
beginner.whatToDo: Create an Audio Mixer via Assets > Create > Audio Mixer. Open the Audio Mixer window (Window > Audio > Audio Mixer). Create groups like 'Music', 'SFX', and 'UI'. Route AudioSources to these groups by setting their Output property.

intermediate.oneLiner: Unity's Audio Mixer is a DAW-style signal routing and processing system with groups, snapshots, sends, and built-in DSP effects.
intermediate.details: The mixer is organized into AudioMixerGroups arranged in a hierarchy. Each group can have effects (lowpass, highpass, reverb, distortion, etc.) and its volume can be exposed as a parameter for runtime scripting. Snapshots save the entire mixer state and can be transitioned between with interpolation. Duck Volume via sends lets you sidechain one group against another -- e.g., ducking music when dialogue plays. The mixer processes audio on the audio thread, not the main thread.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use the mixer when you need: global volume sliders (settings menu), effect buses (reverb zones, underwater filter), dynamic ducking (music ducks under dialogue), or snapshot-based mixing (combat vs. exploration).

advanced.oneLiner: The Audio Mixer provides a DSP graph with exposed parameters, snapshot interpolation, and send/receive routing for complex signal chains.
advanced.technicalNotes: Exposed parameters are set via AudioMixer.SetFloat() and read via GetFloat(). Snapshot transitions use TransitionTo() with configurable interpolation time. The mixer runs on the audio thread at the project sample rate, so effect chains add to DSP CPU load -- profile with the Audio Profiler. Custom DSP effects can be written as native audio plugins. Groups support wet/dry sends for parallel effect processing. The mixer does not support runtime creation -- all groups and routing must be set up at edit time.

vs.FMOD Mixer: FMOD's mixer supports runtime bus creation, more flexible routing, and a wider range of built-in effects. Unity's mixer is simpler but must be fully configured at edit time.
vs.Wwise Busses: Wwise offers actor-mixer hierarchies and game-syncs for parameter-driven mixing that goes beyond what Unity's snapshot system provides.

unityAdapter.location: Window > Audio > Audio Mixer. Assets live in the Project window as AudioMixer assets.
unityAdapter.importTip: Expose the volume parameter of each group (right-click the volume slider > Expose), then control it from your settings UI via AudioMixer.SetFloat(). Use a logarithmic conversion: Mathf.Log10(sliderValue) * 20 to convert a 0-1 slider to decibels.
unityAdapter.gotcha: SetFloat() uses the exposed parameter name, which defaults to something like 'MyExposedParam'. Rename it immediately after exposing or you will lose track. Also, setting volume to 0 in linear requires passing -80 dB, not 0.

misconceptions: The Audio Mixer is not required to play sounds. AudioSources work fine without one. The mixer adds mixing, effects, and routing on top of basic playback.
Snapshots do not create separate copies of the mixer. They store parameter values that can be interpolated at runtime, all within the same mixer instance.

name: Audio Normalization

beginner.oneLiner: Normalization adjusts the volume of an audio file so it reaches a target loudness level without changing the sound's character.
beginner.analogy: Imagine you have a stack of photos taken at different brightnesses. Normalization is like adjusting each photo so they all look equally bright. For audio, it scales the volume up or down so all your sound files have a consistent loudness.
beginner.whatToDo: Before importing sounds into your game, normalize them to a consistent peak level (like -1 dBFS) in your audio editor. This ensures all your sounds start at a similar baseline volume, making in-engine mixing much easier.

intermediate.oneLiner: Normalization scales an audio signal's amplitude so that its peak or integrated loudness reaches a specified target level.
intermediate.details: Peak normalization finds the highest sample value and applies uniform gain so that peak hits the target (e.g., -1 dBFS). Loudness normalization (e.g., to -16 LUFS) adjusts based on perceived loudness over time, which is more perceptually consistent. Peak normalization is simpler but does not account for perceived loudness differences between, say, a snare hit and a dialog line. Loudness normalization using LUFS (Loudness Units relative to Full Scale) is the broadcast standard and more meaningful for consistency.
intermediate.whenToUse: Peak-normalize one-shot sound effects to a consistent level before import to simplify gain staging. For dialog and music, prefer loudness normalization (LUFS-based) to ensure perceptual consistency. Always normalize before compression or other dynamics processing.

advanced.oneLiner: Normalization applies a constant gain offset to bring a signal's peak or integrated loudness (ITU-R BS.1770 measurement) to a target reference level.
advanced.technicalNotes: Peak normalization is a simple operation: gain = target_peak / max(abs(samples)). Loudness normalization per ITU-R BS.1770 measures integrated LUFS across the file and applies a corresponding gain offset. Note that normalization is a linear gain change and does not alter dynamics or introduce distortion, unlike compression or limiting. For game assets, normalizing per-file is useful for consistency, but the real mix balance happens in-engine through volume curves and bus levels. Be careful not to normalize assets that are intentionally quiet (room tones, distant ambiences) to the same peak as transient-heavy effects.

vs.compression: Compression reduces dynamic range by attenuating loud parts; normalization applies uniform gain. Compression changes the signal's dynamics; normalization does not.
vs.limiting: Limiting is extreme compression that prevents peaks from exceeding a ceiling. Normalization simply scales the whole signal and can actually push peaks past 0 dBFS if you normalize up without a ceiling.

unityAdapter.location: AudioClip import inspector (Normalize checkbox) and runtime via AudioSource.volume.
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's 'Normalize' checkbox in the AudioClip importer performs peak normalization. For more control, normalize in your DAW or audio editor (Audacity, Adobe Audition) before importing. Use loudness metering plugins to target a consistent LUFS level across dialog, SFX, and music.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's import-time normalization is peak-based only and does not account for perceptual loudness. A normalized explosion and a normalized whisper will have the same peak but very different perceived volumes. Prefer pre-processing with LUFS-aware tools for perceptual consistency.

misconceptions: Normalization and compression are the same thing. Normalization applies uniform gain to the entire signal; compression dynamically reduces loud parts. They have very different effects on the sound.
Normalizing to 0 dBFS is best practice. This leaves zero headroom for mixing. Normalizing to -1 dBFS or -3 dBFS gives room for additive mixing without clipping.

name: Audio Object Pooling

beginner.oneLiner: Audio object pooling is a technique where you pre-create a set of audio players and reuse them instead of creating and destroying new ones every time a sound plays, which is much faster and avoids stutters.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a restaurant that buys new plates for every customer and throws them away after each meal. That would be absurdly wasteful and slow. Instead, they wash and reuse plates. Audio pooling works the same way — instead of creating a new AudioSource every time a gunshot fires and destroying it when it finishes, you pre-create a stack of AudioSources and recycle them. When a sound finishes, its AudioSource goes back in the pool, ready for the next sound.
beginner.whatToDo: Create a pool of 16-32 AudioSource GameObjects at game start. When you need to play a sound, grab one from the pool, configure it, and play. When it finishes, return it to the pool. Unity's PlayClipAtPoint creates and destroys GameObjects each call — avoid it in production code and use a pool instead.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio object pooling pre-allocates a fixed number of AudioSource instances (voices) at initialization, lending them out for playback and reclaiming them when finished, avoiding the CPU spikes and GC pressure of runtime instantiation.
intermediate.details: The pool maintains a collection of inactive AudioSource GameObjects. When a sound is requested: dequeue an available source from the pool, configure it (clip, volume, pitch, position, mixer group), play it, and mark it as active. When playback completes, reset the source and return it to the pool. If all sources are in use, either reject the new sound, queue it, or use voice stealing (stop the lowest-priority active sound). Pool sizing depends on your game's peak simultaneous sound count — profile your busiest moments. Each pooled AudioSource should have a consistent component configuration to avoid per-play overhead. Use coroutines or a manager update to detect when sources finish playing and return them automatically.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use pooling for any game that plays more than a handful of sounds. It is essential for action games, shooters, or any genre with frequent sound triggers. A pool of 24-48 voices handles most indie games. Size the pool based on profiling your game's peak audio demand, not a guess.

advanced.oneLiner: Audio object pooling implements a fixed-size free list of pre-instantiated AudioSource components, providing O(1) allocation and deallocation that eliminates per-play GC allocation, with integration into the voice management pipeline for priority-based stealing when the pool is exhausted.
advanced.technicalNotes: Implementation: use a stack (LIFO) or queue (FIFO) of available AudioSource references. LIFO is preferred because recently returned sources are cache-warm. Each pool entry should carry metadata: current event ID, priority, start time, fade state, and spatial tracking target. The pool manager runs in Update() to detect finished sources (AudioSource.isPlaying == false && AudioSource.time == 0) and reclaim them. For positional audio, the pooled GameObject must be moved to the sound's world position — either parent it to the emitter or update its transform each frame. A common optimization is to separate the pool into 2D (non-spatial) and 3D (spatial) subsets, since 3D sources have additional overhead (distance calculation, spatialization). Pool exhaustion strategy should integrate with the priority system: when the pool is empty, find the lowest-priority active voice and steal it (fade out over 10-20 ms to avoid clicks).

vs.sound-pool: Sound pool is Unity's informal term for the same concept. Audio object pooling is the general pattern; sound pool typically refers to a specific implementation managing AudioSource GameObjects.
vs.voice-stealing: Voice stealing is the strategy applied when the pool is exhausted. The pool manages instance lifecycle; voice stealing handles the conflict resolution policy.
vs.priority-system: The priority system assigns importance to each sound. The pool uses priority rankings to decide which voice to steal when no free voices are available.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in pool API for audio. Implement using a List or Stack of GameObjects with AudioSource components. Instantiate them in Awake or a loading screen and DontDestroyOnLoad the pool parent.
unityAdapter.apiRef: UnityEngine.AudioSource, Object.Instantiate, GameObject.SetActive. For detection: AudioSource.isPlaying, AudioSource.time
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a parent GameObject called 'AudioPool', add child GameObjects each with an AudioSource component. Disable 'Play On Awake' on all pooled sources. Set spatialBlend to 0 (2D) or 1 (3D) based on pool type. Use AudioSource.PlayOneShot for fire-and-forget sounds on a single pooled source to layer multiple clips without needing separate pool entries.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.isPlaying returns false for paused sources, not just finished ones. If you pause the game and check isPlaying to reclaim sources, you will accidentally reclaim paused sounds. Track play state separately or check AudioSource.time along with isPlaying.

misconceptions: Unity.AudioSource.PlayClipAtPoint is fine for production — it instantiates a new GameObject every call, which causes GC allocation spikes and is far slower than pooling. Use it only for quick prototyping.
You need hundreds of pooled voices — most games rarely exceed 30-40 simultaneous sounds. Profile your game's actual peak and size the pool accordingly. Over-allocating wastes memory and update time.

Pooling eliminates all audio performance issues — pooling solves allocation overhead, but you still need voice limiting, priority management, and DSP budgeting. Pooling is one piece of a complete audio performance strategy.

name: Audio Priority System

beginner.oneLiner: An audio priority system ranks every sound in your game by importance so the engine knows which sounds absolutely must play and which can be skipped or stopped when the system gets busy.
beginner.analogy: Think of an emergency room triage system. When the ER is full, doctors treat the most critical patients first. Someone with a paper cut waits while someone with a broken leg gets immediate attention. An audio priority system does the same for sounds — a player damage sound (critical) always plays, while a distant bird chirp (low priority) might get silenced when the system is overloaded.
beginner.whatToDo: Assign a priority to every sound in your game. Player feedback sounds (damage, health pickup, UI clicks) should be highest priority. Important gameplay sounds (weapons, abilities) should be medium-high. Ambient and environmental sounds should be lower priority. This ensures players always hear what matters most.

intermediate.oneLiner: An audio priority system assigns numerical importance values to sounds, used by the voice manager to determine play/reject/steal decisions when the active voice count approaches the channel budget.
intermediate.details: Priority tiers (example scheme): Critical (0-50): player damage, UI feedback, dialogue — always play, never stolen. High (50-100): player weapons, ability sounds, nearby enemy actions — play unless absolutely no room. Medium (100-180): general SFX, physics impacts, animation sounds — subject to normal voice stealing. Low (180-230): ambient details, distant sounds, decorative audio — first candidates for stealing or rejection. Background (230-256): barely audible ambience, environmental texture — played only if voices are available. Priority interacts with other factors: a high-priority sound far from the listener may still be virtualized due to inaudibility. A low-priority loud nearby sound should play over a high-priority inaudible distant one. The best systems use a composite score combining static priority, dynamic audibility, and listener distance.
intermediate.whenToUse: Implement priorities from the start of your project. Every sound event or AudioSource should have an assigned priority. Test priorities during your game's busiest moments (combat, explosions, many simultaneous events) to verify that the most important sounds always come through. Adjust priorities based on playtesting feedback.

advanced.oneLiner: An audio priority system provides a hierarchical importance ranking consumed by the voice allocator's steal-or-reject decision logic, typically combined with real-time audibility scoring to produce a composite voice importance metric.
advanced.technicalNotes: A robust priority system separates static priority (assigned by the designer, indicating the sound's inherent importance class) from dynamic priority (computed at runtime based on context). Composite score: effectivePriority = staticPriority * w1 + audibilityScore * w2 + distanceScore * w3 + ageScore * w4. AudibilityScore = current gain after all attenuation, bus volume, and fades (mapped to 0-1). DistanceScore = normalized distance from listener. AgeScore = how long the voice has been playing (older = more expendable for one-shots). The voice allocator maintains a min-heap of active voices sorted by effectivePriority. On allocation failure: compare the incoming sound's effective priority against the heap minimum. If higher, steal the minimum; if lower, reject or queue the incoming sound. FMOD and Wwise implement this automatically with configurable priority and max-instance settings per event. For custom engines, evaluate effective priority every 50-100 ms (not every frame) to reduce overhead. Priority should also gate CPU-expensive features: low-priority sounds might skip spatialization HRTF and use cheaper panning.

vs.voice-stealing: Priority system defines the ranking; voice stealing is the enforcement action. Without priorities, voice stealing has no basis for choosing victims. Without stealing, priorities are informational only.
vs.audio-bus: Buses group sounds by category. Priorities rank sounds by importance. A sound's bus (SFX, Music, Ambient) often correlates with its priority tier but they are separate concepts — a critical ambient alert can be high priority despite being on the Ambient bus.
vs.event-driven-audio: The event system triggers sounds; the priority system gates whether those sounds actually get to play based on available resources and importance.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > Priority field (0 = highest, 256 = lowest). Project Settings > Audio > Max Real Voices defines the channel budget that priorities manage.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioSource.priority (int, 0-256). Lower values = higher priority. Default is 128.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a static class or enum with named priority constants: PRIORITY_CRITICAL = 0, PRIORITY_HIGH = 64, PRIORITY_MEDIUM = 128, PRIORITY_LOW = 192, PRIORITY_BACKGROUND = 240. Reference these when configuring AudioSources to maintain consistent priority assignment across your project.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's priority only affects voice virtualization and stealing decisions made by the internal FMOD-based audio system. It does not prevent AudioSource.Play() from being called — a low-priority sound still triggers the Play call, it just may be immediately virtualized if no channels are available. This means you might waste CPU on setup for sounds that never produce output. Pre-check channel availability if this is a concern.

misconceptions: Priority alone determines what plays — audibility matters too. A priority-0 sound that is completely inaudible (behind a wall, past max distance) should still be virtualized. The best systems combine priority with distance and volume.

You can set priorities once and forget them — priorities need tuning based on gameplay. What sounds critical in one scene may not matter in another. Consider dynamic priority adjustments based on game state.
Every sound needs a unique priority value — use priority tiers, not unique values. Grouping sounds into 4-6 tiers is more manageable and effective than assigning 256 different priority levels.

name: Audio Quantization

beginner.oneLiner: Quantization is the process of converting the smooth, continuous values of an analog audio signal into the fixed set of numeric values that a digital system can store, determined by the bit depth.
beginner.analogy: Imagine drawing a smooth curve on graph paper but you can only mark the exact grid intersections, not the spaces between. The curve gets turned into a staircase that approximates the original shape. Quantization does the same to sound — it rounds each sample to the nearest available step, and more steps (higher bit depth) means a more accurate approximation.
beginner.whatToDo: Choose appropriate bit depth for your game assets: 16-bit is standard for final game audio, 24-bit gives more headroom during production. The quantization steps are so small at these bit depths that the rounding errors are inaudible for most content.

intermediate.oneLiner: Quantization maps continuous amplitude values to discrete levels defined by the bit depth (2^N levels for N bits), introducing quantization error — a form of distortion whose character depends on signal level and whether dithering is applied.
intermediate.details: Each additional bit doubles the number of available levels and adds approximately 6 dB of dynamic range. 16-bit offers 65,536 levels (~96 dB range), 24-bit offers 16.7 million levels (~144 dB range). Quantization error is the difference between the true analog value and the nearest available level. Without dithering, this error correlates with the signal, producing harmonic distortion audible on quiet passages. With dithering, the error becomes uncorrelated noise. 32-bit float processing in game engines effectively eliminates quantization concerns during runtime — issues only arise when exporting to lower bit depths.
intermediate.whenToUse: Be aware of quantization when: exporting final game assets to 16-bit (apply dithering), working with very quiet sounds that might be affected by quantization noise at lower bit depths, and when understanding why 32-bit float internal processing eliminates intermediate quantization errors during mixing.

advanced.oneLiner: Quantization implements a nonlinear staircase transfer function that maps continuous amplitude to 2^N discrete levels, with the quantization error signal exhibiting signal-correlated harmonic distortion (undithered) or uncorrelated noise floor (dithered), and a theoretical SNR of approximately 6.02N + 1.76 dB for N-bit uniform quantization.
advanced.technicalNotes: The 6.02N + 1.76 dB SNR formula assumes a full-scale sinusoidal input with uniform quantization. Real-world signals rarely reach full scale, so effective SNR is lower. Midtread quantization (zero level aligns with a step) and midriser quantization (zero falls between steps) produce different distortion characteristics at low levels. In floating-point audio (IEEE 754 32-bit float), the mantissa provides 24 bits of precision with a dynamic range exceeding 1500 dB due to the exponent — quantization noise is effectively irrelevant. Quantization artifacts become audible primarily when: (1) exporting to low bit-depths without dithering, (2) applying extreme gain to quiet passages, or (3) using integer arithmetic in DSP chains. For game audio, the practical concern is the export stage: always dither when reducing to 16-bit PCM.

vs.bit-depth: Bit depth determines how many quantization levels are available. Quantization is the process of rounding continuous values to those levels. Bit depth is the parameter; quantization is the operation.
vs.dithering: Dithering adds noise before quantization to randomize the rounding errors. Without dithering, quantization produces correlated distortion. With dithering, quantization produces a clean noise floor instead.

unityAdapter.location: Unity processes audio internally as 32-bit float, so quantization is not a runtime concern. It matters when preparing assets: choose bit depth in AudioClip import settings.
unityAdapter.importTip: Import source assets at their native bit depth (24-bit preferred for production). Unity's internal processing is 32-bit float, so no quality is lost during mixing. Quantization only matters when you export final builds — compressed formats (Vorbis, AAC) handle their own internal precision.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If your source assets are 16-bit and you apply extreme EQ boosts or gain in Unity, you may amplify the existing quantization noise floor. Start with 24-bit source assets when possible to give yourself more headroom for processing.

misconceptions: Higher bit depth always means better sound — above 16-bit, the quantization noise floor is far below human hearing threshold. 24-bit offers headroom for processing, not audibly less noise in the final output.

Quantization noise is always audible — at 16-bit with dithering, the noise floor is at -93 dB, far below typical listening environments. Quantization noise is only an issue at very low bit depths or without dithering.

32-bit float audio files sound better than 24-bit — 32-bit float is essential for internal processing (prevents overflow), but as a final delivery format it offers no perceptual advantage over properly dithered 24-bit or even 16-bit.

name: Audio Rolloff

beginner.oneLiner: Audio rolloff controls how a sound gets quieter as you move further away from it, just like sounds in the real world.
beginner.analogy: Imagine standing next to a radio. Walk across the room and it gets quieter. Walk outside and you can barely hear it. Rolloff is the rule that decides how quickly that volume drop happens.
beginner.whatToDo: On an AudioSource component, look at the 3D Sound Settings section. Set Min Distance (where the sound is at full volume) and Max Distance (where it becomes silent). Choose a rolloff mode: Logarithmic for realistic falloff, Linear for simple game-friendly falloff.

intermediate.oneLiner: Rolloff defines the attenuation curve between an AudioSource and the AudioListener, determining how volume decreases with distance.
intermediate.details: Unity provides three rolloff modes. Logarithmic Rolloff mimics real-world inverse-square-law behavior where most volume drop happens close to the source. Linear Rolloff decreases volume at a constant rate from Min to Max Distance and is easier to predict and tune. Custom Rolloff lets you draw your own curve for full control. Min Distance is the radius within which the sound plays at full volume. Beyond Max Distance, the sound is silent (Linear) or nearly silent (Logarithmic). The curve is visualized in the AudioSource inspector.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use Linear Rolloff for most game audio -- it is predictable and easy to tune. Use Logarithmic for realistic simulations or VR. Use Custom when you need specific behavior, like a sound that stays loud for a long distance then drops off sharply.

advanced.oneLiner: Rolloff mode selects the distance attenuation function applied per-voice in the spatialization pipeline, with Custom Rolloff providing an AnimationCurve for arbitrary attenuation profiles.
advanced.technicalNotes: Logarithmic Rolloff uses the formula: MinDistance / (MinDistance + RolloffFactor * (Distance - MinDistance)). The rolloffFactor on AudioSource scales this curve. Linear is simply: 1 - ((Distance - MinDistance) / (MaxDistance - MinDistance)). Custom Rolloff evaluates an AnimationCurve where X is normalized distance (0 at Min, 1 at Max) and Y is volume (0-1). For large open worlds, set Max Distance carefully -- excessively large values waste virtual voice slots on inaudible sources. The Spatial Blend must be > 0 for rolloff to have any effect; at spatialBlend = 0 (fully 2D), distance attenuation is bypassed entirely.

vs.FMOD Distance Attenuation: FMOD provides similar min/max distance curves but with additional per-event override capability and built-in virtualization based on audibility.
vs.Real-world inverse square law: True inverse-square attenuation (6 dB per doubling of distance) sounds natural but is hard to mix in games. Logarithmic Rolloff approximates this. Linear Rolloff trades realism for mix control.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > 3D Sound Settings section. Visible when Spatial Blend > 0.
unityAdapter.importTip: Start with Linear Rolloff, Min Distance = 1, Max Distance = 50 as a baseline. Adjust Max Distance to match the gameplay-relevant hearing range. Use the scene view audio preview (toggle the volume icon) to test rolloff in-editor.
unityAdapter.gotcha: With Logarithmic Rolloff, the Max Distance setting does not create a hard cutoff -- the sound asymptotically approaches zero but technically never reaches it. Unity still virtualizes voices beyond Max Distance, but the sound may be faintly audible just inside it. If you need a hard cutoff, use Linear or Custom Rolloff.

misconceptions: Rolloff does not affect 2D sounds. If Spatial Blend is set to 0, the sound is fully 2D and plays at constant volume regardless of distance.
Max Distance is not the distance where the sound starts getting quieter. It is where the sound reaches minimum volume. The sound starts attenuating as soon as the listener moves beyond Min Distance.

name: Audio Sprite

beginner.oneLiner: An audio sprite is a single audio file that contains multiple short sounds packed end to end, played back by seeking to different time offsets.
beginner.analogy: Think of a filmstrip where each frame is a different picture. Instead of carrying a hundred separate photos, you have one long strip and jump to the frame you need. An audio sprite works the same way — one file holds many sounds, and you jump to the right spot to play each one.
beginner.whatToDo: Consider audio sprites when targeting web or mobile platforms where loading many small files is slow. Pack related short sounds (UI clicks, footstep variations, small impacts) into a single file with a JSON map defining each sound's start time and duration.

intermediate.oneLiner: An audio sprite is a concatenated audio file accompanied by a timing map that defines named regions, reducing HTTP requests and load overhead on platforms where individual file loads are expensive.
intermediate.details: Audio sprites originated in web development to work around mobile browser limitations (early iOS Safari required user interaction per audio element). By combining many sounds into one file and using the Web Audio API to play specific time ranges, developers avoid per-file HTTP overhead and autoplay restrictions. The accompanying JSON or data map specifies each sound's offset and duration within the sprite.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use audio sprites for web games with many short sound effects, especially on mobile browsers. In native game engines like Unity, the asset bundling system handles batching already, so audio sprites are less necessary. They remain useful for HTML5 games, browser-based prototypes, and situations with many small sounds that benefit from a single decode.

advanced.oneLiner: Audio sprites amortize decode and network costs by packing multiple samples into a contiguous buffer, indexed by a region map, trading random-access flexibility for reduced I/O overhead in resource-constrained environments.
advanced.technicalNotes: In the Web Audio API, decode the entire sprite into an AudioBuffer once, then use AudioBufferSourceNode.start(when, offset, duration) to play specific regions. Include a few milliseconds of silence between regions to prevent bleeding from imprecise seeking. For compressed formats (MP3), encoder padding can shift region boundaries — use uncompressed WAV or Opus for precise offsets, or account for encoder delay in your timing map. Some tools (audiosprite, TexturePacker Audio) automate sprite generation and map creation.

vs.audio-pool: An audio pool manages multiple pre-instantiated playback channels for concurrent sounds. An audio sprite manages how sounds are stored and loaded. They solve different problems and can be used together — pool the playback of sounds sourced from a sprite.
vs.streaming: Streaming loads audio progressively from disk or network. Audio sprites preload entirely into memory. Sprites trade memory for instant playback of many short sounds; streaming trades memory for reduced up-front load of long sounds.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a built-in audio sprite system. You would implement one manually by loading a single AudioClip and using AudioSource.time or AudioSource.PlayScheduled with offsets.
unityAdapter.importTip: For Unity, audio sprites are rarely needed since the asset system handles batching. If targeting WebGL builds, consider packing sounds into a single clip and writing a simple manager that plays at specific offsets using AudioSource.time and AudioSource.SetScheduledEndTime().
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.time is not sample-accurate for compressed clips — decoding introduces slight timing variations. For precise audio sprite playback in Unity WebGL, prefer uncompressed clips or use the Web Audio API directly through JavaScript plugins.

misconceptions: Audio sprites are needed in all game engines — they were created to solve web browser limitations. Native engines like Unity and Unreal handle asset loading efficiently without needing sprites.

Audio sprites waste memory because you load sounds you do not need — if you carefully group related sounds that are all used in the same context, the overhead is minimal and the loading benefit is significant.
You cannot loop individual sounds in an audio sprite — you can, by tracking the region boundaries and scheduling the loop point precisely with the Web Audio API.

name: Audio Sweetening

beginner.oneLiner: Audio sweetening is the process of adding small enhancements and polish to a sound effect to make it feel more satisfying, impactful, or polished.
beginner.analogy: Sweetening is like adding seasoning to a dish that is already cooked. The core meal is done, but a pinch of salt, a drizzle of sauce, or a sprinkle of herbs takes it from good to great. In audio, sweetening might be adding a subtle reverb tail, a tiny high-frequency sparkle, or a low sub-bass rumble to an otherwise finished sound.
beginner.whatToDo: After you have your basic sound effect in place, listen critically: does it feel satisfying? Try adding a short reverb to give it space, a subtle pitch-shifted layer for depth, or a quick high-frequency transient for snap. Small additions make a big difference. The key is subtlety — sweetening should enhance, not overpower.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio sweetening adds supplementary layers, effects, or processing to existing sounds to enhance their emotional impact, perceived quality, and overall satisfaction without changing their core identity.
intermediate.details: Common sweetening techniques include: adding a sub-bass sine wave layer to impacts for physical weight, layering a pitched-up sparkle or chime on positive UI sounds, applying short reverb or delay for spatial depth, using harmonic exciters to add high-frequency sheen, adding foley texture layers for realism (cloth rustle on a character jump, mechanical click on a sci-fi door), and applying subtle stereo widening for grandeur. Sweetening is a finishing step — the base sound should already be close to final before you sweeten.
intermediate.whenToUse: Sweeten sounds that players interact with frequently (UI, weapons, abilities) or that carry emotional weight (victory fanfares, death sounds, boss encounters). Also sweeten sounds that feel flat or thin in context even if they sound fine in isolation. Compare your sounds in the full game mix, not just in a DAW.

advanced.oneLiner: Audio sweetening is the post-production refinement of audio assets through targeted spectral augmentation, psychoacoustic enhancement, and supplementary layering to optimize perceived quality and emotional resonance within the final mix context.
advanced.technicalNotes: Psychoacoustically effective sweetening targets frequency ranges where human hearing is most sensitive (2-5 kHz for presence, 6-10 kHz for air/brilliance) and sub-bass ranges felt more than heard (30-60 Hz). Harmonic exciters generate new harmonics from existing content via waveshaping or dynamic EQ, adding brightness without boosting noise. Transient enhancement using envelope-following gain boosts improves perceived punch. For interactive media, sweetening must account for dynamic mixing: a sweetened sound in isolation may lose its enhancements when competing with music and ambience at runtime. Use sidechain or ducking to protect sweetened details in the mix.

vs.layering: Layering builds the fundamental structure of a composite sound from multiple core sources. Sweetening adds finishing touches to an already-structured sound. Layering is construction; sweetening is polish.
vs.normalization: Normalization adjusts overall level to hit a target peak or loudness. Sweetening enhances tonal and textural qualities without necessarily changing the overall level.

unityAdapter.location: Apply sweetening using Audio Mixer effects (reverb, EQ), additional AudioSource layers triggered alongside the main sound, or post-processing via Audio Mixer send/return channels.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a dedicated 'Sweetener' Audio Mixer group with a reverb send and subtle EQ boost at 3-5 kHz. Route your key sound effects through this group for consistent polish without per-sound processing.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Sweetening layers add to your voice count and CPU budget. For frequently played sounds, pre-render the sweetened version offline rather than playing extra layers at runtime. Reserve real-time sweetening for important one-off events.

misconceptions: Sweetening is just making sounds louder. Effective sweetening adds tonal character, spatial depth, or textural detail — it is about quality, not volume.
Every sound needs sweetening. Some sounds are effective in their raw form. Over-sweetening leads to a cluttered, fatiguing mix. Apply sweetening selectively to the sounds that matter most.

name: Audio Thread

beginner.oneLiner: The audio thread is a dedicated background process that handles playing and processing sound separately from your game's main loop, so audio never stutters when gameplay gets busy.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a restaurant where the chef (audio thread) works in the kitchen independently from the waiter (game thread) taking orders. Even if the dining room gets chaotic, the chef keeps cooking at a steady pace. If the chef had to wait for the waiter between every dish, meals would be late. The audio thread works the same way — it keeps producing sound at a constant rate regardless of what the game is doing.
beginner.whatToDo: Avoid doing heavy work that blocks the main thread during gameplay, and never try to directly access audio buffers from your game logic. Let the engine handle the audio thread. If you hear audio glitches (clicks or dropouts), check if your frame rate is tanking — that can starve the audio thread of data.

intermediate.oneLiner: The audio thread is a high-priority OS thread that runs on a strict real-time schedule, filling audio buffers at a fixed interval determined by the sample rate and buffer size, independent of the game's frame rate.
intermediate.details: Operating systems give the audio thread elevated priority so it meets its deadlines — if a buffer is not filled in time, you get an audible glitch (dropout or click). The audio thread typically runs every few milliseconds (e.g., every 5.3 ms for a 256-sample buffer at 48 kHz). It pulls data from audio sources, runs DSP effects, mixes channels, and writes the result to the output buffer. Because it runs on a separate thread, you cannot safely read or write shared data (like game state) without synchronization. Most engines use lock-free data structures like ring buffers or atomic variables to communicate between the game thread and audio thread.
intermediate.whenToUse: Be aware of the audio thread whenever you build custom audio systems, write DSP code, or communicate between gameplay and audio (e.g., changing a parameter based on player health). Always use thread-safe communication — never use locks or mutexes on the audio thread, as blocking it causes audible glitches.

advanced.oneLiner: The audio thread is a real-time OS thread running at elevated priority (often SCHED_FIFO on Linux, MMCSS on Windows, real-time thread on macOS) that must complete its buffer-fill callback within a hard deadline of bufferSize/sampleRate seconds to avoid underruns.
advanced.technicalNotes: The audio thread's deadline is deterministic: at 48 kHz with a 512-sample buffer, you have exactly 10.67 ms to fill each buffer. Missing this deadline causes an underrun — the DAC plays silence or stale data, producing an audible click. Because of this hard real-time constraint, the audio thread must never: allocate heap memory (malloc/new), acquire contended locks/mutexes, perform file I/O, or make system calls that may block. Communication with the game thread uses lock-free structures: single-producer single-consumer ring buffers for streaming data, and atomic variables for parameter changes. On Windows, the audio thread runs under MMCSS (Multimedia Class Scheduler Service) with 'Pro Audio' task priority. Unity's audio thread is managed internally via FMOD's mixer thread; in Unreal, it is the FAudioThread. Both engines abstract the raw callback but impose the same constraints on any user code running in the audio pipeline.

vs.ring-buffer: The ring buffer is the data structure used to safely pass audio data between threads. The audio thread is the consumer that reads from the ring buffer; the game or loading thread is the producer that writes to it.
vs.audio-callback: The audio callback is the function that runs on the audio thread each time a buffer needs to be filled. The audio thread is the OS thread; the callback is the user code it executes.
vs.dsp: DSP processing runs on the audio thread. If your DSP code is too expensive to complete within the buffer deadline, you get dropouts. Profile DSP cost relative to the buffer duration.

unityAdapter.location: Project Settings > Audio > DSP Buffer Size controls the audio thread's buffer size. The audio thread itself is managed internally by Unity's FMOD integration and is not directly accessible.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioSettings.dspBufferSize, AudioSettings.outputSampleRate, OnAudioFilterRead (runs on the audio thread)
unityAdapter.importTip: OnAudioFilterRead executes on the audio thread, so you can use it for custom DSP. Keep it lightweight — no allocations, no Debug.Log, no accessing most Unity APIs. Use a ring buffer or volatile fields to exchange data with MonoBehaviour code on the main thread.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Calling Unity APIs (like GetComponent, Instantiate, or Debug.Log) from OnAudioFilterRead will cause crashes or undefined behavior because those APIs are not thread-safe. Only do math and buffer operations inside audio callbacks.

misconceptions: The audio thread runs at the same rate as the game's frame rate — it does not. The audio thread runs on its own schedule determined by buffer size and sample rate, completely independent of FPS.
You can safely read game variables from the audio thread without synchronization — this is a race condition. Use atomic operations or lock-free queues to pass data between threads.

A faster CPU always prevents audio glitches — not necessarily. Audio dropouts are caused by missed deadlines, which can happen due to thread preemption, garbage collection pauses, or blocking operations, even on fast hardware.

name: Audio Worklet

beginner.oneLiner: Audio Worklet is a web browser feature that lets you write custom audio processing code that runs on a dedicated audio thread, enabling real-time sound effects and synthesis in browser-based games.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a web page as a busy office. Normally, everything — animation, user interaction, audio — runs on one worker's desk (the main thread). Audio Worklet is like hiring a dedicated sound engineer who works in a separate soundproof booth. You send instructions to the booth, and the engineer processes audio continuously without being interrupted by the busy office. This is how browser games get smooth, glitch-free audio.
beginner.whatToDo: If you are building a WebGL game with Unity, the engine handles Audio Worklet integration for you. If you are writing custom web audio code, use AudioWorkletNode to create processing nodes and AudioWorkletProcessor to define the DSP logic. This replaces the older (and now deprecated) ScriptProcessorNode.

intermediate.oneLiner: Audio Worklet is the Web Audio API's mechanism for running custom JavaScript DSP code on a real-time audio thread, replacing the deprecated ScriptProcessorNode with a dedicated AudioWorkletGlobalScope that processes 128-sample render quanta.
intermediate.details: An Audio Worklet consists of two parts: the AudioWorkletProcessor (runs on the audio rendering thread, processes 128 samples per call) and the AudioWorkletNode (runs on the main thread, acts as the interface). Communication between them uses MessagePort for infrequent messages and AudioParam for sample-accurate parameter automation. The processor's process() method receives input and output Float32Arrays and a parameters object. It must return true to keep running or false to allow garbage collection. The 128-sample render quantum is fixed by the Web Audio spec — you cannot change it. Audio Worklets run in an isolated AudioWorkletGlobalScope with no access to the DOM, window, or main-thread APIs.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use Audio Worklets for custom DSP in browser games: procedural audio generation, custom effects, real-time voice processing, audio analysis that needs to run on the audio thread. For standard effects (gain, filter, delay, convolution), use built-in Web Audio nodes instead — they are implemented in optimized native code and are faster than JavaScript equivalents.

advanced.oneLiner: AudioWorklet provides a dedicated AudioWorkletGlobalScope on the audio rendering thread with a process() callback receiving 128-frame render quanta, enabling lock-free JavaScript DSP via SharedArrayBuffer for cross-thread data sharing and AudioParam for sample-accurate automation.
advanced.technicalNotes: The 128-sample render quantum means process() fires every 2.67 ms at 48 kHz. JavaScript JIT compilation makes worklet performance viable for moderate DSP (simple filters, synthesis, analysis), but it cannot match native C++ performance for heavy processing (convolution reverb, high-order filters on many channels). SharedArrayBuffer (when available, requires COOP/COEP headers) enables zero-copy data sharing between the main thread and worklet for large datasets (wavetables, impulse responses). Without SharedArrayBuffer, use MessagePort for bulk transfers (which copies data). AudioParams support a-rate (per-sample) and k-rate (per-quantum) automation. For Unity WebGL builds, the engine routes audio through an AudioWorklet internally, but custom OnAudioFilterRead code runs on the main thread (not the audio thread) due to JavaScript threading limitations — watch for performance implications.

vs.audio-callback: Audio Worklet is the browser's equivalent of a native audio callback. The key difference is the fixed 128-sample buffer size and JavaScript execution (vs native code with configurable buffer sizes).
vs.audio-thread: AudioWorklet runs on the browser's audio rendering thread, which is similar to a native audio thread but managed by the browser's audio subsystem rather than the OS directly.
vs.audio-graph: Web Audio API's graph (AudioNode.connect) is the browser's audio graph. AudioWorkletNode is a custom node you insert into that graph for custom processing.

unityAdapter.location: Unity WebGL builds use Audio Worklets internally for audio output. Custom audio code via OnAudioFilterRead works but runs on the main thread in WebGL, not the audio thread.
unityAdapter.apiRef: For custom web audio in Unity WebGL, use jslib plugins to interact with the Web Audio API directly. AudioWorkletProcessor and AudioWorkletNode are standard Web Audio APIs.
unityAdapter.importTip: If you need high-performance custom audio in a Unity WebGL build, consider writing the DSP in a .jslib plugin that creates an AudioWorkletNode directly, rather than relying on OnAudioFilterRead (which runs on the main thread in WebGL and competes with rendering).
unityAdapter.gotcha: In Unity WebGL, OnAudioFilterRead does NOT run on the audio thread — it runs on the main thread, which means heavy audio processing directly impacts frame rate. This is a significant difference from standalone builds where OnAudioFilterRead runs on a dedicated audio thread.

misconceptions: ScriptProcessorNode and AudioWorklet are interchangeable — ScriptProcessorNode runs on the main thread and is deprecated. AudioWorklet runs on the audio thread and is the correct modern approach. ScriptProcessorNode causes audio glitches during heavy main-thread work.

JavaScript is too slow for real-time audio — modern JIT compilers make JavaScript viable for moderate DSP workloads. Simple filters, oscillators, and envelope generators run comfortably within the 2.67 ms deadline of a 128-sample quantum at 48 kHz.

Audio Worklets work the same in all browsers — implementation quality varies. Safari added AudioWorklet support later than Chrome and Firefox, and SharedArrayBuffer availability depends on security headers (COOP/COEP). Always test on your target browsers.

name: AudioClip

beginner.oneLiner: An AudioClip is the actual sound file imported into Unity -- it holds the raw audio data that gets played back.
beginner.analogy: Think of an AudioClip like a vinyl record. It holds the music, but you still need a turntable (AudioSource) and ears (AudioListener) to actually hear it.
beginner.whatToDo: Drag a .wav or .ogg file into your Unity project. Unity automatically creates an AudioClip asset. Assign it to an AudioSource component to play it.

intermediate.oneLiner: AudioClip is Unity's container for decoded or compressed audio sample data, with import settings that control memory footprint and load behavior.
intermediate.details: Each AudioClip has import settings that determine how audio is stored in memory: Decompress On Load, Compressed In Memory, or Streaming. The compression format (PCM, ADPCM, Vorbis) and sample rate settings on the clip directly affect build size and runtime memory. Clips can be mono or multi-channel, and Unity can force them to mono on import to save memory.
intermediate.whenToUse: Every sound in your game starts as an AudioClip. Choose compression and load settings based on clip length and frequency of use: short SFX benefit from Decompress On Load, longer clips from Compressed In Memory or Streaming.

advanced.oneLiner: AudioClip wraps a native audio buffer with metadata, supporting both static allocation and on-demand streaming via the AudioClip.Create API for procedural audio.
advanced.technicalNotes: AudioClip.Create() lets you generate clips at runtime from float sample arrays, enabling procedural audio. The loadState property allows async load tracking. For very large projects, use AudioClip.LoadAudioData() and AudioClip.UnloadAudioData() to manually manage when clip data occupies memory. Addressables can further defer loading until needed. The clip's frequency, channels, and samples properties are read-only after creation.

vs.FMOD Sound: FMOD's Sound object serves a similar role but supports a wider set of codecs and bank-based packaging. AudioClip is simpler but less flexible for complex streaming or bank workflows.

unityAdapter.location: Select any audio file in the Project window to see AudioClip import settings in the Inspector.
unityAdapter.importTip: For short SFX (< 1s), use ADPCM with Decompress On Load for low-latency playback. For music, use Vorbis with Streaming.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Leaving 'Preload Audio Data' checked on many clips increases scene load time. Uncheck it for clips that are not needed immediately and load them via script.

misconceptions: AudioClip is not a component -- it is an asset. You cannot attach it to a GameObject directly. You assign it to an AudioSource component.
Importing a .mp3 file does not mean Unity stores it as mp3 internally. Unity re-encodes to its own supported formats (PCM, ADPCM, Vorbis) based on your import settings.

name: AudioListener

beginner.oneLiner: AudioListener is the 'ears' of your game -- it receives all audio in the scene and is usually attached to the main camera.
beginner.analogy: If AudioSources are speakers placed around a room, the AudioListener is where you are standing. Sound gets louder or quieter based on how far the listener is from each speaker.
beginner.whatToDo: Unity's default Main Camera already has an AudioListener. You usually do not need to add one yourself. Just make sure only one is active in the scene at a time.

intermediate.oneLiner: AudioListener represents the player's receiving point for all audio and determines how 3D-spatialized sounds are mixed relative to its position and orientation.
intermediate.details: The listener's Transform drives all 3D audio calculations: distance attenuation, stereo panning, and doppler. In split-screen multiplayer, only one AudioListener can be active, which is a significant limitation. The static properties AudioListener.volume and AudioListener.pause provide global volume and pause control. Attaching the listener to a camera rig (rather than the camera itself) avoids audio artifacts from camera shake.
intermediate.whenToUse: Place it on whatever represents the player's perspective. For first/third-person games, that is the camera. For top-down games, consider placing it on the player character instead of the camera so distance attenuation feels correct.

advanced.oneLiner: AudioListener feeds world-space position and orientation into Unity's FMOD-based internal mixer for spatialization, and its placement directly affects all distance and angle calculations.
advanced.technicalNotes: Unity uses a single-listener model internally, unlike middleware like FMOD which supports multiple listeners for split-screen. For VR, the AudioListener should be on the head/camera rig so head tracking drives spatialization. AudioListener.GetOutputData() and GetSpectrumData() provide raw PCM and FFT data for visualization or analysis, but these capture the final mixed output, not individual sources. In HDRP/URP camera stacks, ensure the listener is on the base camera only.

vs.FMOD Listener: FMOD supports multiple listeners with configurable weighting, making split-screen audio straightforward. Unity's built-in audio only supports one.
vs.Wwise Listener: Wwise listeners can be added to multiple game objects with distance-based blending between them.

unityAdapter.location: Component > Audio > Audio Listener. Present on Main Camera by default.
unityAdapter.importTip: If your game has camera cuts or transitions, make sure the new camera either has the listener or that you move the listener to the active camera.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Having more than one active AudioListener in a scene produces a warning and undefined spatialization behavior. When switching cameras, disable the old listener before enabling the new one.

misconceptions: The AudioListener does not process or filter audio. It is purely a positional reference point. All DSP and mixing happens elsewhere in the pipeline.
Moving the AudioListener does not cause audio to 'lag behind.' The position is sampled each audio frame for spatialization calculations.

name: AudioSource

beginner.oneLiner: AudioSource is the Unity component that plays sound -- attach it to a GameObject and give it an AudioClip to hear audio in your game.
beginner.analogy: If an AudioClip is a record, the AudioSource is the speaker. You place it in the scene, point it at a clip, and it plays the sound from that position in the world.
beginner.whatToDo: Add an AudioSource component to any GameObject (Component > Audio > Audio Source). Assign an AudioClip, check 'Play On Awake' if you want it to play automatically, and hit Play.

intermediate.oneLiner: AudioSource controls how, when, and where a clip is played, including volume, pitch, spatialization, and routing to mixer groups.
intermediate.details: Key properties include spatialBlend (0 = 2D, 1 = full 3D), volume, pitch, loop, and priority. The Output property routes audio to an AudioMixerGroup for bus-level processing. 3D Sound Settings control rolloff, doppler, and spread. You can have many AudioSources in a scene, but Unity has a limited number of real voices (default 32 real, 256 virtual) -- lower-priority sources get virtualized when the limit is hit.
intermediate.whenToUse: Place an AudioSource on any object that emits sound: characters, weapons, ambient emitters, UI canvases. Use spatialBlend = 0 for UI and music, spatialBlend = 1 for world-positioned sounds.

advanced.oneLiner: AudioSource manages voice allocation and DSP scheduling, with sample-accurate playback control via PlayScheduled and SetScheduledEndTime.
advanced.technicalNotes: PlayScheduled() uses DSP time (AudioSettings.dspTime) for sample-accurate scheduling, essential for rhythm games or seamless music loops. PlayOneShot() is fire-and-forget and does not interrupt the current clip, making it ideal for overlapping SFX. For pooling, reuse AudioSources rather than creating/destroying them. The spatialize property enables a custom spatializer plugin (e.g., Oculus, Resonance Audio). Virtual voices still track position but consume no DSP resources.

vs.FMOD EventInstance: FMOD EventInstances offer parameter-driven adaptive audio and built-in pooling. AudioSource is simpler but requires manual scripting for adaptive behaviors.
vs.Wwise Game Object: Wwise binds emitters to game objects similarly but adds RTPC and state-driven logic at the middleware level.

unityAdapter.location: Component > Audio > Audio Source, or via script: gameObject.AddComponent<AudioSource>()
unityAdapter.importTip: For frequent one-shot SFX, use PlayOneShot() instead of Play() so multiple sounds can overlap on the same AudioSource without interrupting each other.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Calling Play() while a clip is already playing restarts it from the beginning. If you need overlapping sounds from one source, use PlayOneShot(). Also, an AudioSource with no AudioClip assigned will silently do nothing -- no error is thrown.

misconceptions: You do not need one AudioSource per sound effect. A single AudioSource can play many clips via PlayOneShot(), though it shares volume/pitch settings across all of them.
Setting spatialBlend to 1 does not automatically mean the sound uses realistic distance attenuation. You also need to configure the rolloff curve and max distance.

name: Bark Scale

beginner.oneLiner: The Bark scale is a way of measuring frequency that matches how human hearing actually perceives pitch, grouping frequencies into bands based on how the ear processes them.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a piano where every key sounds equally spaced in pitch to your ears. But physically, the lower keys cover huge frequency ranges while the higher keys cover tiny ones. The Bark scale is like relabeling the piano keys so the numbers reflect what you actually hear, not the raw physics.
beginner.whatToDo: You will rarely use the Bark scale directly in a game engine, but understanding it helps you make better EQ and masking decisions. Sounds within the same Bark band compete more for your attention, so spread important sounds across different bands to keep them distinct.

intermediate.oneLiner: The Bark scale divides the audible frequency range into 24 critical bands that correspond to the frequency resolution of the human basilar membrane, used in psychoacoustic analysis and perceptual audio coding.
intermediate.details: Each Bark band represents a region on the basilar membrane roughly 1.3 mm wide. Within a critical band, sounds interact strongly — they mask each other and are perceived as a fused timbre rather than distinct tones. The scale runs from 0 to 24 Bark, covering 20 Hz to about 15.5 kHz. It is closely related to the ERB (Equivalent Rectangular Bandwidth) scale and the mel scale, all of which model perceptual frequency spacing.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use the Bark scale concept when analyzing masking interactions in your mix. If two important game sounds fall within the same critical band, they will mask each other. Understanding this helps you choose EQ frequencies, pitch-shift sounds to reduce masking, and design clearer mixes.

advanced.oneLiner: The Bark scale maps frequency to critical band rate via z = 13·arctan(0.76f/kHz) + 3.5·arctan((f/7.5kHz)²), modeling the tonotopic organization of the basilar membrane and forming the basis of psychoacoustic masking models.
advanced.technicalNotes: The Bark scale underpins perceptual audio codecs like MP3 and AAC, which allocate bits according to masking thresholds computed per critical band. In game audio, the practical takeaway is that two signals within the same critical band (~100 Hz wide at low frequencies, ~3-4 kHz wide at high frequencies) will mask each other strongly. The ISO 532 loudness calculation standard uses Bark-band decomposition. For spectral analysis in game audio tools, converting FFT bins to Bark bands gives a perceptually meaningful frequency axis.

vs.frequency: Frequency is a linear physical measurement in Hz. The Bark scale is a perceptual, non-linear remapping of frequency that reflects how the ear groups and resolves frequencies.
vs.fft: FFT gives linearly spaced frequency bins. Bark-scale analysis groups those bins into perceptually meaningful critical bands, reducing data while preserving information relevant to human hearing.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not expose Bark scale directly. Apply the concept when configuring AudioMixer EQ bands or analyzing frequency content with third-party spectrum analyzers.
unityAdapter.apiRef: N/A — conceptual tool for mixing decisions
unityAdapter.importTip: When setting up parametric EQ in Unity's Audio Mixer, think in terms of critical bands: if two sounds occupy the same band, use EQ to carve space for each. Typical critical bands to watch: 200-400 Hz (muddiness), 1-4 kHz (presence and dialogue clarity), 4-8 kHz (sibilance and attack).
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in EQ filters operate in linear Hz, not Bark. You need to mentally map critical band boundaries when setting filter frequencies. There is no built-in Bark-to-Hz conversion.

misconceptions: The Bark scale is only for scientists — it directly informs practical mixing decisions like EQ placement and avoiding frequency masking between important game sounds.
All frequency scales are interchangeable — linear Hz, mel, Bark, and ERB scales each model different aspects of perception. Bark specifically models critical band masking behavior.
Critical bands are all the same width — they widen as frequency increases: roughly 100 Hz wide below 500 Hz but several kHz wide above 10 kHz.

name: Binaural Audio

beginner.oneLiner: Binaural audio is a technique that creates a realistic 3D sound experience through headphones by mimicking how your two ears hear sounds differently depending on direction.
beginner.analogy: When someone talks to your right, your right ear hears them louder and a tiny bit sooner than your left ear. Binaural audio recreates these tiny differences in a recording or in real-time, tricking your brain into thinking sounds are coming from specific locations all around you — even though you are just wearing headphones.
beginner.whatToDo: To use binaural audio in your game, enable a spatializer plugin that supports HRTF processing and ensure your players use headphones. Use mono source clips for 3D sounds and mark your game as headphone-recommended if binaural is central to the experience.

intermediate.oneLiner: Binaural audio renders 3D spatial cues — interaural time difference, level difference, and spectral shaping — into a two-channel stereo signal optimized for headphone listening.
intermediate.details: Binaural rendering applies HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) filters to each sound source based on its position relative to the listener. The result is a stereo signal that, when heard through headphones, produces convincing directional perception including elevation and front-back distinction. This goes beyond simple stereo panning, which can only move sounds left and right. Binaural processing requires headphones — on speakers, the crossfeed between ears destroys the effect.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use binaural rendering for VR games (essential for immersion), horror games (precise localization creates tension), stealth games (players need to locate enemies by sound), and any game where the target audience uses headphones. Offer a toggle in audio settings so speaker users can switch to a standard panning mode.

advanced.oneLiner: Binaural rendering convolves mono source signals with direction-dependent HRTF impulse responses to synthesize the interaural time difference, interaural level difference, and pinna-related spectral cues that the auditory system uses for 3D localization.
advanced.technicalNotes: Each HRTF is a pair of FIR filters (left ear, right ear) measured or modeled at discrete directions, typically on a sphere with 1-5 degree resolution. Real-time binaural rendering interpolates between measured directions as sources move. Filter lengths range from 128 to 512 taps; shorter filters are cheaper but lose low-frequency ITD accuracy. Individualized HRTFs improve localization but are impractical for games — generic HRTFs from databases like CIPIC, LISTEN, or MIT KEMAR work reasonably well. For many concurrent sources, use ambisonics as an intermediate: encode all sources into an ambisonic bus, then do a single binaural decode, reducing per-source convolutions.

vs.stereo-vs-mono: Standard stereo only positions sounds on the left-right axis. Binaural audio adds front-back and up-down positioning using spectral filtering, producing a full 3D soundfield from just two channels.
vs.ambisonic: Ambisonics encodes a full soundfield into a speaker-independent format with N channels. Binaural is always two channels targeting headphones. Ambisonics can be decoded to binaural as one of many possible output formats.
vs.spatialization: Spatialization is the general concept of positioning sounds in 3D. Binaural audio is a specific rendering method for spatialization optimized for headphone output.

unityAdapter.location: Install a spatializer plugin (Steam Audio, Resonance Audio, Oculus Audio) via Package Manager. Set it in Edit > Project Settings > Audio > Spatializer Plugin. Enable 'Spatialize' on each AudioSource.
unityAdapter.importTip: For best binaural results: use mono clips, set spatialBlend to 1.0, enable the 'Spatialize' checkbox, and test exclusively with headphones. Steam Audio's binaural rendering is free and supports room simulation for added realism.
unityAdapter.gotcha: The 'Spatialize' checkbox is separate from spatialBlend. Setting spatialBlend to 1.0 enables basic 3D panning, but only checking 'Spatialize' activates the HRTF binaural plugin. You need both for true binaural rendering.

misconceptions: Binaural audio works on speakers — it does not. Speaker playback causes acoustic crosstalk between ears that destroys the binaural cues. Always use headphones for binaural content.
Binaural and stereo are the same thing — stereo only places sounds on the left-right axis. Binaural adds vertical and front-back cues using HRTF filtering, creating a full 3D experience.
Binaural audio requires special headphones — any standard stereo headphones work. The spatial processing is in the audio signal, not the hardware.

name: Biquad Filter

beginner.oneLiner: A biquad filter is a versatile building block for audio effects that can boost, cut, or remove specific frequencies from a sound.
beginner.analogy: Think of a biquad filter like an adjustable window blind. You can open it to let certain light (frequencies) through, close it to block them, or tilt it to let only some angles through.
