By adjusting a few knobs — which frequencies to target, how wide the range is, and how much to boost or cut — you can shape any sound.
beginner.whatToDo: Use biquad filters to add EQ to your sounds. Most game engines provide them as low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, or peaking filters. Apply a low-pass filter to muffle sounds behind walls, a high-pass to thin out rumble, or a peaking filter to boost or cut a specific frequency range.

intermediate.oneLiner: A biquad filter is a second-order IIR (Infinite Impulse Response) filter defined by five coefficients that can implement low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, notch, peaking, and shelving filter types.
intermediate.details: The biquad is the workhorse of real-time audio filtering. It processes each sample using two feedforward (zeros) and two feedback (poles) coefficients, making it computationally cheap — just a handful of multiply-add operations per sample. By computing different coefficient sets from frequency, Q (resonance), and gain parameters, the same biquad structure implements any standard filter type. Multiple biquads can be cascaded for steeper slopes (e.g., two biquads for a 4th-order 24 dB/octave filter).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use biquad filters for real-time EQ, occlusion simulation (low-pass with varying cutoff), radio or telephone effects (band-pass), removing rumble (high-pass), and dynamic tone shaping. They are efficient enough to run per-source in most game engines.

advanced.oneLiner: A biquad filter implements the transfer function H(z) = (b0 + b1·z⁻¹ + b2·z⁻²) / (1 + a1·z⁻¹ + a2·z⁻²), providing a second-order IIR topology that serves as the fundamental building block for parametric EQ, crossovers, and dynamic filtering.
advanced.technicalNotes: The direct form II transposed implementation is preferred for floating-point arithmetic due to better numerical stability. Coefficient computation follows the Audio EQ Cookbook (Robert Bristow-Johnson). For parameter smoothing (automating cutoff frequency), recalculate coefficients at a reduced control rate or interpolate coefficients linearly — but be careful, as direct coefficient interpolation can cause instability. For very low cutoff frequencies at high sample rates, use double-precision arithmetic to avoid coefficient quantization issues. Cascade two biquads for Linkwitz-Riley crossovers (4th-order, -6 dB at crossover point).

vs.parametric-eq: A parametric EQ is built from one or more biquad filters. Each EQ band is typically one biquad. The parametric EQ is the user-facing tool; the biquad is the underlying DSP primitive.
vs.low-pass-filter: A low-pass filter is one specific configuration of a biquad filter. The biquad is the general-purpose structure; low-pass, high-pass, band-pass, and others are specific coefficient recipes applied to it.

unityAdapter.location: Unity provides AudioLowPassFilter and AudioHighPassFilter components. For full biquad control, use the Audio Mixer's parametric EQ effect or implement custom filters via OnAudioFilterRead.
unityAdapter.importTip: For simple filtering, attach AudioLowPassFilter or AudioHighPassFilter to an AudioSource and animate the cutoffFrequency property. For full parametric EQ with band-pass, notch, and peaking modes, use the Audio Mixer's built-in EQ effect on a mixer group.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioLowPassFilter and AudioHighPassFilter components do not expose a Q/resonance parameter directly — the resonance is fixed. For precise control, use the Audio Mixer's parametric EQ or implement your own biquad in OnAudioFilterRead.

misconceptions: Biquad filters are complicated to use — while the math behind them is involved, game engines expose them as simple components with frequency and Q knobs. You do not need to calculate coefficients by hand.
One biquad filter is enough for any task — a single biquad is only second-order (12 dB/octave slope). For steeper filtering, you need to cascade multiple biquads.
Biquad filters introduce noticeable latency — they are sample-by-sample IIR filters with zero additional latency (no buffering required), making them ideal for real-time audio.

name: Bit Depth

beginner.oneLiner: Bit depth controls how precisely each audio snapshot is measured — more bits means more detail, especially in quiet sounds.
beginner.analogy: Imagine measuring your height. A ruler with only inch marks gives a rough answer. A ruler with millimeter marks gives a precise one. Bit depth is like the precision of your audio ruler — more bits means finer measurements of the sound's volume at each snapshot.
beginner.whatToDo: Use 16-bit for your final game audio. It provides more dynamic range than you'll ever need for playback. Keep 24-bit versions of your source files for editing and mixing.

intermediate.oneLiner: Bit depth defines the number of discrete amplitude levels available per sample. Each additional bit doubles the resolution and adds roughly 6 dB of dynamic range.
intermediate.details: 16-bit audio has 65,536 possible amplitude values and ~96 dB of dynamic range, which exceeds the useful range for game playback. 24-bit provides ~144 dB and is used during recording and mixing to avoid clipping while preserving quiet details. 32-bit float is used internally by audio engines for mixing, since its floating-point representation handles extreme values without clipping. Quantization noise — the error introduced by rounding to discrete levels — is the practical limit of low bit depths. Dithering adds tiny random noise to mask quantization distortion when converting from higher to lower bit depths.
intermediate.whenToUse: Export final game assets at 16-bit. Record and edit at 24-bit to preserve headroom. You'll never need to ship 24 or 32-bit audio in a game build — the file size increase is not justified by audible improvement. If your engine mixes internally at 32-bit float (Unity does), it handles the precision for you.

advanced.oneLiner: Bit depth determines the quantization step size and thus the theoretical signal-to-noise ratio (SNR = 6.02n + 1.76 dB), with dithering converting quantization distortion into benign white noise.
advanced.technicalNotes: At 16-bit, the quantization step is 1/65536 of full scale, yielding a noise floor around -96 dBFS. Undithered quantization produces harmonic distortion correlated with the signal, which is perceptually worse than random noise of the same energy. Triangular probability density function (TPDF) dither eliminates this distortion at the cost of a ~3 dB noise floor increase. Noise shaping can push dither noise into less audible frequency ranges. Unity stores audio internally as 32-bit float during mixing, then the platform audio driver handles final conversion. When importing 24-bit WAVs, Unity truncates to 16-bit unless you select a format that preserves the extra bits. For game purposes, 16-bit + TPDF dither from your DAW is optimal.

vs.sample-rate: Bit depth controls dynamic range (amplitude precision). Sample rate controls bandwidth (frequency range). They are orthogonal: increasing one does not compensate for the other.

unityAdapter.location: AudioClip Inspector > the imported bit depth is determined by the source file and compression format
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioClip.samples, AudioClip.GetData() returns float[] normalized to -1..1 regardless of source bit depth
unityAdapter.importTip: Import 16-bit WAV files for final assets. Unity's internal mixing is 32-bit float, so 16-bit source files lose nothing meaningful. Importing 24-bit files wastes disk space in your project without audible benefit at runtime.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's GetData() always returns float samples normalized to [-1, 1]. You cannot query the original bit depth at runtime. If you need to know it, store it in metadata or asset labels.

misconceptions: 24-bit audio does not sound better than 16-bit during playback. The extra dynamic range only matters during recording and mixing, where you need headroom to avoid clipping.

Bit depth is not about 'quality' in the way most people think. It's specifically about how quietly a sound can be before it's lost in quantization noise. At 16-bit, that threshold is -96 dB — far below any reasonable listening level.

Going from 16-bit to 8-bit is a dramatic quality loss (noise floor jumps from -96 dB to -48 dB). Going from 16-bit to 24-bit is inaudible in practice. The relationship is not linear in terms of perceived quality.

name: Bitrate

beginner.oneLiner: Bitrate is how much audio data is packed into each second of sound — higher bitrate means better quality but bigger files.
beginner.analogy: Bitrate is like video resolution for audio. A 720p video looks decent but a 4K video looks sharper — similarly, higher bitrate audio sounds more detailed but uses more storage.
beginner.whatToDo: For compressed game audio (OGG), start around 128-160 kbps for music. Listen and increase if you hear artifacts like warbling or metallic sounds. For sound effects, 96-128 kbps is often sufficient.

intermediate.oneLiner: Bitrate (measured in kbps) defines the amount of data used per second of audio, directly governing the trade-off between file size and audio fidelity in compressed formats.
intermediate.details: Bitrate applies primarily to compressed formats like OGG Vorbis and MP3. Uncompressed formats (WAV, AIFF) have a fixed bitrate determined by sample rate, bit depth, and channels: a 44.1kHz/16-bit stereo WAV is always 1,411 kbps. For Vorbis, variable bitrate (VBR) is standard — the encoder uses more bits for complex passages and fewer for silence. Common game audio bitrates: 96 kbps (acceptable for voice), 128-160 kbps (good for music), 192+ kbps (high quality, diminishing returns).
intermediate.whenToUse: Choose bitrate based on content type and platform constraints. Mobile games with tight APK/IPA budgets benefit from lower bitrates (96-128 kbps). PC/console games can afford 160-192 kbps for music. Always A/B test compressed vs. original in your actual game mix — artifacts masked by gameplay audio let you use lower bitrates.

advanced.oneLiner: Bitrate quantifies the data throughput of an audio stream in kilobits per second, with perceptual quality following a logarithmic curve relative to bitrate increases.
advanced.technicalNotes: For Vorbis VBR, the quality parameter (q-1 to q10) maps to target bitrate ranges, not fixed values. Quality transparency (indistinguishable from source) is typically reached around q5-q6 (~160-192 kbps) for stereo music. Below 80 kbps, pre-echo artifacts and spectral smearing become noticeable on transient-heavy game audio. For uncompressed PCM, bitrate = sampleRate * bitDepth * channels (e.g., 48000 * 16 * 2 = 1,536 kbps). ADPCM achieves ~4:1 compression (roughly 350 kbps for stereo 44.1kHz) with negligible decode CPU cost, making it a strong choice for many simultaneous SFX voices.

vs.sample-rate: Sample rate determines the frequency range (Nyquist: half the sample rate). Bitrate in compressed audio determines the overall data budget. You can have a high sample rate at a low bitrate, but the encoder will aggressively discard frequency content to fit.
vs.bit-depth: Bit depth defines dynamic range in uncompressed audio (roughly 6 dB per bit). In compressed formats, the encoder manages dynamic range within the bitrate budget, so bit depth of the source matters less than the target bitrate.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Clip Inspector > Compression Format > Quality slider. The quality percentage maps to Vorbis bitrate when using Vorbis compression.
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's Vorbis quality slider (0-100%) roughly maps to 30-500 kbps. A setting of 70% is a good starting point for music (~160 kbps). Use the preview button in the Inspector to audition quality before building.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's quality percentage is not a direct bitrate value. Quality 50% does not mean 50% of the original bitrate. Use the Inspector's preview to check the actual compressed size and listen for artifacts.

misconceptions: Higher bitrate does not always mean audibly better quality. Above a certain threshold (~160 kbps for Vorbis), most listeners cannot perceive improvements, especially in a game with concurrent audio.

Bitrate is not relevant to uncompressed formats in a meaningful way — WAV and AIFF always use the full data rate determined by their sample rate and bit depth. You only 'choose' a bitrate when encoding to a lossy format.

name: Blend Container

beginner.oneLiner: A blend container mixes multiple sounds together and adjusts their volumes based on a game parameter, like vehicle speed or health level.
beginner.analogy: Picture a DJ with several tracks playing at once, sliding faders up and down based on the energy in the room. A blend container works the same way — as a game value changes (say, engine RPM), it smoothly fades between a low idle rumble and a high-rev roar without any hard cuts.
beginner.whatToDo: Create a blend container in your middleware tool. Add child sounds representing different intensity levels (e.g., engine idle, mid, high). Map a game parameter (like speed) to the volume of each child. The middleware will crossfade between them automatically as the parameter changes at runtime.

intermediate.oneLiner: A blend container plays multiple child sound objects simultaneously, using RTPC (Real-Time Parameter Control) curves to modulate each child's volume, pitch, or filter based on a continuous game parameter.
intermediate.details: Unlike switch containers (which play one child at a time), blend containers can have all children active simultaneously with their mix controlled by parameter curves. This enables smooth, continuous transitions. Typical uses include vehicle engines (RPM drives the blend), wind intensity layers, music tension layers, and character breathing (exertion level). In Wwise, these are Blend Containers with RTPC-driven child volumes. In FMOD, the equivalent is a multi-instrument with continuous parameter automation.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use blend containers when you need smooth, continuous transitions between sound layers driven by a game value. If the transition should be gradual (engine speed, wind, tension), use a blend container. If the change is discrete and state-based (surface type, weapon type), use a switch container instead. Keep the number of simultaneous layers reasonable (3-5) to manage CPU and voice count.

advanced.oneLiner: Blend containers implement multi-layer additive mixing with per-child parameter-driven automation curves for volume, pitch, LPF, and HPF, enabling continuous morphing across a parameter space.
advanced.technicalNotes: Voice count is the primary concern — all children may be active simultaneously even when their volume is near zero. Use voice management rules to virtually mute children below a threshold (e.g., -60 dB) to reclaim voices. Parameter curve design is critical: overlap adjacent layers' volume curves by 3-6 dB at their crossover points to avoid volume dips in the transition zone. For vehicle engines, consider adding pitch curves alongside volume to simulate RPM changes within each layer. HDR (High Dynamic Range) audio systems may further modulate blend volumes based on the loudest emitter in the scene.

vs.switch-container: Switch containers play exactly one child based on a discrete state value. Blend containers can play multiple children simultaneously with volumes driven by a continuous parameter.
vs.crossfade: A crossfade is a single transition between two sounds. A blend container manages ongoing multi-layer mixing that continuously responds to parameter changes throughout playback.

unityAdapter.location: Unity has no native blend container. Implement by running multiple AudioSources simultaneously and scripting their volumes based on a game parameter. Middleware plugins provide this natively.
unityAdapter.apiRef: Multiple AudioSource components with scripted volume curves, or AkSoundEngine.SetRTPCValue() for Wwise
unityAdapter.importTip: If building manually, use AnimationCurves in the Inspector to define volume-vs-parameter curves for each layer, then evaluate them every frame: source.volume = curve.Evaluate(parameter);
unityAdapter.gotcha: Running multiple AudioSources simultaneously for blending burns through your voice budget fast. Make sure to stop or mute sources whose contribution is inaudible (below -60 dB) rather than just setting volume to 0.

misconceptions: Blend containers and switch containers are interchangeable — they serve different purposes. Switches are for discrete state changes; blends are for continuous parameter-driven layering.
Setting a layer's volume to 0 stops it from using CPU — the voice is still active and decoded even at zero volume unless you explicitly virtualize or stop it.
You need dozens of layers for a smooth blend — 3-5 well-designed layers with overlapping curves usually sound convincing and keep performance manageable.

name: Bus Effect

beginner.oneLiner: A bus effect is an audio effect (like reverb or compression) applied to an entire group of sounds at once through a shared audio bus, rather than to each individual sound separately.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a group of singers all performing into one microphone. Instead of giving each singer their own reverb pedal, you add reverb to the shared microphone. Every voice gets the same reverb treatment at once, saving equipment and keeping them sounding like they are in the same room.
beginner.whatToDo: Add effects to your audio mixer groups rather than to individual AudioSources. For example, put all your footstep sounds on a 'Footsteps' bus and add a single reverb and EQ to that bus instead of to every footstep source.

intermediate.oneLiner: A bus effect is a DSP processor inserted on an audio bus or mixer group, processing the summed signal of all sources routed through that bus in a single pass.
intermediate.details: Bus effects come in two flavors: inserts and sends. An insert effect processes the entire bus signal inline (e.g., a compressor on the SFX bus). A send effect receives a copy of the signal from one or more buses (e.g., a shared reverb send). Inserts modify the signal in place; sends blend processed and dry signals. Using bus effects instead of per-source effects saves CPU (one compressor on 20 sounds vs. 20 compressors) and creates a more cohesive mix.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use bus inserts for effects that should uniformly affect a category of sounds: compression on dialogue, EQ on music, limiting on the master bus. Use bus sends for shared spatial effects: one reverb send for all sounds in a room, one delay send for rhythmic effects. Reserve per-source effects for sounds that need unique processing.

advanced.oneLiner: Bus effects are DSP processors operating on the summed output of a mixer group, offering O(1) cost per effect regardless of voice count, and enabling mix-wide dynamic processing, sidechain routing, and send/return architectures.
advanced.technicalNotes: In Unity's Audio Mixer, effects are added as components on AudioMixerGroups and process in top-to-bottom order. The signal flow is: sources sum into the group, then pass through insert effects sequentially, then to the parent group. Send effects tap the signal at a configurable point (pre- or post-fader) and route it to a designated return group. For sidechaining, expose a parameter on the compressor (e.g., threshold) and drive it from another bus's signal via the Audio Mixer's built-in send mechanism. Watch effect ordering — a compressor before an EQ behaves very differently from EQ before compressor.

vs.send-effect: A send effect is a specific type of bus effect that receives copies of signals from multiple buses (e.g., a shared reverb return). A bus insert effect processes only the bus it is on. Sends are for shared effects; inserts are for per-bus processing.
vs.audio-bus: An audio bus is the routing path that groups signals together. A bus effect is the DSP processor applied to that bus. The bus is the pipe; the effect is the processing that happens inside the pipe.

unityAdapter.location: Open the Audio Mixer window (Window > Audio > Audio Mixer). Select a group and click 'Add Effect' in the Inspector to insert effects like EQ, compression, reverb, or custom plugins.
unityAdapter.importTip: Right-click a parameter on any bus effect and choose 'Expose' to make it controllable from scripts via AudioMixer.SetFloat(). This lets you automate reverb wet/dry, compressor threshold, or EQ frequency at runtime based on gameplay.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Effects on a mixer group process in top-to-bottom order. Reordering matters — placing a compressor before vs. after an EQ produces different results. Drag effects in the Mixer Inspector to reorder them.

misconceptions: Every sound needs its own effects — applying effects per-source wastes CPU and makes the mix inconsistent. Bus effects process many sounds at once more efficiently.
Bus effects sound the same as per-source effects — a compressor on a bus responds to the combined level of all sources, which produces different dynamics than individual compression on each source.
You cannot control bus effects at runtime — Unity lets you expose any mixer effect parameter and control it via scripting, enabling dynamic mix changes based on gameplay.

name: Chorus

beginner.oneLiner: Chorus makes a single sound seem like multiple sources playing at once, creating a thicker and richer tone.
beginner.analogy: Imagine one person singing versus a choir of people singing the same note. Even if they try to be perfectly in sync, tiny differences in timing and pitch between the singers create a rich, shimmering sound. A chorus effect fakes this by making slightly detuned and delayed copies of the original.
beginner.whatToDo: Add a chorus effect to sounds you want to feel fuller or more magical — enchanted items, ethereal voices, shimmering UI sounds, or lush ambient pads. Keep the depth and rate moderate to avoid an obviously wobbly sound.

intermediate.oneLiner: Chorus duplicates the input signal with short, modulated delay times and slight pitch variation, creating the illusion of multiple detuned sources playing simultaneously.
intermediate.details: Parameters include rate (how fast the delay time modulates, typically 0.1-5 Hz), depth (how much the delay time varies, controlling the amount of detuning), delay (base delay time, usually 15-35ms), and wet/dry mix. The modulation of delay time causes subtle pitch shifting via the Doppler effect on the delayed copy. Multiple voices (2-4 delay lines with different modulation phases) create a richer effect.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use chorus for magical or ethereal sound design, thickening thin synth sounds, creating shimmering ambient textures, enchanted weapon or item audio, dream sequences or altered states, and giving UI sounds a polished, expensive feel. Subtle chorus (low depth, slow rate) adds width without being obviously an effect.

advanced.oneLiner: Chorus is implemented as one or more short delay lines (15-35ms) with LFO-modulated delay times, where the time-varying delay produces comb filtering and pitch modulation that simulates multiple detuned sources.
advanced.technicalNotes: The pitch variation is a side-effect of modulating the delay read position: advancing the read head faster than the write head compresses the waveform (pitch up), and vice versa. Depth controls the LFO amplitude in samples, and rate controls LFO frequency. For stereo chorus, offset the LFO phases between left and right channels by 90-180 degrees for width. Anti-aliasing interpolation on the delay read (cubic or allpass) is important for clean modulation. Unlike flanger, chorus uses longer base delays (>15ms) so the comb-filter notches are too closely spaced to be individually audible, producing a smoother, less metallic character.

vs.delay: Delay creates distinct, audible echoes with longer delay times and optional feedback. Chorus uses very short delays (15-35ms) modulated by an LFO with no feedback, producing a thickening/doubling effect rather than discrete echoes.
vs.reverb: Reverb simulates room reflections with a dense diffuse tail. Chorus creates a shimmer/doubling effect from modulated short delays. They serve different purposes but can be combined for lush spatial effects.

unityAdapter.location: Add an Audio Chorus Filter component to an AudioSource or AudioListener in the Inspector.
unityAdapter.importTip: Attach AudioChorusFilter to your AudioSource. Key properties: 'dryMix' (0-1), 'wetMix1/2/3' (0-1, three chorus taps), 'delay' (0.1-100ms), 'rate' (0-20 Hz), and 'depth' (0-1).
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioChorusFilter has three wet mix taps (wetMix1, wetMix2, wetMix3) which are not well-documented. Start with wetMix1 at 0.5 and the others at 0 for a simple single-voice chorus, then add more taps for a richer effect. The default values can sound quite heavy — dial back the wet mix for subtlety.

misconceptions: Chorus is just a subtle delay — while it uses delay internally, the modulation of delay time is what creates the characteristic pitch-shifting and shimmer. A static short delay sounds different.
Chorus is only for music — it is very useful in game audio for magical effects, UI polish, ambient textures, and any sound that needs to feel richer.
More chorus depth is always better — high depth values create an obvious wobbling or seasick pitch effect. Subtle settings usually work best in a game mix.

name: Comb Filter

beginner.oneLiner: A comb filter is an effect that occurs when a sound is mixed with a slightly delayed copy of itself, creating a series of evenly spaced peaks and dips in the frequency spectrum.
beginner.analogy: Imagine two identical waves in a pond, one slightly behind the other. Where their peaks line up, the wave gets taller. Where a peak meets a dip, they cancel out and the water goes flat. A comb filter does the same thing with sound — some frequencies get louder and some disappear, creating a hollow, metallic tone.
beginner.whatToDo: Be aware that comb filtering can happen accidentally when the same sound reaches the listener via two paths with a tiny time difference (e.g., a direct sound plus a close reflection). If a sound has an unnatural hollow or metallic quality, check for duplicate or nearly-simultaneous playback.

intermediate.oneLiner: A comb filter results from the interference between a signal and a delayed copy, producing periodic notches and peaks in the frequency spectrum whose spacing is determined by the delay time.
intermediate.details: The notch spacing equals 1/delay_time Hz. A 1 ms delay produces notches every 1000 Hz; a 10 ms delay produces notches every 100 Hz. Feedforward comb filters (mixing original + delayed) create notches. Feedback comb filters (feeding output back to input) create sharp resonant peaks. Flangers are essentially comb filters with a modulated delay time. In reverb design, comb filters are fundamental building blocks of Schroeder reverb algorithms.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use comb filters intentionally for creative effects: flanging, metallic resonance, and as components in reverb algorithms. Avoid unintentional comb filtering by preventing duplicate playback of the same sound with slight timing offsets — randomize timing or use a one-shot pool to prevent overlapping identical sounds.

advanced.oneLiner: A comb filter implements H(z) = 1 + g·z⁻ᴹ (feedforward) or H(z) = 1/(1 − g·z⁻ᴹ) (feedback), producing periodic spectral notches or resonances at intervals of fs/M Hz, forming the basis of Schroeder reverb topologies and flanger effects.
advanced.technicalNotes: In Schroeder reverb, parallel feedback comb filters with mutually prime delay lengths (simulating different reflection path lengths) feed into cascaded allpass filters for diffusion. The feedback gain g controls RT60: higher g means longer decay. For flanging, modulate M continuously with an LFO. In game audio, unintentional comb filtering is common when a sound plays from multiple nearby sources or when early reflections have very short delay times. The perceptual threshold is around 1-30 ms of delay — shorter than 1 ms is perceived as timbral change, longer than 30 ms as a distinct echo.

vs.flanger: A flanger is a comb filter with a slowly modulated delay time (typically 0.5-10 ms swept by an LFO). A static comb filter has a fixed delay. The modulation is what gives flanging its characteristic sweeping, jet-engine sound.
vs.delay: A delay effect produces audible repetitions separated by enough time to hear distinct echoes (50+ ms). A comb filter uses much shorter delays (0.5-30 ms) where the repetitions fuse with the original into a single timbral effect rather than audible echoes.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a built-in comb filter component. Implement one manually in OnAudioFilterRead using a circular delay buffer, or use it as part of a flanger effect in the Audio Mixer.
unityAdapter.importTip: To create a simple comb filter in Unity, write an OnAudioFilterRead script that maintains a delay buffer. Add each incoming sample to the delayed sample multiplied by a gain coefficient. Keep the delay short (1-20 ms) for timbral effects.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Feedback comb filters can become unstable if the feedback gain exceeds 1.0, causing the signal to grow exponentially and produce loud, potentially damaging output. Always clamp feedback below 1.0 and add safety limiters during development.

misconceptions: Comb filtering is always bad — while unintentional comb filtering sounds hollow and unpleasant, deliberate comb filters are the basis of flanging, chorus, and reverb algorithms.
Comb filtering only happens with effects — it frequently occurs naturally when sound reflects off a nearby surface and mixes with the direct signal, such as a speaker near a wall or floor.
You can fix comb filtering with EQ — since comb filter notches are evenly spaced and numerous, EQ cannot practically correct them. The fix is to eliminate the timing offset causing the problem.

name: Compressed In Memory

beginner.oneLiner: Compressed In Memory keeps the sound in its compressed form in RAM, saving memory but requiring the CPU to decompress it each time it plays.
beginner.analogy: It is like keeping a zipped file in memory and unzipping small pieces of it on the fly whenever you need to read it. Uses less storage space, but your computer works harder each time.
beginner.whatToDo: In the AudioClip import settings, set Load Type to 'Compressed In Memory'. Use this for medium-length sounds like dialogue lines, ambient loops, or jingles.

intermediate.oneLiner: Compressed In Memory stores Vorbis/ADPCM data in RAM and decodes it in real-time during playback, balancing memory savings against ongoing CPU cost.
intermediate.details: The compressed data is loaded into memory, and Unity decodes it on the audio thread each time the clip plays. Memory usage is roughly equal to the compressed file size rather than the uncompressed PCM size. The CPU cost scales with the number of simultaneously playing clips using this load type, and Vorbis decoding is more expensive than ADPCM. This is Unity's default load type for longer clips.
intermediate.whenToUse: Ideal for clips between 2 seconds and 1 minute: dialogue, ambient loops, short music stings, and sound effects that are not played in large quantities simultaneously. It is the best middle-ground for most game audio.

advanced.oneLiner: Compressed In Memory retains codec-compressed buffers in heap memory and performs per-frame decode on the audio thread, with CPU cost proportional to active voices using this load type.
advanced.technicalNotes: Vorbis decoding is ~3-5x more CPU-intensive than ADPCM per voice. If you have many simultaneous voices (e.g., 20+ ambient emitters), the cumulative decode cost can become significant on mobile. ADPCM is a safer choice for clips that may have high polyphony. The compressed buffer size depends on the Quality slider in import settings -- lower quality means smaller buffers but more artifacts. Monitor the audio thread CPU usage in the Profiler's Audio section to detect decode bottlenecks.

vs.decompress-on-load: Decompress On Load uses more memory but zero runtime CPU for decoding. Choose it for frequently played short clips.
vs.streaming: Streaming uses even less memory (only a small read buffer) but adds disk I/O overhead and has higher initial latency.

unityAdapter.location: Select an audio file in the Project window > Inspector > Load Type dropdown > Compressed In Memory.
unityAdapter.importTip: Use Vorbis compression with a Quality setting of 70% as a starting point. Listen for artifacts and lower only if memory is tight. For clips with high simultaneous playback count, prefer ADPCM.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Playing many Compressed In Memory clips simultaneously with Vorbis encoding can spike the audio thread CPU, causing audible glitches. Profile early on target hardware.

misconceptions: Compressed In Memory does not decompress the clip once and cache it. It decodes fresh each time the clip plays (or each audio buffer fill during playback). The compressed data stays compressed in RAM.
This is not the same as streaming. The entire compressed clip is held in memory, so there is no disk I/O during playback.

name: Compressor

beginner.oneLiner: A compressor automatically turns down loud parts of a sound, making the overall volume more consistent and controlled.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a helpful sound engineer with their hand on the volume knob. Whenever the sound gets too loud, they quickly turn it down, and when it gets quiet, they let it come back up. A compressor does this automatically, thousands of times per second.
beginner.whatToDo: Use a compressor on your game's master bus to even out volume spikes from explosions or loud effects. Start with a gentle 2:1 ratio and a medium threshold to tame peaks without squashing the life out of your mix.

intermediate.oneLiner: A compressor reduces the dynamic range of an audio signal by attenuating it when it exceeds a threshold, controlled by ratio, attack, release, and makeup gain parameters.
intermediate.details: The key parameters are: Threshold (the level above which compression begins), Ratio (how much gain reduction is applied, e.g., 4:1 means 4 dB of input above threshold produces 1 dB of output), Attack (how quickly the compressor reacts to transients), Release (how quickly it stops compressing after the signal drops below threshold), and Makeup Gain (boosts the output to compensate for the gain reduction). Fast attack times catch transients but can reduce punch; slow attacks let transients through, preserving impact. The knee setting controls how gradually compression engages around the threshold.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use compression on the master bus for overall consistency, on dialogue buses to maintain intelligibility, and on sound effect groups to prevent volume spikes. In games, compression is especially useful for managing the unpredictable mix of layered sounds during gameplay. Avoid over-compressing ambient sounds, which can raise the noise floor and sound unnatural.

advanced.oneLiner: A compressor applies gain reduction based on an envelope follower tracking the signal level relative to a threshold, with the transfer curve defined by ratio, knee, attack, and release time constants.
advanced.technicalNotes: The compressor signal chain is: input -> level detection (peak or RMS) -> gain computer (applies threshold, ratio, knee) -> envelope follower (smooths gain changes via attack/release) -> gain stage (applies computed gain reduction). RMS detection gives smoother, more musical compression; peak detection is more aggressive and catches transients better. In Unity's Audio Mixer, compressors are available as built-in effects on mixer groups. The attack/release times interact with the audio buffer size; very fast attack times (< 1 ms) can introduce distortion on low-frequency content because the compressor modulates within a single wave cycle. For game audio, consider using per-bus compression rather than per-source to reduce CPU overhead.

vs.limiter: A limiter is essentially a compressor with an infinite (or very high) ratio and very fast attack. It acts as a ceiling that prevents the signal from exceeding a set level, while a compressor proportionally reduces the dynamic range.
vs.noise-gate: A noise gate is the inverse of a compressor: it attenuates signals below a threshold (cutting quiet sounds) rather than above it (reducing loud sounds).
vs.normalization: Normalization applies a fixed gain to the entire file. Compression dynamically varies the gain over time based on the signal level.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer > Select a group > Add Effect > Duck Volume (basic) or use the built-in compressor via exposed parameters
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer exposed parameters; no direct script API for compressor settings without exposed parameters
unityAdapter.importTip: Add a compressor effect to your Master mixer group. Expose the Threshold and Ratio parameters so you can tweak them at runtime. Start with Threshold around -20 dB and Ratio at 3:1 for gentle bus compression.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's mixer effects only process audio routed through the mixer. AudioSources not assigned to a mixer group will bypass the compressor entirely. Make sure all sources are routed to the correct mixer groups.

misconceptions: A compressor does not make audio louder by itself. It reduces the loud parts, and then makeup gain is applied separately to bring the overall level back up.
Compression and compressed audio formats (like Vorbis or MP3) are completely different concepts. Dynamic range compression is a mixing tool; format compression reduces file size.
Using a compressor with extreme settings does not improve audio quality. Over-compression removes dynamics and can make audio sound lifeless and fatiguing.

name: Constant Power Panning

beginner.oneLiner: Constant power panning is a technique that keeps the overall loudness of a sound consistent as it moves from left to right across the stereo field.
beginner.analogy: Imagine two spotlights on a performer crossing a stage. With constant power panning, as one spotlight dims, the other brightens at just the right rate so the performer always looks equally well-lit, no matter where they stand. Without it, the performer would look brightest at center stage (both lights at half power) and dimmer at the edges.
beginner.whatToDo: Constant power panning is the default in most game engines and middleware tools, so you usually do not need to do anything special. Just know that your engine is handling this behind the scenes to keep sounds at a consistent volume as they pan.

intermediate.oneLiner: Constant power panning uses sine and cosine gain curves to distribute signal between left and right channels, ensuring that total acoustic power remains constant across all pan positions.
intermediate.details: The gain for the left channel is cos(theta) and the right is sin(theta), where theta ranges from 0 (full left) to pi/2 (full right). At center (theta = pi/4), both channels receive cos(45) = sin(45) = ~0.707, which is -3 dB. Since power sums as G_L^2 + G_R^2 = cos^2 + sin^2 = 1, total power is constant. This compensates for the ~3 dB loudness increase that occurs when two speakers play the same signal, keeping perceived loudness steady as a sound pans.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use constant power panning for most game audio scenarios. It is the standard for 2D stereo panning of UI sounds, music stems, and any non-spatialized audio. If you are building a custom panner (e.g., for a modular synth in your game), implement the sine/cosine curves rather than linear interpolation.

advanced.oneLiner: Constant power panning applies trigonometric gain functions G_L = cos(theta * pi/2) and G_R = sin(theta * pi/2) to satisfy the power-preservation constraint G_L^2 + G_R^2 = 1, appropriate for uncorrelated signals where acoustic energy sums incoherently.
advanced.technicalNotes: The constant-power assumption holds for uncorrelated signals (like noise or different instruments). For correlated signals (like a mono source duplicated to both channels), amplitude preservation (-6 dB law, where G_L + G_R = 1) is more appropriate. In practice, most game audio sources are sufficiently uncorrelated that constant power works well. For SIMD-optimized panners, precompute a lookup table of sin/cos values at 1-degree increments to avoid runtime trig calls. Some implementations use the approximation G_L = sqrt((1-pan)/2), G_R = sqrt((1+pan)/2) where pan ranges from -1 to 1, which is mathematically equivalent.

vs.panning-law: Constant power panning is one specific panning law (the -3 dB law). Other panning laws include linear (-6 dB), compromise (-4.5 dB), and custom curves.
vs.spatialization: Constant power panning handles only left/right balance in a stereo field. Full 3D spatialization uses distance attenuation, HRTF filters, and multi-channel routing that goes far beyond simple stereo pan curves.

unityAdapter.location: Built into Unity's audio engine for 2D sounds. The panStereo property on AudioSource uses a constant-power curve internally.
unityAdapter.importTip: You get constant power panning automatically for 2D sounds. For 3D spatialized sounds, the spatializer handles panning differently (based on listener/source geometry), so panStereo should be left at 0.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If you implement a custom panner in script using linear interpolation (leftVol = 1-pan, rightVol = pan), you will hear a volume dip at center. Use the sine/cosine formula instead for perceptually correct results.

misconceptions: Linear panning is fine — linear panning causes a noticeable 3 dB volume drop at center, which is audible and distracting on moving sounds.
Constant power panning is only for music production — it is equally important in game audio for any stereo-panned sound.
You need to implement this yourself — nearly all game engines and middleware tools use constant power panning by default.

name: Convolution Reverb

beginner.oneLiner: Convolution reverb uses a recording of a real space's acoustics to make your sounds feel like they are actually in that space.
beginner.analogy: Imagine recording a loud clap in a cathedral and capturing how the room responds. Convolution reverb takes that recording and mathematically stamps the cathedral's acoustic character onto any sound, making it sound like it was played inside that cathedral.
beginner.whatToDo: Use convolution reverb when you want realistic room sounds. Find or record impulse responses (IR files) of spaces that match your game environments, then apply them to your audio. Start with a lower wet/dry mix so the effect is felt but not overpowering.

intermediate.oneLiner: Convolution reverb works by convolving the input signal with an impulse response (IR) recording of a real or synthetic acoustic space, faithfully reproducing that space's reverb characteristics.
intermediate.details: An impulse response captures the complete acoustic fingerprint of a space: the early reflections, the diffuse reverb tail, frequency-dependent absorption, and spatial characteristics. The convolution process multiplies every sample of the input with every sample of the IR in the frequency domain (using FFT), producing an output that sounds as if the input were played in that space. The IR length determines the reverb tail length. IRs can be recorded from real spaces using starter pistols or sine sweeps, or synthesized from virtual room models.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use convolution reverb for cinematic quality in cutscenes, realistic environmental audio, and any situation where the reverb must sound like a specific real space. It excels at reproducing unique spaces like stairwells, tunnels, or concert halls. Avoid it for abstract or synthetic reverb textures, and be mindful of CPU cost when using long IRs on many voices simultaneously.

advanced.oneLiner: Convolution reverb performs time-domain convolution (typically via partitioned FFT) of the input signal with an impulse response, enabling exact reproduction of linear time-invariant acoustic systems.
advanced.technicalNotes: Direct time-domain convolution is O(N*M) where N is the input length and M is the IR length, which is impractical for long IRs. Partitioned convolution splits the IR into blocks and uses overlap-add FFT convolution, reducing complexity to O(N*log(N)). Uniform partitioning uses a fixed FFT size; non-uniform partitioning uses small FFTs for early partitions (low latency) and larger FFTs for later partitions (efficiency). In Unity, there is no built-in convolution reverb. Use third-party solutions like Steam Audio, Resonance Audio, or custom implementations via OnAudioFilterRead with an FFT library. IR files are typically stored as WAV. For game use, keep IRs under 2 seconds and consider mono IRs to reduce processing cost.

vs.reverb: Algorithmic reverb uses delay networks and filters to approximate reverberation and is cheaper to compute with more tweakable parameters. Convolution reverb uses actual recorded impulse responses for greater realism but is more CPU-intensive and less flexible to modify.
vs.early-reflections: Convolution reverb inherently includes early reflections as part of the impulse response. Algorithmic reverb systems typically model early reflections separately from the diffuse tail.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in convolution reverb. Use third-party plugins such as Steam Audio, Resonance Audio, or custom DSP in OnAudioFilterRead.
unityAdapter.apiRef: Third-party dependent; Steam Audio: SteamAudioSource component; Resonance Audio: ResonanceAudioRoom
unityAdapter.importTip: For a quick setup, use Steam Audio's Unity plugin which supports convolution reverb with built-in IR management. Import your IR files as AudioClips and assign them to the reverb component. Use shorter IRs (under 1.5 seconds) for real-time game audio.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Convolution reverb is significantly more CPU-expensive than Unity's built-in algorithmic reverb. Profile carefully and limit it to a single bus or a few critical sources rather than applying it to every AudioSource.

misconceptions: Convolution reverb is not always better than algorithmic reverb. Algorithmic reverbs are more flexible, cheaper to run, and often more appropriate for stylized or abstract game audio.
An impulse response does not capture everything about a room. It only captures the linear acoustic response. Non-linear effects like distortion from very loud sounds are not represented.

You cannot easily modify individual parameters of a convolution reverb like decay time or room size. The IR is a fixed recording. To change the reverb character, you need a different IR or must process the IR itself.

name: Crossfade

beginner.oneLiner: Crossfading smoothly blends one sound out while another fades in, creating a seamless transition between two audio clips.
beginner.analogy: Crossfading is like a movie dissolve — one scene gradually fades away while the next one gradually appears on top of it. For a brief moment both are visible (audible) at the same time, and the transition feels smooth instead of jarring.
beginner.whatToDo: Use crossfades when transitioning between music tracks (e.g., going from exploration music to combat music). A crossfade of 1-3 seconds usually sounds natural. Avoid hard cuts between music tracks — they are almost always noticeable and break immersion.

intermediate.oneLiner: Crossfading simultaneously fades out one audio source while fading in another, using overlapping volume curves to create smooth transitions.
intermediate.details: A basic crossfade uses two AudioSources: one fading from volume 1 to 0, the other from 0 to 1, over a specified duration. The fade curve shape matters: linear crossfades cause a perceived loudness dip at the midpoint (both sources at 0.5 = combined amplitude lower than either alone). Equal-power crossfades use sine/cosine curves to maintain constant perceived loudness throughout the transition. Crossfade duration depends on context: 0.5-1s for ambient transitions, 1-3s for music changes, 0.05-0.1s for micro-crossfades within looping audio.
intermediate.whenToUse: Music state changes (menu to gameplay, exploration to combat). Ambient zone transitions (indoor to outdoor). Smoothing loop boundaries when you cannot get a perfectly seamless loop. Transitioning between dialogue lines in different acoustic environments.

advanced.oneLiner: Crossfading applies complementary gain envelopes to overlapping audio signals, with equal-power curves maintaining perceptual loudness constancy during the transition.
advanced.technicalNotes: For equal-power crossfade: outgoing gain = cos(t * pi/2), incoming gain = sin(t * pi/2), where t goes from 0 to 1. This ensures the summed power remains constant. For sample-accurate crossfades, apply the envelope in the audio thread or use AudioSource.PlayScheduled() with DSP time. Unity's Audio Mixer snapshots support transition times which effectively crossfade between mix states. For music, beat-synchronized crossfades (triggered on downbeats) sound more musical than arbitrary timing. FMOD and Wwise provide built-in crossfade transition logic in their music systems with stinger support.

vs.fade-out-fade-in: Fade-out then fade-in has a gap of silence between tracks. Crossfade overlaps both tracks so there is no silence. Crossfade is almost always preferable for music transitions.
vs.audio-mixer-snapshots: Mixer snapshots transition multiple parameters at once (volume, EQ, reverb) across entire mix groups. Crossfade typically refers to blending two specific audio sources. Snapshots are more powerful for holistic mix state changes.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in crossfade component. Implement via coroutine lerping two AudioSource volumes, or use Audio Mixer snapshot transitions with TransitionTo().
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a reusable MusicManager script with two AudioSource components that alternates between them for crossfade transitions. Use a coroutine with an equal-power curve for the smoothest result.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If you linearly lerp both AudioSource volumes from 0-1 and 1-0 simultaneously, you will hear a volume dip at the midpoint. Use equal-power curves (Mathf.Sin/Cos) or at minimum use a square-root curve to compensate.

misconceptions: A linear crossfade (straight line from 0 to 1) does not maintain constant loudness. The midpoint sounds quieter because amplitude and perceived loudness are not linearly related. Use equal-power curves for smooth transitions.
Crossfading is not just for music — it is useful for ambient transitions, smoothing loop seams, blending footstep surfaces, and any situation where two sounds need to transition smoothly.

name: Decibel (dB)

beginner.oneLiner: Decibels (dB) are the standard unit for measuring how loud a sound is, using a scale where small number changes represent big loudness differences.
beginner.analogy: The decibel scale is like earthquake magnitude: going from 3 to 4 on the Richter scale isn't just 'a little more' — it's 10 times more energy. Similarly, +10 dB sounds roughly twice as loud, even though it's 10x more sound energy. The dB scale matches how our ears actually perceive loudness.
beginner.whatToDo: When mixing your game's audio, think in dB rather than linear volume values. Most DAWs and audio mixers show levels in dB. A good target is to keep your overall mix peaking around -6 to -3 dBFS, leaving headroom so nothing clips.

intermediate.oneLiner: The decibel is a logarithmic unit expressing the ratio between two amplitude or power values. In digital audio, dBFS (dB Full Scale) measures levels relative to the maximum representable amplitude.
intermediate.details: For amplitude ratios: dB = 20 * log10(amplitude / reference). Doubling amplitude = +6.02 dB. For power ratios: dB = 10 * log10(power / reference). In digital audio, 0 dBFS is the maximum possible level; all other levels are negative. -6 dBFS is half amplitude, -20 dBFS is one-tenth amplitude. Perceived loudness roughly doubles every +10 dB. Common reference points: normal conversation is ~60 dB SPL, a gunshot is ~140 dB SPL. In game mixing, volume faders and gain controls operate in dB. The logarithmic nature matches human hearing, which is why a linear volume slider (0-1) feels wrong — most of the perceived change happens in the first 20% of the slider.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use dB for all mixing and volume control. Set your audio mixer groups in dB. When exposing volume controls to players, convert between a linear slider value and dB using: dB = 20 * log10(linearValue). This gives a perceptually smooth volume curve. Use -80 dB as your 'silent' floor — anything below that is inaudible.

advanced.oneLiner: The decibel is a dimensionless logarithmic ratio unit that linearizes the multiplicative nature of signal processing chains and aligns with the approximately logarithmic response of human auditory perception.
advanced.technicalNotes: Key dB variants in game audio: dBFS (Full Scale, digital reference where 0 = max), dB SPL (Sound Pressure Level, referenced to 20 micropascals), dBu/dBV (analog voltage references). In mixing, gain stages are additive in dB: a -3 dB clip through a -6 dB fader through a +2 dB bus = -7 dB at output. Unity's AudioMixer operates in dB (-80 to +20 dB range). The exposed parameter API uses dB directly: mixer.SetFloat('MasterVolume', -12f). For player-facing volume sliders, the standard conversion is: dB = 20f * Mathf.Log10(Mathf.Clamp(slider, 0.0001f, 1f)), and the inverse: slider = Mathf.Pow(10f, dB / 20f). LUFS (Loudness Units Full Scale) extends dB with perceptual weighting and time integration for loudness normalization across content.

vs.amplitude: Amplitude is a linear measurement of signal magnitude. Decibels express amplitude ratios on a logarithmic scale. dB = 20 * log10(A1 / A2). They describe the same physical property but on different scales.
vs.bit-depth: Each bit of depth adds ~6.02 dB of dynamic range. 16-bit audio has ~96 dB dynamic range; 24-bit has ~144 dB. The dB scale directly connects bit depth to practical noise floor measurements.

unityAdapter.location: AudioMixer window (all faders are in dB); AudioSource.volume is linear (0-1), not dB
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer.SetFloat(paramName, dBValue), AudioMixer.GetFloat(paramName, out dBValue). Range: -80 dB (silent) to +20 dB (boost).
unityAdapter.importTip: Expose AudioMixer parameters to script by right-clicking a mixer parameter and selecting 'Expose to script.' Then use SetFloat with dB values. For a player volume slider: float dB = Mathf.Log10(Mathf.Max(sliderValue, 0.0001f)) * 20f; mixer.SetFloat('Volume', dB);
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.volume is linear (0.0 to 1.0), but AudioMixer faders are in dB. Don't mix the two mental models. If you're using an AudioMixer, control volume through the mixer's exposed parameters in dB, not through AudioSource.volume.

misconceptions: Decibels are not an absolute unit like meters or kilograms. They express a ratio between two values. dBFS, dB SPL, and dBu all use different reference points. Always specify which type you mean.
0 dB does not mean 'no sound.' In dBFS, 0 means maximum digital level. In dB SPL, 0 means the threshold of human hearing. In mixing, 0 dB on a fader means 'no change' (unity gain).

A common game dev mistake: using AudioSource.volume linearly for player volume settings. Setting volume to 0.1 is -20 dB, which sounds barely reduced. Use a logarithmic conversion for a natural-feeling volume control.

name: Decibel Scale

beginner.oneLiner: The decibel scale is a way of measuring audio loudness using numbers where small changes represent big differences in actual volume.
beginner.analogy: Think of the Richter scale for earthquakes: a magnitude 6 quake is not twice as strong as a magnitude 3 — it is 1,000 times stronger. The decibel scale works similarly. An increase of 10 dB means the sound is 10 times more powerful, even though the number only went up by 10. This is why a small dB change can sound like a big deal.
beginner.whatToDo: Learn a few key reference points: 0 dBFS is the maximum digital level (clipping happens above this). -6 dB is roughly half the perceived volume. -20 dB is noticeably quiet. -60 dB is near silence. Use these as landmarks when mixing.

intermediate.oneLiner: The decibel scale is a logarithmic ratio measurement where each 10 dB increase represents a tenfold increase in signal power, compressing the enormous range of human hearing into a practical numeric scale.
intermediate.details: Decibels express a ratio between two levels: dB = 20 * log10(amplitude / reference). In digital audio, the reference is full scale (dBFS), where 0 dBFS is the maximum representable sample value. The scale is logarithmic because human hearing is logarithmic — we perceive loudness differences in ratios, not linear amounts. Key relationships: +6 dB doubles the amplitude, +10 dB roughly doubles perceived loudness, +20 dB increases amplitude by 10x. Negative dB means quieter than the reference.
intermediate.whenToUse: Always think in dB when mixing, not in linear percentages. A volume slider from 0 to 100% is linear and gives poor control — most of the perceptual change happens in the top 20%. Use dB-scaled faders (which most mixer UIs provide) for intuitive, perceptually even control. When documenting level budgets, specify values in dBFS.

advanced.oneLiner: The decibel is a dimensionless logarithmic unit expressing the ratio of a quantity to a reference, defined as 20*log10(V/Vref) for amplitude or 10*log10(P/Pref) for power, compressing the ~120 dB dynamic range of human hearing into a workable numeric space.
advanced.technicalNotes: In digital audio, 0 dBFS corresponds to the maximum sample value (e.g., 32767 for 16-bit signed integer). Each bit of depth adds approximately 6.02 dB of dynamic range (16-bit = ~96 dB, 24-bit = ~144 dB). When summing signals, dB values cannot be simply added — you must convert to linear, sum, then convert back. For correlated signals at equal amplitude: sum = original + 6 dB (amplitude doubling). For uncorrelated signals at equal power: sum = original + 3 dB (power doubling). Understanding this distinction is critical for accurate gain staging calculations. Note that dBFS, dBu, dBV, and dB SPL all use different reference points and should not be compared directly.

vs.amplitude: Amplitude is the linear measurement of signal level (e.g., sample values from -1.0 to 1.0). The decibel scale is the logarithmic representation of that same information, better matching human loudness perception.
vs.loudness-metering: The dB scale measures instantaneous signal level. Loudness metering (LUFS) applies frequency weighting and time integration to approximate human loudness perception more accurately than raw dB levels alone.

unityAdapter.location: Unity's Audio Mixer uses dB for all fader values. AudioSource.volume uses a linear 0-1 scale, which you must convert to/from dB in code.
unityAdapter.importTip: Convert between linear and dB: dB = 20 * Mathf.Log10(linear), linear = Mathf.Pow(10, dB / 20). The Mixer uses dB; AudioSource uses linear. Do not mix them up.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.volume = 0.5 is NOT -6 dB — it is approximately -6 dB only by coincidence of log10(0.5) * 20 = -6.02. But AudioSource.volume = 0.1 is -20 dB, not -10 dB. Always use the conversion formula rather than guessing linear-to-dB relationships.

misconceptions: Doubling the dB value doubles the loudness — the dB scale is logarithmic. Doubling perceived loudness requires roughly a +10 dB increase, not 2x the dB number.
0 dB means silence — in digital audio (dBFS), 0 dB is the maximum level. Silence is negative infinity dB. In SPL terms, 0 dB SPL is the threshold of hearing, not silence.
A linear volume slider from 0-100% is the same as a dB fader — linear sliders give poor control because most of the perceptual loudness change is crammed into the top portion of the range.

name: Decompress On Load

beginner.oneLiner: Decompress On Load fully unpacks a sound into memory when it loads, making it play instantly but using more RAM.
beginner.analogy: It is like unzipping a file onto your desktop before opening it. The file takes up more space, but when you double-click it, it opens immediately with no delay.
beginner.whatToDo: In the AudioClip import settings, set Load Type to 'Decompress On Load'. Use this for short, frequently played sounds like footsteps, gunshots, and UI clicks.

intermediate.oneLiner: Decompress On Load decodes compressed audio into raw PCM samples at load time, trading higher memory usage for zero runtime decompression overhead.
intermediate.details: When a scene loads (or when AudioClip.LoadAudioData() is called), the compressed data is decoded into uncompressed PCM and stored in memory. A 1-second 44.1kHz stereo clip becomes ~176 KB of PCM regardless of the original compressed size. This eliminates per-frame CPU cost from decompression but can significantly increase memory usage for longer clips. The decompression happens on the loading thread and adds to load time.
intermediate.whenToUse: Best for short clips under 1-2 seconds that play frequently: footsteps, impacts, UI sounds. Avoid for music, ambient loops, or dialogue where the memory cost would be too high.

advanced.oneLiner: Decompress On Load allocates an uncompressed PCM buffer at load time, eliminating runtime codec overhead at the cost of memory equal to duration * sampleRate * channels * 2 bytes.
advanced.technicalNotes: Memory formula: duration * sampleRate * channels * sizeof(Int16). A 2-second 44.1kHz stereo clip uses ~353 KB. For Vorbis-compressed clips, the decompression during load is single-threaded and can cause frame hitches if triggered at runtime via LoadAudioData(). ADPCM clips decompress faster than Vorbis. On mobile, this is the most CPU-friendly option but the most memory-hungry. Profile with the Audio section of the Memory Profiler to track actual allocation sizes.

vs.compressed-in-memory: Compressed In Memory uses ~10x less RAM but requires real-time decompression each time the clip plays, adding CPU load.
vs.streaming: Streaming uses minimal RAM but adds disk I/O latency and is unsuitable for sounds that need instant playback.

unityAdapter.location: Select an audio file in the Project window > Inspector > Load Type dropdown > Decompress On Load.
unityAdapter.importTip: Pair with ADPCM compression for short SFX. ADPCM decodes faster than Vorbis and has a fixed 3.5:1 compression ratio, making load times more predictable.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If you use this on long clips (music, ambience), memory usage will spike. A 3-minute stereo music track at 44.1kHz uses ~31 MB of RAM when decompressed.

misconceptions: Decompress On Load does not mean the clip is stored uncompressed in the build. The build still contains the compressed version. Decompression happens at runtime during loading.
This setting does not affect build size -- only runtime memory usage and load time.

name: Delay

beginner.oneLiner: Delay repeats a sound after a short gap, creating distinct echoes like shouting across a canyon.
beginner.analogy: Imagine yelling into a canyon and hearing your voice come back a moment later, then again quieter, and again even quieter. That is delay — your sound is copied and played back after a time gap, fading with each repetition.
beginner.whatToDo: Use a delay effect on sounds where you want a clear echo, such as a gunshot in a canyon, a magical spell casting, or a UI confirmation sound that needs emphasis. Set the delay time, feedback (number of repeats), and wet/dry mix.

intermediate.oneLiner: Delay records the input signal into a buffer and plays it back after a configurable time interval, with feedback controlling how many times the echo repeats.
intermediate.details: Core parameters: delay time (milliseconds between repeats), feedback (how much of the output is fed back into the input, controlling repeat count), and wet/dry mix. Variants include ping-pong delay (alternates between left and right channels), tape delay (adds subtle pitch wobble and filtering to each repeat), and multi-tap delay (multiple delay lines at different times).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use delay for stylized echoes in large open environments, rhythmic effects on music, or emphasis on important game events. Sync delay time to your music BPM for rhythmic effects. Short delays (20-50ms) can thicken a sound without obvious echoes (slapback). Long delays (300ms+) create distinct rhythmic echoes.

advanced.oneLiner: Delay is implemented as a circular buffer with read/write heads separated by a configurable sample offset, with the output optionally fed back through filtering stages before re-entering the buffer.
advanced.technicalNotes: For tempo-synced delay, calculate delay time as 60000/BPM for quarter-note intervals. Fractional delays require interpolation (linear or allpass) for sub-sample accuracy. High feedback values (>0.9) approach infinite repeat and can cause runaway gain — apply a soft limiter or gentle low-pass filter in the feedback path to prevent buildup. Modulating delay time produces pitch-shifting artifacts (the basis for chorus and flanger effects). Memory cost is sample_rate * delay_time_seconds * channels * bit_depth/8 bytes per delay line.

vs.reverb: Reverb creates a dense, indistinguishable wash of reflections simulating a room. Delay creates discrete, individually audible repetitions of the original signal.
vs.chorus: Chorus uses very short, modulated delay times (typically 15-35ms) with no feedback to create a thickening/doubling effect. Delay uses longer times and feedback to create distinct echoes.

unityAdapter.location: Add an Audio Echo Filter component to an AudioSource or AudioListener via the Inspector or script.
unityAdapter.importTip: Attach the AudioEchoFilter component to your AudioSource. Key properties are 'delay' (in ms, 10-5000), 'decayRatio' (0-1, controls feedback), and 'wetMix' (0-1).
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in AudioEchoFilter is basic and does not support ping-pong or filtered feedback. For more advanced delay effects, use FMOD or Wwise middleware, or implement a custom delay via OnAudioFilterRead. Also note that decayRatio values close to 1.0 can cause the echoes to persist indefinitely and build up in volume.

misconceptions: Delay and reverb are interchangeable — they are not. Delay produces distinct, countable echoes while reverb produces a smooth diffuse wash of reflections.
Delay is only useful for music — delay is great for environmental storytelling in games, emphasizing large open spaces like canyons, tunnels, and stairwells.
Feedback at 100% is fine — feedback at or above 1.0 means each repeat is as loud or louder than the last, causing infinite buildup that can clip and distort.

name: Diegetic Sound

beginner.oneLiner: Diegetic sound is audio that exists within the game world and that characters can hear; non-diegetic sound exists only for the player.
beginner.analogy: In a movie, a radio playing music that the characters react to is diegetic. The dramatic orchestral score that only the audience hears is non-diegetic. In a game, footsteps and NPC dialog are diegetic; the health-low warning beep and the background music score are typically non-diegetic.
beginner.whatToDo: Decide for each sound in your game: Can the characters hear this? If yes, it is diegetic and should be spatialized in 3D. If no, it is non-diegetic and usually plays in 2D (no panning or distance falloff). Route them to separate audio buses for independent volume control.

intermediate.oneLiner: Diegetic audio originates from sources within the game world and is affected by spatial properties; non-diegetic audio exists outside the narrative space and is typically mixed in stereo.
intermediate.details: Diegetic sounds include footsteps, environmental ambience, in-world radios, NPC speech, and weapon impacts. They should use 3D spatialization with distance attenuation, occlusion, and reverb matching the environment. Non-diegetic sounds include score music, UI feedback, narrator voiceover, and HUD alerts. They bypass spatialization and play at consistent volume. Some games use semi-diegetic (or meta-diegetic) sound: audio that blurs the line, like music that intensifies based on gameplay but is not literally in the world.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use the diegetic/non-diegetic distinction to organize your audio bus structure. Create separate mixer groups for World SFX (diegetic), Music (non-diegetic), UI (non-diegetic), and Ambience (diegetic). This lets you apply different spatialization, effects, and snapshot behaviors to each category.

advanced.oneLiner: The diegetic/non-diegetic boundary is an audio design framework that determines spatialization pipeline, bus routing, snapshot behavior, and occlusion processing for each sound category.
advanced.technicalNotes: In implementation, diegetic sounds are typically AudioSources with spatialBlend = 1.0 (full 3D), using distance curves, doppler, and occlusion raycasts. Non-diegetic sounds use spatialBlend = 0.0 (full 2D). The boundary can be dynamic: a diegetic car radio transitions to non-diegetic score when entering a cutscene. Middleware like FMOD and Wwise handle this via bus routing and snapshot transitions. In Unity, use AudioMixer snapshots to crossfade between gameplay (all buses active) and cutscene (mute world SFX, boost dialog) states.

vs.spatial-audio: Spatial audio is the technical mechanism (HRTF, panning, attenuation); diegetic/non-diegetic is the design framework. Diegetic sounds use spatial audio; non-diegetic sounds typically do not.
vs.adaptive-audio: Adaptive audio changes based on gameplay state (combat intensity, health). It can be either diegetic (adaptive environmental sounds) or non-diegetic (adaptive score).

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > Spatial Blend slider (0 = 2D non-diegetic, 1 = 3D diegetic). Bus routing via Audio Mixer groups.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set spatialBlend to 1.0 for all in-world (diegetic) AudioSources and 0.0 for UI and music (non-diegetic). Route diegetic sources to a 'World' mixer group and non-diegetic to 'UI' and 'Music' groups for independent control.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If spatialBlend is left at the default 0.0, your 3D positioned sounds will play as flat 2D stereo with no distance attenuation, breaking immersion. Always verify spatialBlend is set to 1.0 for diegetic sounds.

misconceptions: All music in games is non-diegetic. In-world jukeboxes, radios, and bard performances are diegetic music and should be spatialized accordingly.
Diegetic vs non-diegetic is a fixed property. A sound can transition between the two, such as a diegetic car radio becoming the non-diegetic soundtrack during a transition.

name: Distortion

beginner.oneLiner: Distortion makes a sound harsher and grittier by pushing the audio signal beyond its clean limits, adding new overtones.
beginner.analogy: Imagine yelling into a cheap megaphone: your voice gets crunchy and aggressive because the speaker cannot handle the volume cleanly. Distortion does the same thing to any sound, intentionally adding that grit and edge.
beginner.whatToDo: Use distortion to make weapon impacts feel more powerful, create aggressive monster sounds, or add crunch to UI feedback. Start with a low drive amount and increase until you get the character you want without losing clarity.

intermediate.oneLiner: Distortion reshapes the audio waveform by applying a nonlinear transfer function, clipping peaks and generating harmonic and inharmonic overtones.
intermediate.details: Distortion encompasses several related effects: soft clipping (overdrive) gently rounds off signal peaks, adding warm even harmonics; hard clipping chops peaks abruptly, producing brighter odd harmonics; and waveshaping applies arbitrary curves for more complex tonal changes. The amount of 'drive' controls how much the signal is amplified before the clipping stage. A tone or filter control after the distortion stage helps tame harsh high frequencies. In game audio, distortion is commonly used as a design tool to add aggression, weight, or lo-fi character.
intermediate.whenToUse: Apply distortion to explosion layers, weapon sounds, creature growls, or any element that needs to feel raw and powerful. Use subtle overdrive/saturation on bus groups to add analog warmth and cohesion. Avoid distortion on delicate sounds like footsteps or ambient nature unless you want a stylized, lo-fi aesthetic.

advanced.oneLiner: Distortion applies a nonlinear waveshaping function to the signal, generating harmonic content based on the transfer curve shape, with oversampling typically needed to manage aliasing.
advanced.technicalNotes: The simplest hard clipper is y = clamp(x * gain, -1, 1). Soft clipping functions like tanh(x), atan(x), or polynomial curves produce smoother saturation with more even-harmonic content. Waveshaping introduces frequencies above Nyquist, so oversampling (2-4x) with a low-pass anti-aliasing filter is essential to avoid aliasing artifacts. In Unity, apply distortion in OnAudioFilterRead by processing each sample through the transfer function. For performance, use lookup tables for complex waveshaping curves. Consider applying a DC-blocking filter after distortion, as asymmetric clipping can introduce DC offset that wastes headroom.

vs.compressor: A compressor reduces dynamic range proportionally using an envelope follower, preserving waveform shape. Distortion fundamentally reshapes the waveform, adding harmonic content that was not in the original signal.
vs.clipping: Clipping is a specific type of distortion where the waveform peaks are abruptly cut off. Distortion is the broader category that includes clipping, saturation, overdrive, and arbitrary waveshaping.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Source > Add Component > Audio Distortion Filter
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's built-in distortion filter is basic hard clipping. For better-sounding saturation, implement a custom waveshaper in OnAudioFilterRead using a tanh or soft-clip function, or use a third-party plugin.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioDistortionFilter can produce very harsh, unpleasant results at high settings. Always follow it with a low-pass filter or EQ to tame the added high-frequency content.

misconceptions: Distortion is not always undesirable. In sound design, intentional distortion is a fundamental tool for adding weight, aggression, and character to sounds.
Overdrive and distortion are not the same thing. Overdrive is a milder form of soft clipping, while distortion typically refers to harder, more aggressive clipping.
Adding distortion does not just make things louder. It fundamentally changes the harmonic content and timbre of the sound.

name: Dithering

beginner.oneLiner: Dithering adds a tiny amount of random noise to an audio signal when reducing its bit depth, which paradoxically makes the audio sound smoother and more natural.
beginner.analogy: Imagine you are painting a sunset with only 8 crayons instead of hundreds. You would see harsh color bands where one shade jumps to the next. If you scattered tiny random dots of color along those boundaries, the transitions would look much smoother to your eyes. Dithering does the same thing for audio — the added noise blurs the harsh steps between volume levels.
beginner.whatToDo: Apply dithering when converting audio from a higher bit depth to a lower one (e.g., 24-bit to 16-bit for final game assets). Most DAWs and audio converters offer dithering as an option during export. Always dither as the very last step in the processing chain.

intermediate.oneLiner: Dithering decorrelates quantization error from the signal by adding low-level random noise before bit-depth reduction, replacing harmonic distortion artifacts with a benign, constant noise floor.
intermediate.details: When you reduce bit depth, each sample is rounded to the nearest available level. Without dithering, the rounding error correlates with the signal, producing audible harmonic distortion and modulation noise — especially on quiet passages and reverb tails. Dithering randomizes the rounding decisions, converting correlated distortion into uncorrelated white noise. Noise-shaped dithering goes further by pushing the dither noise energy into less audible frequency ranges (above 10 kHz) using the ear's frequency sensitivity.
intermediate.whenToUse: Dither whenever you reduce bit depth: 32-bit float internal processing to 24-bit masters, or 24-bit to 16-bit for distribution. In game audio, dither when bouncing final assets to 16-bit PCM. Do not dither when staying at the same or increasing bit depth, and only dither once at the final conversion — never in the middle of a processing chain.

advanced.oneLiner: Dithering adds pseudorandom noise with TPDF (triangular probability density function) characteristics prior to quantization, linearizing the quantization transfer function and replacing signal-dependent distortion with a constant, uncorrelated noise floor at the cost of approximately 3 dB of SNR.
advanced.technicalNotes: RPDF (rectangular) dither eliminates signal-correlated distortion but leaves modulation noise. TPDF (triangular) dither — generated by summing two independent RPDF sources — eliminates both distortion and modulation noise, which is why it is the standard for audio. The noise floor penalty is theoretically 4.77 dB (RPDF) or 6.02 dB (TPDF) above the undithered quantization noise floor. Noise shaping (e.g., POW-R, MBIT+) applies a feedback filter to the quantization error, pushing dither energy into less perceptually important frequency bands, effectively gaining 10-15 dB of perceived dynamic range in the midrange. For game audio at 16-bit, noise-shaped dithering is ideal for music and ambient beds; for short SFX, the difference from TPDF is negligible.

vs.quantization: Quantization is the rounding process that introduces distortion when reducing bit depth. Dithering is the technique of adding noise before quantization to replace that distortion with a benign noise floor.
vs.bit-depth: Bit depth determines how many levels are available for representing each sample. Dithering improves how gracefully the signal degrades when moving to fewer levels.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not provide built-in dithering. Apply dithering in your DAW or audio tool when exporting final assets before importing them into Unity.
unityAdapter.apiRef: N/A — dithering is applied during asset preparation, not at runtime
unityAdapter.importTip: When exporting assets from your DAW at 16-bit for Unity, enable TPDF or noise-shaped dithering on the final bounce. If your assets are 24-bit or compressed (Vorbis, AAC), dithering during export is unnecessary since Unity's internal processing is floating-point.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Do not dither assets that will be compressed to Ogg Vorbis or other lossy formats — the lossy encoder has its own noise characteristics and the dither noise just adds an unnecessary noise floor. Only dither for uncompressed PCM output at reduced bit depth.

misconceptions: Adding noise to audio makes it worse — dithering adds a controlled, inaudible noise floor that replaces much more objectionable distortion artifacts. The net result sounds better, not worse.
Dithering is only for mastering studios — any time you convert bit depth for game assets, dithering preserves quality on quiet sounds, reverb tails, and music fades.
