You should always dither — only dither when reducing bit depth. Dithering 32-bit float to 32-bit float or applying dithering to lossy-encoded files is pointless.

name: Doppler Effect

beginner.oneLiner: The Doppler effect is the pitch change you hear when a sound source moves toward or away from you, like a car horn that sounds higher as it approaches and lower as it passes.
beginner.analogy: Think of an ambulance driving past you. As it comes toward you the siren sounds higher-pitched, and the moment it passes and moves away the pitch drops noticeably. This happens because the sound waves get compressed as the source approaches and stretched as it recedes. Games can simulate this for fast-moving objects.
beginner.whatToDo: Enable Doppler on AudioSources for fast-moving objects like vehicles, projectiles, and flyby objects. Keep the Doppler level low (0.1-0.5) for most game objects — realistic Doppler (1.0) can sound exaggerated at game-speed velocities.

intermediate.oneLiner: The Doppler effect pitch-shifts audio based on the relative velocity between the sound source and listener, compressing frequency on approach and stretching it on recession.
intermediate.details: The perceived frequency shift is proportional to the relative velocity between source and listener divided by the speed of sound. In games, the Doppler level parameter scales this effect. Key considerations: only apply Doppler to sources with significant velocity relative to the listener, set the speed of sound appropriately for your game scale, and be conservative with the Doppler factor — real-world Doppler at game velocities often sounds exaggerated.
intermediate.whenToUse: Apply Doppler to racing game vehicles, projectiles and arrows flying past the camera, aircraft and flyby sounds, thrown objects, and any fast-moving source that passes near the listener. Avoid Doppler on ambient sounds, music, UI sounds, and slow-moving sources where the pitch shift would be negligible or distracting.

advanced.oneLiner: Doppler simulation pitch-shifts the audio playback rate by the factor (c + v_listener) / (c + v_source), where c is the speed of sound and velocities are along the source-to-listener axis.
advanced.technicalNotes: Unity calculates Doppler from the velocity vectors of the AudioSource and AudioListener transforms between frames. Teleporting objects (discontinuous position changes) cause extreme velocity spikes and audible pitch glitches — set Doppler to 0 before teleporting and restore it afterward. The speed of sound in Unity defaults to 343.3 m/s (AudioSettings.GetConfiguration().speakerMode). If your game world uses a different scale (e.g., 1 unit = 1 km), you need to adjust the project-wide Doppler factor or source velocities accordingly. For accurate Doppler on the audio thread, some engines interpolate the pitch shift per sample rather than per frame to avoid stepping artifacts.

vs.attenuation: Attenuation reduces volume with distance. Doppler shifts pitch with relative velocity. They are independent: a stationary distant source attenuates but has no Doppler. A fast-moving nearby source has strong Doppler but minimal attenuation.
vs.spatialization: Spatialization positions sound in 3D space (panning, distance). Doppler adds pitch variation based on motion. Spatialization is positional; Doppler is velocity-based. Both contribute to realistic 3D audio.

unityAdapter.location: Set the Doppler Level on each AudioSource component in the Inspector (3D Sound Settings section), and adjust the global Doppler Factor in Edit > Project Settings > Audio.
unityAdapter.importTip: AudioSource.dopplerLevel (0-5) controls per-source Doppler intensity. The global Audio project settings have a Doppler Factor that scales all sources. Start with the global factor at 1 and per-source levels at 0.1-0.3, increasing only for sources that need obvious Doppler like vehicles.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Doppler is calculated from the transform position delta between frames. If you teleport an AudioSource (set position directly without interpolation), the engine computes an enormous velocity spike, causing an extreme pitch glitch. Disable Doppler before teleporting and re-enable it after. Also, Doppler does not apply to 2D (spatialBlend = 0) sounds.

misconceptions: Doppler only changes volume — Doppler changes pitch, not volume. Volume changes with distance are handled by attenuation curves.

Every moving sound needs Doppler — only fast-moving sources that pass near the listener benefit from Doppler. Slow-moving or distant sources produce imperceptible Doppler shifts that waste processing.

Doppler level of 1.0 sounds realistic in games — game objects often move much faster than real-world equivalents relative to the world scale, so a Doppler level of 1.0 can sound exaggerated. Use lower values (0.1-0.5) for most cases.

name: Double Buffering

beginner.oneLiner: Double buffering uses two audio buffers that take turns — one is being played while the other is being filled with new data, so playback never has to wait.
beginner.analogy: Imagine two buckets at a well. While you carry one full bucket to the house, someone else fills the second bucket. When you come back, you grab the full bucket and leave the empty one to be refilled. There is never a pause waiting for water. Double buffering does the same with audio data — one buffer plays, the other fills, then they swap.
beginner.whatToDo: You usually do not need to set up double buffering yourself — audio drivers and engines handle it. But knowing it exists helps you understand why your audio settings include a buffer size. If you hear crackles or pops, your buffers may be too small for your system to fill in time.

intermediate.oneLiner: Double buffering (ping-pong buffering) allocates two equally sized audio buffers: while the audio hardware reads from one, the application fills the other, then they swap, ensuring continuous uninterrupted playback.
intermediate.details: The process: Buffer A is submitted to the audio output device. While the device plays through Buffer A, the CPU decodes or generates audio into Buffer B. When the device finishes Buffer A, the buffers swap roles — Buffer B is now playing and Buffer A is available for writing. This pattern is the simplest way to decouple audio production from consumption. The total output latency is approximately 2x the buffer size (one buffer of latency while the other plays). Most audio APIs (WASAPI, CoreAudio, ALSA) use a variant of this or extend it to N-buffering (triple buffering for extra safety).
intermediate.whenToUse: Double buffering is the default playback model in most audio systems. You interact with it indirectly by choosing your buffer size. Smaller buffers mean lower latency but less time to fill them. If your game has CPU spikes that cause dropouts, consider triple buffering (three buffers in rotation) or slightly increasing the buffer size.

advanced.oneLiner: Double buffering is a ping-pong scheme where two contiguous memory regions alternate between DMA-read by the audio hardware and CPU-write by the mixer, providing exactly one buffer period of scheduling headroom at the cost of 2*N samples of output latency.
advanced.technicalNotes: At the hardware level, the audio DMA controller reads from one buffer while the CPU writes to the other. An interrupt fires at each buffer boundary, signaling the CPU to fill the next buffer. Total round-trip latency is input buffer + output buffer + processing time. For a 256-sample double-buffered setup at 48 kHz, output latency is approximately 256*2/48000 = 10.67 ms. Triple buffering adds another buffer period of headroom (reducing dropout risk under load) at the cost of additional latency. WASAPI exclusive mode and ASIO minimize latency by reducing the buffer chain. In shared mode, the OS audio mixer adds its own buffering layer. The choice between double and ring buffering depends on whether the producer and consumer operate in fixed equal-sized blocks (double buffer) or variable-sized chunks (ring buffer).

vs.ring-buffer: Double buffering swaps two fixed blocks; a ring buffer has fine-grained read/write cursors over a circular array. Ring buffers handle variable-rate producers better; double buffering is simpler when both sides process fixed-size blocks.
vs.buffer-size: Buffer size determines how large each of the two buffers is. Double buffering is the pattern of using two of them. Doubling the buffer size is not the same as double buffering — it increases latency without adding the swap mechanism.
vs.streaming: Streaming audio from disk typically uses ring buffers, not simple double buffering, because disk read times are variable and the circular buffer absorbs that variance.

unityAdapter.location: Project Settings > Audio > DSP Buffer Size. Unity's internal audio pipeline uses double (or multi) buffering managed by FMOD. You configure it indirectly through the buffer size setting (Default, Best Latency, Good Latency, Best Performance).
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioSettings.GetDSPBufferSize(out int bufferLength, out int numBuffers) — returns the buffer length and the number of buffers in the chain
unityAdapter.importTip: Use AudioSettings.GetDSPBufferSize to check how many buffers the system is using. 'Best Latency' uses smaller buffers (higher dropout risk), 'Best Performance' uses larger buffers (more latency, fewer dropouts). Test on your target hardware to find the right tradeoff.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Changing DSP Buffer Size at runtime via AudioSettings.SetDSPBufferSize only takes effect before AudioSettings.Reset() is called. In practice, set this once at startup. Mid-game changes require reinitializing the audio system, which causes a brief silence.

misconceptions: Double buffering doubles your latency compared to no buffering — without buffering, real-time audio is impossible. Double buffering is the minimum viable configuration. The real comparison is against triple or N-buffering.
Double buffering eliminates all audio glitches — it only provides one buffer period of headroom. If your audio processing takes longer than one buffer period, you will still get dropouts.

Double buffering and double the buffer size are the same thing — they are not. Double buffering is a two-buffer swap pattern. Doubling buffer size means each buffer is twice as large, increasing latency.

name: DSP (Digital Signal Processing)

beginner.oneLiner: DSP is the math that transforms audio in real time — it is how your effects like reverb, filters, and pitch shifting actually work under the hood.
beginner.analogy: Think of DSP like a photo filter on your phone, but for sound. Just like a photo filter changes every pixel in an image (brighten, blur, add contrast), DSP changes every audio sample that passes through it. A low-pass filter removes high-pitched sounds. Reverb adds echoes. Distortion clips the peaks. All of these are DSP — math applied to numbers that represent sound.
beginner.whatToDo: You do not need to write DSP code to make a great-sounding game — engines and plugins provide ready-made effects. But understanding that every audio effect is DSP helps you make better decisions: more effects means more CPU cost, and the order you chain effects matters (filter before reverb sounds different than reverb before filter).

intermediate.oneLiner: DSP (Digital Signal Processing) is the application of mathematical operations to discrete audio samples in real time, encompassing filtering, spectral analysis, dynamics processing, and spatial effects.
intermediate.details: All audio effects are DSP algorithms operating on sample buffers. Common categories: Time-domain DSP operates directly on samples (gain, delay lines, waveshaping). Frequency-domain DSP transforms samples via FFT, processes frequency bins, then converts back (spectral effects, convolution reverb, pitch detection). Filter DSP uses difference equations to shape frequency content (IIR biquads for EQ, FIR filters for linear-phase response). DSP code runs in the audio callback on the audio thread, so it must meet real-time constraints — no allocation, no blocking. CPU cost scales with sample rate, channel count, and algorithm complexity. A biquad filter costs a few operations per sample; convolution reverb can cost millions.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use engine-provided DSP for standard effects (reverb, EQ, compression, delay). Write custom DSP when you need unique audio behaviors: procedural wind synthesis, real-time voice effects, gameplay-reactive audio processing. Profile your DSP chain — on mobile, you may only have a few milliseconds of budget per audio callback.

advanced.oneLiner: DSP encompasses the real-time computation of discrete-time signal transforms — IIR/FIR filtering, spectral processing via FFT, sample-rate conversion, dynamics processing, and spatial encoding — executed within the audio callback's hard-real-time deadline.
advanced.technicalNotes: Core DSP building blocks: Biquad filters (2nd-order IIR) are the workhorse — 5 multiply-adds per sample for lowpass, highpass, bandpass, notch, peaking, shelving. Cascading biquads builds higher-order filters. Delay lines (circular buffers) underpin chorus, flanger, phaser, comb filters, and delay effects. FIR convolution (via overlap-add FFT) enables convolution reverb with measured impulse responses. Non-linear waveshaping creates distortion and saturation. Per-sample cost matters: at 48 kHz stereo, you process 96,000 samples/second. A 1024-tap FIR filter costs 96 million multiply-adds per second. SIMD (SSE/NEON) and block processing are essential for heavy DSP. Unity's mixer effects run as native AudioPluginInterface implementations. For custom Unity DSP, OnAudioFilterRead provides per-source processing, while native audio plugins offer full mixer integration with parameters exposed in the Inspector.

vs.fft: FFT is a specific DSP algorithm for converting between time and frequency domains. DSP is the broader field that includes FFT along with filtering, dynamics, synthesis, and more.
vs.audio-callback: The audio callback is the execution context where DSP runs. DSP is the processing logic; the callback is the scheduling mechanism.
vs.reverb: Reverb is a specific DSP effect. It can be implemented via delay networks (algorithmic reverb) or FFT-based convolution (convolution reverb). Both are DSP algorithms.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer > Add Effect (for built-in DSP like reverb, EQ, compression). For custom DSP, use OnAudioFilterRead on a MonoBehaviour or write a native audio plugin implementing the AudioPluginInterface.
unityAdapter.apiRef: MonoBehaviour.OnAudioFilterRead(float[] data, int channels), AudioMixer, AudioMixerGroup, Native Audio Plugin SDK (AudioPluginInterface.h)
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's built-in mixer effects are adequate for most games. If you need custom DSP, start with OnAudioFilterRead for prototyping (C#, easier to iterate) then move to a native plugin (C/C++) for production performance. Native plugins can use SIMD and run 10-50x faster than equivalent C# code.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Each AudioSource with OnAudioFilterRead adds a DSP callback to the audio thread. Having hundreds of active sources with custom DSP will overwhelm the audio thread. Use the Audio Mixer's built-in effects on groups/buses when possible — they process many sources in a single mixed stream.

misconceptions: DSP is only for advanced programmers — you use DSP every time you add reverb, EQ, or any effect to an Audio Mixer group. The engine handles the implementation; you just configure parameters.
More DSP effects always sound better — every effect adds CPU cost, latency, and potential for unintended artifacts. Use only what the mix needs. Subtlety often beats complexity.

DSP quality is the same everywhere — the same algorithm can sound very different depending on sample rate, coefficient precision, and implementation. Cheap mobile reverbs and studio-quality reverbs are both DSP, with vastly different results.

name: Dynamic Range

beginner.oneLiner: Dynamic range is the difference between the quietest and loudest parts of an audio signal, measured in decibels.
beginner.analogy: Think about watching a movie at home. The dialogue is so quiet you turn up the volume, then an explosion blasts your ears off. That huge gap between the whispering and the boom is the dynamic range. A compressor shrinks that gap so you can hear the quiet parts without the loud parts being painful.
beginner.whatToDo: In your game mix, control dynamic range so that important sounds are always audible without the loudest sounds causing clipping. Use compressors on buses to tame peaks, and set appropriate volume ranges for different sound categories.

intermediate.oneLiner: Dynamic range is the ratio between the maximum undistorted signal level and the noise floor of a system, or the peak-to-quiet variation within a specific audio signal, expressed in dB.
intermediate.details: System dynamic range is determined by bit depth: 16-bit PCM offers about 96 dB of dynamic range (6 dB per bit), 24-bit about 144 dB, and 32-bit float offers roughly 1528 dB (though human hearing spans only about 120 dB). Signal dynamic range is the variation within a specific piece of audio. In game mixing, you usually want to reduce the dynamic range of the raw assets so they work in the noisy, variable listening environments players use — phone speakers, TV, headphones in public.
intermediate.whenToUse: Monitor dynamic range on your master bus and on individual category buses. Music and ambient beds often benefit from moderate compression (8-12 dB of range reduction). Dialogue needs tighter control (3-6 dB range). SFX can retain more dynamics for impact, but still need peak control via limiters to prevent clipping.

advanced.oneLiner: Dynamic range encompasses both the system's noise-floor-to-clipping-ceiling ratio and the crest factor of the program material, requiring strategic dynamics processing to optimize loudness, clarity, and transient preservation within the constraints of playback environments.
advanced.technicalNotes: For game audio, the playback environment dynamic range is often the limiting factor — mobile devices may have only 40-50 dB of usable range, compared to a quiet living room's 60-70 dB. Design your mix for the worst-case target. Use multi-band compression to control dynamics independently across frequency ranges: tighten the low end (reduces muddiness), preserve midrange transients (maintains clarity), and gently limit highs (prevents harshness). Implement runtime dynamic range control via a compressor/limiter on the master bus whose parameters adapt to detected output device, or expose a 'dynamic range' setting (night mode / cinema mode) that adjusts bus-level compression ratios.

vs.headroom: Headroom is the space between the signal peak and the maximum level (0 dBFS). Dynamic range is the span from the noise floor to the peak. Headroom is about preventing clipping; dynamic range is about the overall loudness variation.
vs.loudness-unit: Loudness units (LUFS) measure the perceived loudness of a signal over time. Dynamic range measures the variation between loud and quiet. A signal can be loud (high LUFS) with little dynamic range (compressed) or quiet with lots of range.

unityAdapter.location: Control dynamic range by adding compressor and limiter effects to AudioMixer groups. Monitor levels in the Audio Mixer window.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer effects: Duck Volume, Compressor, Limiter
unityAdapter.importTip: Add a compressor effect to your master bus as a safety net. Set the threshold to -6 dB, ratio to 4:1, and adjust attack/release to taste. This prevents unexpected volume spikes during gameplay from causing clipping or startling players.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in mixer compressor is basic. For more control over dynamic range, consider middleware solutions like FMOD or Wwise which offer multi-band compression, sidechaining, and LUFS metering out of the box.

misconceptions: More dynamic range is always better — in game audio, too much dynamic range means quiet sounds get lost in environmental noise and loud sounds cause clipping or startle players. Controlled range is usually preferable.
Compression destroys audio quality — well-applied compression controls dynamics without audible artifacts. Over-compression is the problem, not compression itself.
Dynamic range only matters for music — game SFX, dialogue, and ambient sounds all benefit from appropriate dynamic range management to ensure clarity across varying playback conditions.

name: Early Reflections

beginner.oneLiner: Early reflections are the first echoes that bounce off nearby walls and surfaces before the full reverb tail builds up, helping your brain determine the size and shape of a room.
beginner.analogy: Imagine clapping in a hallway. The very first echoes you hear bouncing off the nearby walls are early reflections. They arrive before the sound has had time to bounce around many times and blur into the smooth wash of reverb that follows.
beginner.whatToDo: Pay attention to early reflections when setting up reverb in your game. They are what make a space feel real and help the player sense room size. A small room has quick, dense early reflections; a large room has slower, more spaced-out ones.

intermediate.oneLiner: Early reflections are the discrete echoes arriving within the first 50-100 ms after the direct sound, caused by first-order and low-order reflections off nearby surfaces, and are critical for spatial perception.
intermediate.details: Early reflections provide the brain with cues about room size, shape, and surface materials. They arrive as distinct, identifiable echoes before blurring into the diffuse reverb tail. The timing between the direct sound and the first reflection (pre-delay) indicates distance from the nearest surface. The spacing between early reflections indicates room dimensions. Their frequency content reveals surface materials (hard surfaces reflect high frequencies; soft surfaces absorb them). In game audio, accurate early reflections are more important for spatial believability than the reverb tail.
intermediate.whenToUse: Configure early reflections carefully when designing reverb for different game environments. Use shorter, denser patterns for small rooms and longer, sparser patterns for large spaces. In middleware like FMOD or Wwise, early reflections can often be controlled independently from the late reverb, allowing you to match the spatial cues to the visual environment.

advanced.oneLiner: Early reflections are the specular sound arrivals within the first 50-100 ms, computed via image-source method or ray tracing, providing the primary perceptual cues for room geometry and listener envelopment.
advanced.technicalNotes: The image-source method models early reflections by mirroring the sound source across each room surface and calculating the resulting arrival times and attenuation. This is accurate for low-order reflections in simple geometries. Ray tracing extends this to complex scenes by casting rays and tracking their paths. Each reflection is characterized by: arrival time (distance/speed_of_sound), attenuation (inverse square law plus absorption), and direction (for spatialized rendering). In Unity, Steam Audio and Resonance Audio can compute geometry-aware early reflections using simplified scene meshes. The computational cost scales with reflection order and geometric complexity. For real-time game audio, limit to 1st or 2nd order reflections and use pre-computed results where possible.

vs.reverb: Reverb (late reverberation) is the dense, diffuse sound field that builds after early reflections. Early reflections are sparse, discrete echoes that convey spatial information, while the late tail conveys the overall ambience and decay character.
vs.pre-delay: Pre-delay is the gap between the direct sound and the first early reflection. It is a single timing parameter, while early reflections are the full pattern of discrete echoes that follow.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Reverb Zone or Audio Reverb Filter > Reflections Level and Reflections Delay parameters
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioReverbFilter.reflectionsLevel, AudioReverbFilter.reflectionsDelay; or Steam Audio for geometry-based reflections
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's AudioReverbFilter exposes Reflections Level (volume of early reflections) and Reflections Delay (time before they arrive). Adjust these per environment. For geometry-aware reflections, integrate Steam Audio which ray-traces your scene geometry.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in reverb uses fixed early reflection patterns that do not adapt to room geometry. For convincing spatial audio, you need a plugin like Steam Audio that calculates reflections based on actual scene surfaces.

misconceptions: Early reflections are not the same as reverb. They are the initial discrete echoes, while reverb is the smooth, dense sound field that follows.
You cannot just increase reverb to simulate a bigger room. The early reflection pattern is what primarily conveys room size to the listener, not the reverb tail length alone.
Early reflections are not optional in realistic audio. Without them, even a long reverb tail will sound disconnected from the space, as the brain relies on early reflections for spatial perception.

name: Envelope Follower

beginner.oneLiner: An envelope follower tracks the volume shape of a sound over time and outputs a control signal that mirrors those volume changes, which can be used to drive other effects.
beginner.analogy: Imagine tracing the outline of a mountain range on a piece of paper. The mountain range is the loudness of a sound going up and down over time, and your traced line is the envelope. An envelope follower creates that traced line automatically, and you can use it to control other things — like making a light pulse brighter when the sound gets louder.
beginner.whatToDo: Use an envelope follower to create audio-reactive effects in your game. For example, track the amplitude of a music track and use the output to drive visual effects like screen shake intensity, particle emission rate, or light brightness.

intermediate.oneLiner: An envelope follower extracts the amplitude envelope of an audio signal using peak detection or RMS smoothing with configurable attack and release times, producing a control signal that mirrors the source's dynamic contour.
intermediate.details: The envelope follower rectifies the audio signal (takes absolute values), then smooths it with attack and release time constants. Fast attack tracks transients closely; slow attack creates a more averaged response. The output control signal ranges from 0 to 1 (or in dB) and can modulate any parameter: filter cutoff (auto-wah), gain (ducking/sidechaining), effects wet/dry, or visual properties. Compressors and gates internally use envelope followers to detect signal level.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use envelope followers for: auto-wah effects (envelope drives filter cutoff), beat-reactive visuals (music amplitude drives game parameters), dynamic ducking (one sound's envelope ducks another), and adaptive mixing (ambient level follows gameplay intensity). In synthesis, use them to transfer the amplitude contour of one sound onto another (vocoder-style effects).

advanced.oneLiner: An envelope follower implements a peak or RMS detector with asymmetric attack/release ballistics, converting an audio signal into a slowly varying control signal suitable for parameter modulation, dynamics processing, or audio-visual synchronization.
advanced.technicalNotes: A basic peak envelope follower: rectify the signal, then apply y[n] = max(|x[n]|, y[n-1]·release_coeff) for detection, with attack_coeff applied when the input exceeds the current envelope. Coefficients relate to time constants: coeff = exp(-1/(time_seconds * sample_rate)). For RMS detection, square the signal, apply the smoothing filter, then take the square root. RMS gives a better measure of perceived loudness; peak responds faster to transients. In game audio middleware (FMOD, Wwise), envelope followers are available as modulators that can drive any exposed parameter. For music-reactive gameplay, run the envelope follower on a dedicated analysis bus to avoid processing artifacts.

vs.adsr: An ADSR envelope is a manually defined shape applied to a synthesizer. An envelope follower extracts the natural amplitude shape from an existing audio signal. ADSR creates envelopes; envelope followers detect them.
vs.compressor: A compressor uses an internal envelope follower to detect signal level, then applies gain reduction. An envelope follower by itself just outputs the detected level as a control signal without modifying the audio.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a built-in envelope follower. Implement one by reading AudioSource.GetOutputData() or GetSpectrumData() and computing a smoothed amplitude value each frame.
unityAdapter.importTip: For a simple envelope follower: call GetOutputData() to retrieve samples, compute the RMS (square root of the mean of squares), and smooth the result over frames using Mathf.Lerp with different rates for attack and release. Use the output to drive visual parameters via script.
unityAdapter.gotcha: GetOutputData() returns the signal after the AudioSource's volume is applied, so a muted source returns silence. If you want to analyze audio without hearing it, route it through a separate mixer group with the volume at the output set to -80 dB, and analyze before that attenuation point.

misconceptions: Envelope followers are only for music synthesis — in game audio, they are powerful tools for audio-reactive visuals, adaptive mixing, and dynamic parameter control.
Envelope followers add latency — a well-tuned envelope follower adds no buffering latency. The attack time parameter controls how quickly it responds, not an inherent processing delay.
You need special plugins for envelope following — a basic implementation is just a few lines of code: rectify, smooth, output. The concept is simple even if the applications are powerful.

name: Equalizer (EQ)

beginner.oneLiner: An equalizer (EQ) lets you boost or cut specific frequency ranges in a sound, like turning up the bass or reducing the treble.
beginner.analogy: Think of an EQ like the bass and treble knobs on a car stereo, but with more control. Instead of just two knobs, you get multiple sliders that each control a different slice of the frequency range, letting you sculpt exactly how the sound feels.
beginner.whatToDo: Use EQ to fix sounds that are too boomy (cut the low frequencies) or too harsh (cut the high frequencies). In your game mix, use EQ on different sound buses to make sure music, dialogue, and effects each have their own space and do not clash.

intermediate.oneLiner: An equalizer is a set of filters that selectively boost or attenuate frequency bands, used to shape the tonal balance and spectral content of audio signals.
intermediate.details: EQs come in several types: Graphic EQ has fixed frequency bands with individual gain sliders, typically in 1/3-octave or octave spacing. Shelving EQ boosts or cuts everything above (high shelf) or below (low shelf) a frequency. Parametric EQ offers adjustable frequency, gain, and bandwidth (Q) per band. In game audio, EQ is essential for separating elements in the mix, simulating environmental filtering (e.g., muffled sound through walls), and ensuring dialogue clarity above background sounds.
intermediate.whenToUse: Apply EQ to audio buses rather than individual sources for efficiency. Use a high-pass filter on non-bass elements to remove rumble, cut competing frequencies between music and dialogue, and simulate occlusion by rolling off high frequencies. Automate EQ parameters to create dynamic filtering effects during gameplay transitions.

advanced.oneLiner: An equalizer implements cascaded biquad IIR filters (peaking, shelving, and pass filters) to apply frequency-dependent gain modification across the audio spectrum.
advanced.technicalNotes: Most digital EQs use second-order IIR (biquad) filter sections. Each band is a biquad with coefficients calculated from the desired center frequency, gain, and Q. Multiple bands are cascaded in series. Phase shift is inherent in IIR EQs; linear-phase EQ avoids this but introduces latency and pre-ringing. For game audio, minimum-phase (standard IIR) EQ is preferred due to zero added latency. In Unity, the Audio Mixer provides a built-in 3-band EQ per group. For more control, use a custom parametric EQ in OnAudioFilterRead with biquad filter implementations. Be cautious of filter instability at extreme Q values or when modulating coefficients rapidly.

vs.parametric-eq: A generic EQ can refer to graphic, shelving, or parametric types. A parametric EQ specifically provides per-band control over frequency, gain, and bandwidth (Q), offering the most surgical precision.
vs.low-pass-filter: A low-pass filter is a single EQ operation that removes frequencies above a cutoff. An EQ encompasses multiple filter types and bands for comprehensive tonal shaping.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer > Select a group > Add Effect > Lowpass, Highpass, or ParamEQ
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer exposed parameters for EQ bands; AudioLowPassFilter and AudioHighPassFilter components
unityAdapter.importTip: Use Unity's Audio Mixer ParamEQ effect for per-bus tone shaping. Expose the center frequency and gain parameters so you can automate them via snapshots for gameplay transitions like going underwater or entering a cave.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's standalone AudioLowPassFilter and AudioHighPassFilter components are per-AudioSource and can be CPU-expensive if applied to many sources. Use mixer group effects instead for bus-level processing.

misconceptions: EQ does not add new frequencies to a sound. It can only boost or cut frequencies that are already present in the signal.
Boosting frequencies is not always better than cutting. Subtractive EQ (cutting problem frequencies) is often more transparent and natural-sounding than additive EQ (boosting desired frequencies).
More EQ bands does not mean better sound. Excessive EQ adjustments can introduce phase issues and make audio sound unnatural.

name: Event-Driven Audio

beginner.oneLiner: Event-driven audio means your game triggers sounds by firing events (like 'player-jumped' or 'enemy-died') instead of directly playing specific audio files, making your audio system flexible and organized.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a stage play where the director shouts cues like 'Thunder!' or 'Doorbell!' and a sound technician in the booth decides exactly which recording to play, how loud, and with what effects. The director does not care about the technical details — they just call the cue. Event-driven audio works the same way: your gameplay code fires events, and the audio system decides what actually happens.
beginner.whatToDo: Instead of writing PlaySound('gunshot_01.wav') in your code, create an event system where gameplay fires events like 'weapon-fire' and a separate audio manager decides which sound to play, with what variation, at what volume. This separates game logic from audio logic and lets your sound designer make changes without touching code.

intermediate.oneLiner: Event-driven audio decouples gameplay logic from audio playback by using named events as an abstraction layer — gameplay fires events with parameters, and the audio system resolves those events to specific sounds, variations, and behaviors.
intermediate.details: In an event-driven system, gameplay code never references audio files directly. Instead, it posts events (e.g., 'footstep' with parameters: surface=grass, speed=running) to the audio system. The audio system maps events to sound definitions that can include: random selection from multiple variations, parameter-driven behavior (louder footsteps when running), cooldown rules (minimum time between triggers), voice management (limit simultaneous instances). This architecture provides several benefits: sound designers can remap, add, or change sounds without code changes; the same gameplay event can trigger different sounds based on context; audio budgets and priorities are managed centrally. FMOD and Wwise are built entirely around this model.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use event-driven audio for any project beyond a simple prototype. Even a lightweight event system (a dictionary mapping event names to AudioClip arrays with random selection) is vastly better than hard-coded PlaySound calls. For larger projects, use FMOD or Wwise, which provide sophisticated event systems with visual authoring tools.

advanced.oneLiner: Event-driven audio implements a publish-subscribe pattern where gameplay producers post typed events with metadata to an audio bus, and the audio system's event resolver performs parameter-driven sound selection, instance management, voice limiting, and real-time parameter binding.
advanced.technicalNotes: Architecture: Event posting should be O(1) — enqueue an event struct (event ID, position, parameters) into a lock-free queue consumed by the audio system. Event resolution maps the event ID to a sound definition containing: clip pool (with randomization weights, no-repeat logic), parameter mappings (RTPC curves), instance limits (max voices, steal behavior), cooldown timers, and spatial settings. For high-frequency events (e.g., particle collision sounds), implement event throttling — merge or drop events that exceed a configurable rate per event type. FMOD Studio events encapsulate this entire pipeline with a visual timeline and parameter sheets. Wwise events similarly bundle all playback logic. For custom engines, implement at minimum: event registry, parameter binding, instance limiting, and random no-repeat selection. The event system should be deterministic for replay/networking — given the same event sequence and random seed, audio playback should be reproducible.

vs.sound-event: A sound event is a specific named trigger in an event-driven audio system. Event-driven audio is the architectural pattern; sound events are the individual triggers within it.
vs.audio-pool: Object pooling manages the lifecycle of audio source instances. Event-driven audio decides what to play; the pool provides the pre-allocated player to play it on.
vs.priority-system: The priority system determines which sounds survive when resources are exhausted. Event-driven audio triggers sounds; the priority system resolves conflicts between them.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a built-in event-driven audio system. Implement one using ScriptableObject-based event definitions, or integrate FMOD/Wwise for a production-ready event system.
unityAdapter.apiRef: Custom implementation using ScriptableObject, AudioSource, and C# events/delegates. FMOD for Unity: FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.PlayOneShot(eventPath). Wwise: AkSoundEngine.PostEvent(eventName, gameObject).
unityAdapter.importTip: For a lightweight custom system: create a SoundEvent ScriptableObject that holds an AudioClip array, volume range, pitch range, and cooldown. Create a SoundManager singleton that accepts SoundEvent references and handles randomization and pooling. This takes an afternoon to build and is infinitely better than scattered AudioSource.PlayClipAtPoint calls.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If you hard-code audio file references throughout your gameplay scripts, refactoring to an event-driven system later is painful. Start with events from day one, even if the initial implementation is simple. The abstraction layer pays for itself quickly.

misconceptions: Event-driven audio is only for big projects — even a small game benefits from separating 'what triggers a sound' from 'what sound plays.' A simple event dictionary takes minimal effort and saves hours of refactoring later.

You need middleware (FMOD/Wwise) for event-driven audio — middleware provides sophisticated event systems, but a basic event-driven architecture can be built with a dictionary and a few classes. Middleware adds visual authoring, advanced features, and designer-friendly workflows.

Event-driven audio adds significant overhead — the event lookup and resolution is negligible compared to actual audio processing. The organizational and workflow benefits far outweigh any minor overhead.

name: Fade Curve

beginner.oneLiner: A fade curve defines the shape of how a sound's volume changes over time when fading in or out, controlling whether the transition sounds smooth, abrupt, or natural.
beginner.analogy: Think of a dimmer switch on a light. A linear dimmer increases brightness at a constant rate — steady but can feel unnatural. A curved dimmer might change slowly at first and then quickly at the end, or vice versa. Fade curves let you choose what shape your volume dimmer follows, so transitions sound smooth to human ears.
beginner.whatToDo: When fading sounds in or out in your game, try different fade curves. A linear fade often sounds unnatural because human hearing is logarithmic. Use an exponential or S-curve fade for smoother-sounding transitions on music and ambience.

intermediate.oneLiner: A fade curve is the mathematical function that maps time to amplitude during a volume transition, with different curve shapes (linear, exponential, logarithmic, S-curve) producing perceptually different fade characteristics.
intermediate.details: Linear fades change amplitude at a constant rate but sound uneven to human ears — the fade seems to happen mostly at the end (fade-in) or beginning (fade-out) because hearing is logarithmic. Exponential fades (matching the dB scale) sound perceptually linear. S-curves provide a smooth slow-fast-slow transition ideal for crossfades. Constant-power (equal-power) curves maintain consistent perceived loudness during crossfades by following sin/cos shapes, preventing the volume dip that occurs with linear crossfades.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use logarithmic/exponential curves for general fade-ins and fade-outs on music and ambience — they sound naturally smooth. Use constant-power (equal-power) curves for crossfading between two sounds to avoid the perceived volume dip at the midpoint. Use linear curves only when you need predictable behavior for programmatic control or very short fades where the shape difference is inaudible.

advanced.oneLiner: Fade curves define the interpolation function mapping normalized time [0,1] to gain [0,1], with perceptually optimal fades requiring logarithmic or constant-power curves to compensate for the ear's nonlinear amplitude response.
advanced.technicalNotes: For a perceptually linear fade, use gain = 10^((t·range_dB)/20) where range_dB is the total fade range (e.g., -60 dB). For constant-power crossfade, outgoing = cos(t·π/2), incoming = sin(t·π/2), which ensures that the sum of squared amplitudes equals 1 at all points. For game engines, pre-compute fade curves as lookup tables or AnimationCurves for efficiency. Consider frame-rate-independent fading by using delta time to step through the curve. For real-time parameter automation, smooth the target value with a one-pole filter to avoid zipper noise from stepped updates.

vs.crossfade: A crossfade blends two sounds together using two complementary fade curves (one fading out, one fading in). A fade curve is the shape function used by a single fade. Crossfade quality depends on choosing the right pair of fade curves.
vs.interpolation: Interpolation is the general mathematical concept of computing values between known points. A fade curve is a specific application of interpolation applied to audio amplitude over time.

unityAdapter.location: Use AnimationCurves to define custom fade shapes. Assign them to AudioSource.volume over time, or use AudioMixer snapshots with transition times.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a public AnimationCurve field in your script and edit the curve visually in the Inspector. Evaluate it over time with curve.Evaluate(t) and apply the result to AudioSource.volume or a mixer parameter. Preset curves: AnimationCurve.EaseInOut(0,0,1,1) for an S-curve, AnimationCurve.Linear(0,0,1,1) for linear.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.volume is linear (0-1), not dB. A linear lerp on this value will not sound perceptually linear. Either use Mathf.Pow(t, 3) for an approximate logarithmic curve, or convert to dB, interpolate, and convert back: volume = Mathf.Pow(10, Mathf.Lerp(-60, 0, t) / 20).

misconceptions: Linear fades sound smooth — linear amplitude fades sound uneven because human hearing is logarithmic. The fade appears to happen mostly at one end. Use exponential or logarithmic curves for perceptually smooth fades.
All crossfades are the same — a linear crossfade drops perceived volume at the midpoint. A constant-power crossfade maintains consistent loudness throughout the transition.
Fade shape does not matter for short fades — even fades as short as 100-200 ms benefit from proper curve selection, especially in crossfades where the volume dip is perceptible.

name: FFT (Fast Fourier Transform)

beginner.oneLiner: FFT is a technique that breaks a sound into its individual frequencies, like splitting white light through a prism into a rainbow of colors.
beginner.analogy: Imagine you hear a chord on a piano — three notes played together. Your ear hears one combined sound, but your brain separates it into the three individual notes. FFT does the same thing mathematically: you give it a chunk of audio (the mixed sound) and it tells you exactly which frequencies are present and how loud each one is. This is how audio visualizers, tuners, and spectrum analyzers work.
beginner.whatToDo: Use Unity's AudioSource.GetSpectrumData to get FFT results for audio visualization or beat detection. You pass in the number of frequency bins you want (powers of 2 like 256, 512, or 1024) and get back an array showing the energy at each frequency band.

intermediate.oneLiner: FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) is an O(N log N) algorithm that converts a time-domain audio buffer into frequency-domain magnitude and phase data, enabling spectral analysis, frequency-domain processing, and visualization.
intermediate.details: The Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) converts N time-domain samples into N/2+1 complex frequency bins spanning 0 Hz to sampleRate/2 (the Nyquist frequency). The FFT is just a fast algorithm for computing the DFT — it reduces complexity from O(N^2) to O(N log N) by exploiting symmetries. Each frequency bin has a magnitude (how loud that frequency is) and a phase (where in its cycle that frequency is). The frequency resolution is sampleRate/N — a 1024-point FFT at 48 kHz gives ~46.9 Hz per bin. Larger FFT sizes give better frequency resolution but worse time resolution and higher latency. This frequency-time tradeoff is fundamental and unavoidable.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use FFT for audio visualization (spectrum displays, beat detection), pitch detection, spectral effects (vocoder, pitch shifting), and convolution reverb. For real-time analysis, 1024 or 2048 samples is a common FFT size. For detailed frequency analysis, use 4096 or larger. Always apply a window function before FFT to reduce spectral leakage.

advanced.oneLiner: FFT computes the DFT in O(N log N) via butterfly decomposition (typically radix-2 Cooley-Tukey), converting N real samples to N/2+1 complex bins with frequency resolution of fs/N Hz and temporal resolution of N/fs seconds, subject to the Heisenberg-Gabor uncertainty limit.
advanced.technicalNotes: For real-valued audio signals, the FFT output is conjugate-symmetric, so only N/2+1 unique bins are needed (real FFT optimization halves the work). Key implementation details: apply a window function (Hann, Blackman-Harris) before FFT to reduce spectral leakage. Zero-padding (e.g., 1024 samples padded to 2048) increases bin interpolation but not true resolution. For real-time spectral processing, use the STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform): window the input, FFT, process frequency bins, IFFT, overlap-add. Phase coherence across frames requires careful overlap (typically 75% with Hann window). In Unity, GetSpectrumData returns magnitude-only data (no phase), which limits it to analysis. For synthesis or modification, you need full complex FFT via a native plugin or library (KissFFT, FFTW, or Accelerate framework). SIMD-optimized FFT libraries can process a 4096-point real FFT in under 10 microseconds on modern desktop CPUs.

vs.dsp: FFT is one tool in the DSP toolbox. It enables frequency-domain processing, but many DSP operations (IIR filters, delay lines, gain) work directly in the time domain without FFT.
vs.windowing: Windowing is applied before FFT to reduce spectral leakage. Without a window function, the FFT assumes the signal repeats perfectly at the buffer boundary, which creates false frequency content.
vs.overlap-add: Overlap-add is the method for reconstructing a continuous time-domain signal from overlapping FFT frames. FFT analyzes; overlap-add synthesizes.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource.GetSpectrumData or AudioListener.GetSpectrumData. Select FFT size and window type. Results are available on the main thread (Unity computes FFT internally).
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioSource.GetSpectrumData(float[] samples, int channel, FFTWindow window) — samples.Length must be a power of 2 (64 to 8192)
unityAdapter.importTip: For a basic spectrum visualizer, call GetSpectrumData with 1024 samples and FFTWindow.BlackmanHarris each frame. The returned values are linear magnitude (not dB). Convert to dB with 20*log10(value) for more perceptually meaningful display. Index i corresponds to frequency i * sampleRate / (2 * samples.Length).
unityAdapter.gotcha: GetSpectrumData returns magnitude only — no phase information. You cannot use it for spectral processing that requires modifying and resynthesizing audio (like pitch shifting). For that, you need a full complex FFT in a native plugin. Also, calling GetSpectrumData is not free — avoid calling it every frame for multiple sources if you are CPU-constrained.

misconceptions: A larger FFT size is always better — larger sizes give finer frequency resolution but smear timing information. For beat detection, a smaller FFT (512-1024) with fast updates is better than a large FFT with sluggish response.

FFT shows you what frequencies are playing right now — FFT analyzes a window of time (N samples). It shows the average frequency content over that window, not a single instant. This is the time-frequency uncertainty principle.

FFT and EQ do the same thing — EQ (equalization) is typically done with IIR biquad filters in the time domain, not FFT. FFT-based frequency shaping exists but is more expensive and introduces latency.

name: FLAC (Free Lossless Audio Codec)

beginner.oneLiner: FLAC compresses audio files to roughly half their original size without losing any quality at all, like a ZIP file but specifically designed for audio.
beginner.analogy: Lossy formats like MP3 are like summarizing a book — you get the gist but lose details. FLAC is like compressing a book into a smaller package for shipping — when you unpack it, every single word is exactly the same. The file is smaller, but the audio is bit-for-bit identical to the original.
beginner.whatToDo: Use FLAC as your archival master format for original recordings. Keep FLAC masters and convert to Vorbis, MP3, or WAV for your actual game builds. If your game engine does not support FLAC directly, convert to WAV (for uncompressed) or Vorbis (for lossy) during your build pipeline.

intermediate.oneLiner: FLAC is an open-source lossless audio codec that achieves 40-60% compression of PCM audio through linear prediction and entropy coding, with perfect bit-for-bit reconstruction on decode.
intermediate.details: FLAC compresses audio by predicting each sample from previous samples using linear predictive coding, then entropy-encoding the residual error. Compression ratio depends on content: simple tones compress well (up to 70%), complex music less so (around 40-50%). FLAC supports up to 32-bit samples and 655,350 Hz sample rate, with metadata support via Vorbis comments. Decoding is fast and requires minimal CPU. Unlike lossy codecs, you can re-encode FLAC files without generational loss.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use FLAC for archiving your original sound recordings and masters. Store your sound library in FLAC to save disk space compared to WAV while preserving full quality. Convert to lossy formats only at the final build step. FLAC is also useful for quality-critical sound design work where you need to edit and re-export without accumulating lossy artifacts.

advanced.oneLiner: FLAC uses fixed and adaptive linear prediction with Rice coding for entropy compression of the prediction residuals, achieving lossless compression with fast, integer-only decoding.
advanced.technicalNotes: FLAC divides audio into blocks (typically 4096 samples), applies one of several LPC models (fixed polynomial up to 4th order, or adaptive LPC up to 32nd order), and Rice-codes the residuals. The format supports seeking via a seek table, cue sheets for CD-like indexing, and application-specific metadata blocks. Decoding is integer-only arithmetic, making it suitable for embedded and low-power platforms. For game audio pipelines, FLAC works well as a source format in asset pipelines: CI/CD scripts convert FLAC masters to engine-native formats. Runtime FLAC decoding is possible but uncommon in games due to the larger file size compared to lossy codecs.

vs.wav: Both are lossless. WAV is uncompressed PCM with no size reduction. FLAC achieves 40-60% compression with identical audio quality. WAV has zero decode overhead; FLAC has minimal decode overhead.
vs.vorbis: Vorbis is lossy and much smaller (typically 10-15% of WAV size). FLAC is lossless and larger (40-60% of WAV size). Use FLAC for archival and editing, Vorbis for final game builds where size matters.
vs.mp3: MP3 is lossy with potential artifacts. FLAC is lossless with no artifacts. FLAC files are 3-5x larger than equivalent MP3. Use FLAC when quality preservation is paramount.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not natively import FLAC files. Convert FLAC to WAV or use Unity's built-in compression (Vorbis, ADPCM) on imported WAV files.
unityAdapter.apiRef: No FLAC API in Unity. Use external tools (ffmpeg, SoX) in a pre-build script to convert FLAC to WAV for import.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set up a build script that converts your FLAC sound library to WAV files in your Unity project's Assets folder. This lets you keep lossless masters while Unity handles the final compression to Vorbis or ADPCM in its import settings.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Dragging FLAC files into Unity will not work — they are not recognized by the importer. Always convert to WAV first. Do not convert FLAC to MP3 to WAV — that introduces lossy artifacts. Go directly from FLAC to WAV.

misconceptions: FLAC sounds better than WAV. FLAC and WAV contain identical audio data. FLAC is just smaller. A decoded FLAC stream is bit-for-bit the same as the original PCM.

FLAC is good for game builds because it saves space. While FLAC is smaller than WAV, it is still much larger than lossy formats. Games typically use Vorbis or ADPCM for shipped assets and keep FLAC only as source masters.
Lossless means uncompressed. FLAC is both lossless and compressed. Lossless means no data is lost, not that no compression was applied.

name: Flanger

beginner.oneLiner: A flanger creates a sweeping, jet-like swoosh by mixing a sound with a slightly delayed copy of itself that shifts over time.
beginner.analogy: Imagine two identical tape recorders playing the same sound. If you press your finger on the edge (flange) of one reel to slow it down slightly, the two copies go in and out of sync, creating that distinctive whooshing sweep.
beginner.whatToDo: Use a flanger to add motion and sci-fi character to sound effects like lasers, whooshes, or UI transitions. Keep the depth low for subtle movement or crank it up for dramatic sweeps.

intermediate.oneLiner: A flanger mixes the dry signal with a short, LFO-modulated delayed copy (typically 1-20 ms), creating a moving comb filter with characteristic peaks and notches.
intermediate.details: The flanger works by feeding the signal through a variable delay line whose delay time is modulated by an LFO. The interaction between the original and delayed signal creates a comb filter pattern that sweeps up and down the frequency spectrum. Feedback (regeneration) intensifies the effect by feeding the delayed output back into the input. Negative feedback produces a hollow, metallic tone, while positive feedback gives a more resonant, jet-engine quality.
intermediate.whenToUse: Apply flanging to create sci-fi weapon sounds, robotic voice effects, or transitional swooshes. It works well on sustained or broadband sounds. Avoid heavy flanging on dialogue or music beds where clarity matters, as the comb filtering can make things sound thin or phasey.

advanced.oneLiner: A flanger applies a time-varying comb filter via a short modulated delay line (0.1-20 ms) with feedback, producing harmonically related notches that sweep through the spectrum.
advanced.technicalNotes: The comb filter notches occur at multiples of 1/delay_time Hz. As the LFO sweeps the delay time, these notches move in a harmonically locked pattern, unlike a phaser whose notch spacing is non-harmonic. In Unity, you can build a flanger using OnAudioFilterRead by maintaining a circular buffer and reading from a modulated tap position. The feedback path should be clamped to avoid instability (keep below 0.95). For game audio, consider pre-baking flanged effects rather than applying real-time flanging to many voices simultaneously, as per-sample delay interpolation across multiple sources can be CPU-intensive.

vs.phaser: A phaser uses all-pass filters to create a smaller number of unevenly spaced notches, giving a smoother, more subtle sweep. A flanger uses a delay line producing many harmonically spaced notches for a more intense, metallic sound.
vs.chorus: Chorus uses longer delay times (20-50 ms) with no feedback, creating a thickening/doubling effect. Flanger uses shorter delays (1-20 ms) with feedback, creating the characteristic comb-filter sweep.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in flanger component. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead on an AudioSource, or use a third-party plugin.
unityAdapter.importTip: For a quick flanger, maintain a circular buffer of ~2048 samples, modulate the read position with a sine LFO, and mix the delayed signal with the dry signal. Use linear interpolation for the fractional delay tap.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Running a custom flanger on many simultaneous AudioSources can spike the audio thread. Pre-render flanged variations of your sound effects offline when possible.

misconceptions: A flanger and a phaser are not the same effect. They sound similar but use different DSP techniques and produce different notch patterns.
Flanging does not require two physical tape machines. Modern flangers are implemented entirely in digital signal processing with a single modulated delay line.
Increasing flanger feedback does not just make it louder. It intensifies the resonant peaks and can produce pitched, metallic overtones.

name: Fletcher-Munson Curves

beginner.oneLiner: Fletcher-Munson curves show that human ears do not hear all frequencies at the same loudness — we are much more sensitive to midrange frequencies and less sensitive to very low and very high ones, especially at quiet volumes.
beginner.analogy: Imagine wearing tinted glasses that block certain colors more than others, and the tinting changes depending on how bright the light is. Your ears work similarly — they naturally muffle bass and treble at low volumes but hear them more evenly at high volumes. Fletcher-Munson curves map out exactly how this uneven hearing works.
beginner.whatToDo: When mixing your game at a low monitoring volume, remember that bass and treble will sound quieter than they really are. Check your mix at different volume levels. If your game will be played at low volumes (mobile, handheld), boost important low-frequency sounds slightly to compensate for reduced hearing sensitivity.

intermediate.oneLiner: Fletcher-Munson curves (equal-loudness contours) map the SPL required at each frequency to be perceived as equally loud, revealing that human hearing sensitivity is frequency-dependent and loudness-dependent.
intermediate.details: The curves show that human hearing is most sensitive around 2-5 kHz (where speech consonants live) and least sensitive below 100 Hz and above 10 kHz. At low listening levels, the disparity is extreme — bass seems to disappear. At higher levels, the response flattens. The modern standard is ISO 226:2003 (equal-loudness contours). The phon scale quantifies perceived loudness: 40 phons means a tone sounds as loud as a 40 dB SPL 1 kHz reference tone, regardless of its actual frequency.
intermediate.whenToUse: Apply this knowledge when mixing your game: important low-frequency cues (footsteps, engine rumble) may be inaudible to players at low volumes. Use the 2-5 kHz presence range to ensure critical sounds cut through. Consider offering a loudness compensation option (like a loudness button on a stereo) that boosts bass and treble at low playback volumes.

advanced.oneLiner: Equal-loudness contours (ISO 226:2003) describe the SPL required for pure tones across the frequency spectrum to produce a given phon level, quantifying the nonlinear, level-dependent sensitivity of the human auditory system and informing loudness-weighted metering (A-weighting, K-weighting).
advanced.technicalNotes: The A-weighting filter used in SPL meters approximates the inverse of the 40-phon equal-loudness contour, making it a rough model of perceived loudness at low levels. K-weighting (used in LUFS/BS.1770) is a more modern psychoacoustic weighting. For game audio, the key implications are: (1) bass content is perceptually fragile at low playback levels — design critical bass cues with harmonic content extending into the midrange; (2) the 2-5 kHz peak in hearing sensitivity means harshness accumulates there — use de-essing or dips in this range if the mix feels fatiguing; (3) loudness normalization targets (e.g., -14 LUFS for streaming) interact with equal-loudness perception — a mix normalized to a low level will sound bass-thin compared to the same mix played louder.

vs.loudness-unit: LUFS measures integrated perceived loudness of a complete signal. Fletcher-Munson curves describe the underlying psychoacoustic phenomenon that LUFS metering accounts for via frequency weighting filters.
vs.eq: EQ changes the frequency balance of a signal. Fletcher-Munson curves describe how the ear naturally applies its own frequency-dependent 'EQ' to what it hears. Understanding the curves helps you use EQ to compensate for the ear's biases.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not implement Fletcher-Munson compensation directly. Apply the concept when setting up EQ on mixer groups and when tuning volume levels for different listening contexts.
unityAdapter.apiRef: N/A — conceptual tool for mixing decisions
unityAdapter.importTip: Test your Unity game's audio at multiple volume levels using the system volume control, not just the in-game slider. Listen on phone speakers and earbuds at low volume to ensure critical sounds are still audible. Add a 'loudness compensation' toggle that slightly boosts a low shelf and high shelf EQ on the master bus.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Mixing exclusively at high monitoring volumes will trick you into thinking your bass and treble levels are fine. When players listen at lower volumes (which most will), those frequencies will disappear. Always reference your mix at low volume.

misconceptions: Human hearing is flat across all frequencies — it is not. We are significantly less sensitive to bass and treble, especially at low listening levels. Fletcher-Munson curves quantify this.

Fletcher-Munson curves only matter for music production — they affect any audio playback. Game sounds need to account for uneven hearing sensitivity, especially for mobile and handheld games played at low volumes.

Turning up the volume fixes everything — while louder playback flattens the perceived frequency response, you cannot control how loud your players listen. Design your mix to work at low to moderate levels.

name: FM Synthesis

beginner.oneLiner: FM synthesis creates complex sounds by using one sound wave to rapidly wobble the pitch of another, generating rich harmonic textures from just a few simple tones.
beginner.analogy: Imagine spinning a jump rope (one wave) while walking forward (another wave). The spinning creates a complex path even though each motion alone is simple. FM synthesis works similarly: one simple wave modulating another produces surprisingly rich and complex sounds.
beginner.whatToDo: Use FM synthesis for metallic sounds, bells, electric pianos, and sci-fi effects. Start with two sine wave oscillators where one modulates the other. Adjust the modulation amount (index) to go from smooth tones to harsh, complex textures.

intermediate.oneLiner: FM synthesis generates complex spectra by modulating the frequency of a carrier oscillator with one or more modulator oscillators, where the modulation depth (index) and frequency ratio determine the resulting timbre.
intermediate.details: In FM synthesis, operators are oscillators that can be carriers (heard in the output) or modulators (affecting other operators' frequencies). The carrier:modulator frequency ratio determines which harmonics are produced. Integer ratios (1:1, 1:2, 2:3) create harmonic tones; non-integer ratios produce inharmonic, metallic, or bell-like timbres. The modulation index controls spectral complexity: low index = simple sine-like tone; high index = many sidebands with rich, bright timbre. Operators are arranged in algorithms (routing configurations) that define how they modulate each other, from simple two-operator chains to complex four or six-operator networks.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use FM synthesis for bell sounds, metallic impacts, electric piano tones, percussive hits, and sci-fi effects. It excels at sounds with strong transient attacks and inharmonic content. In game audio, FM is useful for procedural sound effects because small parameter changes produce dramatic timbral shifts, making it responsive to game state.

advanced.oneLiner: FM synthesis implements the equation y(t) = A*sin(2*pi*fc*t + I*sin(2*pi*fm*t)), where the modulation index I and ratio fc/fm determine the sideband distribution according to Bessel functions of the first kind.
advanced.technicalNotes: The output spectrum contains sidebands at frequencies fc +/- n*fm for integer n, with amplitudes proportional to Bessel functions Jn(I). As the modulation index increases, energy spreads to higher-order sidebands. For band-limited synthesis, sidebands that exceed Nyquist will alias, so moderate the index at high carrier frequencies or oversample. Classic FM (Yamaha DX7 style) uses 6 operators with 32 algorithms. In Unity, implement operators as phase accumulators in OnAudioFilterRead; the modulator output is added to the carrier's phase increment. Use envelopes on both carrier amplitude and modulator index for dynamic, evolving timbres. FM synthesis is very CPU-efficient (a few multiplies and sine lookups per sample per operator).

vs.additive-synthesis: Additive synthesis controls each harmonic individually, giving precise spectral control but requiring many oscillators. FM synthesis generates many sidebands from just 2-4 operators, producing complex spectra very efficiently but with less intuitive control over individual harmonics.
vs.subtractive-synthesis: Subtractive synthesis sculpts timbre by filtering a fixed spectrum. FM synthesis generates the spectral content directly through modulation, enabling timbres (bells, metallic impacts) that are difficult to achieve with subtractive methods.
vs.wavetable-synthesis: Wavetable synthesis reads pre-computed waveforms for predictable results. FM synthesis computes spectra mathematically in real time, offering more dynamic range of timbres but less visual/intuitive feedback.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in FM synthesizer. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead or use third-party synth plugins.
unityAdapter.importTip: Implement a two-operator FM synth: maintain two phase accumulators. Each sample, compute modulator output (sin(modPhase) * modulationIndex), then add it to the carrier phase increment before computing sin(carrierPhase + modOutput). Use a lookup table for sin() to save CPU.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Small changes to FM ratio and index produce dramatic timbral changes, making FM synthesis harder to tune by ear. Use integer ratios for harmonic sounds and document your parameter values, as finding a good sound by accident is easy to lose.

misconceptions: FM synthesis is not the same as vibrato. Vibrato uses slow frequency modulation (a few Hz) for a pitch wobble effect. FM synthesis uses audio-rate modulation (hundreds or thousands of Hz) to generate new harmonic content.
FM synthesis does not require complex hardware. A basic two-operator FM synth can be implemented in a few lines of code and runs efficiently on any modern device.

The complexity of FM synthesis is not in the computation but in the parameter space. The math is simple, but predicting the resulting timbre from the parameters is unintuitive, which is why presets and experimentation are important.

name: FMOD

beginner.oneLiner: FMOD is a professional audio middleware tool that gives you a visual interface to design complex sound behaviors and connect them to your game, without writing audio code from scratch.
beginner.analogy: Imagine building a game with only basic text editing tools versus using a full game engine like Unity. You could technically build everything from scratch, but the engine gives you visual editors, physics systems, and ready-made components. FMOD does the same for audio — instead of hand-coding every sound behavior, you get a visual editor where a sound designer can build complex audio systems (random variations, crossfades, parameter-driven effects) and export them for the game to use.
beginner.whatToDo: Download FMOD Studio (free for indie developers under a revenue threshold) and the FMOD Unity integration package. Create sound events in FMOD Studio, build them into banks, and trigger them from your Unity code using FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.PlayOneShot(). Start with simple one-shot events for your SFX, then explore parameters and snapshots.

intermediate.oneLiner: FMOD is an audio middleware suite consisting of FMOD Studio (a visual authoring tool for sound designers) and the FMOD Engine (a cross-platform runtime library), providing event-based audio with parameters, mixing, spatialization, and voice management.
intermediate.details: FMOD Studio is the authoring application where sound designers create events, configure parameters, set up mixer buses, and define audio behaviors using a timeline and parameter sheet interface. Events can contain multiple sounds with randomization, looping regions, transition logic, and parameter-driven behavior. The FMOD Engine is the runtime that loads banks, plays events, and processes audio in your game. Key features: event-based architecture (gameplay triggers named events, not files), RTPC parameters (real-time continuous control of sound properties), snapshots (preset mixer states for different game contexts), banks (packaged audio data loaded at runtime), virtual voice system (automatically manages polyphony and priority). FMOD supports all major platforms and integrates with Unity, Unreal, and custom engines.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use FMOD when your audio needs exceed what the built-in engine audio provides — which is most projects beyond simple prototypes. FMOD excels when you have a dedicated sound designer who needs a visual workflow, when you need complex adaptive audio behaviors, or when you need consistent audio behavior across platforms. It is free for indie developers under $200K revenue.

advanced.oneLiner: FMOD provides a complete audio pipeline: an authoring DAW (FMOD Studio) producing banked event data, and a cross-platform low-latency runtime (FMOD Engine) with programmable DSP graph, virtual voice management, sample-accurate event scheduling, and live profiling.
advanced.technicalNotes: Architecture: FMOD Studio exports .bank files containing encoded audio assets and event metadata. The runtime loads banks, instantiates event instances on the mixer thread, and processes a DSP graph that can be extended with custom DSP plugins (C/C++). The virtual voice system maintains up to thousands of virtual voices with configurable steal policies; only voices above the audibility threshold consume physical channels. Events support nested instruments: single sounds, multi sounds (random/sequential selection), scatterer (random spatial spawning), programmer instruments (runtime clip selection via callback). The mixer thread runs at a configurable buffer size. Live Connect allows FMOD Studio to connect to a running game build for real-time parameter adjustment and profiling. FMOD's command replay system records all API calls for deterministic audio reproduction. For Unity integration, the FMODUnity package replaces Unity's audio system entirely — AudioSources are not used; instead, StudioEventEmitter components or direct API calls manage playback.

vs.wwise: FMOD and Wwise are the two dominant audio middleware options. FMOD is generally considered easier to learn with a more musician-friendly timeline interface. Wwise offers more granular control with its actor-mixer hierarchy and is favored for AAA productions. Both are free for indie developers.
vs.audio-mixer: Unity's Audio Mixer provides basic mixing and effects. FMOD replaces it entirely with a more powerful mixer, adaptive audio events, parameters, and professional voice management. Using FMOD means Unity's mixer is bypassed.
vs.sound-event: FMOD events are the primary API surface. Each event encapsulates all sound logic (clips, randomization, parameters, effects). Sound events in general are a concept; FMOD events are a specific, feature-rich implementation.

unityAdapter.location: Import the FMOD for Unity package. FMOD menu appears in the Unity menu bar. Events are authored in FMOD Studio (separate application) and loaded in Unity via StudioEventEmitter components or scripting API.
unityAdapter.apiRef: FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.PlayOneShot(string eventPath, Vector3 position), FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.CreateInstance(string eventPath), StudioEventEmitter component, FMOD.Studio.EventInstance
unityAdapter.importTip: Use PlayOneShot for simple fire-and-forget sounds (UI clicks, impacts). Use CreateInstance for sounds you need to control over time (looping ambience, music, engine sounds with parameters). Always release instances when done: instance.release(). Attach StudioEventEmitter to GameObjects for the simplest integration without code.
unityAdapter.gotcha: FMOD replaces Unity's audio pipeline — AudioSource, AudioListener, and Audio Mixer are not used with FMOD. Do not mix Unity audio and FMOD audio systems in the same project unless you specifically need to (advanced use case). Also, FMOD banks must be built in FMOD Studio and present in your StreamingAssets folder before they work at runtime.

misconceptions: FMOD is only for AAA studios — FMOD Studio is free for indie developers under $200K revenue and is widely used in indie games. The learning curve is modest and pays off quickly.

FMOD is just a sound player — it is a complete audio pipeline including mixing, effects, spatial audio, voice management, parameter-driven adaptive audio, and live profiling. It replaces the engine's entire audio system.

You need to choose FMOD at the start of the project — while starting early is ideal, FMOD can be integrated into an existing project. The main effort is replacing direct AudioSource calls with FMOD event triggers.

name: Foley

beginner.oneLiner: Foley is the art of recording real-world sounds — like footsteps, cloth rustling, or object handling — to add believable, physical detail to games and films.
beginner.analogy: Foley is like a voice actor for everyday objects. Just as a voice actor performs dialogue to match a character on screen, a foley artist performs sounds — walking, grabbing, opening — that match the actions happening in the game. The sounds are real, but carefully performed and recorded to fit perfectly.
beginner.whatToDo: You can do basic foley at home with a USB microphone. Record yourself walking on different surfaces, handling objects, or crumpling paper for fire textures. Even rough recordings can be processed and layered to create convincing game sounds. Start with footsteps — they are the most common foley need in games.

intermediate.oneLiner: Foley is the practice of recording performed sounds in sync with on-screen or in-game actions, providing the tactile, physical audio layer that grounds visuals in reality.
intermediate.details: Traditional foley categories include footsteps (character movement on various surfaces), cloth and movement (armor, fabric, leather), and props (doors, weapons, pickups, environmental interactions). In game audio, foley is typically recorded as individual one-shots or short variations rather than continuous synced performances. Key recording considerations: close-mic technique for clean recordings with minimal room sound, performing at the right intensity to match the in-game scale, recording multiple variations (5-10 per action) to avoid repetition, and recording at 24-bit/96kHz for maximum editing flexibility.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use foley for any action that needs to feel physically grounded: footsteps, weapon handling (holster, reload, equip), inventory interactions, environmental touches (pushing through bushes, swimming), and character movement (armor clinks, cloth rustle). Foley adds immersion that synthesized sounds cannot easily replicate.

advanced.oneLiner: Foley is a specialized recording discipline focused on performed sound reproduction, where material selection, microphone technique, and performance dynamics are calibrated to produce semantically appropriate audio assets for interactive media implementation.
advanced.technicalNotes: For game foley, the recording chain matters: a large-diaphragm condenser or shotgun mic, positioned 15-30cm from the source, in a treated (dead) room. Record to a high-quality interface at 24-bit/96kHz — the extra bandwidth gives headroom for pitch shifting and time stretching in post. Performance technique is critical: perform at varied intensities and record takes at different distances for layering options. In implementation, foley sounds should be triggered by animation events or physics callbacks rather than timers for precise sync. Middleware randomization (pitch, volume, round-robin) on foley assets prevents the machine-gun effect. Physically-based foley systems use material properties and impact velocity to select and modulate foley sounds procedurally.

vs.sound-design: Foley is a recording-based technique producing organic, real-world sounds. Sound design is the broader discipline that may also include synthesis, heavy processing, and non-realistic audio creation.
vs.layering: Layering combines multiple sounds (including foley) into composite effects. Foley is one source of raw material that often gets layered with synthetic or processed elements.
