vs.audio-bus: The terms are often used interchangeably. Strictly, an audio bus is any routing channel. A mix bus specifically implies a bus used for combining and processing groups of related sources in a mixing context.
vs.send-effect: A send routes a copy of the signal to a separate processing chain (like a shared reverb). A mix bus is the primary routing path for the signal. They work together: a mix bus might send a portion of its signal to a reverb return bus.

unityAdapter.location: Window > Audio > Audio Mixer. Right-click a group and select 'Add child group' to create sub-buses. Assign AudioSources to groups via the AudioMixerGroup field.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a starter mixer hierarchy: Master > Music, SFX (> Footsteps, Weapons, UI), Dialogue, Ambience. Assign every AudioSource to the appropriate group. This takes 10 minutes to set up and saves hours of mixing work later.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If you do not assign an AudioSource to a mixer group, it routes directly to the master output, bypassing all your bus processing and volume management. Always assign every AudioSource to a group.

misconceptions: You only need a master bus — without sub-buses, you lose the ability to control categories independently. A player wanting to lower music but keep SFX loud requires separate buses.

More buses are always better — every bus adds processing overhead and management complexity. Use as many as you need for meaningful grouping, but avoid buses with only one or two sources unless they need unique processing.

Bus volume is the same as source volume — bus volume scales all sources routed through it multiplicatively. Adjusting one affects everything in the group. Use source volume for individual balance and bus volume for category-level control.

name: Mixer Snapshot

beginner.oneLiner: A mixer snapshot saves the settings of all your audio buses so you can switch between different mix states instantly or with a smooth fade.
beginner.analogy: Think of lighting presets in a theater. One preset has bright lights for an action scene, another has dim blue lights for a night scene. You press a button and all the lights smoothly change. Mixer snapshots work the same way for audio: one snapshot for gameplay, another for pause menus, another for cutscenes.
beginner.whatToDo: Create at least two snapshots: one for normal gameplay and one for the pause menu (where you might mute SFX and lower music). Transition between them when the game state changes. Unity makes this straightforward with the Audio Mixer's snapshot system.

intermediate.oneLiner: A mixer snapshot stores the complete state of an audio mixer (volumes, effect parameters, sends) and allows interpolated transitions between mix states.
intermediate.details: Snapshots capture every exposed parameter on every bus: volume faders, effect wet/dry, EQ bands, send levels, and more. Transitioning between snapshots interpolates all parameters over a specified duration. You can also blend multiple snapshots with weighted influence using TransitionToSnapshots(). Common snapshot pairs: Gameplay (full mix), Pause (muted SFX, lowered music), Cutscene (boosted dialog, muted UI), Underwater (low-pass filter on all buses, boosted reverb), Death (muted everything except a ringing tone).
intermediate.whenToUse: Create snapshots for each distinct game state: main menu, gameplay, pause, inventory, cutscene, special environments (underwater, cave). Trigger snapshot transitions on state change events. Use timed transitions (0.5-1.0 seconds) for smooth crossfades rather than abrupt switches.

advanced.oneLiner: Mixer snapshots serialize the full parameter state of an audio mixing graph and support weighted blending and interpolated transitions for real-time mix automation.
advanced.technicalNotes: In Unity, AudioMixerSnapshot.TransitionTo(float timeToReach) interpolates all parameters linearly over the given duration. AudioMixer.TransitionToSnapshots(AudioMixerSnapshot[] snapshots, float[] weights, float timeToReach) blends multiple snapshots by weighted average. Weights are normalized internally. This enables dynamic mix states: blend between 'calm' and 'combat' snapshots based on a threat meter value. Snapshots only affect exposed parameters; unexposed parameters remain at their current values and are not part of the snapshot. For parameters that need non-linear interpolation (e.g., logarithmic volume fades), apply the curve in the parameter's attenuation units (dB) rather than linear amplitude.

vs.manual-parameter-automation: Manual automation via AudioMixer.SetFloat() gives per-parameter control but requires managing every parameter individually. Snapshots handle all parameters holistically but with less granular control over individual transition curves.
vs.audio-bus: A bus is a single routing channel. A snapshot is a saved configuration of all buses and their parameters.

unityAdapter.location: Window > Audio > Audio Mixer > Snapshots section (top-right of mixer window). Right-click to add new snapshots. Edit by selecting a snapshot and adjusting mixer faders.
unityAdapter.importTip: Name snapshots descriptively (Gameplay, Paused, Cutscene, Underwater). Reference them in scripts via public AudioMixerSnapshot fields assigned in the Inspector, or find them via AudioMixer.FindSnapshot(string name).
unityAdapter.gotcha: Snapshot transitions only affect parameters that are exposed to scripting. If a parameter is not exposed (right-click > Expose to Script), it will not be stored in or transitioned by snapshots. Make sure to expose all parameters you want snapshots to control.

misconceptions: Snapshots are just volume presets. They store ALL exposed mixer parameters including effect settings, send levels, pitch, and any custom exposed properties.
You need to manually reset parameters when leaving a snapshot. Transitioning to a new snapshot automatically interpolates all parameters from the current state to the new snapshot's state.

name: Modulation

beginner.oneLiner: Modulation means using one signal to change a property of another sound over time, creating movement and variation.
beginner.analogy: Imagine holding a flashlight with a shaky hand — the beam wobbles back and forth instead of staying still. Audio modulation is similar: one signal (your shaky hand) continuously nudges a property like pitch or volume, adding life and motion to an otherwise static sound.
beginner.whatToDo: Try adding slow pitch modulation (vibrato) to sustained tones like magic spells or engine hums. A subtle wobble can make a flat, boring sound feel alive. Start with a slow rate and small depth so it enhances rather than distracts.

intermediate.oneLiner: Modulation uses a control signal (typically an LFO or envelope) to vary a sound parameter such as pitch, amplitude, or filter cutoff over time.
intermediate.details: The most common modulation source is an LFO (Low-Frequency Oscillator), running at sub-audio rates (0.1-20 Hz). Pitch modulation at slow rates is called vibrato. Amplitude modulation at slow rates is tremolo. Filter cutoff modulation creates wah or sweep effects. At audio rates (above 20 Hz), modulation enters FM/AM synthesis territory and creates new harmonics rather than perceptible movement. Key parameters are rate (how fast), depth (how much), and shape (sine for smooth, square for abrupt, random for unpredictable).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use pitch modulation for organic, living sounds like creature calls, magical effects, or eerie ambiences. Use amplitude modulation for tremolo on sustained tones or to simulate rotating speakers. Use filter modulation for sweeping, evolving textures or sci-fi UI sounds.

advanced.oneLiner: Modulation maps a time-varying control signal onto a destination parameter, where the control signal's rate relative to the audible spectrum determines whether the result is perceived as movement (sub-audio) or timbral change (audio-rate).
advanced.technicalNotes: FM synthesis (frequency modulation at audio rates) generates sidebands at carrier +/- N*modulator frequency, producing complex harmonic spectra from simple oscillators. The modulation index (depth/modulator frequency) controls spectral richness. Ring modulation (multiplying two signals) produces sum and difference frequencies with no carrier, useful for metallic or inharmonic tones. In Unity, implement modulation in OnAudioFilterRead by applying a per-sample multiplier derived from an LFO. For parameter modulation (e.g., filter cutoff), update at control rate (per audio buffer) to save CPU while avoiding zipper noise.

vs.chorus: Chorus uses modulated delay times to create detuned copies. Modulation is the broader concept — chorus is one specific application of delay-time modulation.
vs.adsr: ADSR is a one-shot envelope modulator triggered by note events. LFO modulation is continuous and cyclical. Both are modulation sources but serve different shaping purposes.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in modulation component. Implement via scripting in OnAudioFilterRead or by animating AudioSource parameters (pitch, volume) over time with coroutines or AnimationCurve.
unityAdapter.importTip: For simple vibrato, use a coroutine that oscillates AudioSource.pitch with Mathf.Sin(Time.time * rate) * depth. For per-sample accuracy, apply the modulation inside OnAudioFilterRead.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Modulating AudioSource.pitch from the main thread only updates once per frame, which can sound stepped at low frame rates. For smooth modulation, apply it per-sample in OnAudioFilterRead or use a large enough depth that per-frame steps are masked.

misconceptions: Modulation always creates a wobble effect. At audio rates (above 20 Hz), modulation creates new frequencies and timbral changes rather than an audible wobble.
Vibrato and tremolo are the same thing. Vibrato is pitch modulation and tremolo is volume modulation — they sound very different even at identical rates and depths.

name: Mono Compatibility

beginner.oneLiner: Mono compatibility means making sure your audio still sounds good when the left and right stereo channels are combined into a single channel, which happens on many real-world playback devices.
beginner.analogy: Imagine painting a picture meant to be viewed with 3D glasses. It looks great with the glasses, but without them, some parts disappear or look weird because the left and right eye images cancel each other out. Mono compatibility is making sure your audio 'picture' still looks good without the 3D glasses — when both channels are merged into one.
beginner.whatToDo: Periodically check your game's audio mix in mono by summing left and right channels. Listen for sounds that get quieter, disappear, or change character. If something sounds wrong in mono, reduce stereo width or check for phase issues in that asset.

intermediate.oneLiner: Mono compatibility refers to a stereo audio signal's ability to maintain its tonal balance, level, and clarity when summed to a single channel, which can be compromised by phase differences between left and right channels.
intermediate.details: When left and right channels are summed, identical content adds (+6 dB) while out-of-phase content cancels (partially or fully). Stereo widening effects, some reverbs, and heavily panned elements can introduce phase differences that cause cancellation in mono. Common mono playback scenarios include phone speakers, Bluetooth speakers, some TV configurations, mono hearing aid users, and PA systems. Mobile gaming is especially relevant — many phones play audio in mono.
intermediate.whenToUse: Always check mono compatibility for game audio that might be played on mobile devices, single speakers, or public installations. Pay special attention to music, stereo ambient beds, and any sound processed with stereo widening effects. A good practice: regularly toggle your monitor to mono during mixing sessions. Most DAWs and middleware have a mono-sum button.

advanced.oneLiner: Mono compatibility measures the correlation between stereo channels and quantifies the magnitude of destructive interference when summed, with correlation coefficients approaching -1 indicating severe cancellation and potential signal loss in mono playback.
advanced.technicalNotes: The Pearson correlation coefficient between L and R channels indicates mono compatibility: +1 is perfect mono (identical channels), 0 is uncorrelated (independent signals), -1 is fully out of phase (complete cancellation in mono sum). Use a goniometer or correlation meter to visualize this in real time. Mid-side (M/S) analysis separates the mono-compatible component (Mid = L+R) from the stereo-only component (Side = L-R). If critical audio information exists only in the Side signal, it will be lost in mono. For game audio, ensure that essential gameplay cues have strong Mid content. Test by monitoring only the Mid channel during mixing.

vs.stereo-vs-mono: Stereo vs mono is about the format of the audio signal. Mono compatibility is about whether a stereo signal maintains quality when collapsed to mono. A stereo signal can be mono-compatible (sounds fine summed) or not.
vs.phase-cancellation: Phase cancellation is the destructive interference that occurs when out-of-phase signals combine. Mono compatibility issues are specifically caused by phase cancellation between left and right channels when summed.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a built-in mono sum button. Test mono compatibility by routing through a mixer group with a custom script that sums L+R, or use your system audio settings to force mono output.
unityAdapter.apiRef: OnAudioFilterRead(float[], int) for custom mono summing
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a development-only mono check: attach a script with OnAudioFilterRead to the AudioListener that sums left and right channels when a debug key is pressed. Use this throughout development to catch mono compatibility issues early.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioSource.panStereo and stereo clips can create mono compatibility issues when combined. Always test with your mono-sum script after adding stereo effects or wide panning to ensure nothing important disappears.

misconceptions: Nobody listens in mono anymore — phone speakers, many Bluetooth speakers, smart home devices, and accessibility settings frequently play audio in mono. A significant portion of your audience may hear mono output.
Mono compatibility problems only affect stereo music — any stereo signal can have phase issues, including stereo ambient beds, stereo reverb returns, and wide sound effects in your game mix.

Making everything mono solves the problem — the goal is not to eliminate stereo but to ensure stereo content degrades gracefully to mono. A mix can be rich and wide in stereo while still being fully mono compatible.

name: MP3

beginner.oneLiner: MP3 is the most widely known lossy audio format. It makes audio files much smaller by removing sounds you're unlikely to notice.
beginner.analogy: It's like summarizing a book: you keep all the important parts and trim the stuff most readers would skip, so the result is much shorter but reads almost the same.
beginner.whatToDo: MP3 works fine for game audio, but most engines prefer Vorbis because it's free to use and slightly better quality. If you already have MP3 assets, they'll import just fine into Unity.

intermediate.oneLiner: MP3 (MPEG-1 Audio Layer III) is a lossy codec using psychoacoustic modeling and MDCT, offering reasonable compression at 128-320 kbps with universal playback support.
intermediate.details: MP3 encodes audio in frames of 1152 samples. It uses a hybrid filterbank (polyphase + MDCT) and a psychoacoustic model to allocate bits where they matter most. Constant bitrate (CBR) and variable bitrate (VBR) modes are available. At 192 kbps CBR, most listeners cannot distinguish MP3 from the original. Joint stereo encoding saves bits by exploiting channel correlation.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use MP3 when you need compatibility with external tools or pipelines that don't support Vorbis. For in-engine use, Vorbis is generally preferred. MP3 is fine for dialogue or music that ships as external files (e.g., mod support).

advanced.oneLiner: MP3 is a mature lossy codec with a hybrid polyphase/MDCT filterbank, well-understood artifacts, and near-universal hardware decoder support, though it lacks gapless playback without auxiliary metadata.
advanced.technicalNotes: MP3's 32-subband polyphase filterbank limits frequency resolution compared to pure MDCT codecs like Vorbis. The bit reservoir allows frames to borrow bits from neighbors, improving quality on transients but complicating seeking. Gapless playback requires encoder-specific metadata (LAME's --nogap or gapless info tag) since the standard mandates decoder delay padding. In Unity, MP3 is decoded to PCM on import; the engine stores it as Vorbis or PCM internally. Historical patent issues (expired 2017 in most jurisdictions) are no longer a concern for new projects.

vs.vorbis: Vorbis generally outperforms MP3 below 160 kbps and offers native gapless playback. MP3 has wider legacy support and hardware decoders but is no longer patent-advantaged.
vs.pcm: PCM provides zero decode cost but 10x+ larger files. MP3 is useful when distribution size matters more than decode performance.
vs.adpcm: ADPCM has much cheaper decoding and fixed compression ratio. MP3 offers better quality at higher compression ratios but costs more CPU.

unityAdapter.location: AudioClip Inspector > Format is auto-detected on import. Final compression is set in Compression Format dropdown.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioImporter.compressionFormat (MP3 imports are transcoded; final format is Vorbis or PCM)
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity transcodes MP3 to its internal format on import. You won't get bit-exact MP3 output at runtime. If you need exact MP3 playback, use a native plugin or stream from disk.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Importing an MP3, then exporting as Vorbis means double lossy encoding. Start from WAV/FLAC source files whenever possible to avoid generational quality loss.

misconceptions: 320 kbps MP3 is not 'lossless.' It is high-quality lossy. Some information is still discarded; it's just very hard to hear the difference.
MP3 patents have expired worldwide as of 2017. You can freely encode and decode MP3 in commercial projects without licensing fees.
MP3 does not inherently loop seamlessly. The encoder adds padding samples that cause a small gap unless you use gapless metadata or trim the decoded output.

name: Noise Gate

beginner.oneLiner: A noise gate silences audio when it drops below a set volume level, cutting out unwanted background noise between sounds.
beginner.analogy: Think of a noise gate like an automatic door that only opens when someone loud enough walks up. Quiet noises like background hum stay locked outside, but when the actual sound you want is loud enough, the door swings open and lets it through.
beginner.whatToDo: Use a noise gate to clean up recorded audio that has background hiss, hum, or room noise between the important sounds. Set the threshold just above the noise floor so quiet noise is silenced while your actual sounds pass through.

intermediate.oneLiner: A noise gate attenuates the signal when its level falls below a user-defined threshold, with attack, hold, release, and range parameters controlling the gating behavior.
intermediate.details: The threshold sets the level below which the gate closes. Attack controls how quickly the gate opens when the signal exceeds the threshold. Hold defines how long the gate stays open after the signal drops below the threshold. Release controls how quickly the gate closes after the hold time expires. Range (or floor) sets how much attenuation is applied when the gate is closed; setting it to -inf gives complete silence, while -20 dB gives a more natural reduction. A sidechain input can trigger the gate from a different signal than the one being gated.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use noise gates on recorded dialogue to remove room tone between lines, on drum recordings to isolate individual hits, or in game audio to clean up noisy source recordings. In a game mix, gates can also be used creatively as a rhythmic effect by sidechaining to a rhythmic source.

advanced.oneLiner: A noise gate applies signal-dependent attenuation using an envelope follower with hysteresis, transitioning between open and closed states based on threshold, attack, hold, and release parameters.
advanced.technicalNotes: The gate envelope follower tracks the signal level (peak or RMS) and compares it to the threshold. Hysteresis (separate open and close thresholds) prevents rapid on/off chattering when the signal hovers near the threshold. The state machine is: closed -> opening (attack) -> open -> holding -> closing (release) -> closed. In Unity, there is no built-in noise gate. Implement in OnAudioFilterRead by computing per-block RMS, comparing to threshold, and applying a smooth gain envelope. For sidechain gating, read from a secondary AudioSource buffer. Frequency-selective gating (using a bandpass filter on the sidechain) can improve accuracy by triggering only on specific frequency content.

vs.compressor: A compressor reduces the level of loud signals above a threshold. A noise gate reduces the level of quiet signals below a threshold. They are complementary dynamics tools.
vs.sidechain: Sidechain is a technique where an external signal controls a dynamics processor. A noise gate can use sidechain input, but sidechain itself is not an effect; it is a routing method.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in noise gate. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead or use middleware like FMOD/Wwise which provide gate effects.
unityAdapter.importTip: For a simple noise gate, compute the RMS level of each audio buffer block. If below the threshold, multiply all samples by the gate attenuation (0 for full gate, or a small value like 0.01 for a softer floor). Smooth the gain transitions over 5-10 ms to avoid clicks.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Setting the threshold too high will cut off quiet tails of sounds, causing unnatural abrupt endings. Always test with the actual audio content and add a generous hold time to let natural decay through.

misconceptions: A noise gate does not remove noise from the signal. It only silences the signal when the overall level is low enough. Noise that occurs during loud passages is still present.
Setting the gate threshold higher does not mean cleaner audio. If the threshold is too high, it will cut off the beginnings and endings of wanted sounds.
A noise gate is not a substitute for recording clean audio. It is a remedial tool, not a replacement for good recording practices.

name: Noise Types (White / Pink / Brown)

beginner.oneLiner: White, pink, and brown noise are different flavors of static-like sound, each with a different brightness, used as raw material for creating game audio effects.
beginner.analogy: Think of rain: white noise is like rain on a tin roof — bright and hissy with lots of high-frequency detail. Pink noise is like steady rain on a window — balanced and natural. Brown noise is like distant thunder rumble — deep and rumbly with very little high end. Each color describes how much high-frequency energy the noise contains.
beginner.whatToDo: Use noise as a starting ingredient. White noise filtered with a low-pass filter makes great wind, rain, and whoosh sounds. Pink noise works well for ambient room tone and ocean surf. Brown noise is excellent for rumble, thunder, and low-end impacts. Start with noise, then shape it with filters and envelopes.

intermediate.oneLiner: Noise colors describe the spectral slope: white noise has equal energy per frequency, pink noise falls off at 3 dB/octave, and brown (Brownian) noise falls off at 6 dB/octave.
intermediate.details: White noise has a flat power spectral density — equal power at every frequency. It sounds bright and hissy because higher octaves contain more individual frequencies. Pink noise (1/f) has equal power per octave, which matches human perception better and sounds natural and balanced. Brown noise (1/f-squared, also called red noise or Brownian noise) has a steep low-frequency emphasis and sounds like a deep rumble. Generation methods: white noise uses random number generation, pink noise can use the Voss-McCartney algorithm (summing multiple random sources at halving rates), brown noise integrates white noise (each sample = previous + random step).
intermediate.whenToUse: White noise: wind, rain, static, sci-fi thrusters, explosion tails, breath sounds. Pink noise: ambient backgrounds, ocean, balanced test signals, room tone. Brown noise: rumble, earthquake, thunder, engine idle, low-end sweetening for impacts. All noise types benefit from envelope shaping and filtering to create specific sound effects.

advanced.oneLiner: Noise colors are defined by their power spectral density slope: white (S(f)=const), pink (S(f)~1/f, -3dB/oct), and brown/Brownian (S(f)~1/f^2, -6dB/oct), each generated by different stochastic processes.
advanced.technicalNotes: White noise is generated via a PRNG with uniform or Gaussian distribution. Pink noise generation methods include: Voss-McCartney (summing octave-band random generators), Paul Kellet's first-order filter approximation (b0-b6 weighted sum), or spectral shaping in the frequency domain via IFFT. Brown noise is the integral of white noise: sample[n] = sample[n-1] + white[n] * scale, clamped to prevent DC drift. For game audio DSP in OnAudioFilterRead, generate noise per-sample and apply biquad filters for shaping. Blue noise (3dB/oct rising) and violet noise (6dB/oct rising) are less common but useful for dithering and high-frequency textures.

vs.waveform: Standard waveforms (sine, saw, square) are periodic and produce pitched sounds. Noise is aperiodic random signal with no definite pitch, making it ideal for unpitched textures and effects.
vs.reverb: Reverb tails naturally decay toward noise-like textures. You can approximate late reverb using shaped noise bursts, which is the basis of convolution reverb and some efficient reverb algorithms.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in noise generator. Generate procedurally in OnAudioFilterRead using System.Random or UnityEngine.Random (cached on the audio thread).
unityAdapter.importTip: For white noise: fill the buffer with random values between -1 and 1. For pink noise, apply the Paul Kellet filter approximation (6 feedback coefficients). For brown noise, accumulate white noise samples and scale down to prevent clipping.
unityAdapter.gotcha: UnityEngine.Random is not thread-safe and cannot be used on the audio thread. Use System.Random with a dedicated instance created on the audio thread, or pre-generate noise buffers on the main thread and read them from the audio thread.

misconceptions: White noise contains more frequencies than other noise types. All noise types contain all frequencies — they differ only in the relative energy distribution across those frequencies.
Brown noise is named after the color brown. It is actually named after Robert Brown and Brownian motion (random walk), though the color naming convention conveniently stuck.

name: Nyquist Theorem

beginner.oneLiner: The Nyquist theorem says you must sample audio at least twice as fast as the highest frequency you want to capture.
beginner.analogy: Imagine filming a spinning wheel. If your camera is too slow, the wheel looks like it spins backward (aliasing). Similarly, if you don't sample audio fast enough, high frequencies get mangled into weird artifacts. Sampling at 44,100 Hz captures up to ~20,000 Hz, which covers human hearing.
beginner.whatToDo: For most games, 44,100 Hz or 48,000 Hz sample rates are fine. Don't waste memory on 96 kHz assets unless you plan to pitch-shift sounds down significantly at runtime.

intermediate.oneLiner: The Nyquist-Shannon theorem states that a bandlimited signal can be perfectly reconstructed if sampled at more than twice its highest frequency component.
intermediate.details: The Nyquist frequency is half the sample rate. At 48 kHz, the Nyquist frequency is 24 kHz. Any frequency above the Nyquist frequency will alias, folding back into the audible range as distortion. Anti-aliasing filters in ADCs remove frequencies above Nyquist before sampling. When pitch-shifting audio up at runtime, you are effectively raising frequencies closer to (or past) the Nyquist limit, so source files with headroom above 20 kHz can sound cleaner when shifted.
intermediate.whenToUse: Choose your project sample rate early. 48 kHz is standard for games and matches most platform audio pipelines. If you pitch sounds down at runtime (slow-motion effects), record or generate at higher sample rates so the shifted-down content retains detail.

advanced.oneLiner: Nyquist defines the theoretical upper frequency limit of a discrete-time system; practical reconstruction relies on anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters with finite roll-off.
advanced.technicalNotes: Real-world ADCs and DACs use oversampling (e.g., 8x) combined with steep digital filters to approximate an ideal brick-wall filter at the Nyquist frequency. In game audio, sample rate conversion (SRC) when loading mismatched assets incurs CPU cost and potential quality loss. Unity's AudioClip resamples at import time to match the project sample rate set in AudioSettings. Aliasing in procedural audio (synthesis) is a common issue; naive waveform generation (e.g., a raw sawtooth) produces aliasing harmonics that must be suppressed via band-limited techniques like PolyBLEP or wavetable synthesis.

vs.oversampling: Oversampling processes audio at a multiple of the target rate internally, then downsamples, reducing aliasing artifacts in nonlinear processing like distortion effects.
vs.bit-depth: Sample rate determines frequency range; bit depth determines dynamic range (noise floor). They are independent axes of digital audio quality.

unityAdapter.location: Project Settings > Audio > System Sample Rate; per-clip settings in the AudioClip import inspector.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set the project sample rate in AudioSettings to match your target platform (usually 48000). In the AudioClip importer, use 'Preserve Sample Rate' to avoid unnecessary resampling if your source files already match.
unityAdapter.gotcha: If your source audio is 44.1 kHz but the project runs at 48 kHz, Unity resamples at import. This is usually fine, but for procedural audio in OnAudioFilterRead, always read AudioSettings.outputSampleRate to know the actual rate rather than assuming 44100.

misconceptions: Higher sample rates always sound better in games. In practice, 48 kHz is sufficient for playback. Higher rates increase memory and CPU cost with no audible benefit for most game audio.
Aliasing only matters for music production. Procedural audio and real-time synthesis in games are just as susceptible to aliasing artifacts.

name: Occlusion

beginner.oneLiner: Occlusion is the muffling of sound when something solid like a wall or door is between you and the sound source.
beginner.analogy: When someone talks to you from the next room with the door closed, you can still hear them but their voice sounds muffled — the high frequencies are blocked by the wall while the low rumble passes through. Audio occlusion in games recreates this by detecting walls between the player and a sound and muffling it accordingly.
beginner.whatToDo: Implement occlusion by raycasting between the listener and sound sources. When geometry blocks the line of sight, apply a low-pass filter and reduce volume on that source. Many middleware solutions (FMOD, Wwise) have built-in occlusion systems.

intermediate.oneLiner: Occlusion simulates the frequency-dependent attenuation of sound passing through solid objects by detecting geometry between the source and listener and applying filtering accordingly.
intermediate.details: A basic occlusion system raycasts from the AudioListener to each AudioSource. If the ray hits geometry, apply a low-pass filter (cutoff 500-2000 Hz depending on material thickness) and volume reduction. More advanced systems use multiple rays for partial occlusion, differentiate materials (glass vs concrete vs wood), and distinguish between occlusion (source behind geometry) and obstruction (geometry partially between source and listener). Portals and openings (doors, windows) allow sound to propagate around corners with less filtering.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use occlusion whenever your game has rooms, walls, or solid barriers that should affect how sound travels. It is essential for immersive first-person games, horror games (hearing enemies through walls), tactical games (locating enemies by sound), and any game where spatial audio fidelity matters.

advanced.oneLiner: Occlusion systems model transmission loss through geometry using raycast-based line-of-sight checks, material-dependent frequency absorption coefficients, and diffraction around edges to produce filtered and attenuated audio that reflects realistic sound propagation.
advanced.technicalNotes: Simple single-ray occlusion creates binary (fully occluded or not) behavior. Use multiple rays spread in a cone or sphere for smoother partial occlusion. For physically-based occlusion, assign transmission loss coefficients per material (e.g., drywall: -10 dB broadband + gentle LPF; concrete: -40 dB + aggressive LPF; glass: -20 dB + moderate LPF). Diffraction (sound bending around edges) can be approximated with portal-based propagation or edge-detection algorithms. Steam Audio, Resonance Audio, and middleware like Wwise Spatial Audio provide built-in occlusion and diffraction. Performance tip: stagger occlusion raycasts across frames and cache results, updating each source every 2-4 frames rather than every frame.

vs.attenuation: Attenuation reduces volume purely based on distance. Occlusion reduces volume and applies filtering based on physical barriers between source and listener. A close sound behind a thick wall may be heavily occluded but has low distance attenuation.
vs.low-pass-filter: A low-pass filter is the primary tool for implementing occlusion, but occlusion is the design concept encompassing geometry detection, material properties, volume reduction, and potentially diffraction. The low-pass filter is one component of a complete occlusion system.
vs.reverb: Reverb simulates room reflections. Occlusion simulates transmission through barriers. They interact: you might hear reverb from an adjacent room leaking through a wall even when direct sound is occluded.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have a built-in occlusion system. Implement manually using Physics.Raycast between AudioListener and AudioSource, then control AudioLowPassFilter.cutoffFrequency and AudioSource.volume. Alternatively, use Steam Audio, Resonance Audio, or FMOD/Wwise plugins.
unityAdapter.importTip: For a basic setup: on each AudioSource, add an AudioLowPassFilter. In a script, raycast from the listener to the source each frame. If the ray hits a collider, lerp the cutoff frequency down (e.g., to 800 Hz) and reduce volume. If line of sight is clear, lerp back to 22000 Hz and full volume. Use LayerMasks to control which geometry occludes sound.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Raycasting every AudioSource every frame is expensive with many sources. Stagger checks across frames, prioritize nearby and audible sources, and use a maximum occlusion check distance. Also, single-ray occlusion gives harsh binary results — use 3-5 rays with averaged results for smoother transitions. Ensure your occluding geometry has colliders on the correct layers.

misconceptions: Unity has built-in audio occlusion — it does not. You must implement it yourself via raycasts and filters, or use third-party solutions like Steam Audio or FMOD.

Occlusion only needs to reduce volume — real occlusion is frequency-dependent. Walls block high frequencies more than low frequencies, so filtering (low-pass) is essential for realism, not just volume reduction.

A single raycast is enough for good occlusion — single rays produce binary results (fully blocked or fully clear). Multiple rays provide smoother partial occlusion when a source is near a doorway or corner.

name: OGG

beginner.oneLiner: OGG is a compressed audio format that dramatically reduces file size with only a small loss in quality — ideal for game music and longer sounds.
beginner.analogy: If WAV is a raw photo, OGG is a high-quality JPEG — you lose a tiny bit of detail, but the file is way smaller and most people cannot tell the difference.
beginner.whatToDo: Use OGG for music tracks, ambient loops, and dialogue. Keep your original WAV masters and export to OGG for the build.

intermediate.oneLiner: OGG is a free, open-source container format typically carrying Vorbis-encoded lossy audio, offering excellent quality-to-size ratios for game audio.
intermediate.details: OGG Vorbis at quality level 5 (~160 kbps) is roughly comparable to MP3 at 192 kbps but with better handling of transients and stereo imaging. A 3-minute stereo track at quality 5 is around 3.5 MB versus 30 MB as WAV. OGG supports arbitrary sample rates and channel counts. Unlike MP3, OGG has no licensing fees, making it the standard compressed format in game engines.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use OGG for music, ambience, voice-over, and any audio longer than a few seconds. For very short SFX (under 1 second), uncompressed formats may be preferable to avoid decode latency.

advanced.oneLiner: OGG Vorbis is a patent-free lossy codec with variable bitrate encoding, offering transparent quality around 160-192 kbps for most game audio content.
advanced.technicalNotes: Vorbis uses an MDCT-based approach with flexible block sizes (64-8192 samples). It supports gapless playback natively, which is critical for seamless music loops — no encoder delay padding like MP3. Quality settings range from -1 to 10, mapping roughly to 45-500 kbps VBR. Unity uses a quality slider (0-100%) when Vorbis compression is selected. On mobile, decoding Vorbis is more CPU-intensive than ADPCM; profile if you have many simultaneous streams.

vs.wav: WAV is lossless but 5-10x larger. Use WAV as source format and OGG for distribution. Short SFX may benefit from WAV/ADPCM to avoid decode overhead.
vs.aiff: Both AIFF and WAV are uncompressed alternatives. OGG replaces both for size-sensitive distribution. Keep AIFF/WAV as masters.

unityAdapter.location: Import .ogg files into Assets. Unity decodes and re-encodes based on import settings in the Audio Clip Inspector.
unityAdapter.importTip: For music, set Load Type to 'Streaming' and Compression Format to 'Vorbis'. This keeps memory usage minimal since only a small buffer is loaded at a time.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Even if you import an OGG file, Unity may re-encode it. Importing a pre-compressed OGG and then setting Vorbis compression in Unity means double lossy encoding — quality degrades. Import WAV sources and let Unity handle the Vorbis encoding.

misconceptions: OGG is not a codec — it is a container format. The audio codec inside is usually Vorbis (sometimes Opus). Saying 'OGG Vorbis' is more precise.

Lossy compression does not mean bad quality. At reasonable bitrates (128+ kbps), most listeners cannot distinguish OGG Vorbis from the uncompressed original in a game context with background noise and sound effects.

name: Ogg Vorbis

beginner.oneLiner: Ogg Vorbis is a free, open-source audio format that shrinks file sizes by removing sounds humans can barely hear.
beginner.analogy: Think of it like a JPEG for audio: it makes the file much smaller, and most people can't tell the difference from the original, but some tiny details are lost.
beginner.whatToDo: Use Vorbis for music, ambient loops, and longer sound effects in your game. It saves a lot of memory compared to uncompressed audio, and the quality loss is minimal.

intermediate.oneLiner: Vorbis is a patent-free lossy audio codec that offers quality comparable to or better than MP3 at equivalent bitrates, with variable bitrate encoding by default.
intermediate.details: Vorbis uses a modified discrete cosine transform (MDCT) and psychoacoustic modeling to discard inaudible frequencies. Quality is controlled via a quality slider (q-1 to q10) rather than a fixed bitrate. At q5 (~160 kbps), it is perceptually transparent for most game audio. The Ogg container adds negligible overhead. Vorbis supports sample rates up to 192 kHz and arbitrary channel counts.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use Vorbis for streamed audio like music tracks, dialogue, and long ambient loops. Avoid it for very short one-shot sounds (< 1 second) where the decode overhead is not worth the size savings. On mobile, be aware of CPU cost for many simultaneous Vorbis streams.

advanced.oneLiner: Vorbis is an MDCT-based lossy codec with floor/residue encoding, offering efficient variable-bitrate compression that is fully royalty-free and widely supported in game engines.
advanced.technicalNotes: Vorbis splits frames into long (2048) and short (256) MDCT windows, chosen adaptively for transient vs. tonal content. The spectral floor is encoded with a piecewise curve, then residues are vector-quantized. Decoding requires an inverse MDCT per channel per frame. In Unity, Vorbis clips are decoded on-the-fly when set to Streaming or Compressed In Memory, which uses less RAM but more CPU than decompressing on load. For mobile titles with many concurrent voices, profile CPU usage carefully. Vorbis latency is higher than ADPCM due to larger frame sizes, making it less suitable for latency-critical one-shots.

vs.mp3: Vorbis generally achieves better quality at the same bitrate, is fully royalty-free, and supports gapless playback natively. MP3 has wider hardware decoder support but is patent-encumbered in some contexts.
vs.adpcm: ADPCM has near-zero decode cost and fixed 3.5:1 compression but lower quality. Vorbis offers 10:1+ compression with much better quality but higher CPU cost.
vs.pcm: PCM is uncompressed and zero-cost to decode, but files are 5-15x larger. Vorbis is preferable when memory is constrained and the sound is long enough to justify the decode overhead.

unityAdapter.location: Select an AudioClip asset in the Inspector > Compression Format dropdown
unityAdapter.importTip: Set Quality between 50-70% for most game audio. For background music, 50% is usually transparent. Use 'Streaming' load type for tracks longer than 10 seconds to reduce memory.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Setting load type to 'Decompress On Load' with Vorbis defeats the purpose: you pay the full PCM memory cost plus decode time at load. Use 'Compressed In Memory' or 'Streaming' instead.

misconceptions: Vorbis is not the same as Opus. Opus is a newer codec from the same community but uses different technology and is optimized for low-latency speech and music.
Ogg is the container format, not the codec. You could theoretically put other codecs inside an Ogg container.
Vorbis quality does not degrade each time you play it. Re-encoding a Vorbis file repeatedly will degrade quality, but playback is lossless from the compressed data.

name: One-Shot Sound

beginner.oneLiner: A one-shot sound is an audio clip that plays once from start to finish when triggered, then stops — like a gunshot, button click, or jump sound.
beginner.analogy: A one-shot is like snapping your fingers — one quick action, one sound, done. It does not repeat or loop. You trigger it, it plays through, and it is finished. Most sound effects in games are one-shots: explosions, footsteps, pickups, UI clicks, and hit sounds.
beginner.whatToDo: When adding sound effects to game events, use one-shots for discrete actions: button presses, jumps, hits, pickups, and any momentary event. Make sure to trim silence from the beginning and end of your audio files so there is no delay between the trigger and when you hear the sound.

intermediate.oneLiner: A one-shot is a non-looping audio event triggered by a discrete game action, typically short in duration, played independently of other sounds, and discarded after completion.
intermediate.details: One-shots are the most common type of game sound. They are fire-and-forget: once triggered, they play to completion without needing ongoing management. Key considerations: file length should be trimmed tightly (no leading/trailing silence), multiple variations should exist for frequently triggered sounds (see: variation), polyphony limits must be managed for rapid-fire events (e.g., machine gun), and priority settings determine which one-shots survive when the voice limit is reached. One-shots can overlap (multiple playing simultaneously) or be set to steal/retrigger (new trigger stops the previous instance).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use one-shots for: UI interactions (clicks, hovers, confirmations), weapon fire and impacts, character actions (jump, land, attack), pickups and collectibles, environmental events (doors, switches, breakables), and any other discrete event. Use looping audio instead when the sound needs to sustain for an unknown duration (engines, ambience, alarms).

advanced.oneLiner: A one-shot is a stateless audio event that consumes a voice from the engine's voice pool, plays a non-looping buffer to completion, and then releases its voice — requiring careful polyphony management and voice-stealing policies in dense soundscapes.
advanced.technicalNotes: In Unity, PlayOneShot is the standard one-shot trigger. It allocates a voice, plays the clip without interrupting other sounds on the same AudioSource, and auto-releases on completion. However, PlayOneShot provides no handle for runtime control (cannot stop, fade, or query progress). For controllable one-shots, use AudioSource.Play() on a dedicated AudioSource with loop=false. Voice management is critical: Unity defaults to 32 real voices and 256 virtual voices. Dense one-shot events (rain drops, crowd, rapid fire) can exhaust real voices, causing priority-based stealing or silent sounds. Use AudioSource.priority (0=highest, 256=lowest) and group one-shots on Audio Mixer groups with appropriate volume attenuation to manage density.

vs.looping: Looping audio repeats continuously until stopped, suitable for sustained sounds. One-shots play once and stop, suitable for discrete events. Some sounds need both: a looping engine hum with one-shot gear-shift sounds.
vs.sound-pool: A sound pool manages multiple variations of the same one-shot event (e.g., 5 different footstep sounds). The pool handles selection logic (random, round-robin, no-repeat) while each individual play is still a one-shot.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource.PlayOneShot(clip, volumeScale) plays a clip as a one-shot. Alternatively, assign the clip to an AudioSource and call Play() with loop set to false.
unityAdapter.importTip: For one-shots under 200ms (clicks, hits), use 'Decompress On Load' with uncompressed PCM or ADPCM to minimize playback latency. For longer one-shots (explosions, voice lines), use 'Compressed In Memory' with Vorbis to save RAM.
unityAdapter.gotcha: PlayOneShot cannot be stopped once triggered — there is no handle returned. If you need to stop a one-shot (e.g., canceling a long sound effect), use a separate AudioSource with Play()/Stop() instead. Also, PlayOneShot does not respect the AudioSource's pitch and spatialBlend changes made after the call.

misconceptions: One-shots are always short sounds. A one-shot can be any length — a 30-second explosion tail or a 2-minute voice line is still a one-shot if it plays once and does not loop.

You only need one version of each one-shot. Using a single sound for repeated events (footsteps, gunfire) creates the machine-gun effect and sounds robotic. Always create 3-5+ variations for frequently triggered one-shots.

name: Opus

beginner.oneLiner: Opus is a modern audio codec that compresses sound very efficiently, delivering excellent quality at small file sizes, especially for voice and music.
beginner.analogy: If audio codecs were shipping methods, MP3 would be standard ground shipping (gets the job done, has been around forever) and Opus would be a modern courier service that is faster, cheaper, and handles every package size well, from tiny letters (voice chat) to heavy boxes (full music).
beginner.whatToDo: Consider Opus for any audio that needs to be small or streamed, especially voice chat or dialogue. It offers better quality than MP3 at the same file size. Check your target platform's support first, as not all game engines have built-in Opus decoding.

intermediate.oneLiner: Opus is an open, royalty-free lossy audio codec standardized by the IETF, combining SILK (optimized for speech) and CELT (optimized for music) to deliver state-of-the-art compression across all bitrates from 6 kbps to 510 kbps.
intermediate.details: Opus is the successor to both Vorbis (music) and Speex (voice). It supports sample rates from 8 kHz to 48 kHz, mono and stereo, and can dynamically switch between its speech and music modes within a single stream. At low bitrates (16-32 kbps) it dramatically outperforms MP3 and AAC for voice. At music bitrates (96-128 kbps) it is competitive with or better than AAC. Opus has very low algorithmic latency (as low as 2.5 ms), making it ideal for real-time communication. It is typically wrapped in an Ogg container (.opus or .ogg).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use Opus for voice chat systems, dialogue lines when file size matters, and streaming audio. It is the default codec for WebRTC, Discord, and many VoIP applications. For game assets, use it when you need smaller files than Vorbis provides at equivalent quality, and your platform supports it.

advanced.oneLiner: Opus is an IETF-standardized (RFC 6716) hybrid codec combining a linear prediction model (SILK) for speech and an MDCT-based transform codec (CELT) for general audio, with seamless bandwidth and mode transitions.
advanced.technicalNotes: Opus operates in three modes: SILK-only (voice, low bitrate), CELT-only (music/general, higher bitrate), and hybrid (both layers for wideband speech). It supports CBR, VBR, and constrained-VBR. Frame sizes range from 2.5 ms to 60 ms. The codec handles packet loss gracefully with built-in forward error correction (FEC). For game integration, libopus is the reference C library and is straightforward to integrate via native plugins. Unity does not natively decode Opus, so you need a plugin (such as NativeAudio or a custom wrapper around libopus). Unreal has Opus support built-in for voice chat. Wwise supports Opus as an encoding option.

vs.vorbis: Vorbis is older and widely supported in game engines. Opus outperforms Vorbis at all bitrates, especially below 96 kbps. For new projects without legacy constraints, Opus is the better choice if platform support exists.
vs.mp3: MP3 is universally supported but technically inferior and patent-encumbered (though patents have expired). Opus delivers better quality at every bitrate and is royalty-free.
vs.aac: AAC is comparable to Opus at high bitrates for music but requires licensing in some contexts. Opus is royalty-free and significantly better for speech and low-bitrate applications.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not natively support Opus decoding. You need a third-party plugin or native audio library to decode Opus files at runtime.
unityAdapter.apiRef: No built-in Unity API for Opus. Use a native plugin wrapping libopus, or use the NativeAudio asset, or pre-convert Opus files to Vorbis/WAV during your build pipeline.
unityAdapter.importTip: If targeting WebGL or mobile voice chat, consider integrating libopus via a native plugin. For offline assets, you can author in Opus and convert to Vorbis for Unity import using ffmpeg in a build script.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Dragging an .opus file into Unity will not import it as an AudioClip. Unity's importer does not recognize the Opus format. You must either use a plugin or convert to a supported format before import.

misconceptions: Opus is only for voice chat. While Opus excels at speech, its CELT mode handles music and general audio as well as or better than most other codecs.
Opus is not widely supported. Opus is the standard codec for WebRTC, used by Discord, Teams, and all major browsers. Engine support is the main gap in gamedev, not platform support.
You need to pay royalties to use Opus. Opus is completely royalty-free and open source under a BSD license.

name: Oscillator

beginner.oneLiner: An oscillator is the basic sound generator in a synthesizer, producing a repeating waveform like a sine, sawtooth, square, or triangle wave.
beginner.analogy: An oscillator is like a hummingbird's wings: it vibrates back and forth at a steady rate, producing a continuous tone. Different wing shapes (waveforms) produce different tones, from smooth and pure (sine) to buzzy and bright (sawtooth).
beginner.whatToDo: When building synthesized sounds, start with an oscillator to generate your raw tone. Choose a sine wave for pure, simple tones; a sawtooth for bright, rich sounds; a square wave for hollow, retro tones; or a triangle wave for soft, mellow sounds.

intermediate.oneLiner: An oscillator generates a periodic waveform at a specified frequency using a phase accumulator, with the waveform shape determining the harmonic content of the output signal.
intermediate.details: The core waveforms and their harmonic content: Sine has only the fundamental frequency (no harmonics). Sawtooth contains all harmonics with amplitudes proportional to 1/n, giving a bright, buzzy tone. Square wave contains only odd harmonics at 1/n, producing a hollow, clarinet-like tone. Triangle wave has only odd harmonics at 1/n^2, creating a soft, mellow sound. Pulse waves (variable duty cycle square waves) shift harmonic emphasis based on the pulse width. Most synthesizers combine multiple oscillators with detuning for a thicker sound. The oscillator's frequency is typically controlled by MIDI note input, pitch envelopes, and modulation sources.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use oscillators as the foundation of any synthesized sound in your game. Combine two or more detuned sawtooth oscillators for thick leads and basses. Use sine oscillators for clean sub-bass or test tones. Use square/pulse waves for retro chiptune aesthetics. In procedural game audio, drive oscillator frequency with game parameters for reactive sound effects.

advanced.oneLiner: An oscillator implements a phase accumulator incremented by (frequency / sampleRate) per sample, with the phase value mapped to amplitude via a waveform function, requiring band-limiting techniques to prevent aliasing.
advanced.technicalNotes: The phase accumulator wraps at 1.0 (or 2*pi). Naive waveform generation (e.g., direct sawtooth: y = 2*phase - 1) produces aliasing because the discontinuities contain infinite harmonics above Nyquist. Band-limiting solutions: polyBLEP (polynomial band-limited step) adds correction terms near discontinuities, is cheap and effective. BLIT (band-limited impulse train) integrates a sinc-based impulse train. Wavetable oscillators use pre-computed mip-mapped tables. In Unity's OnAudioFilterRead, use a double-precision phase accumulator to avoid pitch drift over long playback times. For per-sample processing at 48 kHz with multiple oscillators, polyBLEP offers the best quality-to-CPU ratio. Hard sync, where one oscillator resets another's phase, creates additional harmonics useful for aggressive timbres.

vs.lfo: An LFO is an oscillator running at sub-audio frequencies (0.1-20 Hz) used for modulation rather than direct audio output. A standard oscillator runs at audio frequencies (20 Hz - 20 kHz) and produces audible sound.
vs.waveform: A waveform is the shape of the signal over one cycle. An oscillator is the component that generates and outputs that waveform continuously at a specified frequency.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in oscillator component. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead or use third-party synth plugins.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a simple oscillator: maintain a double-precision phase variable, increment by (frequency / AudioSettings.outputSampleRate) per sample, wrap at 1.0, and map to your waveform. Add polyBLEP anti-aliasing for sawtooth and square waves. Attach the script to a GameObject with an AudioSource to route through Unity's audio pipeline.
unityAdapter.gotcha: OnAudioFilterRead runs on the audio thread, not the main thread. Do not call Unity APIs (except AudioSettings) or allocate memory inside this callback. Use thread-safe variables to communicate parameter changes from the main thread.

misconceptions: An oscillator does not produce music by itself. It generates a raw tone that must be shaped by filters, envelopes, and effects to become a usable sound.

A sawtooth wave is not inherently better than a sine wave. Each waveform has a different harmonic profile suited to different purposes. Sine is ideal for pure tones; sawtooth is ideal for rich, filterable sounds.

Digital oscillators are not inferior to analog ones. While analog oscillators have natural imperfections that some find pleasing, digital oscillators can accurately model these characteristics and offer perfect stability and reproducibility.

name: Overlap-Add Method

beginner.oneLiner: Overlap-add is a technique for smoothly reassembling audio after it has been chopped into overlapping pieces for processing, so you do not hear gaps or clicks.
beginner.analogy: Imagine painting a long wall by having several painters each paint overlapping sections. Where their sections overlap, the paint blends together seamlessly. If they each painted non-overlapping sections, you would see visible seams. Overlap-add works the same way — audio chunks overlap so when they are added back together, the transitions are smooth and invisible.
beginner.whatToDo: You typically will not implement overlap-add yourself unless you are building custom spectral effects. But knowing it exists helps you understand why FFT-based effects like pitch shifting and convolution reverb have inherent latency — they need to collect and overlap multiple chunks of audio.

intermediate.oneLiner: Overlap-add is a method for reconstructing a continuous audio signal from overlapping windowed frames by summing the processed frames at their correct time positions, ensuring smooth transitions and artifact-free output.
intermediate.details: The process: divide the input into overlapping frames (e.g., 1024 samples with 75% overlap, meaning each frame advances by 256 samples). Apply a window function to each frame. Process the frame (typically FFT, modify, IFFT). Place each processed frame at its original position in the output and add (sum) the overlapping portions. If the window and overlap satisfy the Constant Overlap-Add (COLA) condition, the windowing artifacts cancel out during summation, and the output is a perfect reconstruction (minus any intentional modifications). The overlap amount is typically 50% or 75% — more overlap means smoother transitions but more processing per second of audio.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use overlap-add whenever you do STFT-based processing: convolution reverb, spectral filtering, vocoder effects, pitch shifting, time stretching. The frame size determines your frequency resolution and minimum latency. Common setup: 1024-sample frames, 75% overlap (256-sample hop), Hann window. This gives ~21 ms resolution at 48 kHz with ~21 ms minimum latency.

advanced.oneLiner: Overlap-add reconstructs time-domain output from STFT frames by accumulating hop-aligned windowed IFFT outputs into a circular output buffer, with the COLA constraint ensuring that the sum of analysis windows equals unity across all time positions.
advanced.technicalNotes: Implementation: maintain a circular output accumulation buffer. For each frame: window input, FFT, process bins, IFFT, window again (for analysis-synthesis with modified magnitudes), accumulate into output buffer at the current write position, advance write position by hop size. The COLA condition for Hann window: 50% overlap (hop = N/2) yields constant sum of 1.0. At 75% overlap (hop = N/4), the sum is 1.5, requiring normalization. Overlap-save is an alternative that avoids windowing overhead by using circular convolution properties — it processes N-point FFTs on L+N-1 input samples and discards the aliased portion. Overlap-save is more efficient for FIR filtering (convolution reverb), while overlap-add is more flexible for general spectral modifications. Latency for overlap-add is at least one full frame (N samples) plus algorithmic delay. For real-time game audio, keep N small (512-2048) to limit latency.

vs.fft: FFT converts a single frame to frequency domain. Overlap-add is the framework for applying FFT processing across a continuous audio stream by managing how frames are segmented, processed, and reassembled.
vs.windowing: The window function shapes each frame before FFT. Overlap-add is the complementary process that reconstructs the output so that the windowing is transparent. They are two halves of the STFT pipeline.

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not provide a built-in overlap-add API. If you need STFT processing, implement it in OnAudioFilterRead or in a native audio plugin. Unity's built-in effects (reverb, EQ) handle this internally.
unityAdapter.apiRef: OnAudioFilterRead(float[] data, int channels) for custom STFT processing. For production use, consider a native plugin using the AudioPluginInterface SDK.
unityAdapter.importTip: If you are building STFT effects in OnAudioFilterRead, pre-allocate all your buffers (input ring buffer, FFT scratch space, output accumulation buffer) in Awake or Start. The callback buffer size may differ from your FFT size — use a ring buffer to collect enough input samples before processing each frame.
unityAdapter.gotcha: OnAudioFilterRead gives you the engine's buffer size (e.g., 512 samples), but your FFT size might be different (e.g., 2048). You need to buffer incoming samples until you have a full frame, process it, then output from your overlap-add accumulation buffer at the callback's rate. This mismatch is the most common source of bugs in custom STFT code.

misconceptions: Overlap-add is just mixing audio together — it is a precise mathematical technique that requires correct window functions and overlap amounts to achieve artifact-free reconstruction. Random overlapping will produce amplitude modulation artifacts.

More overlap is always better — higher overlap (75% vs 50%) gives smoother transitions but multiplies the processing cost. Each additional overlap means another FFT/IFFT per hop. Balance quality against CPU budget.

Overlap-add and overlap-save are the same — they are different algorithms for frequency-domain processing. Overlap-add accumulates windowed IFFT outputs; overlap-save uses circular convolution and discards aliased samples. They produce equivalent results but differ in implementation.

name: Overtones

beginner.oneLiner: Overtones are all the frequencies in a sound that are higher than its main pitch, and they determine why different instruments sound different even playing the same note.
beginner.analogy: Think of a rainbow. White sunlight looks like a single thing, but it actually contains all the colors layered together. Overtones are the hidden colors inside a sound — you hear one note, but that note is actually a blend of many higher frequencies stacked on top of each other.
beginner.whatToDo: Pay attention to the brightness or dullness of your game sounds. Bright, sharp sounds like swords have strong overtones. Soft, muffled sounds like footsteps on carpet have weak overtones. A low-pass filter removes overtones to make sounds darker and more distant.

intermediate.oneLiner: Overtones are the frequency components above the fundamental that shape a sound's timbre, numbered from the first frequency above the fundamental upward.
intermediate.details: The first overtone is the second harmonic (2x the fundamental), the second overtone is the third harmonic (3x), and so on. Harmonic overtones (integer multiples of the fundamental) produce pitched, musical sounds. Inharmonic overtones (non-integer multiples) produce metallic, bell-like, or noisy tones. A sound's overtone profile is its spectral fingerprint — it is what makes a trumpet sound different from a flute at the same pitch. Filters, EQ, and distortion all work by modifying the overtone content.
intermediate.whenToUse: Consider overtone content when choosing sounds for your game. Metallic impacts need strong inharmonic overtones. Warm, inviting UI tones need controlled harmonic overtones. Use low-pass filters to reduce overtones for distant or muffled sounds, and high-pass or presence boosts to emphasize them for clarity and aggression.

advanced.oneLiner: Overtones are spectral components above f0; when harmonic (integer multiples of f0), they form a harmonic series that enables pitch perception, and when inharmonic, they produce complex timbres with ambiguous pitch.
advanced.technicalNotes: Stiff vibrating bodies (bars, bells, plates) produce inharmonic overtones because the restoring force includes a stiffness term that shifts partial frequencies above the ideal harmonic series. The degree of inharmonicity affects pitch perception: the auditory system uses a template-matching mechanism that tolerates small deviations from integer ratios but fails for highly inharmonic spectra. In sound design, adding subtle inharmonicity (e.g., slightly detuned additive partials) makes synthetic sounds feel more organic. Psychoacoustically, the ear weights lower overtones more heavily for timbre discrimination, so the first 6-8 overtones are the most perceptually important.

vs.harmonics: Harmonics include the fundamental as the first harmonic, while overtones start counting from the first frequency above the fundamental. The second harmonic equals the first overtone.
vs.fundamental-frequency: The fundamental is the lowest frequency component that defines perceived pitch. Overtones are everything above it that defines tonal character.

unityAdapter.location: Overtone content is visible in FFT analysis via AudioSource.GetSpectrumData(). Shaping overtones requires custom DSP in OnAudioFilterRead or using AudioLowPassFilter/AudioHighPassFilter to attenuate high-frequency content.
unityAdapter.importTip: To darken a sound (reduce overtones) at runtime, attach an AudioLowPassFilter and lower the cutoffFrequency. This is useful for occlusion (muffling sounds behind walls) or distance-based rolloff enhancement.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's built-in AudioLowPassFilter has limited slope (one resonant filter). For steeper overtone reduction, chain multiple filters or implement a biquad filter in OnAudioFilterRead.

misconceptions: Overtones and harmonics are completely different things. They describe the same frequency components with different numbering conventions — the Nth overtone is the (N+1)th harmonic.
Removing all overtones makes a sound cleaner. Removing overtones reduces a sound to a featureless sine wave, stripping it of all character and making it harder to distinguish.

name: Panning Law

beginner.oneLiner: A panning law is the rule that determines how volume is distributed between left and right speakers as you move a sound across the stereo field.
beginner.analogy: Imagine walking with a flashlight between two walls. A panning law decides whether the light on one wall gets brighter at the same rate the other wall gets darker — or whether there is a slight overall brightness boost when pointing at both walls equally. Different laws produce different loudness behaviors as the sound moves from left to center to right.
beginner.whatToDo: Most game engines default to a sensible panning law. If a sound seems to get louder or quieter as it passes through the center of the stereo field, your panning law may need adjustment. Constant power panning (the most common law) keeps the perceived loudness even across the pan.

intermediate.oneLiner: A panning law defines the mathematical relationship between pan position and the gain applied to the left and right output channels, compensating for the psychoacoustic summing that occurs when both speakers are active.
intermediate.details: When a sound plays equally from both speakers (center pan), the listener perceives it as roughly 3 dB louder than when it plays from one speaker alone, because two coherent sources sum acoustically. A -3 dB panning law reduces the center level by 3 dB to compensate, keeping perceived loudness constant across the pan range. Other common laws include -4.5 dB (a compromise) and -6 dB (for fully correlated signals). Linear panning applies no compensation and causes an audible volume bump at center.
intermediate.whenToUse: Choose a panning law at project start and keep it consistent. For most game audio, -3 dB constant power panning works well. If you hear a volume bump when sounds pass through center, try -4.5 dB or -6 dB. In middleware, the panning law is usually a project-wide setting.

advanced.oneLiner: Panning laws specify the gain curves G_L(theta) and G_R(theta) as functions of pan angle, where the law's attenuation at center compensates for coherent acoustic summation and varies from -3 dB (power-preserving) to -6 dB (amplitude-preserving) depending on signal correlation assumptions.
advanced.technicalNotes: The -3 dB law assumes uncorrelated signals (noise-like), preserving total power: G_L^2 + G_R^2 = constant. The -6 dB law assumes fully correlated signals, preserving total amplitude: G_L + G_R = constant. Real game audio is a mix of both, so -4.5 dB is a practical compromise. In surround/spatial audio contexts, panning laws extend to VBAP (Vector Base Amplitude Panning) for arbitrary speaker layouts, where gain vectors are calculated from the angle between the virtual source and each speaker pair. For binaural/HRTF rendering, traditional panning laws are bypassed entirely in favor of impulse-response-based spatialization.

vs.constant-power-pan: Constant power panning is a specific implementation of the -3 dB panning law using sine/cosine curves. The term 'panning law' is the broader concept that encompasses all such gain-vs-position relationships.
vs.spatialization: Panning laws handle simple left/right placement. Spatialization systems handle full 3D positioning including distance attenuation, elevation, HRTF, and environmental effects.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > Spatial Blend and Stereo Pan properties. For 2D sounds (Spatial Blend = 0), the pan slider uses Unity's built-in panning law.
unityAdapter.importTip: For 2D UI sounds, use panStereo to position them slightly off-center for variety. For 3D world sounds, spatialization handles panning automatically — do not manually set panStereo on spatialized sources.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's panStereo on a 3D spatialized source conflicts with the spatializer's panning. Leave panStereo at 0 for any source with Spatial Blend > 0 to avoid unexpected level changes.

misconceptions: Panning law does not matter for game audio — it affects every stereo sound in your game. The wrong law causes audible volume bumps or dips as sounds move across the stereo field.
All panning laws sound the same — the difference between linear, -3 dB, and -6 dB is clearly audible, especially on sounds that pan continuously (like moving objects).
Panning law only applies to stereo — while most relevant for stereo, the concept extends to multi-channel panning via VBAP and similar algorithms.

name: Parametric EQ

beginner.oneLiner: A parametric EQ gives you precise control over exactly which frequencies to boost or cut, how much to change them, and how wide the adjustment is.
beginner.analogy: If a regular EQ is like adjusting the bass and treble on your stereo, a parametric EQ is like having a magnifying glass that you can point at any specific frequency, zoom in or out, and then turn it up or down. You choose exactly where to focus.
beginner.whatToDo: Use a parametric EQ when you need to surgically remove a specific annoying frequency from a sound, or to precisely boost a characteristic that makes a sound shine. Most DAWs and middleware tools provide parametric EQs by default.

intermediate.oneLiner: A parametric EQ provides per-band control over three parameters: center frequency, gain (boost/cut in dB), and Q (bandwidth), allowing precise spectral shaping.
intermediate.details: Each band of a parametric EQ lets you choose: the center frequency (where the boost or cut is focused), the gain (how many dB to boost or cut), and the Q factor (how narrow or wide the affected range is). A high Q value creates a narrow, surgical adjustment; a low Q creates a broad, gentle curve. Most parametric EQs also include high-pass and low-pass filter bands at the ends of the chain, plus high and low shelving options. In game audio, parametric EQs are used for precise tonal matching, resonance removal, and dynamic filtering effects.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use a parametric EQ to notch out resonances in recorded sounds, shape the tonal character of synthesized effects, or create automated filter sweeps for transitions. In middleware like FMOD or Wwise, parametric EQ can be driven by game parameters to dynamically change how sounds are filtered based on gameplay context.

advanced.oneLiner: A parametric EQ implements configurable biquad peaking filters with user-defined center frequency, gain, and Q (bandwidth), typically cascaded with shelving and pass filters for full spectral control.
advanced.technicalNotes: Each parametric band is a second-order IIR peaking filter. The Q factor relates to bandwidth: BW = f_center / Q (in octaves, BW = sqrt(1 + 1/(4*Q^2)) / Q approximately). The filter coefficients are derived from the Audio EQ Cookbook formulas. When automating parameters in real-time, coefficient interpolation is necessary to avoid clicks. In Unity, use the AudioMixer's ParamEQ effect for static shaping, or implement a biquad filter array in OnAudioFilterRead for dynamic, game-parameter-driven EQ. For efficiency, process in blocks and recalculate coefficients only when parameters change, not every sample.

vs.eq: A generic EQ may have fixed bands (graphic EQ) or limited controls. A parametric EQ provides full control over frequency, gain, and Q for each band, enabling more precise adjustments.
vs.low-pass-filter: A low-pass filter removes everything above a cutoff frequency. A parametric EQ band can boost or cut a specific frequency range while leaving the rest of the spectrum intact.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer > Select a group > Add Effect > ParamEQ
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioMixer ParamEQ effect with exposed parameters for center frequency, octave range, and frequency gain
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's ParamEQ effect in the mixer provides center frequency, octave range (bandwidth), and gain. Expose these as parameters and link them to game states via AudioMixer.SetFloat() for dynamic filtering during gameplay.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's ParamEQ is a single band. For multi-band parametric EQ, you need to stack multiple ParamEQ effects on the same mixer group, which increases CPU usage per group.

misconceptions: A higher Q value does not mean better quality. Q refers to the bandwidth of the filter. A very high Q creates an extremely narrow band that can sound unnatural if overused.
Parametric EQ is not only for fixing problems. It is equally valuable as a creative tool for shaping the character and tone of sounds during design.
You do not need to use all available bands. Often one or two well-placed parametric adjustments are more effective than touching every band.

name: PCM (Uncompressed Audio)

beginner.oneLiner: PCM is raw, uncompressed audio — what you get when you record sound directly. WAV files typically contain PCM data.
beginner.analogy: If compressed audio is a summary of a book, PCM is the full book with every word intact. Nothing is removed, so it's the biggest but most faithful representation.
beginner.whatToDo: Keep your source assets as PCM (WAV or AIFF) files. Let the game engine compress them for you. For very short, frequently-played sounds like footsteps or UI clicks, PCM can be the best runtime format since there's zero decode cost.

intermediate.oneLiner: PCM (Pulse Code Modulation) stores audio as a sequence of amplitude samples at a fixed sample rate and bit depth, with no compression or data loss.
intermediate.details: Each PCM sample is an integer (or float) representing the amplitude of the waveform at that instant. At 44100 Hz / 16-bit stereo, PCM uses about 10 MB per minute. Common containers for PCM are WAV (RIFF) and AIFF. The format is trivially simple to decode — just read samples directly into memory — which makes it ideal for latency-sensitive sounds. Unity supports 8, 16, 24, and 32-bit PCM as well as 32-bit float.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use PCM at runtime for short, frequently-triggered sounds (UI clicks, footsteps, impacts) where decode latency matters. Use Vorbis or ADPCM for longer assets to save memory. Always keep your original assets in PCM format for your source-of-truth audio pipeline.

advanced.oneLiner: PCM provides bit-exact sample storage with O(1) random access and zero decode cost, making it optimal for latency-critical and frequently-played short audio in games.
advanced.technicalNotes: PCM data is a flat array of interleaved (or planar) samples. Memory cost is: sampleRate * bitDepth/8 * channels * durationSeconds. At 48 kHz / 16-bit / mono, that's 96 KB/sec. For game audio, 16-bit PCM is standard; 24/32-bit is only needed during production mixing. WAV files use little-endian byte order; AIFF uses big-endian. Unity's 'Decompress On Load' option stores audio as PCM in memory regardless of the import compression, so it is functionally PCM at runtime. In-memory PCM clips have zero additional CPU cost for playback beyond the audio mixer itself.

vs.vorbis: Vorbis reduces memory by 5-15x but requires CPU to decode. For short clips under 1 second, the decode setup overhead can exceed the memory savings.
vs.adpcm: ADPCM offers 3.5:1 compression with very cheap decoding. It's a good middle ground when PCM is too large but Vorbis decode cost is too high.
vs.mp3: MP3 offers higher compression than ADPCM but with more CPU cost. PCM is preferred over MP3 for anything latency-sensitive.

unityAdapter.location: AudioClip Inspector > Compression Format > PCM
unityAdapter.importTip: Combine PCM compression format with 'Decompress On Load' for the fastest possible playback. This is ideal for UI sounds and short SFX under 1 second.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Using PCM for long audio (music, ambience) will consume enormous memory. A 3-minute stereo track at 44.1 kHz / 16-bit uses ~30 MB of RAM. Use Vorbis with Streaming for anything over a few seconds.

misconceptions: WAV and PCM are not the same thing. WAV is a container format that usually holds PCM data, but it can also contain compressed audio like ADPCM or even MP3.
Higher bit depth PCM does not always mean better quality for playback. 16-bit is sufficient for game runtime; 24/32-bit is useful during production to preserve headroom during mixing.

PCM is not 'better' than Vorbis in all cases. For long audio, the memory cost of PCM can force the OS to swap or the engine to drop sounds. Vorbis at a reasonable quality setting is perceptually identical for most content.

name: Peak Level

beginner.oneLiner: Peak level is the loudest single instant in a sound — the highest point the waveform reaches.
beginner.analogy: Think of measuring your heart rate. Peak level is like the highest spike on the heart monitor — the single most intense beat.
