unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer window. Add a Send effect to a source group (Add Effect > Send) and a Receive effect to the destination group (Add Effect > Receive). Set the Send's target to the Receive group.
unityAdapter.importTip: Create a dedicated 'Reverb' or 'Effects' group as a child of the Master group. Add Receive as the first effect, then your reverb plugin. On source groups, add Send effects pointing to this group. Adjust send levels to taste.
unityAdapter.gotcha: The Receive effect must be added to the destination group BEFORE the processing effect (reverb, delay). If the effect comes before Receive in the chain, the received signal will not pass through it. Also, Send/Receive only works within the same AudioMixer asset; you cannot send across different mixer assets.

misconceptions: Reverb should be placed directly on each AudioSource or bus. This wastes CPU (multiple reverb instances) and creates an incoherent soundscape where each sound seems to be in a different room.
Send effects are only for reverb. Sends are useful for any shared effect: delay, chorus, distortion, sidechain compression triggers, or even analysis/metering buses.

name: Sidechain Compression

beginner.oneLiner: Sidechain compression automatically ducks one sound when another sound plays, like lowering the music volume whenever a character speaks.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a radio DJ: whenever they speak into the mic, the background music automatically gets quieter so you can hear them clearly. When they stop talking, the music comes back up. Sidechain compression does this automatically between any two audio sources.
beginner.whatToDo: Use sidechain compression to automatically lower music or ambient sounds whenever dialogue plays, ensuring the player always hears important speech. In Unity, set up Duck Volume on your music bus triggered by the dialogue bus.

intermediate.oneLiner: Sidechain compression uses the level of one audio signal (the key/trigger) to control gain reduction on a different signal, commonly used for ducking music under dialogue or creating rhythmic pumping effects.
intermediate.details: In a sidechain setup, the compressor on Bus B receives its level detection signal from Bus A instead of from its own input. When Bus A is loud (e.g., dialogue playing), Bus B (e.g., music) gets compressed/ducked. The threshold, ratio, attack, and release parameters control how and when the ducking occurs. Fast attack makes the duck immediate; slow release creates a smooth fade-back. In game audio, sidechain ducking is essential for maintaining dialogue intelligibility and directing the player's attention to important audio events.
intermediate.whenToUse: Set up sidechain ducking between dialogue and music/ambience buses so speech is always clear. Use it between important gameplay sound effects and the music bus to emphasize critical audio cues like enemy alerts or item pickups. For creative use, sidechain a rhythmic source to an ambient pad to create pulsing, breathing textures.

advanced.oneLiner: Sidechain compression routes the envelope detection input of a compressor from an external key signal, enabling cross-bus dynamic gain control independent of the processed signal's own level.
advanced.technicalNotes: The sidechain signal path is: key input -> sidechain filter (optional HPF/LPF to make the detector frequency-selective) -> level detector -> gain computer -> gain stage applied to the main signal. In Unity's Audio Mixer, the Duck Volume effect implements sidechain ducking natively: place it on the target bus and select the trigger bus. Parameters include threshold, ratio, attack, and release. For more complex sidechain behavior (e.g., frequency-selective sidechaining), implement a custom solution in OnAudioFilterRead that reads from a shared buffer. Be aware that Unity's mixer processes groups in a fixed order; the sidechain source group must be processed before the target group for correct timing.

vs.compressor: A standard compressor uses its own input signal for level detection. Sidechain compression uses an external signal for detection while applying gain reduction to a different signal.
vs.noise-gate: A sidechained noise gate opens based on an external trigger rather than its own input. This is useful for triggered effects like making a pad pulse with a kick drum.

unityAdapter.location: Audio Mixer > Target Group > Add Effect > Duck Volume
unityAdapter.apiRef: Duck Volume effect parameters: Threshold, Ratio, Attack Time, Release Time; triggered by audio routed through the mixer
unityAdapter.importTip: To duck music under dialogue: add Duck Volume to the Music group, then route your dialogue AudioSources to a Dialogue group. Unity automatically detects the send. Expose Attack and Release to fine-tune the ducking speed via scripts.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Duck Volume requires both the trigger and target groups to exist in the same Audio Mixer. If your dialogue and music use separate mixers, ducking will not work. Also, Duck Volume triggers from any audio on the source group, including silence with very low noise, so set the threshold appropriately.

misconceptions: Sidechain compression is not only for the pumping effect heard in electronic dance music. In game audio, its primary use is intelligently ducking competing sounds for clarity.
The sidechain signal is not audible in the output. It only serves as a control signal to tell the compressor when and how much to reduce gain.
Sidechain compression does not require separate hardware. It is a routing configuration within your mixer or middleware that can be set up entirely in software.

name: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)

beginner.oneLiner: Signal-to-noise ratio measures how much louder your wanted sound is compared to unwanted background noise.
beginner.analogy: Imagine trying to have a conversation at a quiet library versus a loud construction site. In the library, your voice (the signal) is much louder than the background hum (the noise), so the SNR is high and communication is clear. At the construction site, the noise is almost as loud as your voice, so the SNR is low and it is hard to understand anything.
beginner.whatToDo: When recording or selecting sound assets, aim for a high SNR. Listen for hiss, hum, or background noise in your audio files. If you hear noise when a sound should be silent, the SNR is too low. Use clean recordings and avoid over-amplifying quiet sounds, which boosts noise along with the signal.

intermediate.oneLiner: Signal-to-noise ratio is the difference in decibels between the intended audio signal level and the noise floor, determining the usable dynamic range of a recording or system.
intermediate.details: SNR is expressed in dB: SNR = 20 * log10(signalAmplitude / noiseAmplitude). A higher number means cleaner audio. Professional recordings typically have SNR above 60 dB. In digital audio, the noise floor is largely determined by bit depth: 16-bit audio has a theoretical SNR of about 96 dB, while 24-bit reaches about 144 dB. Analog components (microphones, preamps) add their own noise. When you normalize a quiet recording, you amplify the noise floor along with the signal, reducing perceived SNR.
intermediate.whenToUse: Evaluate SNR when choosing audio assets for your game. Reject recordings with audible noise floors unless the noise is masked by gameplay audio. When layering many sounds simultaneously, even small amounts of noise per source can accumulate into an audible hiss. For dialogue and UI sounds where clean silence matters, prioritize high-SNR assets.

advanced.oneLiner: SNR quantifies the ratio of signal power to noise power in decibels, defining the effective dynamic range ceiling imposed by system noise, quantization error, or environmental interference.
advanced.technicalNotes: In digital systems, the theoretical SNR from quantization noise alone is approximately 6.02n + 1.76 dB, where n is the bit depth. Dithering replaces quantization distortion with benign noise, preserving signal information below the least significant bit at the cost of a slightly raised but perceptually neutral noise floor. In game audio pipelines, SNR concerns arise when: applying heavy gain to quiet assets, summing many simultaneous sources (noise adds incoherently as sqrt of N), using aggressive compression that raises the noise floor, or streaming low-bitrate audio. For real-time voice chat, SNR is critical and noise gates or spectral subtraction are standard tools.

vs.bit-depth: Bit depth determines the theoretical maximum SNR of a digital system. SNR is the actual measured ratio in a specific signal, which may be lower due to analog noise, processing, or poor recording technique.
vs.normalization: Normalization adjusts gain to reach a target level. It does not improve SNR because both signal and noise are amplified equally. A normalized quiet recording with a poor SNR will have louder noise.

unityAdapter.location: Not a Unity-specific setting. SNR is a property of your source audio files and recording chain. Inspect audio quality before importing into Unity.
unityAdapter.apiRef: No direct Unity API. Use external tools like Audacity's noise profile analysis or an audio spectrum analyzer to measure SNR.
unityAdapter.importTip: Before importing audio, check the silence sections of your files for noise. In Audacity, select a silent section and use Analyze > Contrast to see the noise floor. If the noise floor is above -60 dBFS for sound effects, consider re-recording or applying noise reduction.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Applying Unity's AudioSource volume boost or mixer gain to a noisy asset amplifies the noise proportionally. Always fix SNR issues in the source file, not in the engine.

misconceptions: Turning up the volume improves audio quality. Amplifying a signal also amplifies the noise equally, so the SNR stays the same or worsens if clipping occurs.
Digital audio is inherently noise-free. While quantization noise is very low at 16-bit and above, noise can be introduced by poor recording, lossy encoding artifacts, or summing many quiet sources.
Noise reduction always helps. Aggressive noise reduction can remove parts of the signal along with the noise, creating artifacts that sound worse than the original noise.

name: Sound Cone

beginner.oneLiner: A sound cone defines the directional pattern of an audio source, making it louder in front and quieter to the sides and behind, like a flashlight for sound.
beginner.analogy: A flashlight shines a bright beam in one direction — bright directly ahead, dimmer at the edges, and dark behind. A sound cone works the same way: the sound is at full volume within the inner cone, gradually gets quieter in the outer cone, and is at minimum volume outside both cones. This simulates directional sound sources like speakers, megaphones, and sirens.
beginner.whatToDo: Set up sound cones on directional audio sources in your game: speakers facing a direction, PA announcements, enemy shouts, car horns, and spotlights with audio. Configure the inner cone angle (full volume), outer cone angle (fade-to-minimum), and outer volume.

intermediate.oneLiner: A sound cone defines two angular regions (inner and outer) around an AudioSource's forward direction, with full volume inside the inner cone, attenuated volume between inner and outer cones, and a configurable minimum volume outside the outer cone.
intermediate.details: The inner cone angle defines the full-volume zone. The outer cone angle defines where attenuation ends. Between the two, volume interpolates smoothly. Outside the outer cone, the sound plays at the outer cone volume (which can be zero for full directional masking). Many implementations also apply a low-pass filter outside the cone to simulate the natural high-frequency directivity of real sound sources. Sound cones interact with distance attenuation — the cone affects directionality while rolloff handles distance.
intermediate.whenToUse: Apply sound cones to: speakers and PA systems (narrow cone facing the audience), NPC dialogue in specific directions (character faces toward the player), vehicle horns and sirens (louder from the front), TV and radio sources (sound emanates from the screen direction), and environmental sound effects with natural directivity (waterfalls, machinery with exhaust ports).

advanced.oneLiner: Sound cones model source directivity using a dual-cone attenuation pattern with configurable inner/outer half-angles and outer gain, optionally combined with a directivity-dependent low-pass filter to approximate the frequency-dependent radiation patterns of real acoustic sources.
advanced.technicalNotes: The cone attenuation angle is computed as the angle between the source's forward vector and the source-to-listener direction vector: θ = acos(dot(source.forward, normalize(listener.pos - source.pos))). If θ < innerAngle/2, gain = 1. If θ > outerAngle/2, gain = outerVolume. Between, interpolate linearly or with a custom curve. Real acoustic sources have frequency-dependent directivity — higher frequencies are more directional than lower frequencies. For realism, combine the cone volume attenuation with a low-pass filter whose cutoff decreases as the angle moves outside the cone. In middleware (FMOD, Wwise), cones can also affect reverb send levels — outside the cone, more reverb simulates indirect sound reaching the listener.

vs.audio-rolloff: Rolloff handles volume change with distance (how far). Sound cones handle volume change with direction (which way). They are orthogonal and both contribute to the final audible level of a positioned source.
vs.occlusion: Occlusion reduces sound due to physical barriers between source and listener. Sound cones reduce sound based on the source's facing direction relative to the listener. A speaker facing away from you (cone attenuation) and one behind a wall (occlusion) sound different.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > 3D Sound Settings. Configure 'Spread' for cone width. For custom cone behavior, use the AudioSource's directionality settings or implement angle-based volume in script.
unityAdapter.apiRef: UnityEngine.AudioSource.spread, AudioSource.spatialBlend, custom scripting with Vector3.Angle()
unityAdapter.importTip: Unity's built-in AudioSource does not have explicit inner/outer cone settings like FMOD or Wwise. Use AudioSource.spread (0-360 degrees) for basic directional spreading. For true cone behavior, write a script that calculates the angle between the source's forward direction and the listener, then adjusts volume and optionally a low-pass filter cutoff.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.spread controls stereo spread angle, not a sound cone in the traditional sense. It affects how the 3D sound is spread across the stereo or surround field, not directional volume attenuation. For true sound cone behavior, you need a custom script or middleware.

misconceptions: Sound cones make a sound completely silent outside the cone — the outer cone volume is configurable. Setting it to zero creates full directional masking, but typically a low residual volume (10-20%) sounds more natural since sound diffracts around sources in reality.

All audio sources need sound cones — only directional sources benefit from cones. Point sources like explosions, footsteps, and ambient emitters are naturally omnidirectional and should not have cones.

Sound cones only affect volume — realistic cones also filter high frequencies outside the cone, since real acoustic sources are less directional at low frequencies. Combining volume and filtering creates more believable directional sound.

name: Sound Design

beginner.oneLiner: Sound design is the creative process of crafting audio effects and sonic experiences for games, films, or other media from scratch or by combining existing sounds.
beginner.analogy: Sound design is like cooking. Sometimes you use fresh ingredients (recording real sounds), sometimes you use pre-made components (sound libraries), and you combine, season, and plate them to create the final dish. A great sound designer, like a great chef, knows which ingredients work together and how to combine them into something better than the individual parts.
beginner.whatToDo: Start simple: pick one sound you need for your game (a button click, a jump, a pickup) and try creating it from scratch using free sound libraries or basic synthesis. Layer two or three sounds together, adjust their timing and pitch, and you will be surprised how quickly you develop an ear for what works.

intermediate.oneLiner: Sound design encompasses recording, synthesis, processing, and layering techniques to create purpose-built audio assets that serve the artistic and functional needs of a project.
intermediate.details: The core workflow involves: conceptualization (what should this sound communicate?), sourcing (recording, synthesis, or library pulls), layering (combining multiple sources for complexity), processing (EQ, compression, reverb, pitch shifting, time stretching), and implementation (integrating into the engine with proper triggers, variations, and parameters). Common techniques include pitch shifting recordings to create alien or fantasy sounds, time-stretching impacts for slow-motion effects, reversing audio for magical or UI elements, and using granular synthesis for evolving textures.
intermediate.whenToUse: Every game needs sound design. Even if you use library sounds, you still need to select, edit, and combine them to fit your game. Invest time in sound design for frequently heard sounds (footsteps, UI, weapons) as repetitive sounds get the most scrutiny from players. Create variation sets to avoid listener fatigue.

advanced.oneLiner: Sound design is a multidisciplinary practice combining acoustics, psychoacoustics, signal processing, and creative artistry to construct semantic audio that communicates meaning, emotion, and spatial context within interactive media.
advanced.technicalNotes: Advanced game sound design increasingly involves procedural audio: generating sounds from physical models or synthesis algorithms at runtime rather than playing back recordings. This enables infinite variation, parameter-driven sound (e.g., engine RPM driving synthesis), and reduced memory footprint. Techniques include physically-informed models (spring, string, plate, fluid simulations), granular synthesis for texture, and spectral processing (FFT-based manipulation). The trend toward procedural audio in games requires sound designers to understand DSP fundamentals alongside traditional recording and editing skills. Middleware like FMOD and Wwise support designer-friendly procedural workflows through modular signal routing and parameter binding.

vs.foley: Foley is a specific subset of sound design focused on recording real-world sounds to match on-screen actions. Sound design is the broader discipline that includes foley, synthesis, processing, and implementation.
vs.music-composition: Music composition creates melodic, harmonic, and rhythmic content. Sound design creates non-musical audio (SFX, ambience, UI). In games, both disciplines overlap in areas like musical stingers and adaptive audio.

unityAdapter.location: Sound design assets are imported as AudioClips and played via AudioSource components. The Audio Mixer provides processing chains for mixing and effects.
unityAdapter.importTip: Organize your sound design assets in folders by category (SFX/UI, SFX/Weapons, SFX/Footsteps, etc.). Use descriptive filenames with variation numbers (jump_01, jump_02, jump_03) for easy identification and scripting.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity re-imports audio when you change import settings (compression, sample rate), which can take a long time on large projects. Set your import defaults early and batch-change settings using an AssetPostprocessor script.

misconceptions: Sound design requires expensive equipment and studios. Many professional game sounds are created with a laptop, a basic microphone, free sound libraries, and a DAW. Creativity matters more than gear.
Sound design is just finding the right sound effect in a library. Library sounds are a starting point, but they almost always need editing, layering, and processing to fit a specific game context.

name: Sound Event

beginner.oneLiner: A sound event is a named trigger that tells the audio system 'play this kind of sound,' bundling together everything about how the sound behaves — which files to use, volume randomization, effects, and more.
beginner.analogy: Think of ordering at a restaurant by saying 'I will have the burger' instead of listing every ingredient and cooking instruction. The kitchen knows how to make it — the bun, patty, toppings, cooking temperature are all defined in the recipe. A sound event works the same way. You just say 'play footstep' and the audio system handles everything: picks a random variation, adjusts pitch slightly, applies the right reverb, and plays it at the correct volume.
beginner.whatToDo: In FMOD or Wwise, create an event for each logical sound in your game: 'player-footstep,' 'enemy-death,' 'weapon-fire,' 'ui-click.' Drop your audio files into the event, add randomization and parameter control. In your game code, trigger the event by name. The sound designer can change everything about the sound without touching your code.

intermediate.oneLiner: A sound event is an abstraction layer in audio middleware that encapsulates one or more audio assets with playback logic (randomization, parameter control, effects, voice limiting), triggered by game code via a named reference.
intermediate.details: An event contains: Audio assets — one or more sound files (variations of a footstep, layers of an explosion). Selection logic — how to choose which asset plays (random, sequential, shuffle, weighted). Parameter bindings — RTPCs that control volume, pitch, filter, or select between layers based on game values. Effects — reverb sends, EQ, compression applied to the event. Instance management — maximum simultaneous instances, stealing behavior, cooldown between triggers. Timeline or state logic — for complex events with multiple phases (e.g., a vehicle engine with start, loop, and stop phases). In FMOD Studio, events are authored on a timeline with parameter sheets. In Wwise, events are containers in the actor-mixer hierarchy that trigger actions on sound objects.
intermediate.whenToUse: Create a sound event for every distinct gameplay action that produces sound. Group related sounds under one event (all footstep variations under 'footstep,' all sword swings under 'melee-attack'). Use parameters to differentiate context within an event rather than creating many separate events. For example, one 'footstep' event with a 'surface' parameter is better than separate 'footstep-wood,' 'footstep-grass,' 'footstep-metal' events.

advanced.oneLiner: A sound event is a middleware-defined playback instruction graph that resolves at runtime to a specific audio rendering pipeline — selecting sources via switch/RTPC evaluation, instantiating the voice with property inheritance from the actor-mixer hierarchy, and managing instance lifetime through priority and steal policies.
advanced.technicalNotes: FMOD event resolution: the timeline evaluates instruments at the play cursor position. Multi-instruments select from their playlist using weighted random with no-repeat history. Parameter sheets modulate properties across parameter ranges with designer-defined curves. Nested events and programmer instruments allow runtime sound selection via callbacks. Wwise event resolution: the event triggers one or more actions (Play, Stop, SetSwitch, SetState) on objects in the actor-mixer hierarchy. The hierarchy resolves switches and random containers to select the final sound object. Property evaluation cascades through the hierarchy: bus volume * parent container volume * sound object volume * RTPC modulation = final gain. Both systems support event callbacks (markers, beat/bar sync, programmer instrument selection) that notify game code of event milestones. For networking/replay, events should be deterministic — the same event call with the same random seed and parameter state should produce identical sound selection.

vs.event-driven-audio: Event-driven audio is the architectural pattern. Sound events are the concrete implementation of that pattern in middleware. You can implement event-driven audio without middleware, but middleware events are far more feature-rich.
vs.audio-bank: Events define what happens when a sound is triggered. Banks contain the audio data and event definitions. An event is useless if its bank is not loaded.
vs.rtpc: RTPCs are parameters bound to events that control runtime behavior. Events consume RTPC values; RTPCs are the input mechanism that makes events adaptive.

unityAdapter.location: FMOD: Events are authored in FMOD Studio and referenced in Unity by path (e.g., 'event:/SFX/Footstep'). Wwise: Events are authored in the Wwise authoring tool and referenced by name or ID.
unityAdapter.apiRef: FMOD: FMODUnity.RuntimeManager.PlayOneShot('event:/SFX/Footstep', transform.position). Wwise: AkSoundEngine.PostEvent('Footstep', gameObject). For persistent events: FMOD uses EventInstance, Wwise uses AkEvent component.
unityAdapter.importTip: For one-shot sounds (impacts, UI clicks), use PlayOneShot (FMOD) or PostEvent (Wwise) directly. For sounds you need to control over time (adjusting parameters, stopping manually), create a persistent event instance and hold a reference to it. Release the instance when the sound is done to free the voice.
unityAdapter.gotcha: FMOD event paths are case-sensitive ('event:/SFX/Footstep' is different from 'event:/sfx/footstep'). Wwise event names are not case-sensitive but IDs are preferred for performance (avoids string hashing at runtime). Always test that event names match between the authoring tool and game code — typos result in silent failures.

misconceptions: A sound event is just a sound file with a name — events encapsulate complex logic including randomization, parameters, effects, and instance management. They are closer to audio programs than file references.

More events means better organization — consolidate related sounds under single parameterized events instead of creating dozens of separate events. One 'footstep' event with a surface parameter is more maintainable than 10 surface-specific events.
Events always play a sound — events can trigger actions other than Play: Stop, Pause, SetVolume, SetState. In Wwise especially, events are generic action triggers on the audio hierarchy.

name: Sound Layering

beginner.oneLiner: Sound layering is combining two or more sounds on top of each other to create a single, richer effect that no individual sound could achieve alone.
beginner.analogy: Layering is like making a sandwich. Bread alone is bland, a single slice of meat is okay, but combining bread, meat, cheese, lettuce, and sauce creates something much more satisfying. Each layer of sound adds a different quality — one provides low-end punch, another adds high-end sizzle, and a third brings body in the middle.
beginner.whatToDo: When a single sound effect feels thin or unimpressive, try layering. For an explosion, combine a low sub-boom, a mid-range crackle, and a high-frequency debris scatter. Adjust each layer's volume and timing until they feel like one cohesive sound. Start with two layers before adding more.

intermediate.oneLiner: Sound layering stacks multiple audio sources — each covering different frequency ranges, temporal characteristics, or textural qualities — to build complex composite sounds.
intermediate.details: Effective layering follows a frequency-band strategy: sub/low layer (impact, weight), mid layer (body, character), high layer (definition, air, detail). Each layer should occupy its own frequency space with minimal overlap — use EQ to carve room for each layer. Timing alignment is critical: the transient (attack) of each layer should hit at the same moment for impact sounds, or be intentionally offset for evolving textures. Common layering recipes: explosions (sub boom + mid fire crackle + high debris), gunshots (mechanical click + body punch + tail decay), footsteps (initial impact + surface texture + body resonance).
intermediate.whenToUse: Layer when a single recording or synthesis patch lacks complexity, weight, or detail. Layer to add sub-bass punch to thin impacts, high-frequency definition to dull sounds, or textural interest to simple tones. Be selective — every layer adds to polyphony count and CPU load at runtime.

advanced.oneLiner: Sound layering is a composite design technique that constructs spectrally complete, psychoacoustically rich audio events by summing independent sources with complementary spectral, temporal, and dynamic characteristics.
advanced.technicalNotes: When layering, phase relationships between layers at overlapping frequencies can cause constructive or destructive interference, affecting the composite's frequency response unpredictably. Mitigate this with EQ separation (high-pass the top layer, low-pass the bottom) or by time-aligning transients to ensure coherent summing at the attack. Monitor the summed waveform for clipping — headroom management is essential. In implementation, playing 3-5 AudioSources simultaneously per event is expensive; consider pre-rendering (bouncing) layered sounds offline for frequently triggered events, keeping real-time layering only for dynamic or parameterized sounds. Group layers on a single audio bus with shared effects (reverb send, compression) to glue them together perceptually.

vs.sweetening: Sweetening adds a finishing touch (often a single layer or process) to enhance an existing sound. Layering builds the composite from multiple foundational sources.
vs.crossfade: Crossfading transitions between two sounds over time. Layering plays multiple sounds simultaneously to create a single combined effect.

unityAdapter.location: Play multiple AudioSource.PlayOneShot() calls simultaneously on the same or separate AudioSource components. Route all layers to a shared Audio Mixer group for unified processing.
unityAdapter.importTip: For static layered sounds (always the same layers), consider bouncing/pre-rendering them in a DAW as a single file to save runtime polyphony. Reserve real-time layering for sounds that need dynamic variation per layer.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Each PlayOneShot call consumes a voice in Unity's voice allocator. Layering 4 sounds on 10 simultaneous events means 40 voices. Monitor your voice count and set Unity's Max Real Voices appropriately in Audio Settings, or you will get silent sounds when the limit is exceeded.

misconceptions: More layers always sounds better. Adding too many layers creates a muddy, cluttered sound. Professional sound designers often get better results with 2-3 well-chosen layers than 8 overlapping ones.

You can just stack any sounds and they will blend naturally. Layers need frequency separation, phase coherence, and volume balancing to sound like one cohesive effect rather than multiple disconnected sounds playing at once.

name: Sound Pool

beginner.oneLiner: Sound pooling pre-creates a set of reusable audio players so your game does not stutter when many sounds need to play at once.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a restaurant with a fixed set of plates. Instead of buying a new plate for every customer and throwing it away after, you wash and reuse plates from a stack. Sound pooling works the same way — you reuse a fixed set of audio players instead of constantly creating and destroying them.
beginner.whatToDo: If your game plays many simultaneous sounds (bullets, footsteps, particles), set up a pool of AudioSource objects at startup. When a sound needs to play, grab one from the pool instead of creating a new one.

intermediate.oneLiner: Sound pooling is an object pooling pattern applied to AudioSources, pre-allocating a fixed number of players to avoid runtime allocation overhead and GC pressure.
intermediate.details: Creating and destroying AudioSource components at runtime causes memory allocation, garbage collection spikes, and potential frame hitches — especially on mobile. A sound pool pre-instantiates a configurable number of AudioSource GameObjects at load time. When a sound plays, an idle source is claimed from the pool; when it finishes, it returns to the pool. Key design decisions: pool size (how many simultaneous voices you support), voice stealing policy (what happens when the pool is exhausted — steal the oldest, quietest, or lowest-priority voice), and whether to support pool growth. Typical pool sizes: 16-32 for casual games, 32-64 for action games.
intermediate.whenToUse: Any game with frequent, overlapping sound effects: shooters (gunfire, impacts, ricochets), platformers (collectibles, footsteps), RTS (unit acknowledgments, combat). Also useful for UI sounds in menus with rapid interactions. Less necessary for music and ambience since those are long-lived, persistent sources.

advanced.oneLiner: Sound pooling eliminates per-play allocation overhead by maintaining a pre-warmed ring buffer of AudioSource instances with configurable voice-stealing policies for pool exhaustion.
advanced.technicalNotes: Beyond basic pooling, implement a priority system: assign each sound a priority (0=lowest, 255=highest). When the pool is exhausted, the lowest-priority, oldest, or quietest voice is stolen. Unity's AudioSource has a built-in priority field (0=highest, 256=lowest) that the audio system uses for its own voice limiting, but this operates at the engine level, not the pool level — implement your own priority logic for predictable behavior. Consider distance-based culling: do not play sounds beyond a threshold distance from the listener. Use AudioSource.PlayOneShot() for fire-and-forget SFX on a shared source, which avoids pool usage for simple cases but sacrifices per-instance control. For large pools, store sources in a contiguous array for cache-friendly iteration.

vs.play-one-shot: PlayOneShot() plays a clip on an existing AudioSource without claiming a new one, allowing overlapping sounds on a single source. It is simpler than pooling but offers no per-instance control over stop, volume, or pitch after firing. Use PlayOneShot for trivial SFX; use pooling when you need per-instance management.
vs.voice-limiting: Voice limiting caps how many instances of a specific sound can play simultaneously (e.g., max 3 overlapping footsteps). Sound pooling caps total concurrent audio sources globally. Both are complementary — use voice limiting per-sound-type within a global pool.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in audio pool system. Implement using a MonoBehaviour that manages a collection of AudioSource GameObjects. Unity's built-in Max Real Voices (Project Settings > Audio) provides engine-level voice limiting.
unityAdapter.importTip: Start with a simple pool: create a parent GameObject with N children, each having an AudioSource. Write a manager that finds the first idle source (not isPlaying), configures it (clip, volume, pitch), and calls Play(). Return to pool automatically when done.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioSource.isPlaying returns false for paused sources, not just finished ones. If you pause audio and use isPlaying to reclaim pool sources, paused sounds will be incorrectly recycled. Track active/idle state separately in your pool manager.

misconceptions: Sound pooling is not premature optimization — on mobile platforms, AudioSource creation and garbage collection cause real frame hitches. It is a standard pattern in shipped games, not over-engineering.

A bigger pool is not always better. Each AudioSource consumes memory and the audio system has a real voice limit (typically 32-64 real voices). Size your pool to match your actual peak concurrent sound count, not an arbitrary large number.

name: Sound Propagation

beginner.oneLiner: Sound propagation is how sound travels through a game world — including how it bounces off walls, bends around corners, gets muffled by obstacles, and changes with distance.
beginner.analogy: Think about hearing someone talking in another room. The sound comes through the doorway (diffraction), bounces off the hallway walls (reflection), and sounds muffled because it partially goes through the wall (transmission loss). Sound propagation simulation recreates all of these real-world behaviors in your game.
beginner.whatToDo: For basic propagation, use distance attenuation on your AudioSources and occlusion to muffle sounds behind walls. For more advanced propagation, use a spatializer plugin like Steam Audio that simulates reflections, diffraction, and transmission through geometry.

intermediate.oneLiner: Sound propagation modeling simulates the physical behavior of sound waves in a game environment, including reflection, diffraction, transmission, absorption, and distance attenuation, to create acoustically believable spaces.
intermediate.details: Propagation encompasses several phenomena: reflection (sound bouncing off surfaces), diffraction (sound bending around edges and through openings), transmission (sound passing through materials with attenuation and filtering), absorption (surfaces converting sound energy to heat, reducing reflections), and scattering (rough surfaces diffusing reflections). Game audio middleware can simulate these using ray tracing, path finding, or zone-based approximations. The choice depends on CPU budget and accuracy needs.
intermediate.whenToUse: Implement propagation simulation for games where spatial audio is important: stealth games (hearing guards through walls), horror (sound around corners builds tension), competitive shooters (locating enemies by sound), and open-world games (environmental realism). Start with simple occlusion and distance attenuation, then add reflection and diffraction as budget allows.

advanced.oneLiner: Sound propagation simulation applies geometric acoustics (ray tracing, image-source method, beam tracing) or wave-based methods (FDTD, BEM) to compute the transfer function between source and listener positions, accounting for specular/diffuse reflection, edge diffraction, material-dependent transmission, and atmospheric absorption.
advanced.technicalNotes: Ray-tracing-based propagation (Steam Audio, Project Acoustics) casts rays from source positions, traces reflections off scene geometry with material-dependent absorption coefficients, and accumulates an impulse response. Image-source methods compute exact specular reflection paths but scale poorly with reflection order O(N^order). Wave-based methods (FDTD) solve the wave equation on a grid and capture diffraction naturally but are computationally prohibitive for real-time use above a few hundred Hz. Practical game audio uses hybrid approaches: baked propagation data for static geometry with runtime ray casting for dynamic elements. Pre-compute propagation as directional IRs per listener zone (Project Acoustics approach) to reduce runtime cost. Key parameters: max reflection order (2-4 is typical), frequency-dependent material absorption coefficients, diffraction model (UTD for edge diffraction), and atmospheric absorption (~0.002 dB/m at 1 kHz).

vs.occlusion: Occlusion is one specific aspect of propagation — the muffling of sound by obstacles between source and listener. Propagation encompasses the full picture: occlusion, reflection, diffraction, transmission, and distance effects.
vs.reverb: Reverb simulates the diffuse reflection tail of a space. Propagation simulates how sound reaches the listener through the environment, including the direct path, early reflections, and diffraction paths that precede and inform the reverb.

unityAdapter.location: Basic: AudioSource rolloff settings for distance. Advanced: Install Steam Audio or Project Acoustics for ray-traced propagation including occlusion, reflection, and diffraction.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioSource rolloff, Steam Audio: SteamAudioSource (occlusion, reflections, pathing), Microsoft Project Acoustics
unityAdapter.importTip: Steam Audio offers real-time propagation: enable occlusion, reflection, and pathing on SteamAudioSource components. For best results, tag scene geometry with acoustic materials (concrete, wood, glass) to control reflection and transmission characteristics. Bake propagation probes for faster runtime performance.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Full propagation simulation is CPU-intensive. Limit the number of sources with full propagation enabled (8-16 is typical). Use simple distance attenuation for distant or unimportant sources and reserve full propagation for nearby, important sounds.

misconceptions: Sound only travels in straight lines — sound diffracts around obstacles and through openings, which is why you can hear around corners. Games that only check line-of-sight for audio miss this important behavior.

Propagation simulation is too expensive for games — modern solutions like Steam Audio and Project Acoustics use efficient baking and runtime approximations that run well on current hardware. Start with basic features and scale up.

Distance attenuation is enough for realistic audio — distance only affects volume. Real propagation also changes frequency content (air absorption removes highs), adds reflections, and applies diffraction. These details contribute significantly to immersion.

name: Spatialization

beginner.oneLiner: Spatialization is the process of making sounds appear to come from specific locations in 3D space around the player.
beginner.analogy: Close your eyes and have a friend snap their fingers in different spots around you. You can tell where they are without looking — your brain uses tiny differences in timing and volume between your ears. 3D audio spatialization does the same trick with game sounds, making them seem to come from the right direction and distance.
beginner.whatToDo: Make your AudioSources 3D (set spatialBlend to 1.0) and use mono audio clips for positional sounds. Place AudioSources on the GameObjects that produce sound (enemies, doors, machines). The audio engine will handle panning and volume based on the AudioListener position.

intermediate.oneLiner: Spatialization processes mono audio sources through panning, distance attenuation, and optionally HRTF to position them in a 3D soundfield relative to the listener.
intermediate.details: Basic spatialization uses constant-power panning to place sounds in the stereo or surround field based on angle, and distance-based attenuation for depth. HRTF (Head-Related Transfer Function) spatialization goes further by modeling how the human head, ears, and torso filter sound differently depending on direction, enabling convincing elevation and front-back distinction even on headphones. Key considerations: use mono clips for 3D sources (stereo clips have their own spatial information that conflicts), set appropriate min/max distances, and choose the right rolloff curve.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use full 3D spatialization for any diegetic sound that exists at a location in your game world: footsteps, gunshots, environmental props, NPC dialogue, vehicles, ambient point sources. Keep non-diegetic sounds (music, UI, narration) as 2D (spatialBlend = 0). Consider HRTF spatialization for VR, headphone-focused games, and horror games where precise localization is critical.

advanced.oneLiner: Spatialization encodes positional audio via interaural time difference (ITD), interaural level difference (ILD), and spectral filtering (HRTF) to produce binaural or multichannel output that perceptually places sources in 3D space.
advanced.technicalNotes: Unity's default spatializer uses simple panning and attenuation. For HRTF-based spatialization, integrate a plugin: Steam Audio (free, ray-traced reflections), Resonance Audio (Google, lightweight), Oculus Audio SDK (Meta, optimized for Quest), or Microsoft Spatializer (Windows Sonic). HRTF processing convolves mono input with direction-dependent impulse responses, typically 128-512 samples long, for each ear. Performance cost scales linearly with active spatialized sources. Ambisonics (B-format) is an intermediate representation that encodes a full soundfield and can be decoded to any speaker layout or binaural output. For best results: use measured HRTF datasets (SOFA format), limit concurrent spatialized sources (16-32 is typical), and fall back to simple panning for distant or low-priority sounds.

vs.stereo-vs-mono: Stereo files have pre-baked spatial information (left/right balance). Mono files have no inherent directionality, which is what you need for 3D spatialization — the spatializer adds directionality at runtime based on source position.
vs.attenuation: Attenuation handles volume falloff with distance. Spatialization handles directional placement (left/right/up/down/front/back). Both are needed for convincing 3D audio — attenuation gives depth, spatialization gives direction.
vs.occlusion: Spatialization positions sound in space. Occlusion modifies sound based on physical barriers. They are complementary: a spatialized sound behind a wall should also be occluded.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > 3D Sound Settings. Set Spatial Blend to 1.0 for full 3D. For HRTF, install a spatializer plugin and select it in Edit > Project Settings > Audio > Spatializer Plugin.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set AudioSource.spatialBlend to 1.0 for 3D sounds. Use mono clips for spatialized sources. For HRTF, import Steam Audio or Resonance Audio from the Package Manager, set the Spatializer Plugin in Audio project settings, and check 'Spatialize' on each AudioSource. Configure min/max distance and rolloff curves in the 3D Sound Settings.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Spatializing stereo clips produces odd results because each channel is already pre-panned. Always use mono clips for 3D AudioSources. Also, the 'Spatialize' checkbox and spatialBlend are separate controls: spatialBlend controls basic 3D panning, while 'Spatialize' enables the external spatializer plugin (HRTF). You need both for full HRTF spatialization.

misconceptions: Stereo sounds work fine for 3D spatialization — they do not. Stereo clips have pre-baked left/right information that conflicts with the spatializer. Always use mono clips for 3D positioned sounds.

Spatialization only matters for VR — any game benefits from 3D audio. Players instinctively use sound direction to locate enemies, find objectives, and feel immersed, even in non-VR games on headphones or speakers.

More spatialized sources is always better — HRTF processing is CPU-intensive. Prioritize spatialization for important nearby sources and use simple panning or 2D fallback for distant or unimportant sounds.

name: Spectral Analysis

beginner.oneLiner: Spectral analysis breaks down a sound into its individual frequency components, letting you see what frequencies are present and how loud each one is — like a prism splitting white light into a rainbow.
beginner.analogy: When you see a rainbow, white light has been split into its individual color components. Spectral analysis does the same thing to sound — it takes the complex waveform and splits it into individual frequencies, showing you exactly what is inside the sound. A spectrum analyzer display shows low frequencies on the left and high on the right, with height showing loudness.
beginner.whatToDo: Use a spectrum analyzer to visualize your game audio during development. It helps you identify frequency clashes between sounds, find unwanted rumble or hiss, and verify that your EQ settings are working as intended.

intermediate.oneLiner: Spectral analysis decomposes a time-domain audio signal into its frequency-domain representation using techniques like FFT, revealing the magnitude and phase of each frequency component for visualization, debugging, and mixing decisions.
intermediate.details: The FFT (Fast Fourier Transform) is the standard tool for spectral analysis. It takes a block of audio samples and produces a frequency spectrum. Key parameters: FFT size (larger = more frequency resolution but less time resolution), windowing function (Hanning, Hamming, Blackman — reduces spectral leakage), and overlap (how much consecutive FFT blocks share samples, typically 50-75%). Spectrograms show spectrum over time as a 2D color map. Real-time spectrum analyzers are invaluable mixing tools.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use spectral analysis to: diagnose frequency clashes between concurrent sounds, verify EQ and filter effects are working correctly, identify noise or artifacts in audio assets, analyze the frequency content of sounds for matching or layering decisions, and build audio-reactive visual effects that respond to specific frequency bands.

advanced.oneLiner: Spectral analysis applies the DFT (implemented via FFT) with windowing, overlap, and optional psychoacoustic scaling (Bark/mel/ERB) to transform time-domain signals into frequency-domain representations for analysis, feature extraction, and parametric control.
advanced.technicalNotes: FFT resolution: frequency bin width = sample_rate / FFT_size. A 4096-point FFT at 48 kHz gives ~11.7 Hz resolution. The time-frequency tradeoff (Heisenberg uncertainty) means you cannot have both fine frequency and fine time resolution simultaneously — use larger FFTs for tonal analysis and smaller FFTs for transient detection. For game audio tools, convert linear-frequency FFT bins to perceptual scales (Bark, mel) for more meaningful visualization. The STFT (Short-Time Fourier Transform) applies FFT to overlapping windowed segments for time-varying analysis. Phase vocoder techniques use STFT for pitch shifting and time stretching. For audio-reactive gameplay, band-pass the FFT output into musically meaningful bands (sub-bass, bass, mids, presence, brilliance) and smooth the magnitudes for stable control signals.

vs.fft: FFT is the algorithm used to perform spectral analysis. Spectral analysis is the broader practice of examining frequency content, which may also use other techniques like wavelet transforms, constant-Q transforms, or filter banks.
vs.eq: EQ modifies the frequency content of a signal. Spectral analysis observes and measures frequency content without modifying it. Analysis informs EQ decisions — you analyze first, then EQ to fix what you find.

unityAdapter.location: Use AudioSource.GetSpectrumData() or AudioListener.GetSpectrumData() to retrieve FFT data at runtime for visualization or analysis.
unityAdapter.importTip: Call GetSpectrumData with a power-of-two array size (512, 1024, 2048) and an FFTWindow type (Blackman for best frequency accuracy, Rectangular for best time accuracy). The returned float array contains magnitude values for linearly spaced frequency bins from 0 Hz to half the sample rate.
unityAdapter.gotcha: GetSpectrumData returns linear magnitudes, not dB. Convert to dB for display: dB = 20 * Mathf.Log10(magnitude). Also, the frequency bins are linearly spaced — to display on a logarithmic (perceptually correct) axis, you need to remap the bins, not just plot them directly.

misconceptions: You need special equipment for spectral analysis — most DAWs include spectrum analyzers, and game engines like Unity provide FFT data through simple API calls. Free tools like SPAN by Voxengo are also available.

Spectral analysis is only for mastering engineers — game audio developers benefit from spectral analysis for debugging audio issues, balancing frequency content in mixes, and building audio-reactive features.

Larger FFT sizes are always better — larger FFTs give better frequency resolution but worse time resolution and higher latency. Choose the FFT size based on your needs: large for tonal analysis, small for transient-responsive visualizations.

name: Stereo vs Mono

beginner.oneLiner: Mono audio has one channel (same sound in both ears), while stereo has two channels (different sounds in left and right ears) for a wider, more spatial feel.
beginner.analogy: Mono is like a single speaker sitting in front of you — everything comes from the same point. Stereo is like two speakers, one on your left and one on your right, each playing slightly different things. This lets you hear instruments spread across a stage or a car drive from left to right.
beginner.whatToDo: Use mono clips for 3D positional sounds in your game (footsteps, gunshots, NPC voices, point-source effects) because the game engine needs to position them dynamically. Use stereo clips for music, ambience beds, and UI sounds that should fill both ears equally.

intermediate.oneLiner: Mono audio contains a single channel of audio data, while stereo contains two independent channels (left and right), with the choice between them having significant implications for 3D spatialization, memory, and CPU usage in games.
intermediate.details: Mono files are half the size and memory of equivalent stereo files. For 3D spatialization, mono is required because the engine applies its own panning and HRTF based on position — stereo files have pre-baked spatial information that conflicts with runtime spatialization. Stereo is appropriate for non-positional content: background music, stereo ambience beds, cinematics, and UI sounds. Some developers record in stereo for richness then downmix to mono for 3D game use. Import settings in Unity allow you to force stereo assets to mono.
intermediate.whenToUse: Always use mono for any sound that will be positioned in 3D space (spatialBlend > 0). Use stereo for music tracks, full-scene ambient beds (rain, wind), UI/menu sounds, and cinematic audio. If a stereo file sounds fine in mono (collapse to center and check for phase cancellation), it is safe to convert.

advanced.oneLiner: Mono signals contain one channel of PCM data; stereo contains two interleaved channels, with the channel count directly impacting memory footprint, streaming bandwidth, spatialization pipeline compatibility, and potential phase cancellation artifacts on downmix.
advanced.technicalNotes: Memory impact: a 1-second 44100 Hz 16-bit mono clip is 88 KB; stereo is 176 KB. This doubles across every loaded clip, which matters on memory-constrained platforms. When Unity spatializes a stereo clip, it either downmixes to mono internally (losing the stereo image) or spatializes each channel separately (doubling CPU cost and producing strange results). Phase cancellation risk: if a stereo file has out-of-phase content between channels, summing to mono will cancel those frequencies. Always audition mono downmixes. For ambisonic formats (B-format, first-order = 4 channels), use the Ambisonic import option for sound fields that should rotate with the listener. Streaming stereo is 2x the I/O bandwidth of mono, which matters for mobile and Switch.

vs.spatialization: Spatialization requires mono input to work correctly. Feeding stereo into a 3D spatializer wastes memory and produces incorrect spatial positioning because the pre-baked stereo field conflicts with the computed spatial output.

unityAdapter.location: Select an audio clip in the Project window. In the Inspector, the 'Force To Mono' checkbox converts stereo to mono on import. The 'Channels' field in the clip info shows the current channel count.
unityAdapter.importTip: Check 'Force To Mono' on audio clips used for 3D spatialized AudioSources. This halves memory usage and ensures clean spatialization. Unity downmixes by averaging left and right channels. Leave it unchecked for music, stereo ambience, and UI sounds.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Force To Mono averages left and right channels, which can cause phase cancellation artifacts if the original stereo file has out-of-phase content. This may make certain sounds thinner or lose specific stereo elements. Audition the mono version before shipping. Also, Unity's default AudioClip preview in the Inspector does not reflect the Force To Mono setting — use Play mode to hear the actual result.

misconceptions: Stereo sounds are always better quality than mono — stereo and mono are about channel count, not quality. A high-quality mono recording sounds better than a low-quality stereo one. For 3D game audio, mono is the correct choice.
You lose audio quality when converting stereo to mono — you lose the stereo image (left/right separation) but not the audio content itself, unless there is phase cancellation between the channels.
All game audio should be stereo for the best experience — most in-game 3D sounds should be mono for proper spatialization. Only music, ambience beds, and UI should typically be stereo.

Mono sounds boring or flat — mono sounds are made spatial and directional by the game engine's spatialization system. The engine gives them position, panning, and distance. Mono is not the same as unspatialized.

name: Stinger / Musical Cue

beginner.oneLiner: A stinger is a short musical phrase or sound that plays in response to a game event — like the victory jingle when you complete a level or the dramatic chord when an enemy spots you.
beginner.analogy: Think of the dramatic music sting in a movie when the villain appears — DUN DUN DUN! That short burst of music that tells you something important just happened is a stinger. In games, stingers are those memorable musical moments that punctuate key events: finding an item, dying, winning, or triggering a story beat.
beginner.whatToDo: Create short musical clips (1-5 seconds) for key game events: level complete, game over, item pickup, boss encounter, discovery, danger alert. Trigger them as one-shots layered over the background music. Design them in a key and tempo that works with your game's musical style.

intermediate.oneLiner: A stinger is a short, event-triggered musical cue layered over or replacing the current music to punctuate gameplay moments, requiring careful key, tempo, and mixing integration with the underlying score.
intermediate.details: Stingers serve as musical exclamation points — they communicate game state changes through music. Types include: overlay stingers (play on top of existing music), interrupting stingers (temporarily duck or replace the music), transitional stingers (bridge between two music states), and sequential stingers (chain into new music sections). Key design considerations: match the key and tempo of your background music (or be atonal/percussive enough to work over anything), keep them short enough to not interfere with gameplay, and mix them to cut through without masking important gameplay audio.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use stingers for: state transitions (exploration to combat), achievement moments (completing objectives, leveling up), failure states (death, losing a life), environmental discoveries (entering a new area), narrative beats (plot revelations), and danger cues (enemy detection, low health warnings). Trigger them from game event systems with optional quantization to musical beats for smoother integration.

advanced.oneLiner: Stingers are event-driven musical micro-cues designed for seamless integration with adaptive music systems, requiring beat-quantized triggering, harmonic compatibility analysis, and mix automation for transparent layering within the interactive music framework.
advanced.technicalNotes: For seamless stinger integration: (1) Beat-quantize trigger timing — schedule the stinger to start on the next downbeat or sub-beat to maintain musical coherence. In FMOD, use beat callbacks; in Wwise, use music sync points. (2) Design stingers in multiple keys or make them harmonically neutral (percussive, atonal) to work over varying harmonic contexts. (3) Automate the music mix: duck the music bus by 3-6 dB during the stinger to prevent frequency masking. (4) Layer stingers at appropriate priorities: high-priority stingers (boss encounter) should preempt low-priority ones (item pickup) to prevent overlap chaos. (5) Consider tail allowance — let the stinger's reverb tail fade naturally while the music resumes, using a short crossfade.

vs.sound-event: A sound event is any triggered audio playback. A stinger is specifically a musical cue designed to integrate with the game's music system. All stingers are sound events, but most sound events are not stingers.
vs.crossfade: A crossfade smoothly transitions between two audio states. A stinger is a discrete musical accent layered over or between states. Stingers may use crossfades during their entry or exit, but the stinger itself is the musical content, not the transition technique.

unityAdapter.location: Trigger stingers via AudioSource.PlayOneShot() or through FMOD/Wwise stinger systems which provide beat-quantized triggering and automatic ducking.
unityAdapter.apiRef: UnityEngine.AudioSource.PlayOneShot(), FMOD StudioEventEmitter for quantized stingers
unityAdapter.importTip: For simple stingers, use PlayOneShot() on a dedicated stinger AudioSource routed to a stinger mixer group with higher priority than music. For beat-synchronized stingers, use FMOD's music system with transition regions or Wwise's music stinger feature, which automatically quantizes trigger timing to the musical grid.
unityAdapter.gotcha: PlayOneShot() has no timing quantization — the stinger plays immediately on the frame it is called, which may land off-beat relative to the music. For musically precise stingers, use PlayScheduled() with a calculated time that aligns to the next beat, or use dedicated music middleware.

misconceptions: Stingers are just sound effects — stingers are musical cues designed to complement the score. They require musical design considerations like key, tempo, and harmonic context that sound effects do not.
Any short music clip works as a stinger — effective stingers are specifically composed to layer over or transition from the game's music. A random clip may clash harmonically or rhythmically.

Stingers should always match the music's key — percussive and atonal stingers (cymbal crashes, drum fills, tension risers) can work over any harmonic context, making them more versatile in adaptive music systems.

name: Streaming

beginner.oneLiner: Streaming reads audio from disk in small chunks as it plays, using almost no memory but requiring constant disk access.
beginner.analogy: It is like watching a movie on a streaming service instead of downloading the whole file first. You do not need to wait or store the whole thing, but you need a steady connection to your hard drive.
beginner.whatToDo: In the AudioClip import settings, set Load Type to 'Streaming'. Use this for long audio like background music or ambient soundscapes.

intermediate.oneLiner: Streaming reads and decodes small buffers from disk in real-time, keeping memory usage minimal at the cost of disk I/O and slightly higher playback latency.
intermediate.details: Unity reads a small buffer (around 200 KB) from disk ahead of the playback position and decodes it on the fly. This means a 10-minute music track uses the same ~200 KB of memory regardless of length. However, each streaming clip requires its own I/O read, so having too many streaming clips active simultaneously can saturate disk bandwidth, especially on mechanical hard drives or slower mobile storage. There is a slight startup latency as the initial buffer fills.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use for music tracks, long ambient soundscapes, voiceover narration, and any clip over 1 minute. Avoid for short, timing-critical SFX where instant playback is needed.

advanced.oneLiner: Streaming uses a ring-buffer architecture with asynchronous disk reads, decoding on the audio thread with a fixed memory ceiling per clip regardless of duration.
advanced.technicalNotes: Each streaming clip maintains its own read-ahead ring buffer. On platforms with slow I/O (HDD, SD cards), limit simultaneous streaming voices to 2-4 to avoid stuttering. SSDs handle more concurrent streams. The initial fill of the buffer introduces latency of roughly one buffer size worth of audio. PlayScheduled() with streaming clips requires extra lead time to account for buffer fill. On WebGL, streaming is not supported and falls back to Compressed In Memory. The Profiler's Audio pane shows streaming buffer status.

vs.decompress-on-load: Decompress On Load uses vastly more memory but provides instant playback with no I/O. Use it for short, frequent clips.
vs.compressed-in-memory: Compressed In Memory loads the entire compressed clip into RAM, removing disk I/O during playback but using more memory than streaming.

unityAdapter.location: Select an audio file in the Project window > Inspector > Load Type dropdown > Streaming.
unityAdapter.importTip: Always use Vorbis compression with Streaming -- ADPCM and PCM streaming reads more data per second from disk since they are less compressed, increasing I/O load.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Streaming clips cannot use PlayOneShot() reliably for overlapping playback because each playing instance needs its own disk read stream. Use separate AudioSources for overlapping streaming clips.

misconceptions: Streaming does not mean the audio is downloaded from the internet. It means Unity reads from the local disk in small chunks instead of loading the whole clip into RAM.
Streaming clips are not inherently lower quality. The same compression format and quality settings apply -- only the load behavior differs.

name: Subtractive Synthesis

beginner.oneLiner: Subtractive synthesis creates sounds by starting with a bright, buzzy tone and then using filters to remove unwanted frequencies, sculpting the final sound.
beginner.analogy: Think of a sculptor starting with a block of marble and chipping away material to reveal the shape inside. Subtractive synthesis starts with a raw, harmonically rich sound (the block) and carves away frequencies with filters (the chisel) to shape the final tone.
beginner.whatToDo: Use subtractive synthesis for classic synth sounds like basses, leads, and pads. Start with a sawtooth or square wave oscillator, then sweep a low-pass filter down to remove high frequencies and shape the tone. Add an envelope to the filter for dynamic, expressive sounds.

intermediate.oneLiner: Subtractive synthesis generates harmonically rich waveforms from oscillators and shapes them by removing frequency content with resonant filters, controlled by envelopes and modulation sources.
intermediate.details: The classic subtractive synth signal chain is: Oscillator(s) -> Mixer -> Filter -> Amplifier. Oscillators produce raw waveforms: sawtooth (all harmonics), square/pulse (odd harmonics), triangle, and noise. The filter (usually a resonant low-pass) removes harmonics above its cutoff frequency, and the resonance (Q) boosts frequencies near the cutoff for a more pronounced sweep. ADSR envelopes control how the filter cutoff and amplitude change over time. LFOs add periodic modulation like vibrato or filter wobble. Multiple oscillators can be detuned for a thicker sound.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use subtractive synthesis for procedural game sound effects like engine hums, laser sounds, UI feedback, and musical elements. It is efficient and intuitive for real-time use. In game audio, drive the filter cutoff with gameplay parameters (speed, health, intensity) to create sounds that respond dynamically to the game state.

advanced.oneLiner: Subtractive synthesis applies resonant IIR filters (typically 2nd or 4th order ladder/state-variable) to spectrally rich oscillator waveforms, with the filter characteristics modulated by envelopes, LFOs, and external control signals.
advanced.technicalNotes: The oscillator section requires band-limited waveforms (BLIT, polyBLEP, or wavetable) to avoid aliasing. The filter is typically a 2-pole (12 dB/oct) or 4-pole (24 dB/oct) low-pass, implemented as a state-variable or ladder filter for analog-style resonance behavior. Self-oscillation occurs when resonance reaches unity feedback, producing a sine wave at the cutoff frequency. In Unity, implement the oscillator with a phase accumulator and polyBLEP for anti-aliasing, followed by a state-variable filter updated per sample in OnAudioFilterRead. For game integration, expose filter cutoff and resonance as parameters driven by game state. CPU cost is low (1-3 oscillators + 1 filter per voice), making it suitable for real-time procedural audio.

vs.additive-synthesis: Additive synthesis builds up from silence by adding individual harmonics. Subtractive starts with a full spectrum and carves it down. Subtractive is more intuitive and CPU-efficient for most sounds, while additive offers more granular spectral control.
vs.fm-synthesis: FM synthesis generates complex spectra through frequency modulation, producing timbres that are difficult to achieve with subtractive filtering alone. Subtractive synthesis is more predictable and easier to design with but has a more limited timbral range.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in synthesizer. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead or use third-party synth plugins.
unityAdapter.importTip: Build a minimal subtractive synth: phase-accumulator oscillator with polyBLEP anti-aliasing, a state-variable filter with cutoff and resonance parameters, and an ADSR envelope. This gives you a versatile real-time sound source that responds to game parameters.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Naive (non-band-limited) waveform generation produces aliasing artifacts, especially at higher pitches. Always use band-limited oscillator techniques like polyBLEP or pre-computed wavetables to avoid harsh digital artifacts.

misconceptions: Subtractive synthesis is not limited to old-fashioned analog sounds. Modern subtractive synths can produce a wide range of timbres from warm pads to aggressive basses and everything in between.
The filter does not add new frequencies. It only removes or emphasizes existing content from the oscillator. The resonance peak boosts frequencies already present near the cutoff.
You do not need hardware to do subtractive synthesis. Software implementations can accurately model analog subtractive synthesizers and are perfectly suitable for game audio.

name: Switch Container

beginner.oneLiner: A switch container plays a different sound depending on the current game state, like surface type or weather.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a jukebox that automatically changes the song based on the mood in the room. A switch container works the same way — when your game state changes (say, the player walks from grass to stone), it automatically plays the matching footstep sound without you writing if/else code.
beginner.whatToDo: In your middleware tool, create a switch container and assign a game parameter (like surface material). Add a child sound for each possible value (grass, wood, metal, etc.). The middleware will pick the right one at runtime based on the current game state.

intermediate.oneLiner: A switch container selects one child sound object to play based on the runtime value of a switch group or game parameter, enabling data-driven audio logic without code changes.
intermediate.details: Switch containers evaluate a game-synced switch group or state group and route playback to the matching child object. Transition rules define how the container crossfades or cuts between children when the switch value changes mid-playback. You can nest other container types (random, sequence) inside switch children for layered behavior. In Wwise, switch containers use Switch Groups or State Groups; in FMOD, the equivalent is a multi-instrument with parameter sheets.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use switch containers when the sound that plays must change based on a discrete game variable — surface types for footsteps, weapon types for attack sounds, or weather states for ambient beds. Keep switch values coarse (5-10 options max) to stay manageable. For continuous blending between states, consider a blend container instead.

advanced.oneLiner: Switch containers implement state-driven audio routing via lookup tables keyed on game-sync values, with configurable transition behaviors including crossfades, musical sync points, and voice stealing across switch changes.
advanced.technicalNotes: In Wwise, switch containers internally maintain a mapping table from switch/state values to child objects. Transition rules support fade-in/out curves, sync-to-beat, sample-accurate cuts, and custom transition segments. Voice management is critical: when a switch value changes, the outgoing voice may continue playing during crossfade, temporarily doubling voice count. Set the 'continue to play' flag carefully — enabling it keeps the old child playing underneath. For performance, pre-fetch the most likely switch targets. Memory profiling should account for all children being loaded even though only one plays at a time.

vs.random-container: A random container picks from its children using probability weights without regard to game state. A switch container deterministically selects a child based on an external game parameter value.
vs.blend-container: A blend container can play multiple children simultaneously with blended volumes driven by a continuous parameter. A switch container plays exactly one child at a time based on a discrete state value.

unityAdapter.location: Unity has no native switch container. Implement via scripting: use a Dictionary<string, AudioClip> and swap clips on an AudioSource based on game state. Middleware integrations (Wwise/FMOD) expose switch containers natively.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioSource.clip (manual swap), or AkSoundEngine.SetSwitch() for Wwise, RuntimeManager.StudioSystem for FMOD
unityAdapter.importTip: If using Wwise, call AkSoundEngine.SetSwitch(groupName, valueName, gameObject) before posting the event. The switch container in the Wwise project handles the rest.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Forgetting to set the switch value before posting the event results in the default child playing (or silence). Always set switch values in Awake/Start or immediately before playback.

misconceptions: Switch containers are only for footsteps — they work for any state-driven audio selection: weapon types, character voices, environment ambiences, UI states, and more.
Switching sounds always causes a hard cut — transition rules let you crossfade, sync to beat, or play custom transition segments between switch values.
You need code to handle every switch case — the whole point is that the container handles routing in the authoring tool, keeping logic out of your game code.

name: Timbre

beginner.oneLiner: Timbre (pronounced TAM-ber) is the quality of a sound that lets you tell different instruments or voices apart even when they play the same note at the same volume.
beginner.analogy: A piano and a guitar can play the exact same note at the exact same volume, but you can instantly tell them apart. That difference in character — the bright twang of the guitar versus the rich ring of the piano — is timbre. It is the color or texture of sound, the quality that makes each sound source unique.
beginner.whatToDo: Consider timbre when designing sounds for your game. Different timbres communicate different things: bright timbres feel energetic and alert, dark timbres feel heavy and somber, harsh timbres feel dangerous, smooth timbres feel safe. Use timbre variation to differentiate game elements (different enemy types, weapon categories, UI feedback).

intermediate.oneLiner: Timbre is the perceptual attribute of sound determined by its spectral content (harmonic and inharmonic partials), temporal envelope (attack, sustain, decay characteristics), and spectral evolution over time.
intermediate.details: Three main factors determine timbre: (1) Spectral content — the relative amplitudes of harmonics and overtones. A flute has weak high harmonics (pure timbre); a trumpet has strong high harmonics (bright timbre). (2) Temporal envelope — how the sound starts, sustains, and ends. A piano has a sharp attack; a violin bows smoothly. (3) Spectral change over time — how the harmonic content evolves. A plucked string starts bright (many harmonics) and becomes duller as high harmonics decay faster. All three work together to create the perceptual identity of a sound.
intermediate.whenToUse: Leverage timbre in your sound design vocabulary: use timbral contrast to differentiate game elements (bright UI sounds vs. dark ambient sounds), create timbral consistency within categories (all weapons in a class share a timbral family), and evolve timbre dynamically (a charging weapon gets brighter and more harmonically rich as it charges).

advanced.oneLiner: Timbre is the multidimensional perceptual attribute encompassing spectral envelope, spectral flux, temporal envelope, inharmonicity, roughness, and noise content, distinguishing sounds of identical pitch and loudness through differences in their time-frequency representation.
advanced.technicalNotes: Timbre perception has been studied through multidimensional scaling (MDS) experiments, which consistently reveal 2-3 primary perceptual dimensions: spectral centroid (brightness), attack time (percussiveness), and spectral flux (temporal variation). For game audio synthesis, timbre can be parametrically controlled through: additive synthesis (adjusting partial amplitudes), subtractive synthesis (filter cutoff and resonance), wavetable crossfading (morphing between timbres), and FM synthesis (modulation index controls spectral richness). Timbral similarity metrics (spectral contrast, MFCC distance) can be used programmatically to organize sound libraries or match sounds. For procedural audio, dynamic timbre modulation creates more expressive interactive sounds than volume or pitch modulation alone.

vs.harmonics: Harmonics are the measurable frequency components of a sound. Timbre is the perceived quality that results from those harmonics (among other factors). Harmonics are physical; timbre is perceptual. Changing the harmonics changes the timbre, but timbre also depends on temporal characteristics.
vs.pitch: Pitch is the perceived frequency (high or low). Timbre is the perceived quality (bright, dark, harsh, smooth). They are independent — you can change pitch without changing timbre (same instrument, different notes) or change timbre without changing pitch (different instruments, same note).

unityAdapter.location: Unity does not have explicit timbre controls. Shape timbre using AudioMixer EQ effects, AudioLowPassFilter, AudioHighPassFilter, and AudioDistortionFilter. For synthesis, implement timbral control via OnAudioFilterRead.
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioLowPassFilter, AudioHighPassFilter, AudioDistortionFilter, AudioMixer EQ effects
unityAdapter.importTip: Use EQ on mixer groups to shape the timbral character of sound categories: darken ambient sounds with a low-pass, brighten UI sounds with a high shelf boost. For per-source timbral variation, combine slight pitch randomization with filter randomization to keep repeated sounds (footsteps, gunshots) from sounding identical.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.pitch changes both pitch and timbre simultaneously — it is a simple playback speed change. For independent control, use EQ filters for timbre and formant-preserving pitch shift for pitch, though the latter requires external plugins.

misconceptions: Timbre is just the harmonics of a sound — harmonics are a major component, but the attack shape, noise content, and how the spectrum changes over time all contribute to timbre. Removing the attack from a piano recording can make it unrecognizable.

Timbre and tone are the same — in audio engineering, tone usually refers to frequency balance (bright vs. dark), which is one dimension of timbre. Timbre is the complete perceptual identity of a sound including temporal and spectral characteristics.
