You cannot change timbre without changing the sound source — EQ, filters, distortion, and envelope shaping can all modify timbre dramatically. A single recorded sound can be transformed into many different timbral variations through processing.

name: Transient

beginner.oneLiner: A transient is the very first burst of energy at the start of a sound — the initial snap, click, or impact that happens in the first few milliseconds.
beginner.analogy: A transient is like the crack of a whip or the pop when you clap your hands. It is the sharp, sudden start that grabs your attention before the rest of the sound follows. It happens so fast you might not consciously notice it, but without it, impacts feel soft and sounds lack punch.
beginner.whatToDo: Listen to your impact sounds (hits, buttons, footsteps). If they feel weak or mushy, the transient is probably too quiet or missing. Try boosting the very beginning of the waveform, or layer a short click or snap at the start. Conversely, if a sound is too harsh or clicky, reduce or soften the transient.

intermediate.oneLiner: A transient is the short, high-amplitude, broadband burst at the onset of a sound, typically lasting 5-50 milliseconds, that defines its perceived punch, clarity, and attack character.
intermediate.details: Transients contain energy spread across a wide frequency range in a very short time. They are critical for the perception of impact, rhythm, and articulation. In game audio, transients define how punchy a weapon hit feels, how crisp a UI click sounds, and how present footsteps are in a mix. Transient shaping tools can enhance or reduce transients independently of the sound's sustain portion. Key properties: peak level (how loud), duration (how long), spectral content (bright transients cut through mixes), and rise time (instant = sharp, gradual = soft).
intermediate.whenToUse: Emphasize transients for: weapon impacts, UI clicks/confirmations, footsteps, percussive hits, and any sound that needs to cut through a busy mix. Soften transients for: ambient textures, background music, pads, and sounds that should blend rather than stand out. Use transient shapers or compression to control transient behavior.

advanced.oneLiner: A transient is a non-stationary, high-amplitude onset event characterized by rapid spectral flux and broadband energy distribution, requiring sufficient time-domain resolution (low latency, high sample rate) to preserve in digital processing chains.
advanced.technicalNotes: Transient preservation is a key concern in audio processing. Compressors with slow attack times (>10ms) let transients through before gain reduction engages, preserving punch. Lookahead limiters can catch transients but introduce latency. In game audio, buffer sizes directly affect transient timing: a 1024-sample buffer at 44.1kHz adds 23ms of latency, which can soften the perceived timing of transients relative to visual events. Time-frequency tradeoff: short FFT windows capture transients well but lack frequency resolution; long windows have good frequency resolution but smear transients. Transient detection algorithms typically use envelope followers with fast attack and slow release, comparing short-term energy to long-term average.

vs.adsr: The transient corresponds roughly to the attack phase of an ADSR envelope. However, real transients are more complex than a simple linear ramp — they contain broadband spectral content and nonlinear dynamics that ADSR alone cannot capture.
vs.clipping: Clipping occurs when a signal exceeds the maximum level. Transients are the most likely part of a signal to clip because they have the highest peak levels. Proper headroom management during mixing prevents transient clipping.

unityAdapter.location: No specific transient component. Transient quality depends on import settings (compression format), buffer size (Edit > Project Settings > Audio), and playback timing precision.
unityAdapter.importTip: For sounds where the transient is critical (UI clicks, impacts, weapons), use uncompressed PCM or ADPCM format. Vorbis/MP3 compression can smear transients due to the transform coding process. Keep the audio buffer size low (256 or 512 in Project Settings) for responsive transient timing.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's default audio buffer size may be 1024 samples, adding up to 23ms of latency. For rhythm-critical or impact-heavy games, lower this in Edit > Project Settings > Audio > DSP Buffer Size to 'Best Latency'. This increases CPU usage but gives tighter transient response.

misconceptions: Transients are just loud sounds. Transients are specifically the initial onset burst, not the overall loudness. A quiet sound can have a sharp transient, and a loud sound can have a soft transient.

Compression always ruins transients. Compression with a slow attack time (10-30ms) actually preserves transients while controlling the sustain portion, which can make impacts feel punchier relative to the body of the sound.

name: Virtual Voice

beginner.oneLiner: A virtual voice is a sound that the game is tracking and managing but is not actually playing audibly, usually because there are too many sounds or it is too quiet or too far away to be heard.
beginner.analogy: Imagine an orchestra conductor who has 200 musicians but only 50 chairs on stage. The other 150 musicians wait backstage, ready to be called up when a chair opens. Virtual voices are like those backstage musicians — the game knows about the sounds and keeps track of them, but they are not using any CPU to actually produce audio until they become important enough to be heard.
beginner.whatToDo: Set up a virtual voice system by configuring maximum concurrent voices in your audio settings. Let the engine virtualize low-priority or inaudible sounds automatically. When a virtual voice becomes audible again (player moves closer), it resumes playing at the correct position.

intermediate.oneLiner: A virtual voice is a managed sound instance that has been suspended from audio processing while maintaining its playback state (position, parameters), allowing it to seamlessly resume real playback when it becomes audible or gains sufficient priority.
intermediate.details: Virtual voices exist because hardware and CPU can only process a limited number of simultaneous audio streams (typically 32-256 real voices). When the voice limit is reached, the system virtualizes the least important voices based on priority, distance, and audibility. A well-designed virtual voice system tracks the playback position so the sound resumes at the correct point when re-realized. Virtualization criteria include: distance from listener, priority level, calculated audibility (volume after attenuation), and whether the sound is occluded.
intermediate.whenToUse: Implement virtual voices when your game has more potential concurrent sounds than the engine can play. Open-world games, battlefields, and dense environments commonly exceed voice limits. Configure the max real voice count based on platform capabilities: 32-64 for mobile, 64-128 for console, 128-256 for PC. Set appropriate priorities so critical sounds (dialogue, player actions, UI) are never virtualized.

advanced.oneLiner: Virtual voices implement a priority-scheduled resource management system that separates sound instance state tracking from DSP rendering, enabling seamless voice realization/virtualization transitions governed by composite priority scores derived from designer-set priority, distance attenuation, audibility estimation, and runtime importance modifiers.
advanced.technicalNotes: The virtual voice manager maintains a sorted priority queue of all active sound instances. Each frame, it evaluates a composite score: score = base_priority + distance_factor + audibility_factor + gameplay_importance. The top N scores get real DSP voices; the rest are virtualized. Realization (virtual to real) requires careful handling: resume the sample at the correct playback position (time = time_when_virtualized + elapsed_time), apply a short fade-in (5-20 ms) to prevent clicks, and recalculate all spatial parameters. For looping sounds, this is straightforward. For one-shots, decide whether a virtualized one-shot that would have finished playing should be discarded or allowed to live past its natural end. FMOD and Wwise have sophisticated built-in virtual voice systems. In Unity's native audio, AudioSource.priority and maxVoiceCount provide basic voice management, but custom solutions offer more control.

vs.voice-stealing: Voice stealing takes a real voice from one sound and gives it to a higher-priority sound immediately. Virtual voices are the sounds waiting in the background after their voice was stolen. Voice stealing is the mechanism; virtual voices are the resulting state.
vs.priority-system: The priority system determines which sounds are most important. Virtual voices are the outcome of priority decisions — low-priority sounds become virtual when resources are scarce.

unityAdapter.location: Edit > Project Settings > Audio > Max Real Voices and Max Virtual Voices. Set priority on individual AudioSources (0 = highest, 256 = lowest).
unityAdapter.importTip: Set Max Real Voices to 32-64 for mobile or 128 for PC/console. Set Max Virtual Voices to 256-512 to track many background sounds. Assign priority 0 to critical sounds (player, dialogue, UI) and 128-256 to ambient sounds that can safely be virtualized.
unityAdapter.gotcha: When Unity virtualizes an AudioSource, it stops DSP processing but continues tracking time. However, if the AudioSource is using effects (reverb, filters) when re-realized, there may be a brief artifact as the effects rebuild their internal state. Test virtualization transitions during gameplay to catch these glitches.

misconceptions: Virtual voices are wasted resources — virtual voices use negligible CPU compared to real voices since they skip all DSP processing. They provide seamless sound management that would be impossible if you simply stopped and restarted sounds.

All sounds should be real voices — playing hundreds of real voices simultaneously wastes CPU on sounds the player cannot hear. Virtual voices let you efficiently manage large sound counts by only processing what matters.

Virtual voices always resume perfectly — some sounds (those with effects tails, convolution reverb, or complex DSP chains) may have audible artifacts when transitioning from virtual to real. Test critical sounds and ensure high-priority sounds are never virtualized.

name: Voice Stealing

beginner.oneLiner: Voice stealing is when the audio system stops a less important sound to free up a slot for a more important one, because there is a limit to how many sounds can play at once.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a parking lot with 32 spaces. When it is full and a VIP arrives, the attendant asks the least important car to leave so the VIP can park. Voice stealing works the same way — when all your audio channels are in use and a critical sound needs to play (like a player getting hit), the system stops the least important currently playing sound (like a distant ambient bird chirp) to make room.
beginner.whatToDo: Set voice limits on your audio events or groups so that only a reasonable number of instances of the same sound can play at once. For example, limit footsteps to 3-4 simultaneous voices and gunshots to 6-8. When the limit is hit, the oldest or quietest instance gets stolen.

intermediate.oneLiner: Voice stealing is the process of terminating or virtualizing low-priority active voices to free audio channels for higher-priority incoming sounds, using configurable policies to choose which voice to stop.
intermediate.details: Stealing policies determine which voice to terminate: Oldest — stop the voice that has been playing the longest. Works well for repeating sounds like footsteps. Quietest — stop the voice with the lowest current amplitude (considering distance attenuation). Most perceptually transparent. Lowest priority — stop the voice with the lowest assigned priority value. Most control, requires good priority assignment. Farthest — stop the voice farthest from the listener. Good for spatial audio. Stolen voices should fade out over 5-20 ms to avoid clicks. Some systems virtualize stolen voices instead of stopping them — the voice stops producing audio but retains its playback position, so it can resume if a channel becomes available. Voice stealing applies at two levels: per-event instance limiting (max 4 footsteps) and global voice limiting (max 32 total voices).
intermediate.whenToUse: Configure voice stealing for every sound in your game. Set per-event instance limits based on how many concurrent instances are reasonable (footsteps: 3, gunshots: 6, ambient loops: 2 per type). Set global voice limits based on your target platform's CPU budget. Use 'quietest' or 'farthest' stealing for most sounds; use 'lowest priority' for critical sounds that must always play.

advanced.oneLiner: Voice stealing is the polyphony management policy that selects victim voices for termination or virtualization when voice allocation exceeds the channel budget, using a composite scoring function of priority, audibility, age, and distance to minimize perceptual impact.
advanced.technicalNotes: Optimal stealing uses a scoring function: score = w1*priority + w2*(1-audibility) + w3*age + w4*distance. The voice with the lowest score is stolen. Audibility is computed as the voice's current gain after attenuation, bus volume, and fades — voices below -60 dB are effectively inaudible and are prime steal candidates. Virtualization (FMOD's virtual voice system, Wwise's virtual voices) maintains a voice's state (playback position, parameter values) without consuming a physical channel. When a channel becomes available, the virtual voice resumes at the correct position. This is superior to stopping because it preserves temporal continuity. For implementation: maintain a sorted container (min-heap or priority queue) of active voices keyed by steal score. When the channel budget is exceeded, pop the minimum. Fade-out should use a short exponential ramp (5-20 ms) to avoid the DC offset click that an abrupt stop produces. Re-evaluate steal scores periodically (every 50-100 ms) to handle changing distances and volumes.

vs.priority-system: The priority system assigns importance values to sounds. Voice stealing is the action taken based on those priorities when resources are exhausted. Priority defines the ranking; stealing executes it.
vs.audio-pool: The audio pool manages the lifecycle of AudioSource instances. Voice stealing is the policy for what happens when the pool is exhausted — which active voice gives up its pool entry.
vs.sound-pool: Same relationship as with audio pool — sound pool is the resource container, voice stealing is the eviction policy.

unityAdapter.location: Project Settings > Audio > Max Real Voices and Max Virtual Voices. Per-AudioSource priority is set via AudioSource.priority (0 = highest, 256 = lowest). Unity handles basic voice stealing internally based on priority and audibility.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set AudioSource.priority deliberately for every sound category. Player sounds: 0-50. Gameplay-critical sounds: 50-100. General SFX: 100-180. Ambient/background: 180-256. Unity's internal voice manager uses these values when deciding what to virtualize or stop. Also set Max Real Voices in Audio Settings to match your platform budget (32 for mobile, 64+ for desktop).
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's priority value is inverted from intuition: 0 is highest priority and 256 is lowest. A sound with priority 0 will never be stolen by a sound with priority 128. Also, Unity virtualizes voices that are inaudible (below the audibility threshold) regardless of priority — this is usually desirable but can cause confusion if you expect a quiet sound to keep playing.

misconceptions: Voice stealing means players hear sounds cutting off — when done well with short fade-outs and intelligent victim selection (quietest, farthest), voice stealing is inaudible. Players only notice if critical or prominent sounds are stolen.

More voices is always better — every active voice costs CPU. Unlimited voices on mobile can tank performance. A well-tuned stealing system with 32 voices sounds better than 128 unmanaged voices fighting for CPU time.
All sounds should have the same priority — if everything is high priority, nothing is. Carefully tiering priorities is essential for voice stealing to work effectively.

name: Volume

beginner.oneLiner: Volume controls how loud or quiet a sound plays in your game, ranging from silent to full blast.
beginner.analogy: Volume is like a faucet — turn it up to get more sound, turn it down for less. But just like water pressure, if every faucet in the house is fully open, you get problems (audio clipping and a muddy mix).
beginner.whatToDo: Never set all your sounds to maximum volume. Start with sound effects around 70-80% and music around 40-60%, then adjust relative to each other. Always give players separate volume sliders for music, SFX, and voice.

intermediate.oneLiner: Volume (gain) controls the amplitude of an audio signal, measured linearly (0.0-1.0) in game engines or logarithmically in decibels (dB) for mixing.
intermediate.details: Human hearing is logarithmic — halving the linear volume (0.5) does not sound half as loud. A perceptual halving of loudness is roughly -10 dB (linear ~0.3). Game engines typically expose a 0-1 linear scale on AudioSource components, but mixing should be done in dB using an Audio Mixer. Key mixing levels: 0 dB is full scale (no attenuation), -6 dB is half amplitude, -20 dB is very quiet, -80 dB is effectively silent. Leave headroom in your mix — if the master bus peaks above 0 dB, you get clipping (harsh digital distortion).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use per-source volume for relative balance between individual sounds. Use Audio Mixer groups for category-level control (all SFX, all music). Expose mixer group volumes to player settings. Use volume automation (fading) for transitions between game states.

advanced.oneLiner: Volume is amplitude scaling applied as a linear multiplier on sample values, with perceptual loudness following a logarithmic (Weber-Fechner) relationship to physical intensity.
advanced.technicalNotes: Unity's AudioSource.volume is a linear multiplier (0.0-1.0) applied to the sample buffer. For perceptually uniform fades, convert to dB: dB = 20 * log10(linear). A common fade curve uses an exponential ramp in linear space. Audio Mixer faders operate in dB (-80 to +20 dB). For ducking (lowering music during dialogue), use Audio Mixer snapshots or exposed parameters driven by script. RMS (root mean square) metering gives a better loudness representation than peak metering for balancing. LUFS (Loudness Units Full Scale) is the broadcast standard but rarely used in game engines natively — middleware like FMOD provides loudness metering.

vs.gain: Volume and gain are often used interchangeably in game audio. Technically, gain refers to amplification (can exceed 1.0/0dB) while volume typically implies attenuation (0 to 1.0). In practice, game engine 'volume' is gain clamped to 0-1.
vs.loudness: Volume/gain is an objective measure of signal amplitude. Loudness is a psychoacoustic perception influenced by frequency content, duration, and context. Two sounds at the same volume can have very different perceived loudness.

unityAdapter.location: AudioSource component > Volume slider. For mix-level control: Window > Audio > Audio Mixer. Player-facing settings should control Audio Mixer group volumes via exposed parameters.
unityAdapter.importTip: Use Audio Mixer groups from the start — create groups for Music, SFX, Voice, and Ambience. Expose each group's volume parameter so you can connect them to player settings sliders later.
unityAdapter.gotcha: AudioSource.volume is linear, but the Audio Mixer uses decibels. Do not set mixer volume to 0 dB thinking it is 'zero volume' — 0 dB is full volume. Silence on a mixer is -80 dB. Use Mathf.Log10(linearValue) * 20 to convert a 0-1 slider to dB for mixer control.

misconceptions: Setting volume to 0.5 does not make a sound 'half as loud' to the listener. Due to logarithmic hearing, it sounds only slightly quieter. A perceptual halving requires roughly -10 dB (linear ~0.3).
Making all sounds louder does not make your game sound better — it destroys dynamic range and causes clipping. Good audio is about relative balance, not absolute loudness.

name: WAV

beginner.oneLiner: WAV is an uncompressed audio file format that preserves full sound quality at the cost of large file sizes.
beginner.analogy: Think of WAV like a raw, unedited photograph — it keeps every detail but takes up a lot of storage space.
beginner.whatToDo: Use WAV files as your master/source assets during development. Convert to compressed formats like OGG for your final build to save disk space and memory.

intermediate.oneLiner: WAV (Waveform Audio File Format) stores uncompressed PCM audio data with full fidelity, commonly at 16-bit/44.1kHz or 24-bit/48kHz.
intermediate.details: WAV files use the RIFF container format and typically hold linear PCM data. A one-minute stereo WAV at 44.1kHz/16-bit is roughly 10 MB. WAV supports various sample rates, bit depths, and channel counts. Because there is no decoding overhead, WAV is ideal for low-latency sound effects that must play instantly.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use WAV for short sound effects (footsteps, UI clicks, impacts) where decode latency matters. For music or ambient tracks, prefer a compressed format like OGG to reduce memory usage.

advanced.oneLiner: WAV is a RIFF-based container most commonly carrying linear PCM payloads, offering zero decode cost at the expense of memory bandwidth and storage.
advanced.technicalNotes: WAV files have a 4 GB size limit due to the 32-bit chunk size fields in the RIFF header. For larger recordings, RF64 or BWF extensions exist but are rarely relevant in games. Unity decompresses WAV imports at build time; the runtime format depends on your import compression settings (Vorbis, ADPCM, or PCM). Streaming large WAV files avoids the full memory footprint but adds I/O latency. ADPCM is a middle ground — roughly 3.5:1 compression with minimal decode cost.

vs.ogg: OGG uses lossy Vorbis compression giving ~10:1 size reduction with perceptible but often acceptable quality loss. WAV is lossless but much larger. Use WAV for short SFX; OGG for music and ambience.
vs.aiff: AIFF is functionally equivalent to WAV but uses the IFF container format from Apple. Both store uncompressed PCM. WAV is more universally supported on Windows toolchains; AIFF is common in macOS/iOS pipelines.

unityAdapter.location: Import any .wav file into your Assets folder. Configure compression in the Audio Clip Inspector under 'Compression Format'.
unityAdapter.importTip: Set short SFX clips to 'Decompress On Load' with PCM or ADPCM compression. For longer clips, use 'Compressed In Memory' with Vorbis to save RAM.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity re-encodes WAV files at build time based on your import settings. Your source WAV quality matters, but the in-game format is determined by the compression setting — not the original file format.

misconceptions: WAV does not always mean uncompressed — the container can technically hold compressed data, but in practice nearly all WAV files in game development use uncompressed PCM.
Using WAV in your final build does not automatically mean better audio quality for players — if Unity re-encodes to Vorbis at build time, you get the same result as importing OGG.

name: Waveform

beginner.oneLiner: A waveform is the shape of a sound wave over time. Different shapes produce different tones, from smooth and pure to buzzy and harsh.
beginner.analogy: Imagine drawing a line that goes up and down over time, like a heart monitor. A smooth, rolling wave (sine) sounds pure and clean like a flute. A jagged, square wave sounds buzzy like an old video game. The shape of the line determines the character of the sound.
beginner.whatToDo: Understanding waveforms helps you pick the right starting point when designing sounds. If you use any synthesizer or procedural audio in your game, you'll choose between sine, square, sawtooth, and triangle waves as building blocks.

intermediate.oneLiner: A waveform describes how amplitude changes over time. Basic shapes — sine, square, sawtooth, and triangle — are the building blocks of synthesis, each with a distinct harmonic spectrum.
intermediate.details: A sine wave contains only one frequency (the fundamental) and sounds pure and featureless. A square wave contains odd harmonics (1x, 3x, 5x, 7x...) at decreasing amplitudes, producing a hollow, buzzy tone. A sawtooth wave contains all harmonics (odd and even), giving it a bright, rich sound. A triangle wave has odd harmonics like a square but they fall off much faster, sounding softer. White noise contains all frequencies at equal energy with random amplitude. These basic shapes are combined, filtered, and modulated to create complex sounds. In game audio, waveform visualization is also used to display recorded audio in DAWs and editors.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use sine waves for clean tones, UI feedback beeps, and sub-bass rumble. Square waves work well for retro game sounds and chiptune aesthetics. Sawtooth is great for alarm sounds and leads. Mix and filter these in a synth to create unique SFX procedurally, which saves memory vs. storing samples.

advanced.oneLiner: Waveform shapes define the harmonic spectrum via their Fourier series decomposition, serving as the basis for additive, subtractive, and wavetable synthesis in procedural game audio.
advanced.technicalNotes: By the Fourier theorem, any periodic waveform can be decomposed into a sum of sine waves at integer multiples of the fundamental frequency. A sawtooth's Fourier series is: sum((-1)^(n+1) * sin(2*pi*n*f*t) / n) for n=1 to infinity. Band-limiting is critical in digital synthesis: naive generation of square/sawtooth waves produces aliasing because harmonics above Nyquist fold back into the audible range. Solutions include PolyBLEP (polynomial bandlimited step), wavetable synthesis with pre-filtered tables per octave, or oversampling + filtering. For game audio, wavetable or PolyBLEP oscillators provide efficient, alias-free waveform generation. Unity's OnAudioFilterRead callback allows direct sample-level waveform generation for procedural audio.

vs.frequency: Frequency determines the pitch (how fast the waveform repeats). Waveform shape determines the timbre (the character or color of the sound). Same frequency, different waveform = same pitch, different sound.
vs.amplitude: Amplitude scales the waveform vertically (louder or quieter). Waveform shape determines the distribution of energy across harmonics.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in waveform generator. Use OnAudioFilterRead for procedural synthesis or AudioClip.Create for runtime-generated clips.
unityAdapter.apiRef: MonoBehaviour.OnAudioFilterRead(float[] data, int channels), AudioClip.Create(name, samples, channels, sampleRate, stream)
unityAdapter.importTip: For simple procedural tones (UI beeps, test signals), generate samples in OnAudioFilterRead: data[i] = Mathf.Sin(2f * Mathf.PI * frequency * phase) for a sine wave. Increment phase by 1/sampleRate each sample.
unityAdapter.gotcha: OnAudioFilterRead runs on the audio thread, not the main thread. Do not call Unity API methods, allocate memory, or use locks inside it. Pre-compute lookup tables and use lock-free communication (volatile fields or NativeArray) to pass parameters from the main thread.

misconceptions: Real-world sounds are almost never simple waveforms. A gunshot, a voice, or wind are complex combinations of many frequencies changing over time. Basic waveforms are building blocks for synthesis, not descriptions of natural sound.

A 'square wave' in digital audio is not truly square. A mathematically perfect square wave has infinite harmonics, which would alias in a digital system. Properly generated digital square waves are band-limited.
The waveform display in your DAW is not showing individual samples at normal zoom levels. It's showing a summary (peak or RMS envelope). Zoom in very far to see actual sample values.

name: Wavetable Synthesis

beginner.oneLiner: Wavetable synthesis plays back pre-drawn waveform shapes stored in a table and can smoothly morph between different shapes to create evolving sounds.
beginner.analogy: Imagine a flipbook where each page has a different wave drawn on it. Playing one page over and over makes a steady tone. Flipping through the pages smoothly morphs the sound from one character to another, like blending between a smooth flute and a buzzy saw.
beginner.whatToDo: Use wavetable synthesis for evolving pads, rich leads, and dynamic textures. Load or draw different waveforms into the table positions and use an LFO or envelope to scan through them over time for continuously changing timbres.

intermediate.oneLiner: Wavetable synthesis reads single-cycle waveforms from a table at the oscillator frequency, with the ability to interpolate between adjacent waveforms in the table to smoothly morph timbre over time.
intermediate.details: A wavetable is an array of single-cycle waveforms (typically 256-2048 samples each). The oscillator reads through one waveform at the desired pitch using a phase accumulator and interpolation. A table position parameter selects which waveform to read; intermediate positions blend between adjacent waveforms. Waveforms can be hand-drawn, generated mathematically, or extracted from recorded sounds via FFT analysis. The morphing capability makes wavetable synthesis excellent for sounds that evolve over time. Modern wavetable synths often include multiple oscillators, filters, and modulation routing.
intermediate.whenToUse: Use wavetable synthesis for evolving ambient textures, UI sound design, and musical elements that need timbral movement. In game audio, drive the wavetable position with a game parameter (health, speed, danger level) to create sounds that organically reflect gameplay state. It is more CPU-efficient than additive synthesis while still offering rich timbral variety.

advanced.oneLiner: Wavetable synthesis uses band-limited, pre-computed single-cycle waveforms stored at multiple frequency ranges (mip-mapped) with inter-table interpolation for alias-free timbral morphing.
advanced.technicalNotes: To prevent aliasing, each waveform is stored at multiple frequency ranges (mip-maps), with higher harmonics progressively removed for higher-pitched playback. The oscillator selects the appropriate mip-map level based on the current playback frequency. Sample-accurate interpolation (linear or cubic) within the waveform and crossfading between adjacent table entries provides smooth morphing. In Unity, implement in OnAudioFilterRead: maintain a phase accumulator, index into the current waveform array with cubic interpolation, and blend between wavetable positions. Pre-generate mip-mapped tables at import time using FFT -> zero high bins -> IFFT. Wavetable synthesis is extremely efficient: one table lookup and interpolation per sample, regardless of spectral complexity.

vs.additive-synthesis: Additive synthesis computes each partial in real time, offering dynamic per-partial control at higher CPU cost. Wavetable synthesis pre-computes the spectra into tables, trading real-time flexibility for efficiency.
vs.granular-synthesis: Granular synthesis works with small chunks of recorded audio for textural effects. Wavetable works with single-cycle waveforms for pitched, musical synthesis. Granular is better for textures; wavetable is better for tonal sounds.
vs.subtractive-synthesis: Subtractive synthesis generates raw waveforms and filters them. Wavetable synthesis reads from pre-shaped waveforms, enabling complex spectral content without a filter stage, though filters are often added for further shaping.

unityAdapter.location: No built-in wavetable synthesizer. Implement via OnAudioFilterRead or use third-party plugins.
unityAdapter.importTip: Store wavetables as float arrays. Use a phase accumulator to index into the current table with cubic interpolation. Blend between two table entries based on a morph parameter. For alias-free playback, pre-generate multiple versions of each waveform with progressively fewer harmonics.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Without mip-mapping (multi-resolution tables), wavetable synthesis will produce aliasing at higher pitches. Always generate band-limited versions of your wavetables for different frequency ranges, similar to texture mip-mapping in graphics.

misconceptions: Wavetable synthesis is not the same as sample playback. Wavetables contain single-cycle waveforms (one period) that loop continuously, while samplers play back longer recordings of real instruments.
Morphing between wavetable positions does not crossfade between sounds like a DJ. It interpolates between single-cycle waveform shapes, producing smooth timbral transitions within a continuous tone.
Wavetable synthesis is not limited to the waveforms it ships with. You can create custom wavetables from any source, including FFT analysis of recorded sounds.

name: Wet/Dry Mix

beginner.oneLiner: Wet/dry mix controls the balance between the original unprocessed sound (dry) and the effect-processed version (wet).
beginner.analogy: Think of mixing paint: the dry signal is your original white paint, and the wet signal is a color dye. At 0% wet, you have pure white (original sound). At 100% wet, you have only the color (only the effect). Somewhere in between gives you a tinted blend of both.
beginner.whatToDo: When adding reverb, delay, or other effects, start with a low wet mix (10-30%) and increase until the effect is noticeable but not overwhelming. For game audio, keep the dry signal dominant so sounds remain clear and the effects add atmosphere without muddying the mix.

intermediate.oneLiner: The wet/dry mix parameter blends the unprocessed input signal (dry) with the effect output (wet), typically as a percentage or ratio, controlling effect intensity without changing the effect's own parameters.
intermediate.details: Wet/dry can be implemented as a linear crossfade (dry * (1-mix) + wet * mix) or as independent level controls for wet and dry. When effects are on a send/return bus, the send level acts as the wet control while the dry signal passes through unchanged on the original bus. Using sends is generally preferred in game audio because it allows multiple sources to share one effect instance and provides independent control. A mix of 100% wet is sometimes used for creative purposes (e.g., a fully reverbed ghostly voice).
intermediate.whenToUse: Use wet/dry mix to fine-tune how present an effect is. For reverb on dialogue, keep it subtle (15-25% wet). For atmospheric ambiences, you can push higher. When using effects as sends on a mixer bus, control the wet amount via the send level rather than the effect's own mix parameter (which should be set to 100% wet on a return bus).

advanced.oneLiner: Wet/dry mix implements a linear or equal-power crossfade between the unprocessed and processed signal paths, with the crossfade law affecting perceived loudness at intermediate mix positions.
advanced.technicalNotes: A linear crossfade (out = dry*(1-x) + wet*x) produces a -6 dB dip at 50% mix for correlated signals and -3 dB for uncorrelated signals. An equal-power crossfade (out = dry*cos(x*pi/2) + wet*sin(x*pi/2)) maintains consistent perceived loudness. For game audio effects buses, set the effect plugin to 100% wet and control the blend via send levels, which gives you independent control and allows parallel processing. In Unity's Audio Mixer, effect plugins are typically inserted inline (series), so their internal wet/dry parameter controls the blend. For send-based workflows, use the Send and Receive effects to route signals to a return group with 100% wet processing.

vs.send-effect: A send routes a copy of the signal to an effects bus where the effect runs at 100% wet. Wet/dry mix is the parameter that controls the blend when the effect is inserted directly on the signal path (inline). Both achieve effect blending but with different routing architectures.

unityAdapter.location: Individual effect component parameters (e.g., AudioReverbFilter mix) or Audio Mixer send levels
unityAdapter.apiRef: AudioReverbFilter.dryLevel and AudioReverbFilter.room; or AudioMixer Send/Receive effects for bus-based routing
unityAdapter.importTip: For per-source effects like AudioReverbFilter, adjust the Dry Level and Room parameters to control the blend. For bus-based mixing, use the Audio Mixer's Send effect to route audio to a return group with the effect at 100% wet, and control the send level to set the wet amount.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's AudioReverbFilter has separate parameters for dry level, room level, and reflections level rather than a single wet/dry knob. Adjust these together to achieve the desired blend, but be careful not to boost both dry and wet levels simultaneously, which will make the output louder than the input.

misconceptions: A 50% wet/dry mix does not mean the effect is at half strength. It means the dry and wet signals are equally blended, and the perceived effect intensity depends on the effect's own parameters.
Setting wet/dry to 100% wet does not remove the original sound from the mix if the effect passes the dry signal through internally. Always check whether the effect plugin has its own dry path.
Wet/dry mix is not the only way to control effect intensity. You can also adjust the effect's own parameters (e.g., reverb decay time) or use send levels for more flexible control.

name: Windowing Function

beginner.oneLiner: A windowing function smoothly fades the edges of an audio chunk to zero before analysis, preventing glitchy artifacts when breaking sound into pieces.
beginner.analogy: Imagine cutting a photo into tiles with sharp scissors — the edges of each tile have hard cuts that do not match the neighboring tiles. Now imagine using a cookie cutter that feathers the edges, blending them smoothly to transparent. Windowing does this for audio: it gently fades the start and end of each chunk so there are no harsh discontinuities when the chunk is analyzed or reassembled.
beginner.whatToDo: When calling Unity's GetSpectrumData, choose a window type. BlackmanHarris is the safest default — it minimizes false frequency readings. You can experiment with Hann or Hamming, but avoid Rectangular unless you know what you are doing.

intermediate.oneLiner: A window function is a smooth taper (Hann, Hamming, Blackman-Harris, etc.) multiplied with an audio buffer before FFT to reduce spectral leakage caused by the assumption of signal periodicity at buffer boundaries.
intermediate.details: The FFT assumes the input signal repeats perfectly outside the analysis window. In reality, the signal is not periodic within the window, so the discontinuity at the edges creates spectral leakage — energy smearing across all frequency bins, producing false frequency content. Window functions solve this by tapering the signal to zero at both edges. Common windows: Hann (good general purpose, moderate frequency resolution), Hamming (slightly better sidelobe suppression than Hann), Blackman-Harris (excellent sidelobe suppression, wider main lobe), Rectangular (no tapering, best frequency resolution but worst leakage). The tradeoff: stronger tapering reduces leakage but widens the main lobe, reducing frequency resolution.
intermediate.whenToUse: Always apply a window function before FFT for audio analysis. Use Hann for general spectral analysis and audio processing (STFT). Use Blackman-Harris when you need to detect quiet frequencies near loud ones (high dynamic range analysis). Use Hamming for speech processing. The rectangular window is only appropriate when the signal is exactly periodic within the frame (which rarely happens with real audio).

advanced.oneLiner: Window functions are finite-support tapering kernels whose spectral characteristics (main-lobe width, sidelobe attenuation, sidelobe rolloff) determine the tradeoff between frequency resolution and spectral leakage in STFT analysis-synthesis systems.
advanced.technicalNotes: Key window metrics: main-lobe width at -3 dB (frequency resolution), highest sidelobe level (leakage floor), sidelobe rolloff rate. Hann: 1.5-bin main lobe, -31 dB sidelobes, 18 dB/octave rolloff. Blackman-Harris (4-term): 2.0-bin main lobe, -92 dB sidelobes. For STFT processing, the window choice and overlap determine the COLA (Constant Overlap-Add) condition — the sum of overlapping windows must be constant across all time positions for perfect reconstruction. Hann with 50% or 75% overlap satisfies COLA. For analysis-synthesis (spectral modification), use the Hann window at 75% overlap with a synthesis window to ensure perfect reconstruction. Kaiser windows offer a continuous parameter (beta) to tune the resolution-leakage tradeoff. Flat-top windows sacrifice resolution for amplitude accuracy, useful for calibration. In game audio, windowing matters for spectrum visualizers, pitch detection, and any STFT-based effect.

vs.fft: FFT is the transform; the window function is pre-processing applied before the FFT. Without proper windowing, FFT results are contaminated by spectral leakage.
vs.overlap-add: Windowing segments the input; overlap-add reconstructs the output. Together with FFT/IFFT they form the STFT processing pipeline. The window choice directly determines the required overlap for artifact-free reconstruction.

unityAdapter.location: Used as a parameter in AudioSource.GetSpectrumData and AudioListener.GetSpectrumData. The FFTWindow enum provides: Rectangular, Triangle, Hamming, Hanning, Blackman, BlackmanHarris.
unityAdapter.apiRef: FFTWindow enum — FFTWindow.BlackmanHarris, FFTWindow.Hanning, FFTWindow.Hamming, FFTWindow.Blackman, FFTWindow.Triangle, FFTWindow.Rectangular
unityAdapter.importTip: Use FFTWindow.BlackmanHarris as your default for spectrum visualization — it gives the cleanest display with minimal spectral leakage. Switch to FFTWindow.Hanning if you need slightly better frequency resolution for pitch detection.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Unity's FFTWindow enum uses 'Hanning' (with a trailing g) rather than the mathematically standard name 'Hann.' They are the same thing. Also, the window is applied internally by GetSpectrumData — do not apply your own window on top, or you will double-window the data.

misconceptions: Windowing is optional for FFT analysis — without a window function (or equivalently, using a rectangular window), you get severe spectral leakage that makes the results unreliable for real audio signals.

One window function is best for everything — each window trades off frequency resolution against sidelobe suppression. The best choice depends on whether you need precision (narrow main lobe) or cleanliness (low sidelobes).
Windowing reduces audio quality — windowing is a tool for analysis and processing. When used properly with overlap-add, it enables perfect reconstruction of the original signal.

name: Wwise

beginner.oneLiner: Wwise is a professional audio middleware tool by Audiokinetic that provides sound designers with a powerful visual environment to build complex, interactive audio systems for games.
beginner.analogy: If FMOD is like GarageBand for game audio — approachable and musician-friendly — then Wwise is more like a professional recording studio control room. It has more knobs, more routing options, and more ways to fine-tune every detail. It takes more time to learn, but it gives you incredibly precise control over how every sound in your game behaves, reacts, and adapts to gameplay.
beginner.whatToDo: Download Wwise from Audiokinetic's website (free for indie projects under a sound limit). Install the Wwise Unity integration. Create events in the Wwise authoring tool, generate SoundBanks, and trigger them in Unity using AkSoundEngine.PostEvent(). Audiokinetic provides extensive tutorials and certification courses.

intermediate.oneLiner: Wwise (Wave Works Interactive Sound Engine) is an audio middleware platform featuring a hierarchical actor-mixer architecture, event-action system, state/switch-based adaptive audio, and SoundBank packaging for cross-platform game deployment.
intermediate.details: Wwise uses a hierarchical object model: sounds are organized in an Actor-Mixer hierarchy where properties (volume, pitch, effects, positioning) cascade from parent to child containers. Events trigger actions (play, stop, pause, set state, set switch) on these objects. Switches and States alter which sounds play based on game context (e.g., surface type switches for footsteps, or a 'combat' state that changes the music mix). RTPCs provide continuous parameter control. The Interactive Music Hierarchy handles adaptive music with transitions, stingers, and cue-based segment sequencing. Wwise Spatial Audio provides geometry-based occlusion, diffraction, and room/portal systems. SoundBanks package audio assets and metadata for runtime loading. The Wwise Profiler connects to a running game for real-time monitoring of voices, CPU usage, and memory.
intermediate.whenToUse: Wwise excels in projects with complex interactive audio needs: adaptive music systems, dialogue with dynamic ducking, environment-aware spatial audio, and large-scale voice management. It is the standard in AAA development and is increasingly used in indie games. Choose Wwise over FMOD when you need advanced spatial audio features (geometry diffraction), sophisticated music systems, or when your sound designer is already experienced with it.

advanced.oneLiner: Wwise implements a hierarchical actor-mixer graph with cascading property evaluation, event-driven action dispatch, state machine-based sound selection, RTPC-driven continuous parameter binding, and a SoundBank-packaged asset pipeline with streamed and in-memory loading strategies.
advanced.technicalNotes: The actor-mixer hierarchy evaluates properties top-down: the master audio bus sets global properties, which are overridden or blended by child buses, random/sequence containers, and leaf sound objects. Evaluation is lazy — only active voices compute their full property chain. Sound selection uses switch containers (discrete selection based on game state) and blend containers (crossfaded layering based on RTPC values). The music engine runs a separate state machine: music segments have entry cues, exit cues, and transition rules that govern how segments connect. Stingers are overlay sounds triggered by game events during music playback. Wwise Spatial Audio uses computational geometry to calculate diffraction paths around obstacles and through portals between rooms, feeding the results to the spatialization pipeline. Memory management: SoundBanks are loaded/unloaded explicitly. Sounds can be set to 'in memory' or 'streamed from disk.' Stream-from-disk uses a ring buffer with configurable prefetch. Wwise's SDK is C++ with wrappers for C#, Java, and other languages.

vs.fmod: Wwise's actor-mixer hierarchy provides more granular organizational control than FMOD's event-focused model. FMOD's timeline-based event editor is more intuitive for simple behaviors. Wwise's spatial audio (geometry diffraction) is more advanced out of the box. Both are free for indie developers.
vs.audio-mixer: Unity's Audio Mixer is a simple static mixer. Wwise provides a full hierarchical mixing system with dynamic routing, ducking, and state-aware mixing that changes based on gameplay context.
vs.rtpc: RTPCs are a core feature of Wwise, mapping game parameters to audio properties via designer-defined curves. This is Wwise's primary mechanism for adaptive audio behavior.

unityAdapter.location: Install via the Wwise Launcher's Unity integration. Wwise adds a Wwise menu and AkComponents to the Inspector. Events and banks are authored in the Wwise authoring tool (separate application).
unityAdapter.apiRef: AkSoundEngine.PostEvent(string eventName, GameObject gameObject), AkSoundEngine.SetRTPCValue(string rtpcName, float value), AkSoundEngine.SetState(string stateGroup, string state), AkSoundEngine.SetSwitch(string switchGroup, string switchValue, GameObject gameObject)
unityAdapter.importTip: Attach AkGameObj to any GameObject that emits or receives sound — this is Wwise's equivalent of Unity's AudioSource/AudioListener. Use AkEvent components for designer-friendly event triggering without code. Generate SoundBanks in the Wwise authoring tool and ensure they are in your Unity StreamingAssets/Wwise folder.
unityAdapter.gotcha: Wwise completely replaces Unity's audio system — do not use AudioSource or AudioListener with Wwise. Wwise uses its own listener system (AkAudioListener). Also, SoundBanks must be regenerated in the Wwise authoring tool every time sounds or events are modified; forgetting to rebuild banks is the most common source of missing audio.

misconceptions: Wwise is only for AAA studios — Wwise is free for projects using fewer than 500 media assets and is increasingly popular with indie developers. The learning curve is steeper than FMOD but the documentation is excellent.

Wwise and FMOD do the same things — while they share many features, they have different architectures and strengths. Wwise's spatial audio and music systems are more feature-rich; FMOD's event editor is more approachable. The choice depends on your project's needs and your team's experience.

You need a dedicated sound designer to use Wwise — while Wwise shines with a sound designer on the team, solo developers can use it effectively for straightforward event-based audio with some learning investment.
